FORTIOS-ADMIN-001.risk: When the admin GUI/API is reachable over plaintext HTTP (or HTTPS redirect is off), an attacker on any transit or LAN segment can passively sniff or actively man-in-the-middle the session and capture administrator credentials and session cookies in cleartext, then log in as a full administrator. Cleartext management planes are also the exact attack surface abused by FortiOS admin-interface auth-bypass bugs (CVE-2022-40684, CVE-2024-55591). Business impact: total device compromise, firewall policy tampering, VPN pivot into the internal network, and log deletion to cover tracks.
steps: Inventory which interfaces expose management: `show system interface | grep -A20 allowaccess` and note any interface listing `http` or `telnet`.
Enable global HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect: `config system global` then `set admin-https-redirect enable` then `end`.

Remove HTTP (and Telnet) from every management-facing interface, keeping only secure protocols: `config system interface` / `edit port1` / `set allowaccess ping https ssh` / `next` / `end` (repeat per interface).
Move the HTTPS admin port off the default if desired: `config system global` / `set admin-sport 8443` / `end` (optional, reduces automated scanning noise).
Enforce strong TLS for the admin GUI: `config system global` / `set admin-https-ssl-versions tlsv1-2 tlsv1-3` / `set strong-crypto enable` / `end`.
Confirm no firewall policy or local-in policy permits inbound TCP/80 to the management IP from untrusted zones.
Re-run the scanner (or `get system global`) to confirm `admin-https-redirect` is enable and `admin-sport` is not 80.
Document the change and notify admins of the new HTTPS-only URL/port.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-001.gui: FortiGate: System > Settings > Administration Settings (set 'Redirect to HTTPS', HTTPS port, and enable administrative access); per-interface: Network > Interfaces > edit interface > Administrative Access (untick HTTP/Telnet, keep HTTPS/SSH). FortiManager: Device Manager > Device & Groups > [device] > System > Settings, or push via Provisioning Templates > System Templates / CLI Template.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-001.cli:
config system global
  set admin-https-redirect enable
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-001.verify: get system global | grep admin-https-redirect  ->  admin-https-redirect : enable   (and: show system interface port1 | grep allowaccess  ->  set allowaccess ping https ssh  with no 'http')
FORTIOS-ADMIN-001.rollback: config system global / set admin-https-redirect disable / end and re-add http to the interface allowaccess (set allowaccess ping https ssh http). Reverting re-exposes credentials in cleartext; only do so temporarily for a legacy tool that cannot use HTTPS, and restrict it with a local-in policy.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-001.impact: Non-disruptive to data-plane traffic and no reboot. Any admin currently using plain HTTP is redirected to HTTPS; active HTTP-only GUI sessions are dropped and must reconnect over HTTPS. No impact to SSL-VPN, IPsec, or HA.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Administration Settings / secure management access (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.1)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide — 'Disable unused administrative protocols (HTTP/Telnet)' and 'Change the default administrative access ports'
FortiOS Administration Guide — System > Settings: Administrative access / HTTPS redirect
PSIRT FG-IR-22-377 / CVE-2022-40684 and FG-IR-24-535 / CVE-2024-55591 (FortiOS admin-interface authentication bypass)
CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information

FORTIOS-ADMIN-002.risk: An admin idle timeout longer than 5 minutes leaves authenticated GUI/CLI sessions open on unattended workstations. An attacker with brief physical or remote-desktop access to an admin's machine (or who steals a still-valid session cookie) can ride the live session to reconfigure the firewall without re-authenticating. Business impact: privilege abuse, policy tampering, and audit-trail actions attributed to a legitimate admin.
steps: Check the current value: `get system global | grep admintimeout`.
Set the idle timeout to 5 minutes or less: `config system global` / `set admintimeout 5` / `end`.
(Optional, SSH grace) tighten the login grace period: `config system global` / `set admin-ssh-grace-time 60` / `end`.

If some console workflows legitimately need a longer idle window, isolate them with a per-profile override (`config system accprofile` / `edit <profile>` / `set admintimeout-override enable` / `set admintimeout <minutes>`) rather than raising the global value.
Verify the setting took effect with `get system global | grep admintimeout`.
Communicate to administrators that sessions now expire after 5 minutes of inactivity.
Re-run the scanner to confirm the finding clears.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-002.gui: FortiGate: System > Settings > Administration Settings > 'Idle timeout' (minutes). FortiManager: Device Manager > [device] > System > Settings > Idle timeout, or Provisioning Templates > System Templates.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-002.cli:
config system global
  set admintimeout 5
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-002.verify: get system global | grep admintimeout  ->  admintimeout : 5
FORTIOS-ADMIN-002.rollback: config system global / set admintimeout <old-value> / end. Increasing it again re-widens the unattended-session window; prefer keeping <=5 and using a per-profile override for exceptions.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-002.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Only effect is that idle admin sessions are logged out sooner; active/in-use sessions are unaffected. No impact to VPN, HA, or traffic.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Ensure administrator idle timeout is configured (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.2)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Set administrator idle timeout'
FortiOS Administration Guide — Administration Settings (admintimeout)
CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration
PCI-DSS 8.2.8, NIST SP 800-53 AC-11 (Session Lock)

FORTIOS-ADMIN-003.risk: A password-policy minimum length below 12 characters lets administrators set short passwords that fall quickly to offline cracking or online brute-force/credential-stuffing against the exposed admin login. Combined with no lockout, an attacker can iterate through common and leaked passwords until an admin account yields. Business impact: full administrative takeover of the firewall and the perimeter it protects.
steps: Enable the password policy and scope it to admin passwords: `config system password-policy` / `set status enable` / `set apply-to admin-password` .

Set the minimum length to 12 or greater and add complexity: `set minimum-length 12` / `set min-upper-case-letter 1` / `set min-lower-case-letter 1` / `set min-number 1` / `set min-non-alphanumeric 1` / `end`.
Verify with `get system password-policy`.

Force existing admins to update on next login by rotating current passwords (`config system admin` / `edit <name>` / `set password <new>` / `next` / `end`) since the policy applies to new passwords going forward.
Consider enabling `set change-4-characters enable` so a password change must differ by at least 4 characters.
Where possible, replace local passwords with certificate/token or remote (RADIUS/LDAP/TACACS+) admin auth for stronger controls.
Re-run the scanner to confirm minimum-length is >= 12.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-003.gui: FortiGate: System > Settings > Password Policy (enable, set Minimum length = 12 and character requirements). FortiManager: Provisioning Templates > System Templates > Password Policy, or Device Manager > [device] > System > Settings.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-003.cli:
config system password-policy
  set minimum-length 12
  set min-upper-case-letter 1
  set min-lower-case-letter 1
  set min-number 1
  set min-non-alphanumeric 1
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-003.verify: get system password-policy | grep minimum-length  ->  minimum-length : 12   (and status : enable, apply-to includes admin-password)
FORTIOS-ADMIN-003.rollback: config system password-policy / set minimum-length <old> / end (or set status disable to remove enforcement). Lowering the requirement weakens all future admin passwords; do not disable without a compensating control.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-003.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. The policy governs the next password set/change; existing passwords keep working until changed. No impact to VPN/HA/sessions.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Ensure 'Password Policy' is enabled with adequate length (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.3)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Enforce a strong password policy'
FortiOS Administration Guide — config system password-policy
CWE-521: Weak Password Requirements
PCI-DSS 8.3.6, NIST SP 800-53 IA-5 (Authenticator Management)

FORTIOS-ADMIN-004.risk: The built-in 'admin' account name is universally known, so it is the first target for password-spray and brute-force campaigns and is hard-coded into many FortiOS exploitation tools. Leaving the default-named super-administrator in place gives attackers a guaranteed valid username, halving the guessing problem. Business impact: faster credential compromise and a well-known foothold for full device takeover.
steps: Create a new super-administrator with a non-obvious name, a strong password, trusted hosts, and MFA: `config system admin` / `edit "fgtadmin"` / `set accprofile "super_admin"` / `set password <strong-password>` / `set trusthost1 10.10.10.0 255.255.255.0` / `set two-factor fortitoken` / `set fortitoken <serial>` / `next` / `end`.
Log out of the current session and log back in as the new account to confirm it has full access.
Once confirmed, delete the default account (FortiOS permits deleting 'admin' as long as another super_admin exists and you are not logged in as it): `config system admin` / `delete "admin"` / `end`.
If policy forbids deletion (e.g., recovery procedures reference it), instead harden it hard: set a long random password, restrict `trusthost1` to the management subnet, and enable two-factor.
Update break-glass/runbook documentation and password vault with the new emergency account name.
Confirm no automation, backups, or FortiManager scripts still authenticate as 'admin'.
Re-run the scanner to confirm the default 'admin' finding clears.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-004.gui: FortiGate: System > Administrators > Create New > Administrator (new named super_admin), then delete the 'admin' entry. FortiManager: Device Manager > [device] > System > Administrators (admin accounts are per-device; manage there or via CLI Template).
FORTIOS-ADMIN-004.cli:
config system admin
  edit "fgtadmin"
    set accprofile "super_admin"
    set password <strong-password>
    set trusthost1 10.10.10.0 255.255.255.0
    set two-factor fortitoken
    set fortitoken <serial>
  next
end
# Log in as the new account and verify full access, THEN remove the default:
config system admin
  delete "admin"
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-004.verify: show system admin | grep -i 'edit "admin"'  ->  (no output; the default account no longer exists) and show system admin | grep -c 'set accprofile "super_admin"'  ->  shows your replacement super_admin
FORTIOS-ADMIN-004.rollback: Recreate the account: config system admin / edit "admin" / set accprofile super_admin / set password <pw> / next / end. Note you can never delete the last remaining super_admin, so keep at least one privileged account at all times to avoid lockout.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-004.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot, no data-plane effect. Operational care required: verify the replacement super_admin works BEFORE deleting 'admin' to avoid administrative lockout. Any tooling still using 'admin' will fail after deletion and must be updated.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Ensure default 'admin' account is renamed/removed (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.4)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Change the default administrator account name'
FortiOS Administration Guide — Administrators (config system admin)
CWE-1188: Initialization of a Resource with an Insecure Default (well-known default account)

FORTIOS-ADMIN-005.risk: An administrator account protected by a password alone falls entirely to any single credential-compromise event: phishing, reuse of a breached password, keylogging, or brute-force against the exposed login. Without a second factor, that one secret equals full firewall control. Business impact: single-factor admin takeover leading to policy tampering, VPN pivot, and log destruction.
steps: Provision a second factor for the admin: assign a hardware/software FortiToken (User & Authentication > FortiTokens) or enroll the device in FortiToken Cloud.

Enable two-factor on the account: `config system admin` / `edit <admin-name>` / `set two-factor fortitoken` / `set fortitoken <serial>` / `set email-to admin@example.com` / `next` / `end` (use `set two-factor fortitoken-cloud` for FortiToken Cloud).
For token delivery via email/SMS, ensure `config system email-server` (and an SMS gateway if used) is configured so codes can be sent.
Repeat for every administrator returned by the scanner, including break-glass accounts (store their seed/token securely offline).
Prefer centralizing MFA by binding admins to a RADIUS/SAML server that enforces MFA where an enterprise IdP exists.
Test login end-to-end to confirm the second factor is prompted before granting access.
Re-run the scanner to confirm no admin has two-factor=disable.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-005.gui: FortiGate: System > Administrators > edit admin > enable 'Two-factor Authentication' and select the FortiToken; tokens under User & Authentication > FortiTokens. FortiManager: Device Manager > [device] > System > Administrators (per-device); FortiToken management via the managed device or FortiAuthenticator/FortiToken Cloud.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-005.cli:
config system admin
  edit <admin-name>
    set two-factor fortitoken
  next
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-005.verify: show system admin <admin-name> | grep two-factor  ->  set two-factor fortitoken   (repeat per admin; none should read 'disable')
FORTIOS-ADMIN-005.rollback: config system admin / edit <admin-name> / set two-factor disable / next / end. Disabling MFA returns the account to single-factor and re-exposes it to credential theft; keep at least the break-glass token seed archived to avoid lockout when a token is lost.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-005.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Risk of admin lockout if a token is unassigned or the delivery channel (email/SMS/FortiToken Cloud) is misconfigured, so validate one account fully before rolling out to all. No effect on VPN/HA/data plane.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Ensure multi-factor authentication for administrators (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.5)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Enable two-factor authentication for administrators'
FortiOS Administration Guide — Two-factor authentication / FortiToken
CWE-308: Use of Single-factor Authentication

FORTIOS-ADMIN-006.risk: An admin account with no trusted-host restriction can be logged into from any source IP that can reach the management interface, including the internet if management is exposed. This is precisely the condition that turned FortiOS admin-interface auth-bypass bugs (CVE-2022-40684, CVE-2024-55591) into mass-exploitation events. Business impact: worldwide brute-force exposure and immediate exploitability of any admin-plane CVE, leading to full compromise.
steps: Identify the legitimate management source networks (jump hosts / NOC subnets).

Restrict each admin to those networks using up to ten trusthost entries: `config system admin` / `edit <admin-name>` / `set trusthost1 10.10.10.0 255.255.255.0` / `set trusthost2 192.168.50.5 255.255.255.255` / `next` / `end`.
Apply the same restriction to every admin and API user; leaving even one at 0.0.0.0/0 negates the control.
Add defense-in-depth with a local-in policy that permits management ports only from the trusted subnet and denies all else: `config firewall local-in-policy`.
Ensure the management interface's `allowaccess` exposes only https/ssh and only on internal/management interfaces, not the WAN.
Double-check that YOUR current IP is inside the trusted set before committing, to avoid locking yourself out.
Verify with `show system admin <name> | grep trusthost` and test a login from an out-of-scope IP (should be refused).
Re-run the scanner to confirm the finding clears for all accounts.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-006.gui: FortiGate: System > Administrators > edit admin > enable 'Restrict login to trusted hosts' and add subnets. FortiManager: Device Manager > [device] > System > Administrators > Trusted Hosts (per-device), or push a CLI Template.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-006.cli:
config system admin
  edit <admin-name>
    set trusthost1 <trusted-network/mask>
  next
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-006.verify: show system admin <admin-name> | grep trusthost  ->  set trusthost1 10.10.10.0 255.255.255.0   (and trusthost1 is NOT 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0)
FORTIOS-ADMIN-006.rollback: config system admin / edit <admin-name> / set trusthost1 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 / next / end restores unrestricted login. Reverting re-exposes the account to the entire reachable network; if you accidentally lock yourself out, recover via console (physical/serial) which bypasses trusthost.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-006.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Real lockout risk if the administrator's own IP is not included in the trusted set — always add your management subnet first. No impact to SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA or user traffic.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Ensure trusted hosts are configured for administrators (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.6)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Restrict administrative access to trusted hosts'
FortiOS Administration Guide — Trusted hosts / Local-in policy
PSIRT FG-IR-22-377 / CVE-2022-40684 (mass exploitation of exposed, unrestricted admin interfaces)
CWE-284: Improper Access Control
PCI-DSS 7.2/1.2.1, NIST SP 800-53 AC-6 & AC-17

FORTIOS-ADMIN-007.risk: Administrators assigned the built-in super_admin (or prof_admin) profile have unrestricted read-write over the entire device and all VDOMs, so any compromise of that account, or a mistake by that operator, has maximum blast radius. Day-to-day operators using an all-powerful profile violate least privilege and remove any technical guardrail. Business impact: a single phished operator or careless change can wipe policies, disable logging, or reroute traffic device-wide.
steps: Design least-privilege roles for how staff actually work (e.g., network read-write but no VPN/UTM write, log read-only).

Create a custom access profile: `config system accprofile` / `edit "netops_rw"` / `set sysgrp read-write` / `set netgrp read-write` / `set fwgrp read-write` / `set loggrp read` / `set vpngrp read` / `set utmgrp read` / `set authgrp read` / `next` / `end`.
Reassign day-to-day admins to the custom profile: `config system admin` / `edit <admin-name>` / `set accprofile "netops_rw"` / `next` / `end`.
Keep super_admin only on the primary administrator plus one break-glass account (see FORTIOS-ADMIN-019).
If the device uses VDOMs, scope admins to specific VDOMs with `set vdom` instead of granting global super_admin.
Validate each reassigned admin can still perform required tasks and is denied out-of-scope actions.
Re-run the scanner to confirm no day-to-day admin uses super_admin/prof_admin.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-007.gui: FortiGate: System > Admin Profiles > Create New (set per-area Read/Read-Write/None), then System > Administrators > edit admin > set Administrator Profile to the custom profile. FortiManager: Device Manager > [device] > System > Admin Profiles / Administrators, or Provisioning Templates.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-007.cli:
config system accprofile
  edit "netops_rw"
    set sysgrp read-write
    set netgrp read-write
    set fwgrp read-write
    set loggrp read
    set vpngrp read
    set utmgrp read
    set authgrp read
  next
end
config system admin
  edit <admin-name>
    set accprofile "netops_rw"
  next
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-007.verify: get system admin <admin-name> | grep accprofile  ->  accprofile : netops_rw   (not super_admin/prof_admin)
FORTIOS-ADMIN-007.rollback: config system admin / edit <admin-name> / set accprofile super_admin / next / end restores full privilege. Reverting removes the least-privilege guardrail; if a custom profile is too tight and blocks a needed task, widen the specific group (e.g., set vpngrp read-write) rather than reverting to super_admin.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-007.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Operational risk: an over-restrictive profile can block a needed administrative action, so validate the role before broad rollout. No impact to VPN/HA/traffic.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Ensure least-privilege admin profiles are used (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.7)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Create least-privilege administrator profiles'
FortiOS Administration Guide — config system accprofile / Administrator profiles
CWE-269: Improper Privilege Management
PCI-DSS 7.2.1, NIST SP 800-53 AC-3 & AC-6(1)

FORTIOS-ADMIN-008.risk: A REST API administrator (token) with no trusted-host restriction can be used from any source IP that reaches the management interface. API keys are frequently embedded in scripts, CI pipelines, and config-management repos where they leak; without a source-IP allowlist, a leaked key is usable from anywhere on the internet. Business impact: a stolen API token grants scripted, headless control of the firewall with no MFA prompt.
steps: Determine the fixed IPs of systems that legitimately call the API (automation servers, CI runners, monitoring).

Bind each API user to those hosts via the trusthost sub-table: `config system api-user` / `edit "<api-user>"` / `config trusthost` / `edit 1` / `set type ipv4-trusthost` / `set ipv4-trusthost 10.10.10.0 255.255.255.0` / `next` / `end` / `next` / `end`.
Add additional entries (edit 2, edit 3, ...) for each automation source; avoid broad ranges.
Reinforce with a local-in policy permitting the HTTPS admin port only from those hosts.
Rotate the API key after tightening, in case it was previously exposed: regenerate under System > Administrators > the REST API admin.
Confirm the automation still authenticates from its allowed IP and is refused from others.
Verify with `show system api-user <api-user>` and re-run the scanner.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-008.gui: FortiGate: System > Administrators > edit the REST API Admin > 'Trusted Hosts' (add source subnets). FortiManager: manage the target device's api-user via CLI Template / Device Manager (API admins are per-device).
FORTIOS-ADMIN-008.cli:
config system api-user
  edit "<api-user>"
    config trusthost
      edit 1
        set type ipv4-trusthost
        set ipv4-trusthost 10.10.10.0 255.255.255.0
      next
    end
  next
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-008.verify: show system api-user <api-user>  ->  shows a populated 'config trusthost' block with your automation subnet(s) and no 0.0.0.0/0 entry
FORTIOS-ADMIN-008.rollback: Remove the restriction: config system api-user / edit "<api-user>" / config trusthost / delete 1 / end / next / end. Reverting makes the token usable from any reachable IP; if automation breaks after tightening, add the caller's real egress IP as an additional trusthost rather than removing all entries.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-008.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Risk of breaking API automation if the calling host's source IP is not included (watch for NAT/egress-IP differences). No impact to interactive admins, VPN, HA, or traffic.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Ensure trusted hosts restrict API/admin access (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.8)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Restrict administrative access to trusted hosts' (applies to REST API admins)
FortiOS REST API / Administration Guide — config system api-user (trusthost)
CWE-284: Improper Access Control
PCI-DSS 7.2.1, NIST SP 800-53 AC-6

FORTIOS-ADMIN-009.risk: An interface permits the administrative GUI/API over plaintext HTTP. Credentials, API tokens and session cookies traverse the network in cleartext, so any attacker on a transit or LAN segment can passively sniff or actively man-in-the-middle the session and authenticate as a full administrator, then tamper with policy or pivot via VPN.
steps: List what the interface exposes: show system interface <intf> | grep allowaccess.
Remove http (and telnet) while keeping only secure protocols: config system interface / edit <intf> / set allowaccess ping https ssh / next / end.
Enable the global HTTPS redirect so any HTTP request is upgraded: config system global / set admin-https-redirect enable / end.
Enforce strong admin TLS: config system global / set admin-https-ssl-versions tlsv1-2 tlsv1-3 / set strong-crypto enable / end.
Confirm no local-in or firewall policy still permits inbound TCP/80 to this interface.
Re-run the scanner or check the interface to confirm http is gone.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-009.gui: Network > Interfaces > edit <interface> > Administrative Access: untick HTTP (and Telnet), keep HTTPS/SSH. FortiManager: Device Manager > [device] > interface administrative access.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-009.cli:
config system interface
  edit <intf>
    set allowaccess ping https ssh
  next
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-009.verify: show system interface <intf> | grep allowaccess  ->  set allowaccess ping https ssh   (no 'http')
FORTIOS-ADMIN-009.rollback: Re-add http to allowaccess (set allowaccess ping https ssh http). This re-exposes credentials in cleartext; only do so briefly for a legacy tool and restrict it with a local-in policy.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-009.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Active plain-HTTP GUI sessions on this interface are dropped and must reconnect over HTTPS. No effect on data traffic, VPN or HA.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - secure administrative access (CIS 2.1.1)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' - disable HTTP/Telnet management
CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
NIST SP 800-53 AC-17, SC-8

FORTIOS-ADMIN-010.risk: An interface permits Telnet, an unencrypted remote-shell protocol. Administrator credentials and the entire CLI session cross the network in cleartext, trivially captured by an on-path attacker who can then log in and reconfigure the firewall.
steps: Check the interface: show system interface <intf> | grep allowaccess.
Remove telnet, keep SSH for CLI: config system interface / edit <intf> / set allowaccess ping https ssh / next / end.
Ensure SSH uses strong algorithms: config system global / set strong-crypto enable / set admin-ssh-grace-time 60 / end.
Optionally restrict admin source IPs with trusted hosts on each admin account (config system admin / edit <name> / set trusthost1 <mgmt-subnet>).
Verify telnet is no longer present.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-010.gui: Network > Interfaces > edit <interface> > Administrative Access: untick Telnet, keep SSH.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-010.cli:
config system interface
  edit <intf>
    set allowaccess ping https ssh
  next
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-010.verify: show system interface <intf> | grep allowaccess  ->  no 'telnet' in the list
FORTIOS-ADMIN-010.rollback: Re-add telnet to allowaccess. Strongly discouraged - Telnet is cleartext; use SSH instead.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-010.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Any active Telnet CLI session drops; reconnect via SSH. No effect on traffic, VPN or HA.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - disable Telnet (CIS 2.1.1)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' - use SSH not Telnet
CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
PCI-DSS 2.2.7, NIST SP 800-53 AC-17

FORTIOS-ADMIN-011.risk: A WAN (internet-facing) interface responds to ICMP ping. While low severity on its own, it confirms the device's presence and IP to any internet scanner, aiding reconnaissance and target selection for follow-on attacks against exposed services.
steps: Confirm the WAN interface responds to ping: show system interface <wan> | grep allowaccess (look for 'ping').
Remove ping unless an upstream monitor genuinely requires it: config system interface / edit <wan> / set allowaccess https ssh / next / end (on WAN, typically remove all management access).
If a monitoring system needs reachability, restrict ICMP with a narrow local-in policy to the monitor's source IP instead of enabling ping for the world.
Verify ping is removed.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-011.gui: Network > Interfaces > edit <WAN interface> > Administrative Access: untick PING.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-011.cli:
config system interface
  edit <wan>
    set allowaccess https ssh
  next
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-011.verify: show system interface <wan> | grep allowaccess  ->  'ping' no longer listed
FORTIOS-ADMIN-011.rollback: Re-add ping to the WAN allowaccess (set allowaccess ... ping) if a monitoring dependency is discovered.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-011.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. The WAN interface stops replying to ICMP echo; external ping-based monitoring of this IP fails until re-scoped via a local-in policy.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - restrict interface administrative access
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' - minimise WAN-facing services
CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information
NIST SP 800-53 SC-7

FORTIOS-ADMIN-012.risk: A WAN (internet-facing) interface exposes management protocols (HTTPS/SSH/SNMP/FGFM) directly to the internet. This is the single highest-risk misconfiguration on a FortiGate: the management plane is exactly the surface abused by the FortiOS admin-interface authentication-bypass CVEs (CVE-2022-40684, CVE-2024-55591, both CISA KEV / exploited in the wild). An attacker who reaches it can brute-force or bypass authentication and take full administrative control of the firewall, then pivot into the internal network.
steps: Identify the exposure: show system interface <wan> | grep allowaccess (look for https/ssh/snmp/fgfm).
Remove ALL management protocols from every WAN interface: config system interface / edit <wan> / unset allowaccess / next / end (or restrict to only what a monitor strictly needs).
Provide admin access instead via a dedicated management interface on a trusted segment, or via SSL-VPN/IPsec then manage over the internal IP.

If remote admin over the internet is unavoidable, restrict it to specific source IPs with trusted hosts on each admin (config system admin / edit <name> / set trusthost1 <admin-ip>/32) AND a local-in policy limiting the mgmt ports to those IPs.
Move admin ports off defaults and enable HTTPS redirect + strong crypto.
Patch FortiOS to a build fixed for the admin-interface CVEs.
Re-run the scanner to confirm no management protocols remain on WAN.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-012.gui: Network > Interfaces > edit <WAN interface> > Administrative Access: untick HTTPS/SSH/SNMP/FMG-Access. Provide admin access via a dedicated mgmt interface or VPN. Policy & Objects > Local In Policy to constrain any remaining mgmt access.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-012.cli:
config system interface
  edit <wan>
    unset allowaccess
  next
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-012.verify: show system interface <wan> | grep allowaccess  ->  empty / only non-management entries; from outside, the admin ports no longer respond
FORTIOS-ADMIN-012.rollback: Re-add the required protocol(s) to allowaccess, but ALWAYS pair with trusted-host restrictions and a local-in policy limiting source IPs. Never expose SSH/HTTPS/SNMP/FGFM to the whole internet.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-012.impact: Non-disruptive to data traffic and no reboot, but any admin/FortiManager currently reaching the device over its WAN IP will lose access - ensure an alternative management path (dedicated interface or VPN) is in place first. Internet SNMP polling will stop.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - do not expose management on WAN (CIS 2.1.7)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' - restrict administrative access, trusted hosts
PSIRT FG-IR-22-377 / CVE-2022-40684, FG-IR-24-535 / CVE-2024-55591 (admin auth bypass, CISA KEV)
CWE-284: Improper Access Control
NIST SP 800-53 AC-17, SC-7; PCI-DSS 1.2.1

FORTIOS-ADMIN-013.risk: Without an uppercase-character requirement, admins can set all-lowercase passwords that occupy a far smaller keyspace and match common wordlist patterns, making offline cracking and online guessing markedly faster. Business impact: weakened admin credentials that shorten the time-to-compromise of the management plane.
steps: Confirm the password policy is enabled and scoped to admins: `config system password-policy` / `set status enable` / `set apply-to admin-password`.
Require at least one uppercase letter: `set min-upper-case-letter 1` / `end` (raise to 2 for stricter environments).
Set the companion complexity classes at the same time for a complete policy (see FORTIOS-ADMIN-014/015/016).
Rotate existing admin passwords so the stronger rule takes effect for current accounts.
Verify with `get system password-policy | grep min-upper-case-letter`.
Re-run the scanner to confirm the requirement is >= 1.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-013.gui: FortiGate: System > Settings > Password Policy > character requirements ('Upper case'). FortiManager: Provisioning Templates > System Templates > Password Policy.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-013.cli:
config system password-policy
  set min-upper-case-letter 1
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-013.verify: get system password-policy | grep min-upper-case-letter  ->  min-upper-case-letter : 1
FORTIOS-ADMIN-013.rollback: config system password-policy / set min-upper-case-letter 0 / end removes the requirement. Reverting weakens future admin passwords; keep >=1 unless an external IdP enforces complexity instead.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-013.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Applies to the next password change; existing passwords keep working. No effect on VPN/HA/sessions.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Password complexity requirements (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.3 family)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Enforce a strong password policy'
FortiOS Administration Guide — config system password-policy (min-upper-case-letter)
CWE-521: Weak Password Requirements
PCI-DSS 8.3.6, NIST SP 800-53 IA-5(1)

FORTIOS-ADMIN-014.risk: Without a lowercase-character requirement, admins can choose passwords lacking character diversity, reducing the effective keyspace and making dictionary and brute-force attacks against the admin login faster. Business impact: weaker admin credentials and a shorter path to management-plane compromise.
steps: Ensure the policy is enabled: `config system password-policy` / `set status enable` / `set apply-to admin-password`.
Require at least one lowercase letter: `set min-lower-case-letter 1` / `end`.
Combine with the other complexity classes (upper/number/special) for a complete policy.
Rotate current admin passwords so the rule applies to existing accounts.
Verify with `get system password-policy | grep min-lower-case-letter`.
Re-run the scanner to confirm the requirement is >= 1.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-014.gui: FortiGate: System > Settings > Password Policy > character requirements ('Lower case'). FortiManager: Provisioning Templates > System Templates > Password Policy.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-014.cli:
config system password-policy
  set min-lower-case-letter 1
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-014.verify: get system password-policy | grep min-lower-case-letter  ->  min-lower-case-letter : 1
FORTIOS-ADMIN-014.rollback: config system password-policy / set min-lower-case-letter 0 / end removes the requirement, weakening future admin passwords. Keep >=1 unless complexity is enforced by a remote IdP.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-014.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Governs the next password change only; existing passwords remain valid. No impact to VPN/HA/traffic.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Password complexity requirements (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.3 family)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Enforce a strong password policy'
FortiOS Administration Guide — config system password-policy (min-lower-case-letter)
CWE-521: Weak Password Requirements
PCI-DSS 8.3.6, NIST SP 800-53 IA-5(1)

FORTIOS-ADMIN-015.risk: Without a numeric-digit requirement, admin passwords can be purely alphabetic, which are the easiest to guess with standard wordlists and rule-based cracking. This lowers the cost of brute-forcing the exposed admin login. Business impact: weaker admin credentials and faster time-to-compromise of the firewall.
steps: Ensure the policy is enabled and scoped to admin passwords: `config system password-policy` / `set status enable` / `set apply-to admin-password`.
Require at least one digit: `set min-number 1` / `end`.
Set the remaining complexity classes at the same time (upper/lower/special).
Rotate existing admin passwords to apply the rule to current accounts.
Verify with `get system password-policy | grep min-number`.
Re-run the scanner to confirm the requirement is >= 1.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-015.gui: FortiGate: System > Settings > Password Policy > character requirements ('Numbers'). FortiManager: Provisioning Templates > System Templates > Password Policy.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-015.cli:
config system password-policy
  set min-number 1
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-015.verify: get system password-policy | grep min-number  ->  min-number : 1
FORTIOS-ADMIN-015.rollback: config system password-policy / set min-number 0 / end removes the requirement and weakens future admin passwords; keep >=1 unless enforced by a remote IdP.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-015.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Applies to the next password change only. No impact to VPN/HA/sessions.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Password complexity requirements (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.3 family)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Enforce a strong password policy'
FortiOS Administration Guide — config system password-policy (min-number)
CWE-521: Weak Password Requirements
PCI-DSS 8.3.6, NIST SP 800-53 IA-5(1)

FORTIOS-ADMIN-016.risk: Without a special-character requirement, admin passwords are limited to alphanumerics, shrinking the keyspace and matching predictable patterns favored by cracking rules. Special characters meaningfully increase brute-force cost. Business impact: weaker admin credentials that reduce the effort required to seize the management plane.
steps: Ensure the policy is enabled and scoped to admins: `config system password-policy` / `set status enable` / `set apply-to admin-password`.
Require at least one non-alphanumeric character: `set min-non-alphanumeric 1` / `end`.
Set upper/lower/number requirements together so the full complexity policy is coherent.
Rotate existing admin passwords so the rule applies to current accounts.
Verify with `get system password-policy | grep min-non-alphanumeric`.
Re-run the scanner to confirm the requirement is >= 1.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-016.gui: FortiGate: System > Settings > Password Policy > character requirements ('Special characters'). FortiManager: Provisioning Templates > System Templates > Password Policy.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-016.cli:
config system password-policy
  set min-non-alphanumeric 1
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-016.verify: get system password-policy | grep min-non-alphanumeric  ->  min-non-alphanumeric : 1
FORTIOS-ADMIN-016.rollback: config system password-policy / set min-non-alphanumeric 0 / end removes the requirement, weakening future admin passwords. Keep >=1 unless a remote IdP enforces complexity.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-016.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Governs the next password change only; current passwords keep working. No impact to VPN/HA/traffic.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Password complexity requirements (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.3 family)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Enforce a strong password policy'
FortiOS Administration Guide — config system password-policy (min-non-alphanumeric)
CWE-521: Weak Password Requirements
PCI-DSS 8.3.6, NIST SP 800-53 IA-5(1)

FORTIOS-ADMIN-017.risk: With password expiry disabled (expire-day 0), admin passwords live forever, so a credential that leaks via phishing, a breached reuse, or an old backup remains valid indefinitely with no forced rotation to cut the attacker off. Business impact: long-lived compromised credentials that provide persistent, undetected access to the firewall.
steps: Enable expiry and set a maximum age on the password policy: `config system password-policy` / `set status enable` / `set apply-to admin-password` / `set expire-status enable` / `set expire-day 90` / `end`.
Choose an interval consistent with policy (e.g., 90 days); note that `expire-day` is only settable when `expire-status` is enable.
Communicate the rotation schedule to administrators so expirations are not a surprise.
Pair with reuse prevention (FORTIOS-ADMIN-018) so rotation cannot cycle back to old passwords.
For accounts backed by a remote IdP (RADIUS/LDAP), enforce expiry there instead and document the delegation.
Verify with `get system password-policy | grep expire`.
Re-run the scanner to confirm expiry is configured (expire-day != 0).
FORTIOS-ADMIN-017.gui: FortiGate: System > Settings > Password Policy > enable 'Password expiration' and set days. FortiManager: Provisioning Templates > System Templates > Password Policy.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-017.cli:
config system password-policy
  set expire-status enable
  set expire-day 90
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-017.verify: get system password-policy | grep expire  ->  expire-status : enable  and  expire-day : 90
FORTIOS-ADMIN-017.rollback: config system password-policy / set expire-status disable / end (or set expire-day 0). Reverting means admin passwords never expire again; only disable if rotation is enforced by an external IdP.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-017.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Admins will be forced to change their password when the interval elapses — schedule communications to avoid surprise lockouts at expiry. No impact to VPN/HA/traffic.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Ensure password expiry is configured (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.3 family)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Enforce a strong password policy' (expiration)
FortiOS Administration Guide — config system password-policy (expire-status / expire-day)
CWE-262: Not Using Password Aging
PCI-DSS 8.3.9, NIST SP 800-53 IA-5(1)

FORTIOS-ADMIN-018.risk: When password reuse is allowed, an administrator can 'rotate' their password back to a previous (possibly already-leaked) value, defeating the purpose of expiry and history controls. An attacker who learned an old password can regain access after a rotation cycle. Business impact: rotation becomes cosmetic and compromised credentials stay effective.
steps: Disable password reuse on the policy: `config system password-policy` / `set status enable` / `set apply-to admin-password` / `set reuse-password disable` / `end`.
On FortiOS versions that support it, set a history depth as well: `set reuse-password-limit 5` (blocks the last N passwords).
Combine with expiry (FORTIOS-ADMIN-017) so rotation is both required and non-repeating.
Inform administrators they must choose genuinely new passwords at each change.
Verify with `get system password-policy | grep reuse-password`.
Re-run the scanner to confirm reuse is disabled.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-018.gui: FortiGate: System > Settings > Password Policy > disable 'Allow password reuse' (or set history count). FortiManager: Provisioning Templates > System Templates > Password Policy.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-018.cli:
config system password-policy
  set reuse-password disable
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-018.verify: get system password-policy | grep reuse-password  ->  reuse-password : disable
FORTIOS-ADMIN-018.rollback: config system password-policy / set reuse-password enable / end re-permits reuse, which undermines rotation; avoid unless a remote IdP enforces history.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-018.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Only effect is that admins cannot re-select a recent password at change time. No impact to VPN/HA/sessions.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Ensure password reuse is restricted (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.3 family)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Enforce a strong password policy' (reuse)
FortiOS Administration Guide — config system password-policy (reuse-password / reuse-password-limit)
CWE-262: Not Using Password Aging (weak rotation control)
PCI-DSS 8.3.7, NIST SP 800-53 IA-5(1)

FORTIOS-ADMIN-019.risk: More than two accounts holding super_admin means an oversized population of unrestricted, device-wide administrators, each an equally valuable target and each a potential source of a catastrophic misconfiguration. The more super-admins exist, the higher the odds one is phished, reuses a breached password, or is left over from a departed employee. Business impact: expanded attack surface for total device takeover and weaker accountability for privileged change.
steps: Enumerate privileged accounts: `show system admin | grep -B1 'set accprofile "super_admin"'` and list who owns each.
Decide the minimal set: one primary administrator plus one break-glass account (2 total).
Create/verify least-privilege custom profiles for everyone else (see FORTIOS-ADMIN-007).
Downgrade extra super-admins to the appropriate custom profile: `config system admin` / `edit "<daily-ops-admin>"` / `set accprofile "netops_rw"` / `next` / `end`.
Delete stale/departed privileged accounts entirely: `config system admin` / `delete "<redundant-admin>"` / `end`.
Ensure the two retained super-admins have MFA and trusted hosts; store the break-glass credential offline in a vault.
Verify the count of super_admin accounts is <= 2 and re-run the scanner.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-019.gui: FortiGate: System > Administrators — review the 'Profile' column, reassign or delete surplus super_admin accounts; profiles under System > Admin Profiles. FortiManager: Device Manager > [device] > System > Administrators.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-019.cli:
# Reassign day-to-day admins to a least-privilege profile
config system admin
  edit "<daily-ops-admin>"
    set accprofile "netops_rw"
  next
end
# Remove redundant privileged accounts (retain primary + one break-glass)
config system admin
  delete "<redundant-admin>"
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-019.verify: show system admin | grep -c 'set accprofile "super_admin"'  ->  2 or fewer
FORTIOS-ADMIN-019.rollback: Re-grant super_admin where genuinely required: config system admin / edit "<name>" / set accprofile super_admin / next / end. Note the last super_admin can never be deleted; keep at least the primary and break-glass to avoid lockout.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-019.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Operational risk: an operator downgraded to a custom profile may lose an action they relied on — validate roles first. Deleting the wrong account could remove needed access, so confirm ownership before deletion. No impact to VPN/HA/traffic.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Least-privilege / limit privileged accounts (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.7 family)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Create least-privilege administrator profiles' / limit super-admins
FortiOS Administration Guide — Administrators & Admin profiles
CWE-269: Improper Privilege Management
PCI-DSS 7.2.1, NIST SP 800-53 AC-6 & AC-6(1)

FORTIOS-ADMIN-020.risk: An administrator account with guest-auth enabled is tied to the guest-management authentication path rather than the normal, fully controlled admin authentication, which can weaken the authentication assurance for that account and blur the boundary between guest-portal management and device administration. On a privileged account this misconfiguration risks under-authenticated access to management functions. Business impact: an authentication-bypass-class weakness on an administrator, hence the CRITICAL rating.
steps: Identify the flagged account: `show system admin | grep -B3 'set guest-auth enable'`.
Confirm whether the account is genuinely meant to be a limited guest-user administrator or a real device admin that was misconfigured.
Disable guest auth on the account: `config system admin` / `edit "<admin-name>"` / `set guest-auth disable` / `next` / `end`.
Ensure the account uses standard strong authentication instead: a strong password (per the password policy), trusted hosts, and MFA (`set two-factor fortitoken`).
If guest-user management is actually required, implement it through a dedicated, tightly scoped account with a least-privilege profile rather than on a broad admin.
Verify with `show system admin <admin-name> | grep guest-auth`.
Re-run the scanner to confirm no admin has guest-auth enabled.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-020.gui: FortiGate: System > Administrators > edit the account and disable the guest-management option; use User & Authentication > User Definition / Guest Management for actual guest workflows. FortiManager: Device Manager > [device] > System > Administrators.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-020.cli:
config system admin
  edit "<admin-name>"
    set guest-auth disable
  next
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-020.verify: show system admin <admin-name> | grep guest-auth  ->  set guest-auth disable   (or the line is absent, since disable is the default)
FORTIOS-ADMIN-020.rollback: config system admin / edit "<admin-name>" / set guest-auth enable / next / end re-enables guest authentication for that admin. Only do this on a purpose-built, least-privilege guest-management account, never on a device super-admin.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-020.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. If the account legitimately administers the guest portal, disabling guest-auth changes how it authenticates — validate the guest-management workflow still functions via a dedicated account. No impact to VPN/HA/traffic.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Administrator authentication integrity (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1 family)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Administrator account security'
FortiOS Administration Guide — config system admin (guest-auth / guest-usergroups) and Guest Management
CWE-287: Improper Authentication
PCI-DSS 8.3.1, NIST SP 800-53 IA-2 & AC-2

FORTIOS-ADMIN-021.risk: A REST API user granted super_admin (or prof_admin) holds unrestricted, headless, non-interactive control of the entire device via a single bearer token that has no MFA prompt. API keys routinely leak through source repos, CI logs, and scripts; a leaked super_admin key equals instant full compromise from anywhere the key can reach the box. Business impact: a single exposed token yields device-wide takeover with maximum blast radius.
steps: Determine the minimum operations the automation actually needs (usually read-only monitoring or a narrow write scope).

Create a least-privilege access profile for API use: `config system accprofile` / `edit "api_readonly"` / `set sysgrp read` / `set netgrp read` / `set fwgrp read` / `set loggrp read` / `set vpngrp read` / `set utmgrp read` / `next` / `end` (grant read-write only on the specific group the automation requires).
Reassign the API user to the scoped profile: `config system api-user` / `edit "<api-user>"` / `set accprofile "api_readonly"` / `next` / `end`.
Bind the API user to trusted hosts (see FORTIOS-ADMIN-008) so the token is only usable from automation IPs.
Rotate/regenerate the API key after downgrading, in case the old key with super_admin rights leaked.
Confirm the automation still works with reduced rights; widen only the specific group if a call is denied.
Verify with `get system api-user <api-user> | grep accprofile` and re-run the scanner.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-021.gui: FortiGate: System > Admin Profiles > Create New (read-only or narrow scope), then System > Administrators > edit the REST API Admin > set its Administrator Profile. FortiManager: manage the device api-user via CLI Template / Device Manager.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-021.cli:
config system accprofile
  edit "api_readonly"
    set sysgrp read
    set netgrp read
    set fwgrp read
    set loggrp read
    set vpngrp read
    set utmgrp read
  next
end
config system api-user
  edit "<api-user>"
    set accprofile "api_readonly"
  next
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-021.verify: get system api-user <api-user> | grep accprofile  ->  accprofile : api_readonly   (not super_admin/prof_admin)
FORTIOS-ADMIN-021.rollback: config system api-user / edit "<api-user>" / set accprofile super_admin / next / end restores full API privilege. Reverting re-creates the single-token-owns-everything risk; if automation needs a specific write action, grant read-write only on that one accprofile group instead.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-021.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Risk of breaking automation if the new profile is too restrictive for a required API call — validate the automation before broad rollout. No impact to interactive admins, VPN, HA, or traffic.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Least-privilege for API/service accounts (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.7/2.1.8 family)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Create least-privilege administrator profiles' (applies to REST API admins)
FortiOS REST API / Administration Guide — config system api-user & accprofile
CWE-269: Improper Privilege Management
PCI-DSS 7.2.1, NIST SP 800-53 AC-6 & AC-6(1)

FORTIOS-ADMIN-022.risk: An API user with no comments/description is an undocumented credential: nobody knows its owner, purpose, or rotation schedule, so it tends to become an orphaned, long-lived token that is never revoked and never audited. Orphaned API keys are a classic persistence mechanism after staff turnover or a past breach. Business impact: unaccountable standing access that evades key-rotation and offboarding controls.
steps: List API users lacking documentation: `show system api-user` and note which have no `set comments`.
Identify the true owner, purpose, and rotation cadence for each token (interview teams if unknown).
Record that metadata in the comments field: `config system api-user` / `edit "<api-user>"` / `set comments "Owner: NetOps; Purpose: Ansible automation; Rotate: quarterly"` / `next` / `end`.
If no owner or purpose can be established, treat the token as orphaned and delete it: `config system api-user` / `delete "<api-user>"` / `end`.
Confirm each documented token also has trusted hosts (ADMIN-008) and least-privilege profile (ADMIN-021).
Add the token to the credential inventory / vault with its documented rotation date.
Verify with `show system api-user <api-user> | grep comments` and re-run the scanner.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-022.gui: FortiGate: System > Administrators > edit the REST API Admin > 'Comments' field. FortiManager: Device Manager > [device] > System > Administrators (or CLI Template).
FORTIOS-ADMIN-022.cli:
config system api-user
  edit "<api-user>"
    set comments "Owner: NetOps; Purpose: Ansible automation; Rotate: quarterly"
  next
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-022.verify: show system api-user <api-user> | grep comments  ->  set comments "Owner: NetOps; Purpose: Ansible automation; Rotate: quarterly"
FORTIOS-ADMIN-022.rollback: config system api-user / edit "<api-user>" / unset comments / next / end removes the documentation (not recommended). Deleting a token that turns out to be in use will break that automation, so confirm the owner before deletion.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-022.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Adding comments has zero functional effect. The only risk is if step 4 (deleting a truly orphaned token) removes a key that was actually in use — verify ownership first. No impact to VPN/HA/traffic.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.0.0 — Account inventory / documentation of service accounts (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 2.1.8 family)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' — 'Administrator account security' (inventory and lifecycle)
FortiOS REST API / Administration Guide — config system api-user (comments)
CWE-1078: Inappropriate Source Code Style or Formatting (undocumented/unmanaged credential) — track via credential inventory
NIST SP 800-53 AC-2 (Account Management), PCI-DSS 8.2.1

FORTIOS-ADMIN-023.risk: Not all administrator accounts require multi-factor authentication, so any admin protected by a password alone can be compromised by phishing, credential stuffing or brute force - giving an attacker full control of the firewall. MFA is the single most effective control against admin-account takeover.
steps: List admins and their two-factor state: show system admin (check two-factor per account).
Provision tokens: FortiToken Mobile (two free), hardware FortiTokens, or email/SMS 2FA. Register a token to each admin.
Enable MFA per admin: config system admin / edit <name> / set two-factor fortitoken / set fortitoken <serial> / set email-to <addr> / next / end (or set two-factor email).
For remote-auth admins (RADIUS/LDAP), enforce MFA at the IdP or use FortiToken RADIUS.
Test each admin can log in with the second factor before removing any exception.
Re-run the scanner to confirm 100% MFA coverage.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-023.gui: System > Administrators > edit <admin> > enable Two-factor Authentication and assign a FortiToken / email. System > FortiTokens to import/activate tokens.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-023.cli:
config system admin
  edit <name>
    set two-factor fortitoken
    set fortitoken <token-serial>
  next
end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-023.verify: show system admin  ->  set two-factor fortitoken (for every admin account)
FORTIOS-ADMIN-023.rollback: config system admin / edit <name> / set two-factor disable / next / end. Disabling MFA materially weakens admin security; keep it enabled.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-023.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot, but each admin must have an enrolled, working second factor BEFORE enforcement or they will be locked out. Stage token delivery and test logins first.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - require MFA for administrators (CIS 2.1.4/2.1.5)
Fortinet Administration Guide - FortiToken two-factor authentication
CWE-308: Use of Single-factor Authentication
NIST SP 800-53 IA-2(1), PCI-DSS 8.4/8.5

FORTIOS-ADMIN-024.risk: The built-in 'admin' account exists with the super_admin profile. Its username is universally known, making it the first target for brute-force and default-credential attacks; a single compromise yields unrestricted control. Best practice is individual, named, least-privilege admin accounts so actions are attributable and the well-known default is removed.
steps: Create named per-person admin accounts with appropriate profiles and MFA: config system admin / edit <alice> / set accprofile super_admin / set password <strong> / set two-factor fortitoken / next / end.
Log in as a new named super_admin and confirm it works.

Restrict or remove the default account: rename it, or set a long random password, add trusted-host restrictions and reduce its profile; to delete: config system admin / delete admin (only after a replacement super_admin exists).
Apply trusted hosts to every admin (set trusthost1 <mgmt-subnet>).
Ensure the Security Audit Log records admin logons.
Re-run the scanner to confirm the default super_admin exposure is remediated.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-024.gui: System > Administrators: create named administrators, then delete or restrict the default 'admin' (assign a non-super profile / trusted hosts / strong password).
FORTIOS-ADMIN-024.cli:
config system admin
  edit <named-admin>
    set accprofile super_admin
    set password <strong-password>
    set two-factor fortitoken
  next
end
# after confirming the new account works:
# config system admin
#   delete admin
# end
FORTIOS-ADMIN-024.verify: show system admin  ->  named super_admin account(s) exist; the default 'admin' is removed or has a restricted profile + trusted hosts
FORTIOS-ADMIN-024.rollback: Recreate an 'admin' super_admin for recovery if needed: config system admin / edit admin / set accprofile super_admin / set password <strong> / next / end. Keep at least one working super_admin at all times.
FORTIOS-ADMIN-024.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Risk is lock-out: never delete the last super_admin - verify a replacement named super_admin logs in first. No effect on traffic/VPN/HA.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - remove/rename default admin, named accounts (CIS 2.1.x)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' - create administrator accounts, trusted hosts
CWE-1188: Insecure Default Initialization of Resource
NIST SP 800-53 AC-2, AC-6, IA-4

FORTIOS-APP-001.risk: The application control list explicitly passes high-risk categories (P2P, proxy-avoidance, botnet). Proxy-avoidance/anonymizer apps let users and malware tunnel past URL/category and IPS controls, P2P opens uncontrolled inbound/outbound file transfer, and 'botnet' category traffic is literally C2 - allowing it is allowing command-and-control. Business impact: data exfiltration, malware C2 egress, and defeat of the surrounding web/IPS controls via encrypted tunnels.
steps: Identify the offending list and entries: 'show application list <name>' and find entries with 'set action pass' on high-risk categories.
Edit the list: config application list / edit "<name>".
Change the high-risk entry to block: config entries / edit <id> / set action block / next.
Ensure P2P, Proxy, and Botnet categories are covered by a block entry (verify numeric IDs in the GUI category view for your FortiOS version).
Optionally block specific stubborn evasion apps by application override in addition to the category block.
Save with 'end / next / end'.
Confirm the list is applied to the relevant egress policies.
Monitor Application Control logs to confirm the categories now show 'blocked' and tune any legitimate app caught by category.
FORTIOS-APP-001.gui: Security Profiles > Application Control > edit <profile> > set the P2P / Proxy / Botnet category (or specific applications) Action to Block. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Application Control.
FORTIOS-APP-001.cli:
config application list
    edit "<list>"
        config entries
            edit <entry-id>
                set action block
            next
        end
    next
end
FORTIOS-APP-001.verify: show application list <list> | grep -B3 action  -> high-risk category entry now 'set action block'. Live: attempt a proxy/P2P app from a test host and confirm a block event in Application Control logs.
FORTIOS-APP-001.rollback: Set 'set action pass' (or 'monitor') on the specific entry: config application list / edit <list> / config entries / edit <id> / set action monitor / next / end. Re-allowing proxy/P2P/botnet categories re-opens tunneling and C2 paths; if a legitimate app is mis-categorized, add a granular application override to allow just that app.
FORTIOS-APP-001.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Blocking these categories may break a sanctioned tool that shares a category (e.g., a legitimate remote-support or file-sync app) - use per-application overrides for exceptions rather than passing the whole category.
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Application control > Categories and application overrides
CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4 Security Profiles (Application Control)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
PCI-DSS v4.0 1.2.5
NIST SP 800-53 CM-7 Least Functionality, SC-7 Boundary Protection
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-AUTH-001.risk: The LDAP server object binds to the directory in cleartext (secure=disable, port 389), so every LDAP simple bind carries the bind-account credentials and, for user authentication, the end-user password in plaintext. An attacker with any on-path position between the FortiGate and the domain controller (SPAN/tap, ARP-spoofing on the server VLAN, a compromised switch, or a rogue hypervisor) can sniff the AD service-account password and any authenticating user's password, then pivot to full Active Directory compromise. Because the same LDAP source is often reused for SSL-VPN, admin login, and captive portal, one cleartext bind can leak the credentials that gate remote access and firewall administration. Business impact: domain-wide credential theft, regulated-data exposure, and failure of PCI/HIPAA transmission-encryption controls.
steps: Enumerate affected LDAP objects: run `get user ldap` and note any with `secure : disable` or `port : 389`.

Import the CA that issued the domain-controller LDAPS certificate under System > Certificates > Create/Import > CA Certificate (CLI: `config vpn certificate ca` / `edit "AD-CA"` / `set ca "<PEM-blob>"` / `end`); record the CA cert name.
Make sure the LDAP `server`/`secondary-server` value is the DC FQDN that matches the certificate CN/SAN: `config user ldap` / `edit <name>` / `set server "dc01.corp.example.com"`.
Switch transport to LDAPS on 636: `set secure ldaps` then `set port 636` (for STARTTLS instead use `set secure starttls` and keep `set port 389`).
Bind the CA and enable identity validation in the same edit: `set ca-cert "AD-CA"` and `set server-identity-check enable`.
Commit: `next` / `end`.
Functionally test before trusting the change: `diagnose test authserver ldap <name> <test-user> <password>` and expect an authenticate-succeeded result.
Confirm no cleartext LDAP remains on the wire: `diagnose sniffer packet any 'tcp port 389' 4 10` should show no plaintext simple binds (traffic is now on TCP/636 TLS).
Verify the object: `get user ldap <name>` shows `secure : ldaps` and `server-identity-check : enable`.
Repeat for every LDAP server object and in every VDOM.
FORTIOS-AUTH-001.gui: FortiGate: User & Authentication > LDAP Servers > (select server) > set Connection type to LDAPS (or STARTTLS), set Port 636, choose the imported CA under Certificate, and enable Server identity check. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > User & Authentication > LDAP Servers > (edit object) then install to devices.
FORTIOS-AUTH-001.cli:
config user ldap
    edit <ldap-server>
        set server "dc01.corp.example.com"
        set secure ldaps
        set port 636
        set ca-cert "AD-CA"
        set server-identity-check enable
    next
end
FORTIOS-AUTH-001.verify: get user ldap <name>  ->  expect  secure : ldaps  and  port : 636  and  server-identity-check : enable ; functional check: diagnose test authserver ldap <name> <user> <password>  ->  'authenticate user against ... succeeded'.
FORTIOS-AUTH-001.rollback: Revert with: config user ldap / edit <name> / set secure disable / set server-identity-check disable / next / end. This restores cleartext LDAP. Roll back only if the LDAPS switch broke logins (missing/incorrect CA cert, wrong FQDN, or a firewall blocking TCP/636); keep a locally-authenticated fallback admin so you are never locked out during the change.
FORTIOS-AUTH-001.impact: No reboot; does not drop IPsec/SSL-VPN tunnels or force HA failover by itself. However it re-negotiates the directory transport, so if the CA cert or DC FQDN is wrong, LDAP-authenticated admins and SSL-VPN/captive-portal users can fail to log in. Treat as service-affecting for LDAP consumers: schedule a short maintenance window and verify a local fallback admin first.
references: CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5: SC-8, SC-23 (Transmission Confidentiality/Integrity)
PCI-DSS v4.0: Req 4.2.1 (strong cryptography for account/authentication data in transit over untrusted networks)
Fortinet FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - Authentication > LDAP servers ('Configuring LDAP over SSL', secure = ldaps/starttls, server-identity-check)
FortiGate Hardening Guide - encrypt management and directory traffic
Scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP['FORTIOS-AUTH-001']: CIS 13.1.1, PCI-DSS 8.3.1, NIST IA-2(1), SOC2 CC6.1, HIPAA 164.312(d)

Note: the scanner's REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-AUTH-001'] currently stores an admin two-factor block that does NOT remediate this LDAP-cleartext finding; the correct LDAPS block is shown above (that block is mis-keyed under REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-AUTH-002']).

FORTIOS-AUTH-002.risk: The RADIUS server object uses a shared secret shorter than 16 characters. The RADIUS secret keys the Response Authenticator MD5 hash and the User-Password attribute obfuscation; a short/low-entropy secret lets an attacker who can capture a single Access-Request/Access-Accept exchange run an offline dictionary/brute-force attack to recover the secret. With the secret recovered, the attacker can forge Access-Accept responses to authenticate as any user, decrypt captured user passwords, and bypass MFA prompts driven through RADIUS. Because RADIUS commonly backs SSL-VPN, IPsec XAuth, admin login, and 802.1X, cracking this one secret can hand over remote access and firewall administration. Business impact: authentication bypass, credential decryption, and lateral movement into the protected network.
steps: List RADIUS objects and identify the flagged short-secret servers: `get user radius`.

Generate a strong shared secret of at least 22 characters with mixed character types (RFC 2865 recommends a secret larger than 16 octets and as unpredictable as practical, up to 128 octets); store it in the secrets vault.
Change the secret on the RADIUS/NPS server side (or plan a synchronized change) so both ends match at cutover.
Update the FortiGate object: `config user radius` / `edit <name>` / `set secret <new-strong-secret>` / `next` / `end`.
If secondary/tertiary servers are defined, update `set secondary-secret` / `set tertiary-secret` to match their servers.
Functionally validate the new secret: `diagnose test authserver radius <name> mschap2 <test-user> <password>` and expect 'authenticate ... succeeded'.
Test a real consumer path (RADIUS admin login or an SSL-VPN user) to confirm no outage before leaving the window.
Confirm the encrypted secret is stored: `show user radius <name>` shows `set secret ENC ...` (plaintext length is not visible; validation is functional).
Repeat for every RADIUS object and VDOM and record the new secret.
FORTIOS-AUTH-002.gui: FortiGate: User & Authentication > RADIUS Servers > (select server) > update the Secret field (and Secondary server secret if configured) > OK. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > User & Authentication > RADIUS Servers > (edit object) then install to devices.
FORTIOS-AUTH-002.cli:
config user radius
    edit <radius-server>
        set secret <22+char-random-secret>
        set secondary-secret <22+char-random-secret>
    next
end
FORTIOS-AUTH-002.verify: diagnose test authserver radius <name> mschap2 <user> <password>  ->  'authenticate ... succeeded' (proves FortiGate and RADIUS secrets now match); show user radius <name>  ->  set secret ENC ... (length not shown, so validate functionally).
FORTIOS-AUTH-002.rollback: Revert the secret to the previous value on BOTH the FortiGate (config user radius / edit <name> / set secret <old> / next / end) AND the RADIUS/NPS server. Any mismatch between the two ends fails all RADIUS authentication for that server - admin login, SSL-VPN, IPsec XAuth, and 802.1X - so both sides must always match.
FORTIOS-AUTH-002.impact: No reboot, but service-affecting: the secret must change on the FortiGate and the RADIUS server at the same time. During the change window, RADIUS-backed authentication (admin, SSL-VPN, IPsec XAuth, wireless 802.1X) fails until both sides match. Schedule a maintenance window and keep a non-RADIUS fallback admin.
references: CWE-521: Weak Password Requirements
RFC 2865 (RADIUS), Section 3 - shared secret should exceed 16 octets and be as unpredictable as practical
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5: IA-5(1) (password-based authenticator management), SC-12
PCI-DSS v4.0: Req 8.2.1 / 8.3.x (strong authentication credentials)
Fortinet FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - Authentication > RADIUS servers (secret, secondary-secret)
Scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP['FORTIOS-AUTH-002']: CIS 13.1.2, PCI-DSS 8.2.1, NIST IA-5(1), SOC2 CC6.1
Note: the scanner's REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-AUTH-002'] currently stores an LDAP 'set secure ldaps' block, not a RADIUS-secret fix; the correct RADIUS CLI is shown above.

FORTIOS-AUTH-003.risk: The RADIUS server object has an authentication timeout greater than 30 seconds. An excessive timeout means each in-flight authentication holds FortiGate auth resources for a long time while waiting on the RADIUS server. If the RADIUS server is slow, saturated, or deliberately targeted, requests queue up and legitimate users experience login delays or failures; an attacker can amplify this by flooding auth attempts (SSL-VPN, captive portal, admin login) to exhaust the auth daemon's pending-request capacity and cause a denial of service. The long timeout also masks a dead primary server, delaying failover to a secondary. Business impact: degraded or unavailable authentication for remote access and management during load or attack.
steps: Identify servers with a long timeout: `get user radius` and look for `timeout` greater than 30.
Set a sane timeout (5 seconds is the FortiOS default; keep it at 5-10): `config user radius` / `edit <name>` / `set timeout 5`.
Add redundancy so a slow or unreachable primary does not stall auth: `set secondary-server <ip>` and `set secondary-secret <secret>` (optionally `set tertiary-server`/`set tertiary-secret`).
Commit: `next` / `end`.
Verify the values: `get user radius <name>` shows `timeout : 5` and the secondary server populated.
Test authentication still works: `diagnose test authserver radius <name> mschap2 <user> <password>`.
Optionally validate failover by blocking the primary and confirming the secondary answers within the configured timeout.
Repeat across all RADIUS objects and VDOMs.
FORTIOS-AUTH-003.gui: FortiGate: the per-server authentication timeout is a CLI-only setting (config user radius > timeout) and is not exposed in the GUI; the secondary/tertiary RADIUS server can be added under User & Authentication > RADIUS Servers > (edit) > add Secondary server. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > User & Authentication > RADIUS Servers for the secondary server; timeout via CLI/CLI-only object override.
FORTIOS-AUTH-003.cli:
config user radius
    edit <radius-server>
        set timeout 5
        set secondary-server <secondary-radius-ip>
        set secondary-secret <secondary-secret>
    next
end
FORTIOS-AUTH-003.verify: get user radius <name>  ->  expect  timeout : 5  and a populated  secondary-server ; diagnose test authserver radius <name> mschap2 <user> <password>  ->  'authenticate ... succeeded'.
FORTIOS-AUTH-003.rollback: Revert with: config user radius / edit <name> / set timeout <old-value> / unset secondary-server / next / end. Non-disruptive; reverting only re-lengthens the timeout and removes redundancy (no sessions dropped).
FORTIOS-AUTH-003.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot, and changing the timeout or adding a secondary server does not drop existing sessions or tunnels. Can be applied during business hours.
references: CWE-400: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5: SC-5 (Denial of Service Protection), IA-2
Fortinet FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - Authentication > RADIUS servers (timeout default 5s; secondary-server / tertiary-server for redundancy)
No scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP entry exists for FORTIOS-AUTH-003

FORTIOS-AUTH-004.risk: Neither SAML 2.0 IdP integration nor Fortinet Single Sign-On (FSSO) is configured, so authentication relies solely on local accounts and/or direct LDAP/RADIUS. Without a central IdP, the organization loses centralized MFA enforcement, conditional access (device posture, geo/risk), instant deprovisioning on HR termination, and unified audit of who accessed what. Stale or shared local/LDAP credentials persist, and there is no single choke point to revoke access during an incident. An attacker who phishes or brute-forces one directory credential faces no adaptive/conditional-access barrier. Business impact: weaker identity assurance, slow offboarding, fragmented audit, and higher account-takeover risk for SSL-VPN and admin access. Severity is low because this is an architectural gap rather than an exposed misconfiguration.
steps: Choose an SSO model: SAML 2.0 (Entra ID/Azure AD, Okta, ADFS, etc.) for SSL-VPN/admin/captive-portal login, and/or FSSO for transparent AD identity in firewall policies.
For SAML, register the FortiGate as an SP in the IdP and import the IdP's signing certificate onto the FortiGate (System > Certificates > Import > Remote/CA).

Configure the SAML SP object: `config user saml` / `edit "idp"` / set `entity-id`, `single-sign-on-url`, `idp-entity-id`, `idp-single-sign-on-url`, `idp-single-logout-url`, `idp-cert`, `user-name "username"`, `group-name "group"` / `next` / `end`.
For FSSO, install a Collector Agent on a Windows AD server, then: `config user fsso` / `edit "fsso"` / `set server <collector-ip>` / `set password <secret>` / `next` / `end`.
Bind the SSO to a consumer: reference the SAML object in the SSL-VPN portal/authentication rule, or add FSSO groups to firewall policy via `config user group` (set `member`).
Enforce MFA and conditional access at the IdP for defense in depth (this is where MFA policy should live for federated users).
Verify configuration: `show user saml` / `show user fsso`; for FSSO logon state use `diagnose debug authd fsso list`.
Pilot with a small test group and keep a local fallback admin before broad rollout.
FORTIOS-AUTH-004.gui: FortiGate: FSSO via Security Fabric > External Connectors > (create) FSSO Agent on Windows AD; SAML SSO via User & Authentication > Single Sign-On (much of the SAML SP definition is CLI-driven under `config user saml`). FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > User & Authentication > Single Sign-On / FSSO Agent.
FORTIOS-AUTH-004.cli:
# Option A - SAML 2.0 SP (SSL-VPN / admin / captive portal)
config user saml
    edit "idp"
        set cert "Fortinet_Factory"
        set entity-id "https://<fgt-fqdn>/remote/saml/metadata/"
        set single-sign-on-url "https://<fgt-fqdn>/remote/saml/login"
        set single-logout-url "https://<fgt-fqdn>/remote/saml/logout"
        set idp-entity-id "<idp-entity-id>"
        set idp-single-sign-on-url "<idp-sso-url>"
        set idp-single-logout-url "<idp-slo-url>"
        set idp-cert "<imported-idp-cert>"
        set user-name "username"
        set group-name "group"
    next
end

# Option B - FSSO collector agent
config user fsso
    edit "fsso-collector"
        set server "<collector-agent-ip>"
        set password <collector-secret>
    next
end
FORTIOS-AUTH-004.verify: show user saml  and  show user fsso  ->  the configured object is present with idp-* fields / server populated; for FSSO logons: diagnose debug authd fsso list  ->  shows active user-to-IP mappings.
FORTIOS-AUTH-004.rollback: Delete the object and remove all references: config user saml / delete "idp" (or config user fsso / delete "fsso-collector"), and remove the object from SSL-VPN portals, authentication rules, and user groups. Ensure an alternate auth method (local/LDAP/RADIUS) remains so users relying on the new SSO are not locked out.
FORTIOS-AUTH-004.impact: Additive and non-disruptive to existing sessions (no reboot), but this is a design change that requires IdP coordination, certificate exchange, and testing. Roll out via pilot; retain a local fallback admin during cutover.
references: CWE-287: Improper Authentication
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5: IA-2, IA-8 (Identification and Authentication), AC-2 (Account Management)
Fortinet FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - Single Sign-On: SAML SSO and FSSO (config user saml, config user fsso)
No scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP entry exists for FORTIOS-AUTH-004

FORTIOS-AUTH-005.risk: An enabled local user account has two-factor authentication disabled, so a single knowledge factor (the password) is the only barrier to whatever that account can reach - typically SSL-VPN, IPsec, or captive-portal access. Local passwords are frequently weak, reused, or leaked in third-party breaches, and single-factor accounts are the primary target of credential-stuffing and password-spray campaigns against the VPN portal. An attacker who obtains the password logs in unchallenged and gains an internal network foothold or remote-access session.
