Business impact: account takeover leading to data theft, ransomware staging, or lateral movement; single-factor remote access also breaches PCI-DSS and common cyber-insurance MFA requirements.
steps: Enumerate enabled local users lacking MFA: `get user local` (flagged where `two-factor : disable` and `status : enable`).

Provision a token: assign a FortiToken Mobile or hardware token and activate it - `config user fortitoken` / `edit <serial>` / `set status active` / `next` / `end` (hardware seeds import via `exec fortitoken import ...`).

Bind MFA to the user: `config user local` / `edit <name>` / `set two-factor fortitoken` / `set fortitoken <serial>` / `set email-to <user@example.com>` / `next` / `end` (use `set two-factor email` or `set two-factor sms` if not using FortiToken).
If using email OTP ensure `config system email-server` is configured; for SMS ensure an SMS service/`config system sms-server` is set so codes actually deliver.
Deliver/activate the token to the user (FortiToken Mobile activation code or hardware seed) before enforcing.
Verify the binding: `get user local <name>` shows `two-factor : fortitoken`; check token state with `diagnose fortitoken info`.
Test the user's login end-to-end (SSL-VPN or portal) to confirm the OTP challenge appears and succeeds.
Repeat for every enabled local user; for scale, migrate users to a central IdP with MFA (see FORTIOS-AUTH-004).
FORTIOS-AUTH-005.gui: FortiGate: User & Authentication > User Definition > (edit user) > enable Two-factor Authentication and select the token/delivery method; manage tokens under User & Authentication > FortiTokens. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > User & Authentication > User Definition (and FortiToken management).
FORTIOS-AUTH-005.cli:
config user local
    edit <username>
        set two-factor fortitoken
        set fortitoken <token-serial>
        set email-to <user@example.com>
    next
end
# Activate the assigned token
config user fortitoken
    edit <token-serial>
        set status active
    next
end
FORTIOS-AUTH-005.verify: get user local <name>  ->  expect  two-factor : fortitoken  (and fortitoken serial / email-to populated); diagnose fortitoken info  ->  token status shows active/provisioned.
FORTIOS-AUTH-005.rollback: Revert with: config user local / edit <name> / set two-factor disable / next / end. This removes the MFA requirement (weakening the account). Roll back only if token provisioning failed and the legitimate user is locked out; re-provision the token and re-enable MFA as soon as possible.
FORTIOS-AUTH-005.impact: No reboot; affects only the specific user's next authentication by adding an OTP prompt. Existing sessions are not dropped. Risk is a self-inflicted lockout if the token is not delivered/activated before enforcement, so provision and test per user.
references: CWE-308: Use of Single-factor Authentication
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5: IA-2(1)/IA-2(2) (Multifactor Authentication)
PCI-DSS v4.0: Req 8.4 / 8.5 (MFA for access)
Fortinet FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - FortiTokens and two-factor authentication (config user local set two-factor fortitoken/email/sms)

CIS FortiGate Benchmark - administrator MFA control (the scanner maps admin MFA under FORTIOS-ADMIN-005 -> CIS 2.1.5; FORTIOS-ADMIN-004 -> CIS 2.1.4 is the separate 'default admin account exists' rule, not the MFA control)

FORTIOS-AUTH-006.risk: The LDAP object uses TLS (LDAPS or STARTTLS) but has server-identity-check disabled, so the FortiGate encrypts the session yet never validates that the presented certificate actually belongs to the intended directory server. An on-path attacker can present any certificate (self-signed or from an unrelated CA) and complete a man-in-the-middle: the FortiGate happily binds through the attacker, who now sees the bind-account credentials and every authenticating user's password despite the 'encrypted' connection. This defeats the entire purpose of LDAPS. Because the LDAP source usually backs SSL-VPN, admin, and captive-portal auth, a successful MITM yields the credentials guarding remote access and firewall management. Business impact: credential theft and directory compromise even though traffic appears encrypted.
steps: Find TLS-enabled LDAP objects missing identity validation: `get user ldap` (secure is ldaps/starttls but `server-identity-check : disable`).
Ensure the LDAP `server` value is the FQDN that matches the DC certificate CN/SAN (the identity check compares against it): `config user ldap` / `edit <name>` / `set server "dc01.corp.example.com"`.
Import the issuing CA if not already present (System > Certificates > Import > CA Certificate) and bind it: `set ca-cert "AD-CA"`.
Enable validation: `set server-identity-check enable` / `next` / `end`.
Functionally test - a CN/SAN or CA mismatch will surface here: `diagnose test authserver ldap <name> <user> <password>` must return success.
Verify the object: `get user ldap <name>` shows `server-identity-check : enable`.
If the test fails with a certificate-identity error, fix the DC certificate SAN or the `server` FQDN / import the correct CA - do NOT re-disable the check in production.
Repeat for every TLS-enabled LDAP object and VDOM.
FORTIOS-AUTH-006.gui: FortiGate: User & Authentication > LDAP Servers > (edit the LDAPS/STARTTLS server) > enable Server identity check and select the CA certificate; the Server field must be the FQDN matching the certificate. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > User & Authentication > LDAP Servers > (edit object) then install.
FORTIOS-AUTH-006.cli:
config user ldap
    edit <ldap-server>
        set server "dc01.corp.example.com"
        set ca-cert "AD-CA"
        set server-identity-check enable
    next
end
FORTIOS-AUTH-006.verify: get user ldap <name>  ->  expect  server-identity-check : enable ; functional check: diagnose test authserver ldap <name> <user> <password>  ->  succeeds (it fails if the certificate CN/SAN does not match the configured server FQDN).
FORTIOS-AUTH-006.rollback: Revert with: config user ldap / edit <name> / set server-identity-check disable / next / end. This restores binds even when the certificate identity does not match (re-opening the MITM exposure). Use only as a temporary measure if enabling the check broke LDAP auth because of a certificate SAN/FQDN mismatch; fix the certificate and re-enable.
FORTIOS-AUTH-006.impact: No reboot and no session/tunnel drop, but if the LDAP `server` value is an IP or an FQDN that does not match the certificate CN/SAN (or the CA is not imported), new LDAP binds fail and LDAP-authenticated admins/users can be locked out. Test with `diagnose test authserver` before relying on it; keep a local fallback admin.
references: CWE-295: Improper Certificate Validation
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5: SC-8, SC-23 (Transmission/Session Integrity), IA-5
Fortinet FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - LDAP servers: server-identity-check (validate the LDAP server certificate; requires matching FQDN and imported CA)
No scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP entry exists for FORTIOS-AUTH-006

FORTIOS-AV-001.risk: The AV profile exists but HTTP (web) scanning is in monitor mode or unset, so infected files downloaded over HTTP are logged but delivered to the user. An attacker hosting malware on a compromised or attacker-controlled site sees payloads pass through the gateway unblocked, defeating the primary web malware control and enabling ransomware/loader delivery. Business impact: endpoint infection despite an apparently 'configured' AV profile - a dangerous false sense of security.
steps: Identify the affected profile and current mode: 'show antivirus profile <name>' and look at 'config http / set av-scan'.
Enter the profile: config antivirus profile / edit "<name>".
Set HTTP to block: config http / set av-scan block / set outbreak-prevention block / end.

HTTPS downloads are covered by this same 'config http' handler once a deep-inspection ssl-ssh-profile is applied on the policy - there is no separate 'config https' section in the AV profile, so ensure deep SSL inspection is enabled on the web-egress policy.
Save with 'next / end'.
Confirm the profile is bound to the web-egress policy with an SSL/SSH deep-inspection profile: show firewall policy <id> | grep -e av-profile -e ssl-ssh-profile.
Run a controlled EICAR-over-HTTP test and confirm a block event in the AntiVirus log.
Roll the change to all profiles used on user-facing policies.
FORTIOS-AV-001.gui: Security Profiles > AntiVirus > edit <profile> > set the HTTP inspection action to Block (in flow mode, ensure the option to treat scan results as blocked). FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > Security Profiles > AntiVirus > edit profile.
FORTIOS-AV-001.cli:
config antivirus profile
    edit "<profile>"
        config http
            set av-scan block
            set outbreak-prevention block
        end
    next
end
# HTTPS downloads are scanned by this same 'config http' handler once a
# deep-inspection ssl-ssh-profile is applied to the policy; the AV profile has
# no 'config https' subsection.
FORTIOS-AV-001.verify: show antivirus profile <profile> | grep -A3 'config http'  -> set av-scan block. Live: download EICAR over HTTP and confirm the transfer is blocked and logged under Log & Report > Security Events > AntiVirus.
FORTIOS-AV-001.rollback: Revert with 'config antivirus profile / edit <profile> / config http / set av-scan monitor / end / next / end'. Reverting to monitor allows infected web downloads again; only do so temporarily to unblock a verified false positive while you build a file/URL exception.
FORTIOS-AV-001.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session teardown. HTTPS blocking only takes effect where a deep-inspection SSL/SSH profile is applied on the policy. Watch for false positives on legitimate large/software downloads.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4.2 Antivirus (block action)
FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Antivirus (flow-based inspection actions)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
PCI-DSS v4.0 5.2
NIST SP 800-53 SI-3 Malicious Code Protection
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-AV-002.risk: Outbreak Prevention is disabled, so the FortiGate cannot query the FortiGuard real-time hash database for very-recently-discovered malware that is not yet in the downloaded signature set. During the critical hours between a new campaign launch and the next signature push, zero-hour malware passes inspection cleanly. Business impact: exposure to fast-moving ransomware/loader outbreaks that signature-only AV misses.
steps: Verify the FortiGuard Outbreak Prevention entitlement is active: 'diagnose autoupdate versions' and check the AntiVirus/Outbreak Prevention contract in System > FortiGuard.
Edit the profile: config antivirus profile / edit "<name>".

Enable outbreak prevention on web: config http / set outbreak-prevention block / end. HTTPS is covered by the same http handler after deep SSL inspection - there is no 'config https' section in the AV profile.
Enable it on mail flows: config smtp / set outbreak-prevention block / end, config pop3 / set outbreak-prevention block / end, config imap / set outbreak-prevention block / end.

Ensure the profile is applied to the relevant policies and that a protocol-options profile with appropriate archive/oversize handling is attached (archive-scan depth is controlled in config firewall profile-protocol-options, not in the AV profile).
Save with 'next / end'.
Confirm FortiGuard connectivity for real-time queries: 'diagnose debug rating' and 'execute update-now'.
Monitor AntiVirus logs for 'Outbreak Prevention' verdicts over the next 24 hours.
FORTIOS-AV-002.gui: Security Profiles > AntiVirus > edit <profile> > enable 'FortiGuard Outbreak Prevention' (Block). Ensure System > FortiGuard shows a valid AntiVirus/Outbreak Prevention license. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > AntiVirus.
FORTIOS-AV-002.cli:
config antivirus profile
    edit "<profile>"
        config http
            set outbreak-prevention block
        end
        config smtp
            set outbreak-prevention block
        end
        config pop3
            set outbreak-prevention block
        end
        config imap
            set outbreak-prevention block
        end
    next
end
# HTTPS is covered via the http handler after deep SSL inspection; the AV
# profile has no separate 'config https' section.
FORTIOS-AV-002.verify: show antivirus profile <profile> | grep outbreak-prevention  -> set outbreak-prevention block (per protocol). Confirm live FortiGuard reachability with 'diagnose debug rating' (AV/rating servers reachable).
FORTIOS-AV-002.rollback: Set 'set outbreak-prevention disable' under each protocol in the profile. Disabling it removes real-time zero-hour hash lookups; the network falls back to periodic signatures only. Roll back only if outbreak queries cause verified false positives.
FORTIOS-AV-002.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Adds a real-time FortiGuard hash lookup per scanned object (negligible latency with local caching). Requires reachable FortiGuard servers / active subscription.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4.2 Antivirus (enable outbreak prevention database)
FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Antivirus > FortiGuard Outbreak Prevention
Fortinet FortiGuard AntiVirus / Outbreak Prevention service documentation
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
NIST SP 800-53 SI-3 Malicious Code Protection
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-AV-003.risk: FortiGuard cloud sandbox / AI analytics (ftgd-analytics) is disabled, so suspicious-but-unknown files are not detonated for behavioral analysis. Advanced or targeted malware that evades signatures and outbreak hashes is neither convicted nor fed back into protection, leaving a blind spot for APT and novel ransomware. Business impact: undetected advanced threats establish persistence; no retrospective conviction of files already delivered.
steps: Confirm a FortiSandbox (appliance or FortiCloud/FortiGuard) entitlement exists: System > FortiGuard, or 'show system fortisandbox'.

Point the device at a sandbox. Cloud: config system fortisandbox / set status enable / set forticloud enable / end. On-prem: config system fortisandbox / set status enable / set server <fortisandbox-ip> / end.
Edit the AV profile: config antivirus profile / edit "<name>".
Enable analytics submission of suspicious files: set ftgd-analytics suspicious (use 'everything' only for high-scrutiny segments).
Set the upload ceiling and file handling: set analytics-max-upload 10 and set analytics-ignore-filetype / set analytics-accept-filetype as needed.
Optionally enable block-and-log for high-risk flows: set av-block-log enable and configure quarantine as appropriate.
Save with 'next / end' and confirm the profile is applied to egress policies.
Monitor Security Fabric / FortiSandbox submissions and the AntiVirus / Sandbox logs for verdicts.
FORTIOS-AV-003.gui: Security Profiles > AntiVirus > edit <profile> > enable 'Send files to FortiSandbox for inspection' (Suspicious files). Configure the sandbox under Security Fabric > Fabric Connectors > FortiSandbox (or System > FortiGuard for cloud). FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > AntiVirus.
FORTIOS-AV-003.cli:
config system fortisandbox
    set status enable
    set server <fortisandbox-ip-or-fqdn>
end
config antivirus profile
    edit "<profile>"
        set ftgd-analytics suspicious
        set analytics-max-upload 10
    next
end
# For FortiGate Cloud sandbox instead of an on-prem appliance, use
# 'set forticloud enable' under config system fortisandbox in place of 'set server'.
FORTIOS-AV-003.verify: show antivirus profile <profile> | grep ftgd-analytics  -> set ftgd-analytics suspicious. Confirm sandbox connectivity with 'get system fortisandbox' (status/connection) and Security Fabric > Fabric Connectors showing FortiSandbox connected; verify submissions/verdicts in Log & Report > Security Events (Sandbox / AntiVirus).
FORTIOS-AV-003.rollback: Set 'set ftgd-analytics disable' in the profile (and 'config system fortisandbox / set status disable' if desired). Disabling removes zero-day behavioral detection and retrospective conviction. Roll back only for privacy/compliance constraints on file upload.
FORTIOS-AV-003.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Suspicious files are uploaded to the sandbox (bandwidth/privacy consideration - files leave the network to FortiCloud unless using an on-prem FortiSandbox). Verdicts are asynchronous unless inline blocking is used.
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Antivirus > FortiSandbox / FortiGuard analytics (ftgd-analytics)
Fortinet FortiSandbox Administration / Integration Guide
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
PCI-DSS v4.0 5.2
NIST SP 800-53 SI-3 Malicious Code Protection, SI-4 System Monitoring
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-BACKUP-001.risk: With no FortiManager (or equivalent) holding versioned copies of the running configuration, the FortiGate is a single point of failure. If the appliance is bricked by a bad upgrade, hit by ransomware/config-wiping malware (e.g. the post-exploitation config tampering seen in FortiOS CVE-2022-40684 and CVE-2024-21762 campaigns), physically stolen, or misconfigured by an admin, there is no authoritative, off-box, timestamped config to restore from. Recovery becomes a manual rebuild from memory, extending an outage from minutes to days and risking that a subtly attacker-modified policy set is reinstated. Absence of central change tracking also means malicious or accidental policy changes go undetected and are not attributable.
steps: Establish an immediate out-of-band encrypted backup so recovery is not solely dependent on this device: 'execute backup config ftp <filename>.conf <ftp-server> <user> <password> <encrypt-passwd>' (the trailing password encrypts the archive with AES-GCM so it cannot be read if the file leaks).
On the FortiManager, pre-stage the device: Device Manager > Add Device, or plan to authorize it under Device Manager > Unauthorized Devices once it dials in.
On the FortiGate, point central management at FortiManager: 'config system central-management' / 'set type fortimanager' / 'set fmg <fmg-ip>' / 'end'.
(Optional, tighter egress control) Pin the source IP used to reach the FMG: 'config system central-management' / 'set fmg-source-ip <local-ip>' / 'end'.
Ensure the FGFM management tunnel (TCP/541) from the FortiGate egress interface to the FortiManager is permitted and not blocked by any upstream ACL or NAT device.
On FortiManager, authorize the incoming registration (Device Manager > Unauthorized Devices > Authorize) and retrieve the running config to seed the per-device revision history.

In FortiManager confirm automatic per-device revision retention (Device Manager > device > Revision History) and schedule a FortiManager system backup: 'execute backup all-settings ftp <server> <path> <user> <password>'.
Verify the tunnel and registration are up (see verify), then document a tested recovery runbook describing how to push a stored revision to a replacement unit.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-001.gui: FortiGate: Security Fabric > Fabric Connectors > Central Management (double-click/Edit the FortiManager card, set Status = Enabled, Type = FortiManager/On-Premise, and enter the FortiManager IP). FortiManager: Device Manager > Device & Groups (authorize the device under Unauthorized Devices, then use Revision History for per-device config retention; schedule global backups from the System Settings > Dashboard 'System Information' widget > Backup).
FORTIOS-BACKUP-001.cli:
config system central-management
  set type fortimanager
  set fmg <fmg-ip>
end
FORTIOS-BACKUP-001.verify: get system central-management  ->  confirms the configured management target: expect 'type : fortimanager' and 'fmg : <fmg-ip>'. Then confirm the FortiManager registration and FGFM (TCP/541) tunnel state with: diagnose fdsm central-mgmt-status  ->  expect 'Registration status: registered' and 'Connection status: up'. (For live FGFM tunnel debugging: diagnose debug application fgfmd -1.)
FORTIOS-BACKUP-001.rollback: Remove central management: 'config system central-management' / 'set type none' / 'end', and delete the device on the FortiManager side. Breaks: FortiManager stops storing config revisions and automated backups, change tracking/attribution is lost, and any FMG-driven policy push workflow halts.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-001.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot; no SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA/admin session drop. It opens an outbound FGFM tunnel on TCP/541 and requires authorization on the FortiManager side. Operational change: FortiManager becomes the source of truth, so direct CLI edits may be reported out-of-sync or overwritten on the next install from FMG.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - central management / configuration backup recommendation (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP -> 12.1.1)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Requirement 12.10.1 (incident response and recovery)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CP-9 (System Backup), CM-2 (Baseline Configuration)
SOC 2 (2017 TSC) CC7.5 (recovery from incidents)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Central Management / Configuring central management (registering a FortiGate with FortiManager)
Fortinet FortiManager Administration Guide - Device Manager: adding/authorizing devices and Revision History
CWE-693: Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-BACKUP-002.risk: With revision-backup-on-logout disabled, the FortiGate does not automatically snapshot the configuration when an administrator logs out, and (if revision-image-auto-backup is also off) it does not snapshot before a firmware upgrade. An admin who fat-fingers a policy, an attacker who quietly weakens a rule, or a failed upgrade then leaves no clean, timestamped local revision to roll back to. There is no before/after config history to prove what changed, when, or by whom, which lengthens incident investigation and outage recovery and undermines change-control auditing.
steps: Log in to the CLI via console or SSH.
Enable automatic revision backup when an admin logs out: 'config system global' / 'set revision-backup-on-logout enable'.
In the same block, enable an automatic revision snapshot before firmware changes: 'set revision-image-auto-backup enable' / 'end'.
List the stored configuration revisions to confirm the store is active: 'execute revision list config'.
On flash-constrained models, offload long-term history by also registering the unit with FortiManager/FortiAnalyzer so revisions are retained off-box.
Test the workflow end to end: make a trivial change, log out, log back in, and confirm a new revision was recorded with 'execute revision list config'.
Document the restore procedure for operators: 'execute restore config flash <id>' (restores the selected on-flash configuration revision; the unit reboots to apply it).
Run the verify command to confirm both revision settings are enabled.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-002.gui: This toggle is CLI-only in current FortiOS builds (config system global). Stored configuration revisions themselves can be listed/restored from the CLI via 'execute revision list config' / 'execute restore config flash <id>'. On builds that expose it, the revision list appears under the System dashboard's configuration/revision widget.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-002.cli:
config system global
  set revision-backup-on-logout enable
  set revision-image-auto-backup enable
end
FORTIOS-BACKUP-002.verify: get system global | grep revision  ->  expect 'revision-backup-on-logout : enable' and 'revision-image-auto-backup : enable'. Then 'execute revision list config' should list at least one saved revision after the next logout.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-002.rollback: 'config system global' / 'set revision-backup-on-logout disable' / 'set revision-image-auto-backup disable' / 'end'. Breaks: no automatic config history on logout or before upgrades, removing the local rollback safety net; frees a small amount of flash.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-002.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot and no SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA/admin session impact. Only cost is minor additional flash writes on each admin logout and before firmware upgrades.
references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CM-2 (Baseline Configuration), CM-3 (Configuration Change Control), CP-9 (System Backup)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Configuration backups and reset / configuration revisions
Fortinet FortiOS CLI Reference - config system global (revision-backup-on-logout, revision-image-auto-backup)
CIS Controls v8 - Control 4.1 (secure configuration) and Control 11 (Data Recovery)
CWE-693: Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-BACKUP-003.risk: In an HA cluster without session pickup, the secondary unit does not hold synchronized session state, so on failover every established stateful session is dropped. Users lose active SSL-VPN/IPsec tunnels, long-lived TCP sessions (database, file transfer, VoIP calls) and NAT mappings, forcing reconnects at the exact moment of a device failure or upgrade. For a DR/resilience control this defeats much of the point of HA: instead of a transparent sub-second cutover you get a visible outage and cascading application errors, and maintenance-window firmware upgrades cannot be done non-disruptively.
steps: Confirm the cluster is healthy and in sync before changing HA: 'get system ha status' and 'diagnose sys ha checksum show' (both nodes should match).
Schedule the change in a low-traffic window because HA setting changes can cause a brief cluster renegotiation.
On the primary unit enter HA config: 'config system ha'.
Enable stateful session failover: 'set session-pickup enable'.
Reduce sync overhead by only synchronizing longer-lived sessions: 'set session-pickup-delay enable'.
If UDP/ICMP and NAT session survivability are required, also set 'set session-pickup-connectionless enable' and 'set session-pickup-nat enable', then 'end'.
Let the setting propagate to the secondary automatically over the HA link and confirm both units still show a matching config checksum ('diagnose sys ha checksum show').

Note that only sessions established AFTER enabling will survive; optionally validate with a controlled failover during the window (e.g. 'diagnose sys ha reset-uptime' on the primary) and confirm traffic continues.
Run the verify command to confirm session-pickup is enabled and session-sync counters are incrementing on both nodes.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-003.gui: System > HA: edit the HA configuration and enable the 'Session pickup' option (visible when Mode is Active-Passive or Active-Active). The connectionless/NAT/delay sub-options are CLI-only under 'config system ha'.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-003.cli:
config system ha
  set session-pickup enable
  set session-pickup-delay enable
  set session-pickup-connectionless enable
  set session-pickup-nat enable
end
FORTIOS-BACKUP-003.verify: get system ha | grep session-pickup  ->  expect 'session-pickup : enable'. Then confirm session-sync traffic with 'diagnose sys ha session-sync-dev' -> the SES (session) packet transmit/receive counters should be advancing on both the primary and secondary units; 'get system ha status' should also report both members in sync.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-003.rollback: 'config system ha' / 'set session-pickup disable' / 'end'. Breaks: sessions are once again dropped on every failover (reintroducing the outage risk); synchronized session state is cleared and HA sync CPU/bandwidth drops.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-003.impact: Applied live without a reboot; the setting change itself does not drop admin/VPN sessions. It does increase HA heartbeat/session-sync bandwidth and CPU. Best applied in a maintenance window because HA config changes can trigger a brief cluster renegotiation, and any test failover used to validate it IS disruptive and must be scheduled.
references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CP-7 (Alternate Processing Site / failover), CP-10 (System Recovery and Reconstitution)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Requirement 12.10.1 (business continuity / recovery)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - High Availability > Session pickup (docs.fortinet.com/document/fortigate/7.6.3/administration-guide/955521/session-pickup)
Fortinet FortiOS CLI Reference - config system ha (session-pickup, session-pickup-delay, session-pickup-connectionless, session-pickup-nat)
CWE-693: Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-BACKUP-004.risk: With cfg-save set to automatic, every configuration change is written to persistent storage immediately with no confirmation step. A remote administrator who applies a bad firewall or routing/interface change (for example an incorrect trusthost, a policy that blocks the management path, or an SSL-VPN change) is instantly and permanently locked out, and an attacker or careless operator's changes persist through the next reboot with no built-in undo. Automatic save also removes the natural 'stage then confirm' checkpoint that catches self-inflicted lockouts before they become permanent, increasing mean-time-to-recover for remote sites.
steps: Take a current backup first: 'execute backup config flash <comment>' (and/or an off-box FTP/TFTP backup).
Switch the save mode so unconfirmed changes roll back automatically: 'config system global' / 'set cfg-save revert'.
Set a sane confirmation window in seconds: 'set cfg-revert-timeout 600' / 'end'.
Brief all administrators on the new workflow: after applying and validating a change, persist it with 'execute cfg save'; otherwise it auto-reverts after the timeout and is lost on reboot.
(Stricter alternative) For change-controlled environments use 'set cfg-save manual', which never auto-saves and requires an explicit 'execute cfg save' for every change with no timed revert.
Validate the safety net in a lab or window: make a benign change, do NOT run 'execute cfg save', wait past cfg-revert-timeout (or reboot) and confirm the change reverts.
Update the change-management runbook to include the mandatory 'execute cfg save' confirmation step.
Run the verify command to confirm the mode and timeout are set.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-004.gui: CLI-only. cfg-save and cfg-revert-timeout are not exposed in the FortiGate GUI; configure them under 'config system global' and confirm saved changes with the CLI command 'execute cfg save'.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-004.cli:
config system global
  set cfg-save revert
  set cfg-revert-timeout 600
end
# After applying and validating each change set, persist it with:
# execute cfg save
FORTIOS-BACKUP-004.verify: get system global | grep cfg-save  ->  expect 'cfg-save : revert'; and get system global | grep cfg-revert-timeout  ->  expect 'cfg-revert-timeout : 600'.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-004.rollback: 'config system global' / 'set cfg-save automatic' / 'end' returns to immediate auto-save (the original risky behavior); no outage. Watch: if changes were staged under revert/manual mode, run 'execute cfg save' before switching or they may be lost.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-004.impact: Non-disruptive to apply; no reboot and no SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA/admin session drop. It changes the administrator workflow: operators must run 'execute cfg save' to persist changes, and in revert mode an unconfirmed remote change is dropped when cfg-revert-timeout expires (which can also drop the change made in the current session if forgotten).
references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CM-3 (Configuration Change Control), CM-5 (Access Restrictions for Change)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Configuration revert mode (cfg-save) and confirming changes with execute cfg save
Fortinet FortiOS CLI Reference - config system global (cfg-save, cfg-revert-timeout)
CIS Controls v8 - Control 4 (Secure Configuration of Enterprise Assets and Software)
CWE-693: Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-BACKUP-005.risk: With USB auto-install enabled, the FortiGate will, at boot, automatically load a configuration and/or firmware image from an inserted USB device if a matching file is found - with no authentication. An attacker (or malicious insider) with brief physical access can insert a USB stick and power-cycle the appliance to silently overwrite the running configuration or downgrade/replace firmware, effectively taking full control of the firewall or planting a backdoored config. Even without malice it is a foot-gun: a stale staging USB left in a port can clobber production config on the next reboot. It maps to CWE-306 (Missing Authentication for a Critical Function).
steps: Confirm USB auto-install is not part of a legitimate provisioning process (in production it almost never is).
Enter the auto-install context: 'config system auto-install'.
Disable configuration auto-install from USB: 'set auto-install-config disable'.
Disable firmware auto-install from USB: 'set auto-install-image disable', then 'end'.
If a USB config/image file was previously referenced, clear it: 'config system auto-install' / 'unset default-config-file' / 'unset default-image-file' / 'end'.
Restrict physical access to the appliance and its USB ports as defense in depth (locked rack/room, tamper-evident port blockers).
Add USB auto-install disablement to the golden hardening baseline pushed via FortiManager so rebuilt or RMA-replaced units inherit it automatically.
Run the verify command to confirm both auto-install options are disabled.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-005.gui: Primarily CLI-controlled via 'config system auto-install'. On models/versions that expose it, the option appears under System > Settings as a 'USB Auto-Install' / 'USB firmware and configuration installation' checkbox; the CLI setting is authoritative. Push the hardened setting fleet-wide from FortiManager via Device Manager provisioning templates / CLI template.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-005.cli:
config system auto-install
  set auto-install-config disable
  set auto-install-image disable
end
FORTIOS-BACKUP-005.verify: get system auto-install  ->  expect 'auto-install-config : disable' and 'auto-install-image : disable'.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-005.rollback: 'config system auto-install' / 'set auto-install-config enable' / 'set auto-install-image enable' / 'end'. This re-enables unauthenticated USB provisioning (reintroducing the physical config/firmware-overwrite risk) and should only be done temporarily for a controlled bench provisioning workflow.
FORTIOS-BACKUP-005.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot and no SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA/admin session impact; it only alters boot-time USB behavior, which takes effect at the next power-on.
references: Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' Guide - Disable auto USB installation (config system auto-install / set auto-install-config disable / set auto-install-image disable)
Fortinet FortiOS CLI Reference - config system auto-install (auto-install-config, auto-install-image, default-config-file, default-image-file)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: MP-7 (Media Use), CM-7 (Least Functionality), PE-3 (Physical Access Control)
CIS Controls v8 - Control 10.3 (disable autorun/autoplay for removable media)
CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

FORTIOS-CERT-001.risk: The FortiGate is presenting a built-in Fortinet factory/self-signed certificate (e.g. Fortinet_Factory, Fortinet_SSL) whose private key is shared and well-known across every unit of that model and firmware. An attacker who has extracted that key from any identical appliance (or from public research) can impersonate the HTTPS admin GUI or the SSL-VPN portal and perform a man-in-the-middle attack, decrypting administrator and remote-user credentials, MFA tokens and session cookies. Because staff are conditioned to click through the certificate warning, the interception is unlikely to be noticed. Business impact: full administrative takeover of the firewall and interception of all remote-access VPN traffic.
steps: Generate a CSR with a unique 2048-bit (or ECDSA P-256) key. GUI: System > Certificates > Create/Import > Certificate > Generate CSR. CLI: execute vpn certificate local generate rsa MyOrgCert 2048 fw.example.com US California SanJose ExampleCorp IT admin@example.com DNS:fw.example.com
Submit the CSR to your enterprise or public Certificate Authority and obtain the signed certificate (request SHA-256 signing).
Import the signed certificate: System > Certificates > Import > Local Certificate.
Import the issuing CA chain: System > Certificates > Import > CA Certificate (so clients build a trusted path).
Bind the new certificate to the admin GUI: config system global / set admin-server-cert "MyOrgCert" / end
Bind the new certificate to SSL-VPN: config vpn ssl settings / set servercert "MyOrgCert" / end
Rebind any other service still on the factory cert (captive portal, load-balance/SSL-offload VIPs under config firewall vip, deep-inspection profile CA).
Confirm no remaining reference to Fortinet_Factory/self-sign, then optionally delete unused factory references from service configs.
Test the admin GUI and SSL-VPN from a client browser and confirm a trusted padlock with your CA as issuer.
FORTIOS-CERT-001.gui: FortiGate: System > Certificates > Create/Import (Generate CSR / Import Local & CA Certificate); then System > Settings > Administration Settings > HTTPS server certificate; and VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > Server Certificate. FortiManager: Device Manager > Device & Groups > (device) > System > Certificates (import), and Provisioning Templates > Certificate Templates for SCEP-based enrollment.
FORTIOS-CERT-001.cli:
config vpn certificate local
  Generate or import a CA-signed certificate to replace the default self-signed certificate.
end
FORTIOS-CERT-001.verify: get system global | grep admin-server-cert  -> admin-server-cert : MyOrgCert ; show vpn ssl settings | grep servercert -> set servercert "MyOrgCert" ; get vpn certificate local details MyOrgCert -> Issuer shows your CA, not 'Fortinet'.
FORTIOS-CERT-001.rollback: Rebind the defaults: config system global / set admin-server-cert self-sign / end and config vpn ssl settings / set servercert Fortinet_Factory / end. This restores the shared insecure certificate and the browser trust warning; only revert if the new certificate breaks admin or VPN access, and re-attempt with a corrected chain.
FORTIOS-CERT-001.impact: Generating/importing is non-disruptive. Binding admin-server-cert restarts the HTTPS admin daemon and drops your current GUI session (reconnect immediately). Binding the SSL-VPN servercert restarts the SSL-VPN daemon and briefly drops active SSL-VPN tunnels, so schedule a short maintenance window. No reboot required.
references: FortiOS Administration Guide - Certificates chapter (Generating a CSR / Importing a certificate)
FortiOS 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide - use trusted (non-default) certificates for administrative access and SSL-VPN
NIST SP 800-52 Rev.2 (TLS server certificate configuration)
CWE-295: Improper Certificate Validation

FORTIOS-CERT-002.risk: A certificate bound to a live service (admin GUI, SSL-VPN, IPsec, or LDAPS server-identity check) has already passed its validity end date. Every client now receives a hard certificate error and is trained to bypass it, permanently normalising click-through of warnings and masking a genuine man-in-the-middle. During the warning window an attacker can present their own self-signed certificate and users will accept it, yielding credential capture and full VPN/admin session interception. Depending on the consuming feature it may instead fail closed, causing an availability outage for remote access. Because this is a live expiry it is treated as CRITICAL.
steps: Identify which service uses the expired cert and its exact name: get vpn certificate local details <cert-name> (look at 'Valid to').

Generate a fresh CSR/key: execute vpn certificate local generate rsa MyOrgCert2 2048 fw.example.com US California SanJose ExampleCorp IT admin@example.com DNS:fw.example.com (or GUI: System > Certificates > Create/Import > Generate CSR).
Have your CA sign it (SHA-256) and import: System > Certificates > Import > Local Certificate; import the CA chain as a CA Certificate.
Rebind admin GUI: config system global / set admin-server-cert "MyOrgCert2" / end
Rebind SSL-VPN: config vpn ssl settings / set servercert "MyOrgCert2" / end
Rebind any IPsec phase1 that referenced the cert: config vpn ipsec phase1-interface / edit <name> / set certificate "MyOrgCert2" / next / end
Test admin GUI, SSL-VPN and any cert-authenticated tunnels; confirm no client warning and a future 'Valid to' date.
Delete the expired certificate once no service references it, and set a renewal reminder / ACME auto-renew to prevent recurrence.
FORTIOS-CERT-002.gui: FortiGate: System > Certificates (view expiry, import replacement); System > Settings > Administration Settings > HTTPS server certificate; VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > Server Certificate; VPN > IPsec Tunnels > (tunnel) > Authentication for cert-based tunnels. FortiManager: Device Manager > (device) > System > Certificates.
FORTIOS-CERT-002.cli:
# 1) Generate a new key/CSR, get it CA-signed, then import (GUI or 'execute vpn certificate local import ...').
# 2) Rebind the service to the new cert:
config system global
  set admin-server-cert "MyOrgCert2"
end
config vpn ssl settings
  set servercert "MyOrgCert2"
end
FORTIOS-CERT-002.verify: get vpn certificate local details MyOrgCert2  -> 'Valid to:' shows a future date; get system global | grep admin-server-cert -> admin-server-cert : MyOrgCert2.
FORTIOS-CERT-002.rollback: Re-point the service to the previous certificate name (or self-sign/Fortinet_Factory). Reverting to the expired certificate re-introduces the client error and MitM exposure, so this is only a stop-gap if the replacement chain is misconfigured.
FORTIOS-CERT-002.impact: Import is non-disruptive. Rebinding admin-server-cert restarts the HTTPS admin daemon (drops your GUI session). Rebinding SSL-VPN servercert restarts SSL-VPN and drops active tunnels; rebinding an IPsec certificate renegotiates that tunnel. No reboot. Treat as urgent/CRITICAL - do it in the next available window.
references: RFC 5280 - X.509 certificate validity period
FortiOS Administration Guide - Certificates (renewing/importing)
CWE-298: Improper Validation of Certificate Expiration
CWE-295: Improper Certificate Validation

FORTIOS-CERT-003.risk: A certificate bound to a production service will expire within 30 days. If it lapses before replacement, the admin GUI or SSL-VPN portal begins throwing certificate errors that users click through (normalising warnings and enabling MitM), or cert-authenticated IPsec/LDAPS features fail closed and cut off remote access. A short 30-day window means an unplanned change or a slow CA turnaround can turn into an outage or a credential-interception opportunity.
steps: Confirm the expiring cert and consuming service: get vpn certificate local details <cert-name>.

Generate a renewal CSR reusing the same subject/SANs: System > Certificates > Create/Import > Generate CSR, or execute vpn certificate local generate rsa <name>-2026 2048 fw.example.com US California SanJose ExampleCorp IT admin@example.com DNS:fw.example.com
Submit to the CA (SHA-256) and import the signed cert plus CA chain: System > Certificates > Import.
Rebind the service (config system global / set admin-server-cert; config vpn ssl settings / set servercert; IPsec phase1 set certificate).

For public-facing names, consider enabling ACME auto-renewal so this never recurs: config vpn certificate local / edit le-cert / set enroll-protocol acme2 / set acme-ca-url "https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory" / set acme-domain "fw.example.com" / set acme-email "admin@example.com" / next / end (requires inbound TCP/80 for HTTP-01).
Add SNMP/FortiAnalyzer certificate-expiry alerting so future renewals are triggered 60+ days out.
Validate the service from a client and delete the old cert after cutover.
FORTIOS-CERT-003.gui: FortiGate: System > Certificates (check 'Valid To', import renewal); System > Settings > Administration Settings > HTTPS server certificate; VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > Server Certificate. For automation: System > Certificates > Create/Import > Certificate > 'Use ACME'. FortiManager: Device Manager > (device) > System > Certificates.
FORTIOS-CERT-003.cli:
# Renew (CSR + CA signing), then rebind. Optional ACME auto-renewal for public names:
config vpn certificate local
  edit "le-cert"
    set enroll-protocol acme2
    set acme-ca-url "https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory"
    set acme-domain "fw.example.com"
    set acme-email "admin@example.com"
  next
end
config vpn ssl settings
  set servercert "le-cert"
end
FORTIOS-CERT-003.verify: get vpn certificate local details <new-cert>  -> 'Valid to:' at least ~12 months out; get system global | grep admin-server-cert (or show vpn ssl settings | grep servercert) points to the new cert.
FORTIOS-CERT-003.rollback: Rebind the previous certificate name; it remains valid until its expiry date, so this is a safe temporary revert while a CSR/CA issue is resolved. Disable ACME by setting enroll-protocol back to none if auto-enrollment misbehaves.
FORTIOS-CERT-003.impact: Non-disruptive to import/renew. Rebinding restarts the affected daemon (admin GUI session drop; brief SSL-VPN tunnel drop). ACME enrollment requires reachability to the ACME CA and TCP/80 for HTTP-01 validation. No reboot.
references: FortiOS Administration Guide - Certificates: ACME certificate / automatic renewal
RFC 8555 - Automatic Certificate Management Environment (ACME)
CWE-295: Improper Certificate Validation

FORTIOS-CERT-004.risk: A non-factory self-signed certificate is in use. Because it is not issued by a CA that clients trust, browsers and VPN clients cannot verify it and present a warning that users are trained to bypass. An attacker on the path can substitute their own self-signed certificate and users will accept it just as readily, enabling a man-in-the-middle that harvests admin/VPN credentials and session tokens. Self-signed certs also cannot be revoked through PKI, so a compromised key stays trusted by anyone who pinned it.
steps: Identify the self-signed cert and where it is bound: get vpn certificate local details <cert-name> (Issuer == Subject indicates self-signed).

Generate a CSR with the correct CN/SANs: System > Certificates > Create/Import > Generate CSR, or execute vpn certificate local generate rsa MyOrgCert 2048 fw.example.com US California SanJose ExampleCorp IT admin@example.com DNS:fw.example.com
Get it signed by an enterprise or public CA (SHA-256) and import it: System > Certificates > Import > Local Certificate.
Import the full CA chain: System > Certificates > Import > CA Certificate.
Rebind services: config system global / set admin-server-cert "MyOrgCert" / end and config vpn ssl settings / set servercert "MyOrgCert" / end.
Rebind any other consumers (deep-inspection CA, VIP SSL offload, LDAPS client cert).
Test from a client for a trusted padlock, then delete the self-signed certificate once unreferenced.
FORTIOS-CERT-004.gui: FortiGate: System > Certificates > Create/Import (Generate CSR, Import Local & CA); System > Settings > Administration Settings > HTTPS server certificate; VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > Server Certificate. FortiManager: Device Manager > (device) > System > Certificates.
FORTIOS-CERT-004.cli:
# Generate CSR, obtain CA signature, import, then bind the CA-signed cert:
config system global
  set admin-server-cert "MyOrgCert"
end
config vpn ssl settings
  set servercert "MyOrgCert"
end
FORTIOS-CERT-004.verify: get vpn certificate local details MyOrgCert  -> Issuer differs from Subject and names your CA; get system global | grep admin-server-cert -> MyOrgCert.
FORTIOS-CERT-004.rollback: Rebind the previous self-signed cert name. This restores the untrusted-warning/MitM exposure and should only be a temporary measure while a CA-chain problem is fixed.
FORTIOS-CERT-004.impact: Non-disruptive to import. Rebinding admin-server-cert drops the current GUI session; rebinding SSL-VPN servercert briefly drops active tunnels. No reboot.
references: FortiOS Administration Guide - Certificates (Generating and importing certificates)
NIST SP 800-52 Rev.2 (server certificates from a trusted CA)
CWE-295: Improper Certificate Validation
CWE-599: Missing Validation of OpenSSL Certificate

FORTIOS-CERT-005.risk: A certificate uses an RSA key shorter than 2048 bits (e.g. 1024-bit). Sub-2048-bit RSA keys are within reach of well-resourced adversaries; a factored private key lets an attacker decrypt captured TLS/VPN sessions and fully impersonate the FortiGate service (admin GUI, SSL-VPN, IPsec) without any warning to the user. Because a key size cannot be 'strengthened' in place, the weak key remains exploitable until the certificate is reissued with a stronger key. Business impact: retrospective decryption of intercepted admin/VPN traffic and credential theft.
steps: List certificates and key sizes: get vpn certificate local details <cert-name> (check the key length).

Generate a NEW keypair/CSR at 2048-bit RSA or stronger: execute vpn certificate local generate rsa MyOrgCert 2048 fw.example.com US California SanJose ExampleCorp IT admin@example.com DNS:fw.example.com
(Alternative) Generate an ECDSA P-256 key: execute vpn certificate local generate ec MyOrgCertEC secp256r1 fw.example.com US California SanJose ExampleCorp IT admin@example.com
Get the CSR signed by your CA (SHA-256) and import the signed cert and CA chain: System > Certificates > Import.
Rebind services to the strong cert: config system global / set admin-server-cert "MyOrgCert" / end and config vpn ssl settings / set servercert "MyOrgCert" / end.
Rebind IPsec phase1 certificates and any SSL-offload VIPs still using the weak cert.
Validate services from a client, then delete the weak certificate once unreferenced.
Consider enforcing strong crypto globally: config system global / set strong-crypto enable / end.
FORTIOS-CERT-005.gui: FortiGate: System > Certificates > Create/Import > Generate CSR (choose Key Size 2048 or ECDSA); System > Settings > Administration Settings > HTTPS server certificate; VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > Server Certificate. FortiManager: Device Manager > (device) > System > Certificates.
FORTIOS-CERT-005.cli:
# Reissue with a >=2048-bit RSA (or ECDSA) key, then bind:
execute vpn certificate local generate rsa MyOrgCert 2048 fw.example.com US California SanJose ExampleCorp IT admin@example.com DNS:fw.example.com
# (get the CSR signed and import the CA-signed cert)
config system global
  set admin-server-cert "MyOrgCert"
end
config vpn ssl settings
  set servercert "MyOrgCert"
end
FORTIOS-CERT-005.verify: get vpn certificate local details MyOrgCert  -> the certificate shows a 2048-bit RSA (or 256-bit ECDSA P-256) public key; the bound service references the new cert.
FORTIOS-CERT-005.rollback: Rebind the previous certificate name. Reverting restores the weak key and its cryptographic exposure, so use only as a temporary fallback while a signing/chain issue is corrected.
FORTIOS-CERT-005.impact: Reissuance and import are non-disruptive. Rebinding restarts the affected daemon (admin GUI session drop; brief SSL-VPN tunnel drop; IPsec renegotiation). Enabling strong-crypto also affects admin SSH/TLS ciphers and may drop legacy clients. No reboot.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - Recommendation 2.1.9 (Enable Global Strong Encryption / strong-crypto)
NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev.5 (key length recommendations - 2048-bit RSA minimum)
CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements (minimum 2048-bit RSA / P-256 ECDSA)
CWE-326: Inadequate Encryption Strength

FORTIOS-CERT-006.risk: A certificate is signed with the SHA-1 hash algorithm. SHA-1 is broken against chosen-prefix collisions (SHAttered/2017 and cheaper attacks since), allowing a capable adversary to forge a certificate that chains to the same signature, or to substitute a colliding certificate in a man-in-the-middle against the admin GUI or SSL-VPN. Modern browsers and OS trust stores already reject SHA-1, so the cert also produces hard errors that condition users to bypass warnings. Business impact: certificate forgery/impersonation and interception of administrative and VPN sessions.
steps: Confirm the signature algorithm: get vpn certificate local details <cert-name> (look for sha1WithRSAEncryption).

Generate a new CSR: System > Certificates > Create/Import > Generate CSR (or execute vpn certificate local generate rsa MyOrgCert 2048 fw.example.com US California SanJose ExampleCorp IT admin@example.com DNS:fw.example.com).
Instruct the CA to sign with SHA-256 or SHA-384 (the signing hash is chosen by the CA, not the CSR); confirm the intermediate/root are also SHA-256.
Import the SHA-256-signed cert and CA chain: System > Certificates > Import.
Rebind services: config system global / set admin-server-cert "MyOrgCert" / end and config vpn ssl settings / set servercert "MyOrgCert" / end.
Rebind IPsec/LDAPS/VIP consumers that referenced the SHA-1 cert.
Validate from a client (issuer shows SHA-256), then delete the SHA-1 certificate once unreferenced.
FORTIOS-CERT-006.gui: FortiGate: System > Certificates > Create/Import > Generate CSR; System > Settings > Administration Settings > HTTPS server certificate; VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > Server Certificate. Ensure the CA profile signs with SHA-256. FortiManager: Device Manager > (device) > System > Certificates.
FORTIOS-CERT-006.cli:
# The signing hash is set by the CA when it signs the CSR - request SHA-256/384.
# After importing the SHA-256-signed cert, bind it:
config system global
  set admin-server-cert "MyOrgCert"
end
config vpn ssl settings
  set servercert "MyOrgCert"
end
FORTIOS-CERT-006.verify: get vpn certificate local details MyOrgCert  -> Signature Algorithm shows sha256WithRSAEncryption (or ecdsa-with-SHA256); no SHA-1 in the chain.
FORTIOS-CERT-006.rollback: Rebind the previous SHA-1 certificate name. Reverting restores collision/forgery exposure and client rejection, so use only as a short-term fallback while a compliant chain is prepared.
FORTIOS-CERT-006.impact: Non-disruptive to import. Rebinding restarts the affected daemon (admin GUI session drop; brief SSL-VPN tunnel drop; IPsec renegotiation). No reboot.
references: RFC 6194 - Security Considerations for the SHA-0 and SHA-1 Message-Digest Algorithms
NIST SP 800-131A Rev.2 (SHA-1 disallowed for digital signature generation)
CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements (SHA-1 prohibited for TLS certificates)
CWE-328: Use of Weak Hash

FORTIOS-CERT-007.risk: No local certificates are installed, so the FortiGate is serving every TLS service (admin GUI, SSL-VPN, deep inspection, LDAPS) with built-in default/self-signed certificates that share well-known keys and are untrusted by clients. Users are forced to click through certificate warnings, normalising exactly the behaviour a man-in-the-middle attacker relies on to substitute their own certificate and capture administrator and VPN credentials. Business impact: no cryptographic identity for the appliance, enabling impersonation and credential interception on both management and remote-access paths.
steps: Generate a CSR for the appliance's management/VPN FQDN: System > Certificates > Create/Import > Generate CSR, or execute vpn certificate local generate rsa MyOrgCert 2048 fw.example.com US California SanJose ExampleCorp IT admin@example.com DNS:fw.example.com
Submit to your enterprise or public CA (SHA-256) and obtain the signed certificate.
Import the signed cert: System > Certificates > Import > Local Certificate.
Import the issuing CA chain: System > Certificates > Import > CA Certificate.
Bind to admin GUI: config system global / set admin-server-cert "MyOrgCert" / end.
Bind to SSL-VPN: config vpn ssl settings / set servercert "MyOrgCert" / end.
Bind to any other TLS service in use (VIP SSL offload, deep-inspection CA, LDAPS).
Test each service from a client and confirm a trusted padlock; set an expiry-monitoring reminder or ACME auto-renewal.
FORTIOS-CERT-007.gui: FortiGate: System > Certificates > Create/Import (Generate CSR, Import Local & CA); System > Settings > Administration Settings > HTTPS server certificate; VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > Server Certificate. (If the Certificates menu is hidden, enable it under System > Feature Visibility > Certificates.) FortiManager: Device Manager > (device) > System > Certificates.
FORTIOS-CERT-007.cli:
# Generate a CSR, get it CA-signed, import it and the CA chain, then bind:
execute vpn certificate local generate rsa MyOrgCert 2048 fw.example.com US California SanJose ExampleCorp IT admin@example.com DNS:fw.example.com
config system global
  set admin-server-cert "MyOrgCert"
end
config vpn ssl settings
  set servercert "MyOrgCert"
end
FORTIOS-CERT-007.verify: get vpn certificate local  -> lists your imported CA-signed certificate; get system global | grep admin-server-cert -> MyOrgCert; show vpn ssl settings | grep servercert -> set servercert "MyOrgCert".
FORTIOS-CERT-007.rollback: Rebind admin-server-cert self-sign and servercert Fortinet_Factory to return to defaults. Reverting re-introduces the untrusted-default exposure; only do so if the imported certificate breaks access, then re-import a corrected chain.
FORTIOS-CERT-007.impact: Import is non-disruptive. Binding admin-server-cert drops the current GUI session; binding SSL-VPN servercert briefly drops active tunnels. No reboot.
references: FortiOS Administration Guide - Certificates (Generating/importing and binding certificates)
FortiOS 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide - management-interface TLS certificate
NIST SP 800-52 Rev.2
CWE-295: Improper Certificate Validation

FORTIOS-CERT-008.risk: A certificate bound to a service will expire within 90 days. This is an early-warning finding: if the renewal is not scheduled, the certificate will eventually lapse and cause admin GUI / SSL-VPN client errors (normalising warnings and enabling MitM) or fail-closed outages on cert-authenticated IPsec/LDAPS. Ninety days' notice exists specifically so renewal can be completed through change control before any exposure or outage occurs.
steps: Record the certificate and its consuming service: get vpn certificate local details <cert-name> ('Valid to').
Schedule the renewal within the next ~60 days through change management.

Generate a renewal CSR with the same subject/SANs: System > Certificates > Create/Import > Generate CSR (or execute vpn certificate local generate rsa <name>-renew 2048 fw.example.com US California SanJose ExampleCorp IT admin@example.com DNS:fw.example.com).
Obtain the CA-signed cert (SHA-256) and import it plus the CA chain.
Rebind the service at the change window (set admin-server-cert / set servercert / IPsec set certificate).

For public FQDNs, enable ACME auto-renewal to eliminate manual renewals: config vpn certificate local / edit le-cert / set enroll-protocol acme2 / set acme-domain "fw.example.com" / set acme-email "admin@example.com" / next / end.
Add certificate-expiry alerting (SNMP trap / FortiAnalyzer) so the next renewal fires 60+ days out.
FORTIOS-CERT-008.gui: FortiGate: System > Certificates (review 'Valid To' column); System > Settings > Administration Settings > HTTPS server certificate; VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > Server Certificate; optional ACME via System > Certificates > Create/Import > 'Use ACME'. FortiManager: Device Manager > (device) > System > Certificates.
FORTIOS-CERT-008.cli:
# Renew via CSR + CA signing, then rebind. Optional ACME auto-renew for public names:
config vpn certificate local
  edit "le-cert"
    set enroll-protocol acme2
    set acme-ca-url "https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory"
    set acme-domain "fw.example.com"
    set acme-email "admin@example.com"
  next
end
config vpn ssl settings
  set servercert "le-cert"
end
FORTIOS-CERT-008.verify: get vpn certificate local details <new-cert>  -> 'Valid to:' roughly 12 months out; the bound service references the renewed certificate.
FORTIOS-CERT-008.rollback: Continue using the current certificate (valid for up to ~90 more days) if the renewal must be deferred; rebind the previous cert name if a new one is faulty. ACME can be disabled by resetting enroll-protocol to none.
FORTIOS-CERT-008.impact: Non-disruptive - this is proactive. When you eventually rebind, the affected daemon restarts (admin GUI session drop; brief SSL-VPN tunnel drop). No reboot.
references: FortiOS Administration Guide - Certificates: ACME certificate / automatic renewal
RFC 8555 - Automatic Certificate Management Environment (ACME)
CWE-295: Improper Certificate Validation

FORTIOS-CERT-009.risk: A wildcard certificate (*.example.com) is installed. A single wildcard key secures every subdomain, so if the FortiGate's private key is stolen (or the key is shared across many hosts) an attacker can impersonate ALL subdomains at once - VPN portal, management, internal apps - dramatically widening the blast radius of one compromise. Wildcards also frustrate least-privilege key segregation and per-service revocation. This is rated LOW: the certificate is valid and trusted, but its scope should be minimised and its key tightly protected.
steps: Identify the wildcard cert and everywhere it is bound: get vpn certificate local details <cert-name> (CN begins with *.).
Where feasible, issue dedicated single-name (or explicit SAN) certificates per service instead of the wildcard: System > Certificates > Create/Import > Generate CSR with the exact FQDN.
Rebind the most exposed services (SSL-VPN portal, admin GUI) to the specific certificate: config vpn ssl settings / set servercert "vpn-specific-cert" / end.
Restrict where the wildcard remains in use to only the services that genuinely need it.
Ensure the wildcard private key is not exported/reused on other systems; keep it only on the appliances that require it.
Enable revocation checking (see FORTIOS-CERT-010) so a compromised wildcard can be revoked and rejected.
Monitor issuance/usage and prefer shorter validity periods to limit exposure of the broad key.
FORTIOS-CERT-009.gui: FortiGate: System > Certificates (identify wildcard); System > Settings > Administration Settings > HTTPS server certificate; VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > Server Certificate (rebind to a specific cert where possible). FortiManager: Device Manager > (device) > System > Certificates.
FORTIOS-CERT-009.cli:
# Prefer specific per-service certs over a shared wildcard; rebind the exposed service:
config vpn ssl settings
  set servercert "vpn-specific-cert"
end
config system global
  set admin-server-cert "mgmt-specific-cert"
end
FORTIOS-CERT-009.verify: get vpn certificate local details <cert>  -> CN is a specific FQDN (not *.example.com) for the rebound services; wildcard remains only where required.
FORTIOS-CERT-009.rollback: Rebind the wildcard certificate name to the service. This is functionally safe (the wildcard is valid); it simply restores the broader blast radius, which is the condition being mitigated.
FORTIOS-CERT-009.impact: Non-disruptive to import specific certs. Rebinding restarts the affected daemon (admin GUI session drop; brief SSL-VPN tunnel drop). Largely a governance/architecture change - no reboot.
references: NIST SP 800-52 Rev.2 (server certificate scoping)
CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements (wildcard issuance considerations)
CWE-295: Improper Certificate Validation

FORTIOS-CERT-010.risk: No CRL distribution points or OCSP responders are configured, so the FortiGate performs no revocation checking. A certificate that has been revoked - for example a lost/stolen SSL-VPN or IPsec user certificate, or a mis-issued CA cert - is still accepted as valid. An attacker holding a revoked-but-still-trusted certificate can authenticate to certificate-based SSL-VPN/IPsec or impersonate a server the FortiGate connects to, defeating the entire point of PKI revocation. Business impact: revoked credentials remain usable, enabling unauthorised remote access and MitM despite a documented revocation.
steps: Import the issuing CA certificate(s) if not already present: System > Certificates > Import > CA Certificate.
Configure a CRL source (auto-updating URL): config vpn certificate crl / edit "MyCRL" / set http-url "http://crl.example.com/ca.crl" / set update-interval 3600 / next / end.
Define an OCSP responder: config vpn certificate ocsp-server / edit "MyOCSP" / set url "http://ocsp.example.com" / set cert "CA_Cert_1" / set unavail-action revoke / next / end.

Enable OCSP checking globally and point at the responder: config vpn certificate setting / set ocsp-status enable / set ocsp-default-server "MyOCSP" / set ocsp-option server / set strict-ocsp-check enable / end.
(Optional) Tighten peer validation: within config vpn certificate setting also confirm check-ca-cert / check-ca-chain / cn-match / subject-match as required by policy.
Bind the checking to the consuming feature - e.g. a PKI user/peer group used by SSL-VPN/IPsec references the CA and inherits revocation checking.
Test with a known-good and a known-revoked certificate to confirm the revoked one is rejected.
Ensure the FortiGate can reach the CRL/OCSP URLs (firewall policy + source-ip/routing) so checks do not silently fail.
FORTIOS-CERT-010.gui: FortiGate: System > Certificates > Create/Import > CRL (import a CRL locally or configure a remote CRL/OCSP URL). CLI is authoritative for OCSP settings via config vpn certificate setting. Peer validation is applied where a PKI user/peer group (User & Authentication > ...) is referenced by SSL-VPN/IPsec. FortiManager: Device Manager > (device) > System > Certificates.
FORTIOS-CERT-010.cli:
config vpn certificate crl
  edit "MyCRL"
    set http-url "http://crl.example.com/ca.crl"
    set update-interval 3600
  next
end
config vpn certificate ocsp-server
  edit "MyOCSP"
    set url "http://ocsp.example.com"
    set cert "CA_Cert_1"
    set unavail-action revoke
  next
end
config vpn certificate setting
  set ocsp-status enable
  set ocsp-default-server "MyOCSP"
  set ocsp-option server
  set strict-ocsp-check enable
end
FORTIOS-CERT-010.verify: show vpn certificate setting  -> ocsp-status enable, strict-ocsp-check enable, ocsp-default-server "MyOCSP"; get vpn certificate ocsp-server -> responder listed; get vpn certificate crl -> CRL present with a recent 'Last Updated'.
FORTIOS-CERT-010.rollback: Disable checking: config vpn certificate setting / set ocsp-status disable / set strict-ocsp-check disable / end, and remove the crl/ocsp-server entries. Reverting re-opens acceptance of revoked certificates; only do so if legitimate users are being blocked by an unreachable responder.
FORTIOS-CERT-010.impact: Configuration is non-disruptive to import, but enabling strict-ocsp-check with unavail-action revoke means cert-based SSL-VPN/IPsec authentication will FAIL if the OCSP/CRL source is unreachable (fail-closed). Validate responder reachability first and consider unavail-action revoke vs. ignore per risk appetite. No reboot; no session drop from the setting change itself.
references: FortiOS CLI Reference - config vpn certificate setting (ocsp-status, strict-ocsp-check, ocsp-default-server) and config vpn certificate ocsp-server / crl
RFC 6960 - X.509 Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP)
RFC 5280 - Certificate Revocation List (CRL) profile
CWE-299: Improper Check for Certificate Revocation

FORTIOS-CERT-011.risk: A self-signed certificate is in use (e.g. for the admin GUI, SSL-VPN or SSL inspection). Clients cannot validate it against a trusted CA, so users are trained to click through certificate warnings - masking real man-in-the-middle attacks - and the certificate cannot be centrally revoked if its key is compromised.
steps: Identify where the self-signed cert is bound (admin GUI, SSL-VPN portal, or deep-inspection).
Obtain a certificate from a trusted CA (public CA for internet-facing services; enterprise CA for internal). Generate a CSR in System > Certificates, or import an existing key/cert pair.
Import the signed cert and its chain: config vpn certificate local / edit <name> / set certificate <PEM> / set private-key <PEM> / next / end; import the CA chain under config vpn certificate ca.
Bind it to the service - admin GUI: config system global / set admin-server-cert <name> / end; SSL-VPN: config vpn ssl settings / set servercert <name> / end.
Remove/replace the self-signed cert once nothing references it.
Verify clients now see a trusted certificate with no warning.
FORTIOS-CERT-011.gui: System > Certificates: Generate/Import the CA-signed certificate and chain, then bind it under System > Settings (HTTPS server certificate) and/or VPN > SSL-VPN Settings (Server Certificate).
FORTIOS-CERT-011.cli:
config system global
  set admin-server-cert <ca-signed-cert-name>
end
config vpn ssl settings
  set servercert <ca-signed-cert-name>
end
FORTIOS-CERT-011.verify: Browse the service and confirm a valid, trusted chain (no warning); get vpn certificate local shows the imported CA-signed cert bound to the service.
FORTIOS-CERT-011.rollback: Rebind the previous certificate: config system global / set admin-server-cert Fortinet_Factory (or the prior cert) / end. Reverting to self-signed restores browser warnings.
FORTIOS-CERT-011.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot. Changing the admin/SSL-VPN certificate briefly resets active HTTPS/SSL-VPN sessions as the new cert is presented; users reconnect. Import the full chain so clients validate correctly.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - use CA-signed certificates (CIS 8.1.x)
Fortinet Administration Guide - Certificates: import/generate CSR, bind server certificate
CWE-295: Improper Certificate Validation
NIST SP 800-53 SC-17, IA-5

FORTIOS-CVE.risk: The device is running a FortiOS build affected by one or more published FortiGuard PSIRT vulnerabilities. Depending on the CVE these range from remote pre-authentication code execution and authentication bypass on the SSL-VPN, administrative or fgfm interfaces (several are CISA KEV, actively exploited in the wild) to information disclosure and denial of service. An attacker who can reach the affected service can achieve full device compromise, pivot into the protected network, or disrupt connectivity.
steps: Identify the exact running build: get system status | grep Version (e.g. v7.2.4 build1396).
Read the specific finding's CVE ID and the fixed version stated in its recommendation, then confirm against the FortiGuard PSIRT advisory for your platform and train.
Download the target fixed FortiOS build for your exact model from https://support.fortinet.com (Download > Firmware Images) and follow the supported upgrade path (do not skip trains).
Back up the configuration first: execute backup config flash, and to an external server (execute backup config tftp <file> <server>).
Schedule a maintenance window; on an HA cluster enable uninterruptible upgrade (config system ha / set uninterruptible-upgrade enable / end) so the secondary upgrades first.
Upgrade via System > Firmware, or execute restore image tftp <fixed-image.out> <server>; the unit reboots into the new build.
As interim mitigation until patched, apply the advisory workaround (e.g. disable SSL-VPN web mode, restrict admin/fgfm access to trusted IPs with local-in policies, or disable the affected feature).
After upgrade, verify the build and re-run the scanner to confirm the CVE findings for this train clear.
FORTIOS-CVE.gui: FortiGate: System > Firmware (review upgrade path, back up config, upload/select the fixed image, upgrade). FortiManager: Device Manager > Firmware management to stage and push builds fleet-wide.
FORTIOS-CVE.cli:
get system status | grep Version
# after downloading the fixed build for your exact model:
execute backup config flash
execute restore image tftp <fixed-image.out> <tftp-server-ip>
# the unit reboots into the new build
FORTIOS-CVE.verify: get system status | grep Version  ->  Version: FortiGate-XXXX vX.Y.Z build NNNN  equal to or later than the fixed version; then re-run the scanner and confirm the FORTIOS-CVE finding for this train no longer fires.
FORTIOS-CVE.rollback: If the upgrade causes problems, boot the previous build from the secondary flash partition: execute set-next-reboot secondary then execute reboot; restore the pre-upgrade config if needed. Always keep the pre-upgrade config backup.
FORTIOS-CVE.impact: A firmware upgrade REBOOTS the device and briefly interrupts all traffic; on a standalone unit this is an outage, on an HA cluster use uninterruptible-upgrade to minimise it. Plan a maintenance window. Some interim workarounds (e.g. disabling SSL-VPN) disconnect remote users until patched.
references: FortiGuard PSIRT advisories: https://www.fortiguard.com/psirt?product=FortiOS
CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog (several FortiOS SSL-VPN/admin CVEs are KEV-listed)
Fortinet FortiOS Upgrade Path Tool: https://docs.fortinet.com/upgrade-tool
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' - keep firmware current
CWE-1035 / OWASP A06:2021 Vulnerable and Outdated Components

FORTIOS-DLP-001.risk: The DLP sensor is in log-only mode, so sensitive-data matches are recorded but the data still leaves the network. An insider or compromised host exfiltrating PII/PCI/PHI succeeds; the alerts only prove the breach after the fact. Business impact: DLP provides detection theatre without prevention - regulated data is lost while the control 'works' on paper.
steps: Identify the sensor and its filters: 'show dlp sensor <name>' (or 'show dlp profile <name>') and check each 'set action'.
Confirm with data owners which patterns are high-confidence enough to enforce (credit-card, SSN, fingerprinted documents).
Edit the sensor: config dlp sensor / edit "<name>".
Set the high-confidence filter(s) to block: config filter / edit <id> / set action block / next / end.
Keep noisier/low-confidence patterns in log-only or quarantine while tuning, but enforce the critical ones.
Save with 'next / end'.
Verify the sensor is still applied on the outbound policies with deep SSL inspection.
Run a controlled exfil test and confirm the transfer is now blocked, and monitor for false positives before broadening enforcement.
FORTIOS-DLP-001.gui: Security Profiles > Data Leak Prevention (DLP) > edit <sensor/profile> > set the filter/rule Action to Block (or Block + Quarantine). FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > DLP.
FORTIOS-DLP-001.cli:
config dlp sensor
    edit "<sensor>"
        config filter
            edit <filter-id>
                set action block
            next
        end
    next
end
# FortiOS 7.2+: config dlp profile / edit <name> / config rule / edit <id> / set action block / next / end / next / end
FORTIOS-DLP-001.verify: show dlp sensor <sensor> | grep action  -> set action block. Live: attempt to send matching sensitive data over an inspected channel and confirm the DLP block event.
FORTIOS-DLP-001.rollback: Set 'set action log-only' (or 'allow') on the filter. Reverting to log-only stops preventing exfiltration; only do so per-filter to work around a confirmed false positive while refining the pattern.
FORTIOS-DLP-001.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Moving from log-only to block will drop matching transfers, including false positives - validate pattern accuracy first, and note enforcement over HTTPS needs deep SSL inspection.
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Data leak prevention (DLP) filter/rule actions
CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4 Security Profiles (DLP)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
PCI-DSS v4.0 3.x; HIPAA 164.312(e)(1)
NIST SP 800-53 AC-4 Information Flow Enforcement, SC-7 Boundary Protection
CWE-200 Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

FORTIOS-DNS-001.risk: Botnet C&C domain blocking (block-botnet) is disabled in the DNS filter, so even where DNS filtering exists, the device does not use the FortiGuard botnet domain feed to block command-and-control lookups. A compromised host's malware resolves its C2 domain and establishes control, and DGA-based beaconing succeeds. Business impact: active malware retains its command channel through the gateway, enabling data theft and ransomware staging.
steps: Identify the profile: 'show dnsfilter profile <name>' and check 'set block-botnet'.
Confirm the FortiGuard botnet/DNS feed is current: 'diagnose autoupdate versions' (Botnet C&C / IPS DB).
Edit the profile: config dnsfilter profile / edit "<name>".
Enable botnet blocking: set block-botnet enable.
Enable DNS domain logging to see convictions: set sdns-domain-log enable.
Save with 'next / end'.
Confirm the profile is applied to outbound policies and that clients resolve through the FortiGate.
Monitor DNS Filter logs for 'Botnet C&C' block events and investigate any hits as potential compromise.
FORTIOS-DNS-001.gui: Security Profiles > DNS Filter > edit <profile> > enable 'Redirect botnet C&C requests to Block Portal' (block-botnet). FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > DNS Filter.
FORTIOS-DNS-001.cli:
config dnsfilter profile
    edit "<profile>"
        set block-botnet enable
        set sdns-domain-log enable
    next
end
FORTIOS-DNS-001.verify: show dnsfilter profile <profile> | grep block-botnet  -> set block-botnet enable. Live: a host resolving a known botnet C&C test domain is blocked and logged under Log & Report > Security Events > DNS Query.
FORTIOS-DNS-001.rollback: Set 'config dnsfilter profile / edit <profile> / set block-botnet disable / next / end'. Disabling it re-opens C2 domain resolution; there is rarely a legitimate reason to disable botnet blocking - if a domain is mis-rated, use a domain-filter allow entry instead.
FORTIOS-DNS-001.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Relies on a current FortiGuard botnet feed and reachable FortiGuard servers. Very low false-positive risk (the botnet feed is high-confidence).
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > DNS filter > Botnet C&C domain blocking
Fortinet FortiGuard Botnet C&C / IP Reputation service
CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4.3 DNS Filter (Botnet C&C blocking)
NIST SP 800-53 SI-4 System Monitoring, SC-7 Boundary Protection
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-HA-001.risk: A standalone FortiGate at the network edge is a single point of failure: one hardware fault, PSU/fan failure, kernel panic, or a failed firmware upgrade takes down all Internet ingress/egress, inter-segment routing, IPsec and SSL-VPN tunnels, and all inspection/logging at once. An attacker who can crash the device (triggering a DoS bug or a memory-corruption CVE) causes a total perimeter outage with no automatic recovery. Business impact: complete loss of connectivity and security visibility until a human manually replaces or reboots the box (typically hours), plus a logging blind spot during the outage.
steps: Procure a second FortiGate of the IDENTICAL model, running the IDENTICAL FortiOS build, with matching FortiGuard licensing and VDOM count.
Cable at least two dedicated heartbeat links directly between the units (e.g. port3<->port3 and port4<->port4) on an isolated segment.
On the intended primary: 'config system ha' then 'set mode a-p', 'set group-name <ha-group>', 'set group-id <1-255>'.
Set the shared secret and secure the control plane: 'set password <strong-ha-password>', 'set authentication enable', 'set encryption enable', 'set key <ha-key>'.
Define heartbeat interfaces with priorities: 'set hbdev "port3" 50 "port4" 100'.
Enable session sync and role selection: 'set session-pickup enable', 'set priority 200', 'set override disable', then 'end'.
Configure the secondary identically but with a lower priority ('set priority 100') and the SAME group-name/group-id/password/key.
