Optionally reserve out-of-band management: 'set ha-mgmt-status enable' and 'config ha-mgmt-interfaces'.
Confirm cluster formation with 'get system ha status' and config sync with 'diagnose sys ha checksum cluster'.
FORTIOS-HA-001.gui: FortiGate: System > HA (set Mode = Active-Passive, Device priority, Group name, Password, Heartbeat interfaces, Monitor interfaces, Management Interface Reservation). Heartbeat authentication/encryption/key and override are CLI-only. FortiManager: Device Manager > Device & Groups > [device] > System > HA, or push via Provisioning Templates > CLI Templates for fleet-wide consistency.
FORTIOS-HA-001.cli:
config system ha
  set mode a-p
  set group-name <ha-group>
  set password <ha-password>
end
FORTIOS-HA-001.verify: get system ha status  -> expect "Mode: HA A-P" with two members (Master/Slave) listed and health Healthy; diagnose sys ha checksum cluster -> all members report matching checksums (in sync).
FORTIOS-HA-001.rollback: On each unit: config system ha / set mode standalone / end. Reverting removes the HA virtual MACs (surviving unit falls back to physical MACs, causing ARP/switch MAC re-learn and a brief blip) and you lose all automatic failover. If both units remain cabled after reverting, watch for duplicate-IP conflicts.
FORTIOS-HA-001.impact: Requires a second appliance and a maintenance window. Forming the cluster switches data interfaces to HA virtual MAC addresses, causing a brief (seconds) traffic interruption and downstream ARP/switch MAC re-learning. No reboot of the primary is required to enable HA, but link negotiation on join can blip traffic. Not a routine change - schedule it.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark 7.1.1 (High Availability)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - High Availability chapter (FGCP active-passive cluster setup)
NIST SP 800-53 CP-7 (Alternate Processing Site), CP-9
PCI-DSS 12.10.1; SOC2 CC7.5
CWE-654 (Reliance on a Single Factor in a Security Decision - as tagged by scanner; availability aspect maps to NIST CP controls)

FORTIOS-HA-002.risk: Without heartbeat authentication, FGCP control-plane packets are trusted on the heartbeat network based only on group-id/group-name. An attacker or rogue device with L2 access to the heartbeat segment can inject forged HA negotiation frames to force repeated cluster elections (failover flapping = denial of service), or attempt to join/hijack the cluster and pull the synchronized configuration - which contains admin password hashes, IPsec pre-shared keys, SSL-VPN settings and private keys. Business impact: perimeter instability plus potential full disclosure of firewall secrets enabling downstream VPN/admin compromise.
steps: Set a strong cluster secret: 'config system ha' then 'set password <strong-32+char-secret>'.
Enable heartbeat authentication: 'set authentication enable'.
Recommended (pairs with HA-005): also 'set encryption enable' and 'set key <ha-key>' so heartbeat is authenticated AND encrypted.
'end' to commit; the change renegotiates the heartbeat association.
Apply the IDENTICAL password/key/group-name/group-id on every member - mismatched values prevent a unit from joining.
Move heartbeat links onto a dedicated, non-user-reachable segment (direct cable or private VLAN) as defense-in-depth.
Confirm all members re-formed the cluster: 'get system ha status'.
Confirm authentication is active: 'get system ha | grep authentication'.
FORTIOS-HA-002.gui: FortiGate: System > HA exposes Group name and Password, but the 'authentication' toggle and 'key' are CLI-only - set via 'config system ha'. FortiManager: apply through a CLI Template (Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > CLI Templates) targeting the cluster.
FORTIOS-HA-002.cli:
config system ha
  set authentication enable
  set password <strong-ha-password>
  set encryption enable
  set key <ha-heartbeat-key>
end
FORTIOS-HA-002.verify: get system ha | grep authentication  -> authentication : enable; diagnose sys ha status shows the cluster still intact/in-sync after the change.
FORTIOS-HA-002.rollback: config system ha / set authentication disable / end. Reverting removes heartbeat authentication and re-exposes the cluster to forged FGCP packets. Must be applied consistently on all members or a member drops out of the cluster.
FORTIOS-HA-002.impact: No reboot. Committing briefly renegotiates the HA heartbeat and in A-P can trigger a momentary re-election. Apply during a maintenance window and to all members near-simultaneously (mismatched auth settings cause a member to leave the cluster). No SSL-VPN/IPsec data-session loss beyond any brief failover.
references: Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - HA heartbeat / FGCP heartbeat authentication
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - Section 7 (High Availability)
CWE-306 (Missing Authentication for Critical Function)
NIST SP 800-53 IA-3 (Device Identification and Authentication), SC-8
PCI-DSS 2.2

FORTIOS-HA-003.risk: With session-pickup disabled the standby never receives the primary's session table, so on failover every stateful session is dropped - TCP connections, NAT/SNAT mappings, IPsec and SSL-VPN tunnels, and VoIP calls must all re-establish. Impact: a user-visible outage/reset on every failover (broken downloads, dropped DB and VPN sessions), turning what should be a seamless HA event into a mass reconnect. An attacker able to trigger repeated failovers weaponizes each one into a mass session reset - an availability attack on long-lived connections.
steps: 'config system ha'.
Enable session synchronization: 'set session-pickup enable'.
Optional (UDP/ICMP flows): 'set session-pickup-connectionless enable'.
Optional overhead control: keep 'set session-pickup-delay enable' so only sessions older than the sync threshold are synced; disable it only if short-lived sessions must survive failover.
'end'.
Capacity-plan: session sync raises CPU, memory and heartbeat bandwidth - ensure dedicated heartbeat links (HA-006) can carry it.
Apply the same setting on all members.
Validate with 'get system ha | grep session-pickup' and observe runtime sync via 'diagnose sys session sync'.
FORTIOS-HA-003.gui: FortiGate: System > HA - 'Session pick-up' checkbox (present in current FortiOS GUI). Connectionless/delay sub-options are CLI-only. FortiManager: Device Manager > [device] > System > HA, or a CLI Template.
FORTIOS-HA-003.cli:
config system ha
  set session-pickup enable
  set session-pickup-connectionless enable
end
FORTIOS-HA-003.verify: get system ha | grep session-pickup  -> session-pickup : enable; runtime: diagnose sys session sync shows sync state active on both units.
FORTIOS-HA-003.rollback: config system ha / set session-pickup disable / end. Reverting stops session sync (frees CPU/memory) but restores full session loss on failover.
FORTIOS-HA-003.impact: Non-disruptive to enable and no reboot. It increases CPU, memory and heartbeat-link utilization on both units - on heavily loaded firewalls enable during a lower-traffic window and monitor resource use. It only benefits FUTURE failovers; it does not instantly synchronize the current session table.
references: Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Session failover (session pick-up) in FGCP
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - Section 7 (High Availability)
CWE-654 (Reliance on a Single Factor - resiliency gap)
NIST SP 800-53 CP-7, SC-5
SOC2 CC7.5

FORTIOS-HA-004.risk: Cluster members on different FortiOS builds is an unsupported state: FGCP config synchronization can fail or apply only partially, a member can stay out of sync or never fully join, and a failover to a peer running an older build silently reintroduces already-patched CVEs (for example a fixed SSL-VPN pre-auth RCE running again after failover). Attacker impact: after any failover the perimeter may run vulnerable firmware; operationally, config drift means new security policy does not propagate, leaving rules unenforced.
steps: Inventory each member's build: 'get system ha status', or 'execute ha manage <peer-index> <admin>' into each unit then 'get system status'.
Read Fortinet's supported upgrade path to the target build (do NOT skip major versions) and back up config: 'execute backup config ...'.
Enable rolling upgrade to minimize downtime: 'config system ha' / 'set uninterruptible-upgrade enable' / 'end'.

Upload the target firmware to the primary (System > Firmware or 'execute restore image tftp <file> <tftp-ip>'); FGCP distributes and upgrades the subordinate(s) first, fails over, then upgrades the former primary.
If the mismatch is from a partially failed prior upgrade, log into the lagging member with 'execute ha manage <peer-index> <admin>' and load the matching firmware directly.
After upgrade, confirm every member reports the identical version.
Confirm config re-synchronized: 'diagnose sys ha checksum cluster' (checksums must match).
Re-check that FortiGuard AV/IPS engine and signature versions also align across members.
FORTIOS-HA-004.gui: FortiGate: System > Firmware (upload the build; with uninterruptible-upgrade the cluster upgrades as a unit). FortiManager: Device Manager > Firmware / Firmware Templates, or right-click the cluster > Upgrade - FMG orchestrates the rolling upgrade and shows per-member versions.
FORTIOS-HA-004.cli:
# Firmware upgrade action - not a pure config change.
config system ha
  set uninterruptible-upgrade enable
end
# Then load the SAME build on the primary; FGCP pushes it to all members:
#   execute restore image tftp <image-file> <tftp-server-ip>
# or upload via GUI: System > Firmware. Every member MUST run the same build.
FORTIOS-HA-004.verify: get system ha status (or diagnose sys ha status)  -> every member shows the same "Version: ... v7.x.x build XXXX"; diagnose sys ha checksum cluster -> in-sync.
FORTIOS-HA-004.rollback: FortiOS retains a previous-firmware partition: 'execute set-next-reboot {primary|secondary}' or reload the prior image via 'execute restore image' if the new build regresses. Reverting a cluster to older firmware re-exposes fixed CVEs, so only do it for a functional regression and re-plan the upgrade.
FORTIOS-HA-004.impact: Disruptive - firmware upgrade reboots each member. With 'uninterruptible-upgrade enable' it is a rolling upgrade (one unit at a time) with a single HA failover, so data-plane downtime is limited to failover time; without it, both units reboot and the cluster is fully down. Schedule a maintenance window; failover briefly resets non-synced sessions and can blip IPsec/SSL-VPN.
references: Fortinet FortiOS Upgrade Guide - Upgrading a FortiGate HA cluster / uninterruptible (in-service) upgrade
Fortinet PSIRT: patch every member to the same fixed build - e.g. FG-IR-22-398 / CVE-2022-42475 (SSL-VPN heap overflow pre-auth RCE) illustrates why a mismatched/older member is dangerous
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - Section 10 (Firmware) and Section 7 (High Availability)
CWE-1104 (Use of Unmaintained Third-Party Components / firmware drift)
NIST SP 800-53 SI-2 (Flaw Remediation), CM-3

FORTIOS-HA-005.risk: FGCP heartbeat and config-sync traffic - including the synchronized configuration with admin password hashes, IPsec pre-shared keys, SSL-VPN settings and private keys - traverses the heartbeat links in cleartext. An attacker with access to the heartbeat segment (a compromised switch, a network tap, a shared/trunked VLAN, or an L2 misconfiguration) can passively capture the entire firewall configuration and session data, or MITM cluster negotiation. Business impact: complete disclosure of firewall secrets, enabling downstream compromise of VPNs and administrative access.
steps: 'config system ha'.
Enable encryption of heartbeat/sync traffic: 'set encryption enable'.
Set the AES key (exposed once encryption is enabled): 'set key <ha-key>'.
Also enable authentication (pairs with HA-002): 'set authentication enable'.
'end' to commit.
Apply the IDENTICAL encryption/key/authentication/password on every member - mismatched keys prevent a member from joining.
Keep heartbeat links on a dedicated, isolated segment as defense-in-depth.
Confirm the cluster re-formed ('get system ha status') and the setting took ('get system ha | grep encryption').
FORTIOS-HA-005.gui: FortiGate: CLI-only - the 'encryption' toggle and 'key' are NOT exposed in System > HA; configure via 'config system ha'. FortiManager: apply via a CLI Template (Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > CLI Templates) to the cluster.
FORTIOS-HA-005.cli:
config system ha
  set encryption enable
  set key <ha-heartbeat-key>
  set authentication enable
end
FORTIOS-HA-005.verify: get system ha | grep -e encryption -e authentication  -> encryption : enable and authentication : enable; get system ha status shows the cluster healthy/in-sync afterward.
FORTIOS-HA-005.rollback: config system ha / set encryption disable / end. Reverting returns heartbeat/sync traffic to cleartext. The change must be consistent across all members or a member drops out of the cluster.
FORTIOS-HA-005.impact: No reboot. Enabling encryption renegotiates the heartbeat association and recomputes cluster sync; in A-P this may cause a momentary re-election. Apply in a maintenance window and to all members together (mismatch = member leaves cluster). Minor added CPU for crypto on heartbeat traffic.
references: Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - HA heartbeat encryption and authentication
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - Section 7 (High Availability)
CWE-319 (Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information)
NIST SP 800-53 SC-8 (Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity), SC-13
PCI-DSS 4.2.1

FORTIOS-HA-006.risk: With no dedicated heartbeat interface (hbdev empty), FGCP heartbeat has no reliable dedicated path or shares a data interface. Heartbeat then contends with production traffic; under load or a brief data-link cut the peers can miss heartbeats and BOTH declare themselves primary - a split-brain that puts duplicate IP/MAC on the network, causing dropped/looping traffic and an outage. An attacker who can saturate the shared link (volumetric DoS) can deliberately induce heartbeat loss and split-brain. A single heartbeat path also means no redundancy for the HA control plane.
steps: Physically cable at least two dedicated interfaces between members (e.g. port3<->port3 and port4<->port4), direct or on an isolated VLAN.
'config system ha'.
Assign heartbeat interfaces with priorities (higher = preferred): 'set hbdev "port3" 50 "port4" 100'.
Optional tuning of detection: 'set hb-interval 2' (units of 100ms) and 'set hb-lost-threshold 6'.
'end'.
Ensure the chosen ports carry no user/data VLANs and are not referenced by any firewall policy.
Apply matching hbdev on all members.
Validate: 'get system ha | grep hbdev' and confirm both links are up with 'diagnose sys ha status'.
FORTIOS-HA-006.gui: FortiGate: System > HA - 'Heartbeat interfaces' selector with a per-interface priority value. FortiManager: Device Manager > [device] > System > HA, or a CLI Template.
FORTIOS-HA-006.cli:
config system ha
  set hbdev "port3" 50 "port4" 100
end
FORTIOS-HA-006.verify: get system ha | grep hbdev  -> hbdev : "port3" 50 "port4" 100; diagnose sys ha status shows both heartbeat devices up.
FORTIOS-HA-006.rollback: config system ha / unset hbdev (or restore the prior value) / end. Removing heartbeat interfaces breaks HA supervision and can cause split-brain - do not leave hbdev empty in a live cluster.
FORTIOS-HA-006.impact: Requires physical cabling. Changing hbdev briefly renegotiates the heartbeat; done wrong (e.g. removing the only working link) it can trigger a failover or split-brain, so add the new links and confirm they are up BEFORE removing old ones. No reboot; perform in a maintenance window.
references: Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Heartbeat interfaces / HA heartbeat and split-brain prevention
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - Section 7 (High Availability)
CWE-654 (Reliance on a Single Factor - single control-plane path)
NIST SP 800-53 CP-7, SC-5

FORTIOS-HA-007.risk: With no monitored (port-monitored) interfaces, FGCP fails over only on a full unit or heartbeat failure - never on a link failure of a critical data port. If the primary's WAN uplink or core LAN port (or its upstream) goes down while the unit itself stays 'healthy', the cluster keeps that unit primary and traffic blackholes even though the standby has good links. Result: an avoidable outage; an attacker (or a stuck dead link) that downs a single upstream link causes sustained loss of connectivity with no failover.
steps: Identify the business-critical interfaces whose failure should trigger failover (typically WAN uplinks and the core LAN).
'config system ha'.
Configure interface monitoring: 'set monitor "wan1" "internal"' (list every critical port).
'end'.
Set device priorities / override so the unit with healthy links becomes primary on link loss.
Do NOT monitor the heartbeat interfaces themselves.
Apply matching monitor config on all members.
Validate: 'get system ha | grep monitor', then test by administratively downing a monitored port on the primary and confirming failover in 'get system ha status'.
FORTIOS-HA-007.gui: FortiGate: System > HA - 'Monitor interfaces' selector. FortiManager: Device Manager > [device] > System > HA, or a CLI Template.
FORTIOS-HA-007.cli:
config system ha
  set monitor "wan1" "internal"
end
FORTIOS-HA-007.verify: get system ha | grep monitor  -> monitor : "wan1" "internal"; failover test: down a monitored port and confirm the role change via get system ha status.
FORTIOS-HA-007.rollback: config system ha / unset monitor / end. Removing monitored interfaces means link failures no longer trigger failover (reverts to unit/heartbeat-only failover).
FORTIOS-HA-007.impact: No reboot. Adding monitoring is generally non-disruptive, BUT if a listed interface is already down on the current primary, enabling monitoring immediately triggers a failover - verify all listed interfaces are up first, and apply in a maintenance window. Avoid monitoring a marginal/flapping link (it can cause repeated failovers).
references: Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Monitor interfaces (interface/port monitoring) in FGCP
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - Section 7 (High Availability)
CWE-654 (Reliance on a Single Factor - failover blind to link loss)
NIST SP 800-53 CP-7, SI-13 (Predictable Failure Prevention)

FORTIOS-HA-008.risk: With override enabled, the highest-priority unit always reclaims the primary role as soon as it recovers, forcing a SECOND failover (failback) on top of the first. Each failover resets non-synced sessions and briefly interrupts traffic, so a flapping or marginal primary - or an attacker repeatedly bouncing it - produces repeated outages. Override also makes primary selection depend on a static priority rather than cluster stability/uptime, which is usually operationally undesirable. Impact is availability/stability, hence LOW severity.
steps: Decide the design: most deployments want the surviving unit to STAY primary (no auto-failback) -> override disabled. Enable override only when a deterministic primary is truly required.
'config system ha'.
Disable auto-failback: 'set override disable'.
'end'.
With override disabled, review 'set priority' values so the intended selection order (uptime, then priority) still reflects intent.
Apply consistently across all members.
If a deterministic primary IS required, instead tune priorities, accept the double failover, and document the exception.
Validate: 'get system ha | grep override', then after a test failover confirm in 'get system ha status' that the recovered unit does NOT preempt.
FORTIOS-HA-008.gui: FortiGate: CLI-only - 'override' is not exposed in System > HA (Device priority IS in the GUI); set via 'config system ha'. FortiManager: apply via a CLI Template.
FORTIOS-HA-008.cli:
config system ha
  set override disable
end
FORTIOS-HA-008.verify: get system ha | grep override  -> override : disable; after a failover test, get system ha status shows the recovered unit remaining subordinate (no automatic preemption).
FORTIOS-HA-008.rollback: config system ha / set override enable / end restores auto-failback to the highest-priority unit (expect an immediate failover if a higher-priority unit is currently subordinate).
FORTIOS-HA-008.impact: No reboot. Toggling override recalculates primary selection and can itself cause a single failover at the moment of change (brief traffic blip / non-synced session reset). Minor; still perform during a low-traffic window.
references: Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - FGCP primary unit selection (override, priority, uptime) and failover behavior
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - Section 7 (High Availability)
CWE-654 (Reliance on a Single Factor - auto-failback destabilizes cluster)
NIST SP 800-53 CP-7

FORTIOS-IPS-001.risk: An IPS sensor is applied but every entry is set to pass/monitor, so exploit attempts are logged but never blocked. Attackers exploiting known CVEs against exposed services succeed while the SOC merely receives after-the-fact alerts. Business impact: detection without prevention - the IPS provides no protection and creates a false assurance that the perimeter blocks exploits.
steps: Identify the sensor and its monitor-only entries: 'show ips sensor <name>' and check each 'set action'.
Edit the sensor: config ips sensor / edit "<name>".
For the high/critical filter, switch to block: config entries / edit <id> / set action block / next.
For remaining entries, use 'set action default' so FortiGuard's recommended action (block for high-severity) applies, rather than pass.
Save with 'end' then 'next / end'.
If a specific signature must stay in monitor due to a known false positive, isolate it in its own entry rather than leaving the whole sensor in monitor.
Confirm the sensor is still bound to the intended policies: show firewall policy <id> | grep ips-sensor.
Watch IPS logs to confirm verdicts change from 'detected/pass' to 'dropped/reset'.
FORTIOS-IPS-001.gui: Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention > edit <sensor> > set each signature/filter entry Action to Block (or Default). FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention.
FORTIOS-IPS-001.cli:
config ips sensor
    edit "<sensor>"
        config entries
            edit <entry-id>
                set action block
            next
        end
    next
end
FORTIOS-IPS-001.verify: show ips sensor <sensor> | grep action  -> set action block (or default) instead of pass. Live: trigger a benign IPS test signature and confirm a 'dropped'/'reset' action in Log & Report > Security Events > Intrusion Prevention.
FORTIOS-IPS-001.rollback: Set 'set action monitor' (or 'pass') on the affected entry. Reverting to monitor stops blocking exploits again; only do this per-signature to work around a confirmed false positive, not sensor-wide.
FORTIOS-IPS-001.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Moving from monitor to block can drop traffic that matches a signature, including false positives - validate high-volume signatures before enforcing, and use rate-based/exempt tuning as needed.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4.1 Intrusion Prevention (IPS action = block/default)
FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Intrusion prevention (signatures and filters / actions)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
PCI-DSS v4.0 11.5.1
NIST SP 800-53 SI-4 System Monitoring
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-IPS-002.risk: The IPS sensor contains exempt-ip entries that whitelist specific source/destination IPs from inspection. Any host that can spoof or reach from an exempted address bypasses IPS entirely, and stale exemptions left from past troubleshooting become permanent blind spots an attacker can pivot through. Business impact: an unmonitored exploitation path straight past the IPS, often undocumented and forgotten.
steps: Inventory exemptions: 'show ips sensor <name>' and note every 'config exempt-ip' block under each entry.
For each exemption, confirm business justification and owner; remove any that are undocumented or expired.
Edit the sensor: config ips sensor / edit "<name>" / config entries / edit <entry-id>.
Remove all exemptions on that entry: config exempt-ip / purge / end (or delete specific numbered exemptions).
Save with 'next / end / next / end'.
Where an exemption is genuinely required, narrow it to exact src/dst hosts and document it: config exempt-ip / edit 1 / set src-ip <host/32> / set dst-ip <host/32> / next / end.
Re-verify the sensor is still applied to the correct policies.
Schedule a recurring review of IPS exemptions so they do not become permanent.
FORTIOS-IPS-002.gui: Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention > edit <sensor> > open the signature/filter entry > remove the IP Exemptions list. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention.
FORTIOS-IPS-002.cli:
config ips sensor
    edit "<sensor>"
        config entries
            edit <entry-id>
                config exempt-ip
                    purge
                end
            next
        end
    next
end
FORTIOS-IPS-002.verify: show ips sensor <sensor> | grep -A2 exempt-ip  -> no exempt-ip entries remain (or only the documented, host-scoped ones).
FORTIOS-IPS-002.rollback: Re-add the specific exemption: config ips sensor / edit <sensor> / config entries / edit <id> / config exempt-ip / edit 1 / set src-ip <ip> / set dst-ip <ip> / next / end. Removing a needed exemption may cause IPS to block a sanctioned scanner/backup host; re-scope narrowly rather than exempting broad ranges.
FORTIOS-IPS-002.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Removing exemptions re-enables inspection for those IPs, which may surface previously-hidden false positives from a legitimate host - verify that vulnerability scanners or backup agents still function.
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Intrusion prevention > IPS sensor (exempt IP)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide - least-exception principle
CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4.1 Intrusion Prevention (IPS)
NIST SP 800-53 SI-4 System Monitoring, AC-4 Information Flow Enforcement
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-IPSEC-001.risk: In IKEv1 aggressive mode the responder sends its identity and a hash derived from the pre-shared key before an encrypted channel exists, so anyone who can sniff or actively solicit the handshake (e.g. ike-scan --aggressive against a dial-up gateway) captures the PSK hash and runs it through an offline dictionary/brute-force attack (psk-crack). Once the PSK is recovered the attacker impersonates a branch office or remote peer, brings up a rogue tunnel, and gains routed access into the internal network, bypassing the perimeter entirely. Business impact: full internal network access, data exfiltration and lateral movement from a single sniffed handshake.
steps: Confirm the exposure: 'get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name>' and check that mode is aggressive and ike-version is 1.
Open a change window with the remote-peer owner - mode must be changed on BOTH ends at the same time or the tunnel will fail to negotiate.
For a static-IP peer, switch to main mode: 'config vpn ipsec phase1-interface' / 'edit <name>' / 'set mode main' / 'next' / 'end'.

If aggressive mode was chosen because the peer is a dynamic-IP group/dial-up PSK, prefer migrating to IKEv2 (which has no aggressive/main distinction): 'set ike-version 2'; or move to certificate auth: 'set authmethod signature' + 'set certificate <local-cert>'.
Reconfigure the remote peer's phase-1 to main mode (or IKEv2) to match.
Rotate the pre-shared key, which must be assumed compromised: 'set psksecret <new-long-random-secret>' on both ends.
Force renegotiation: 'diagnose vpn ike gateway flush name <name>'.
Confirm the tunnel re-establishes in the new mode: 'diagnose vpn ike gateway list name <name>' and 'diagnose vpn tunnel list name <name>'.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-001.gui: FortiGate: VPN > IPsec Tunnels > select tunnel > Edit > Convert To Custom Tunnel (if wizard-created) > Authentication > with IKE Version 1 selected, set Mode = Main (the Aggressive/Main toggle only appears for IKEv1); or set IKE Version = 2 to remove the mode concept. FortiManager: VPN Manager > IPsec VPN > edit the phase-1 gateway (or Device Manager > [device] > VPN), change Mode/IKE version, then Install.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-001.cli:
config vpn ipsec phase1-interface
  edit <vpn-name>
    set mode main
  next
end
# Dynamic-IP / dial-up peers: prefer IKEv2 instead of main mode:
# config vpn ipsec phase1-interface
#   edit <vpn-name>
#     set ike-version 2
#   next
# end
FORTIOS-IPSEC-001.verify: get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep mode   -> mode: main   (or: get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep ike-version -> ike-version: 2)
FORTIOS-IPSEC-001.rollback: Revert with 'config vpn ipsec phase1-interface' / 'edit <name>' / 'set mode aggressive' / 'next' / 'end' (and restore the prior ike-version). Reverting re-opens the offline PSK-cracking weakness and should only be done if the remote peer genuinely cannot support main mode or IKEv2 and the tunnel will not come up otherwise.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-001.impact: Disruptive to the single affected tunnel only; no device reboot. The phase-1 SA drops and renegotiates, and the tunnel stays DOWN until the remote peer is also set to the matching mode/IKE version. Schedule a short maintenance window with the peer owner. No effect on SSL-VPN, GUI/SSH admin, or HA heartbeat; HA syncs the config automatically. NOTE: the scanner's REMEDIATION_COMMANDS["FORTIOS-IPSEC-001"] stores a *proposal* command, which does NOT fix aggressive mode - the correct fix is 'set mode main' / IKEv2 as above.
references: CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - IPsec VPN section 5.2.1 (per tool COMPLIANCE_MAP)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - IPsec VPN > Phase 1 (IKE) configuration
Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide - VPN hardening
NIST SP 800-77 Rev.1, Guide to IPsec VPNs (IKE aggressive mode weakness)
PCI DSS v4.0 Req 4.2.1 (strong cryptography) / v3.2.1 Req 4.1

FORTIOS-IPSEC-002.risk: The phase-1 proposal offers a broken/weak encryption algorithm (scanner flags des, 3des, rc4, null, rc2, idea, seed, or aria128). An attacker who captures IKE/tunnel traffic can exploit 64-bit block ciphers (Sweet32 birthday attack against 3DES), tiny key spaces (single-DES), or keystream biases (RC4) to recover key material or plaintext offline, or perform a downgrade to the weakest common proposal. Result: decryption of site-to-site or remote-access VPN traffic carrying credentials, database records and internal application data - a confidentiality breach of data in transit.
steps: List the current proposal: 'get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep proposal'.
Agree the target algorithm set with the remote-peer owner (both ends must share at least one proposal).
Set a strong AES/AEAD-only proposal: 'config vpn ipsec phase1-interface' / 'edit <name>' / 'set proposal aes256-sha256 aes256gcm-prfsha384' / 'next' / 'end'.
Do NOT append any legacy cipher (des/3des) as a fallback - a single weak proposal permits downgrade.
Update the remote peer to offer the same strong proposal set.
Flush and renegotiate: 'diagnose vpn ike gateway flush name <name>'.
Confirm the negotiated cipher on the live SA: 'diagnose vpn tunnel list name <name>' (check the enc= field).
Repeat for every phase-1 that shares this template or peer.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-002.gui: FortiGate: VPN > IPsec Tunnels > [tunnel] > Edit > Convert To Custom Tunnel > Phase 1 Proposal > set the Encryption columns to AES256 / AES256GCM and Authentication to SHA256/SHA384; remove any DES/3DES rows with the delete (trash) icon. FortiManager: VPN Manager > IPsec VPN > phase-1 gateway > Phase 1 Proposal, then Install.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-002.cli:
config vpn ipsec phase1-interface
  edit <vpn-name>
    set proposal aes256-sha256 aes256gcm-prfsha384
  next
end
FORTIOS-IPSEC-002.verify: get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep proposal   -> proposal: aes256-sha256 aes256gcm-prfsha384   (and: diagnose vpn tunnel list name <name> shows enc=aes256/aes-gcm, not des/3des)
FORTIOS-IPSEC-002.rollback: Re-add the previous proposal string, e.g. 'set proposal 3des-sha1 aes256-sha256'. Rolling back restores the weak-cipher/downgrade exposure; only do so temporarily if a legacy peer cannot negotiate AES and cannot be upgraded.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-002.impact: Disruptive to the affected tunnel only; no reboot. Changing the proposal tears down and rebuilds the phase-1 (and dependent phase-2) SAs; traffic on that tunnel pauses for a few seconds and stays down if the peer has no matching proposal. Coordinate with the peer. No impact to SSL-VPN, admin or HA sessions; HA syncs automatically. NOTE: the scanner stores this correct proposal string under REMEDIATION_COMMANDS["FORTIOS-IPSEC-001"]; REMEDIATION_COMMANDS["FORTIOS-IPSEC-002"] actually holds a dhgrp command (which belongs to the DH-group finding IPSEC-004) - the CLI above is the accurate fix for weak phase-1 encryption.
references: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - IPsec VPN section 5.2.2 (per tool COMPLIANCE_MAP)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - IPsec VPN > Phase 1 proposal / cryptography
Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide - strong cryptographic algorithms
RFC 8247 - Algorithm Implementation Requirements and Usage Guidance for IKEv2; NIST SP 800-77 Rev.1
Sweet32 (CVE-2016-2183) - 64-bit block cipher birthday attack; PCI DSS v4.0 Req 4.2.1

FORTIOS-IPSEC-003.risk: The phase-1 proposal uses a weak integrity/PRF hash (scanner flags md5 or md5-96). MD5 is collision-broken and, as an HMAC/PRF in IKE, weakens integrity protection and key derivation and enables downgrade to the weakest common proposal. An on-path attacker can attempt to forge or tamper with IKE messages or reduce the assurance of the negotiated keys, undermining the integrity of the VPN and potentially aiding session manipulation of data in transit.
steps: Inspect the proposal: 'get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep proposal' and look for '-md5' pairs.
Because FortiOS proposals are encryption-hash pairs (you cannot set the hash alone), replace the whole proposal with SHA-2 pairs.
Apply: 'config vpn ipsec phase1-interface' / 'edit <name>' / 'set proposal aes256-sha256 aes256-sha384' / 'next' / 'end' (or use AEAD 'aes256gcm-prfsha384' which carries integrity internally).
Remove any remaining md5 pair so no weak option remains for downgrade.
Mirror the change on the remote peer.
Flush the gateway: 'diagnose vpn ike gateway flush name <name>'.
Verify the SA re-establishes and check the negotiated auth: 'diagnose vpn ike gateway list name <name>'.
Repeat for peers sharing the same template.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-003.gui: FortiGate: VPN > IPsec Tunnels > [tunnel] > Edit > Convert To Custom Tunnel > Phase 1 Proposal > set each Authentication column to SHA256/SHA384/SHA512 and delete any MD5 row. FortiManager: VPN Manager > IPsec VPN > phase-1 gateway > Phase 1 Proposal > Install.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-003.cli:
config vpn ipsec phase1-interface
  edit <vpn-name>
    set proposal aes256-sha256 aes256-sha384
  next
end
FORTIOS-IPSEC-003.verify: get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep proposal   -> proposal: aes256-sha256 aes256-sha384   (no '-md5' pairs present)
FORTIOS-IPSEC-003.rollback: Restore the previous proposal string containing the md5 pair, e.g. 'set proposal aes256-md5 aes256-sha256'. Reverting reintroduces the broken-hash/downgrade weakness; only for a legacy peer that cannot be upgraded.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-003.impact: Disruptive to the affected tunnel only; no reboot. The phase-1 (and its phase-2) SAs renegotiate and the tunnel briefly drops; it stays down if the peer offers no SHA-2 pair. Coordinate with the peer owner. No effect on SSL-VPN, admin or HA sessions; HA syncs the config.
references: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - IPsec VPN section 5.2.3 (per tool COMPLIANCE_MAP)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - IPsec VPN > Phase 1 proposal (encryption-authentication pairs)
RFC 6151 - Updated Security Considerations for MD5 and HMAC-MD5
RFC 8247 - IKEv2 algorithm requirements (SHA-2 mandatory); NIST SP 800-77 Rev.1
PCI DSS v4.0 Req 4.2.1 (strong cryptography)

FORTIOS-IPSEC-004.risk: The phase-1 negotiation offers a weak Diffie-Hellman group (scanner flags DH group 1 = 768-bit MODP, group 2 = 1024-bit MODP, or group 5 = 1536-bit MODP). 768/1024-bit MODP groups are within reach of nation-state precomputation (the Logjam class of attack), letting an adversary who records the key exchange later derive the shared secret and decrypt the entire tunnel's traffic offline. Business impact: retrospective decryption of all VPN-protected data in transit and, if PFS is also weak, of past sessions.
steps: Show the configured groups: 'get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep dhgrp'.
Choose strong groups agreed with the peer: MODP-2048 (14) and/or ECP groups 19/20/21.
Apply: 'config vpn ipsec phase1-interface' / 'edit <name>' / 'set dhgrp 14 20 21' / 'next' / 'end'.
Ensure groups 1, 2 and 5 are no longer in the list (the command above replaces the set).
Configure the same strong groups on the remote peer.
Also raise the phase-2 DH group where PFS is enabled (see FORTIOS-IPSEC-006).
Flush and renegotiate: 'diagnose vpn ike gateway flush name <name>'.
Verify the live negotiation: 'diagnose vpn tunnel list name <name>' (check the DH/PFS group).
FORTIOS-IPSEC-004.gui: FortiGate: VPN > IPsec Tunnels > [tunnel] > Edit > Convert To Custom Tunnel > Phase 1 Proposal > Diffie-Hellman Groups > tick 14, 20 and 21 and untick 1, 2 and 5. FortiManager: VPN Manager > IPsec VPN > phase-1 gateway > Phase 1 Proposal > DH Groups > Install.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-004.cli:
config vpn ipsec phase1-interface
  edit <vpn-name>
    set dhgrp 14 20 21
  next
end
FORTIOS-IPSEC-004.verify: get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep dhgrp   -> dhgrp: 14 20 21   (groups 1/2/5 absent)
FORTIOS-IPSEC-004.rollback: Re-add the previous group set, e.g. 'set dhgrp 5 14'. Reverting reintroduces the Logjam-class precomputation risk; only for a legacy peer that cannot negotiate group 14+ and cannot be upgraded.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-004.impact: Disruptive to the affected tunnel only; no reboot. Changing the DH group forces phase-1 (and phase-2) renegotiation; the tunnel drops briefly and stays down if the peer shares no strong group. Coordinate with the peer. No effect on SSL-VPN, admin or HA sessions; HA syncs automatically. NOTE: this exact dhgrp command is stored in the scanner as REMEDIATION_COMMANDS["FORTIOS-IPSEC-002"] (reused verbatim here) - that dict key is misaligned with the finding numbering.
references: CWE-326: Inadequate Encryption Strength
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - IPsec VPN section 5.2 (DH group hardening)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - IPsec VPN > Phase 1 proposal (Diffie-Hellman groups)
Logjam - Weak Diffie-Hellman and the TLS/IKE 1024-bit MODP threat (Adrian et al., 2015)
RFC 8247 (deprecates MODP-1024, group 2) and RFC 3526; NIST SP 800-77 Rev.1 and SP 800-57 Part 1
PCI DSS v4.0 Req 4.2.1

FORTIOS-IPSEC-005.risk: Dead Peer Detection is disabled on the tunnel, so the FortiGate does not proactively detect a dead or unreachable peer. Half-open/stale IKE and IPsec SAs accumulate and consume IKE daemon and memory resources, failover to a backup path or redundant tunnel is delayed (traffic is black-holed until SAs age out), and a resource-exhaustion condition can degrade all VPN services. This is primarily an availability and resiliency risk for site-to-site and redundant VPN designs.
steps: Check the current setting: 'get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep dpd'.
Decide the DPD mode: on-idle sends R-U-THERE only when no traffic is seen (good for redundancy/failover); on-demand probes only when there is outbound traffic with no response.
Enable DPD: 'config vpn ipsec phase1-interface' / 'edit <name>' / 'set dpd on-idle' / 'next' / 'end'.
Tune the probe timers as needed: 'set dpd-retryinterval 60' and 'set dpd-retrycount 3'.
Ensure the remote peer also supports/enables DPD for bidirectional detection.
For redundant tunnels, confirm route/link-monitor or SD-WAN failover keys off DPD-driven tunnel-down state.
Renegotiate if required: 'diagnose vpn ike gateway flush name <name>'.
Verify: 'get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep dpd' shows on-idle (or on-demand).
FORTIOS-IPSEC-005.gui: FortiGate: VPN > IPsec Tunnels > [tunnel] > Edit > Convert To Custom Tunnel > Network section > Dead Peer Detection > select On Idle (or On Demand). Retry count/interval are CLI-tunable. FortiManager: VPN Manager > IPsec VPN > phase-1 gateway > Network > Dead Peer Detection > Install.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-005.cli:
config vpn ipsec phase1-interface
  edit <vpn-name>
    set dpd on-idle
    set dpd-retryinterval 60
    set dpd-retrycount 3
  next
end
FORTIOS-IPSEC-005.verify: get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep dpd   -> dpd: on-idle   (dpd-retryinterval: 60, dpd-retrycount: 3)
FORTIOS-IPSEC-005.rollback: Disable again with 'set dpd disable'. Reverting removes stale-tunnel detection and can slow VPN failover; only disable if a peer sends malformed DPD that repeatedly resets a healthy tunnel.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-005.impact: Low impact and effectively non-disruptive - enabling DPD does not by itself tear down an established SA; it only adds keepalive probing. No reboot, no effect on SSL-VPN/admin/HA. Applying the change may briefly touch the phase-1 config; HA syncs automatically.
references: CWE-400: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - IPsec VPN > Dead Peer Detection / Network settings
Fortinet Knowledge Base - Configuring and troubleshooting DPD (on-idle vs on-demand)
RFC 3706 - A Traffic-Based Method of Detecting Dead IKE Peers
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - IPsec VPN availability recommendations (section 5.2)

FORTIOS-IPSEC-006.risk: Perfect Forward Secrecy is disabled on the phase-2 selector, so IPsec rekeys reuse phase-1 key material rather than performing a fresh Diffie-Hellman exchange. If the phase-1 key or the tunnel's long-term secret is ever compromised (or captured now and cracked later), the attacker can derive the keys for all past and future phase-2 sessions and decrypt previously recorded traffic. Business impact: a single key compromise retroactively exposes the entire history of VPN-protected data - the classic 'harvest now, decrypt later' scenario.
steps: Check phase-2: 'get vpn ipsec phase2-interface <name> | grep pfs'.
Enable PFS and bind a strong DH group: 'config vpn ipsec phase2-interface' / 'edit <name>' / 'set pfs enable' / 'set dhgrp 14 20 21' / 'next' / 'end'.
Ensure the phase-1 DH groups are also strong (see FORTIOS-IPSEC-004) so the fresh exchange is meaningful.
Enable PFS with a matching DH group on the remote peer.
Confirm the phase-2 keylife is short enough that rekeys (and thus new DH exchanges) happen regularly (see FORTIOS-IPSEC-011).
Flush the phase-2 SA: 'diagnose vpn tunnel flush <name>' (or flush the gateway).
Verify PFS is active on the live SA: 'diagnose vpn tunnel list name <name>' (look for the PFS/DH group).
Repeat for every phase-2 selector under the tunnel.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-006.gui: FortiGate: VPN > IPsec Tunnels > [tunnel] > Edit > Phase 2 Selectors > [selector] > Advanced > tick Enable Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) and select Diffie-Hellman Group 14/20/21. FortiManager: VPN Manager > IPsec VPN > phase-2 selector > Advanced > Enable PFS > Install.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-006.cli:
config vpn ipsec phase2-interface
  edit <phase2-name>
    set pfs enable
    set dhgrp 14 20 21
  next
end
FORTIOS-IPSEC-006.verify: get vpn ipsec phase2-interface <name> | grep "pfs\|dhgrp"   -> pfs: enable / dhgrp: 14 20 21   (diagnose vpn tunnel list name <name> shows the PFS DH group)
FORTIOS-IPSEC-006.rollback: Disable with 'set pfs disable'. Reverting removes forward secrecy so a future key compromise exposes all sessions; only disable if a peer cannot negotiate PFS and cannot be upgraded.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-006.impact: Disruptive to the affected phase-2 SA only; no reboot. Enabling PFS/changing the group triggers a phase-2 renegotiation - traffic on that selector pauses briefly and stays down if the peer does not also have matching PFS/DH. Coordinate with the peer. No effect on SSL-VPN, admin or HA sessions; HA syncs automatically.
references: CWE-326: Inadequate Encryption Strength
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - IPsec VPN > Phase 2 selectors > Perfect Forward Secrecy
Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide - enable PFS on IPsec phase 2
NIST SP 800-77 Rev.1 (PFS recommendation); RFC 7296 IKEv2 (CREATE_CHILD_SA with new DH)
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - IPsec VPN section 5.2; PCI DSS v4.0 Req 4.2.1

FORTIOS-IPSEC-007.risk: The tunnel uses IKEv1, a legacy key-exchange protocol. IKEv1 has no built-in DoS/anti-replay cookie protection comparable to IKEv2, a more complex and attack-prone state machine, weaker or absent EAP/MFA integration, and (in aggressive mode) the offline-PSK-cracking exposure. An attacker on-path can more easily flood or manipulate the IKEv1 negotiation, and IKEv1's fragmentation/NAT-traversal handling is more fragile. Business impact: increased likelihood of VPN DoS, downgrade and negotiation-manipulation attacks, and reduced ability to enforce modern strong authentication.
steps: Confirm the version: 'get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep ike-version'.
Verify the remote peer supports IKEv2 (nearly all modern endpoints do) and schedule a coordinated cutover.
Switch the FortiGate: 'config vpn ipsec phase1-interface' / 'edit <name>' / 'set ike-version 2' / 'next' / 'end'.
Re-check the proposal/DH/PFS still contain only strong algorithms after the switch (IKEv2 supports AEAD such as aes256gcm-prfsha384).
If PSK is retained, note IKEv2 removes the aggressive/main mode distinction; consider certificate auth (see FORTIOS-IPSEC-009).
Switch the remote peer to IKEv2 to match.
Flush and renegotiate: 'diagnose vpn ike gateway flush name <name>'.
Verify: 'diagnose vpn ike gateway list name <name>' shows the IKEv2 gateway established.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-007.gui: FortiGate: VPN > IPsec Tunnels > [tunnel] > Edit > Convert To Custom Tunnel > Authentication > IKE Version = 2. FortiManager: VPN Manager > IPsec VPN > phase-1 gateway > Authentication > IKE Version 2 > Install.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-007.cli:
config vpn ipsec phase1-interface
  edit <vpn-name>
    set ike-version 2
  next
end
FORTIOS-IPSEC-007.verify: get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep ike-version   -> ike-version: 2   (diagnose vpn ike gateway list name <name> shows IKEv2)
FORTIOS-IPSEC-007.rollback: Revert with 'set ike-version 1'. Reverting reintroduces the legacy-protocol weaknesses; only do so if the remote peer truly cannot support IKEv2.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-007.impact: Disruptive to the affected tunnel only; no reboot. Changing the IKE version tears down and rebuilds the SAs - both peers must run IKEv2 or the tunnel stays down. Coordinate the cutover. No effect on SSL-VPN, admin or HA sessions; HA syncs automatically.
references: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm (legacy protocol)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - IPsec VPN > IKE version selection
Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide - prefer IKEv2
RFC 7296 - Internet Key Exchange Protocol Version 2 (IKEv2); NIST SP 800-77 Rev.1
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - IPsec VPN section 5.2

FORTIOS-IPSEC-008.risk: The phase-1 (IKE SA) key lifetime exceeds 86400 seconds (24 hours). A long-lived key increases the volume of traffic protected by a single key set and widens the window during which a compromised or brute-forced phase-1 key remains valid, giving an attacker more time and more captured material to attack and a longer period of unauthorized tunnel access before the next rekey forces re-authentication. Business impact: prolonged exposure of the VPN to key-compromise and reduced cryptographic hygiene.
steps: Read the current value: 'get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep keylife'.
Agree a shorter lifetime with the peer (phase-1 keylife should be greater than or equal to phase-2 keylife; 8 hours is a common target).
Apply: 'config vpn ipsec phase1-interface' / 'edit <name>' / 'set keylife 28800' / 'next' / 'end' (valid range 120-172800 seconds).
Ensure the phase-2 keylife is shorter than phase-1 (see FORTIOS-IPSEC-011) to avoid mismatched rekey behavior.
Peers do not have to match phase-1 keylife exactly (each side rekeys on its own timer), but aligning them avoids asymmetric rekeys.
The change takes effect at the next rekey; to apply immediately, flush: 'diagnose vpn ike gateway flush name <name>'.
Verify: 'get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep keylife' shows 28800.
Repeat for other tunnels sharing the template.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-008.gui: FortiGate: VPN > IPsec Tunnels > [tunnel] > Edit > Convert To Custom Tunnel > Phase 1 Proposal > Key Lifetime (seconds) = 28800. FortiManager: VPN Manager > IPsec VPN > phase-1 gateway > Phase 1 Proposal > Key Lifetime > Install.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-008.cli:
config vpn ipsec phase1-interface
  edit <vpn-name>
    set keylife 28800
  next
end
FORTIOS-IPSEC-008.verify: get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep keylife   -> keylife: 28800
FORTIOS-IPSEC-008.rollback: Restore the previous value, e.g. 'set keylife 86400' (or higher). Reverting lengthens the key-compromise window; generally undesirable but harmless to connectivity.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-008.impact: Non-disruptive if applied without flushing - the new lifetime simply governs the next rekey; no reboot and no immediate SA teardown. If you flush to apply immediately, that tunnel briefly renegotiates. No effect on SSL-VPN, admin or HA; HA syncs automatically.
references: CWE-326: Inadequate Encryption Strength
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - IPsec VPN > Phase 1 key lifetime
NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev.5 - Recommendation for Key Management (cryptoperiods)
NIST SP 800-77 Rev.1, Guide to IPsec VPNs (SA lifetimes)
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - IPsec VPN section 5.2

FORTIOS-IPSEC-009.risk: The tunnel authenticates with a pre-shared key rather than X.509 certificates. A single static PSK is a shared secret that is often reused across sites, stored in config backups and configuration-management systems, rarely rotated, and (in aggressive mode) crackable offline. If the PSK leaks or is guessed, any party holding it can impersonate the peer and establish a tunnel into the network; there is no per-device identity, no revocation, and no non-repudiation. Business impact: credential-theft-based VPN impersonation and internal network access, with no ability to revoke a single compromised endpoint.
steps: Confirm the method: 'get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep authmethod'.
Establish PKI: import/enroll a device certificate and the issuing CA - 'config vpn certificate local' (import the FortiGate cert) and 'config vpn certificate ca' (import the CA).
Switch phase-1 to certificate auth: 'config vpn ipsec phase1-interface' / 'edit <name>' / 'set authmethod signature' / 'set certificate <local-cert-name>' / 'next' / 'end'.
Optionally enforce peer-identity validation: define 'config user peer' / 'config user peergrp' and bind with 'set peer <peer>' or 'set peergrp <grp>'.
Provision a certificate and matching trust on the remote peer and configure it for signature auth.
If PSK must remain temporarily, at minimum rotate it to a long random value with 'set psksecret <new-secret>'.
Flush and renegotiate: 'diagnose vpn ike gateway flush name <name>'.
Verify: 'get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep authmethod' and 'diagnose vpn ike gateway list name <name>'.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-009.gui: FortiGate: first import certs under System > Certificates (Import > Local/CA), then VPN > IPsec Tunnels > [tunnel] > Edit > Convert To Custom Tunnel > Authentication > Method = Signature > select the Certificate (and optionally a Peer/Peer group for identity checking). FortiManager: Device Manager > [device] > System > Certificates, then VPN Manager > IPsec VPN > phase-1 gateway > Authentication = Signature > Install.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-009.cli:
config vpn ipsec phase1-interface
  edit <vpn-name>
    set authmethod signature
    set certificate <local-cert-name>
    # optional peer identity validation:
    # set peer <peer-name>   (or: set peergrp <peer-group>)
  next
end
FORTIOS-IPSEC-009.verify: get vpn ipsec phase1-interface <name> | grep "authmethod\|certificate"   -> authmethod: signature / certificate: <local-cert-name>
FORTIOS-IPSEC-009.rollback: Revert with 'set authmethod psk' and 'set psksecret <secret>' (and unset the certificate). Reverting drops per-device identity, revocation and non-repudiation; acceptable only where PKI cannot yet be deployed and the PSK is strong and rotated.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-009.impact: Disruptive to the affected tunnel only; no reboot. Both peers must switch to certificate auth simultaneously with valid, mutually-trusted certs, or the tunnel stays down. Requires PKI groundwork (cert issuance/trust). No effect on SSL-VPN, admin or HA sessions; HA syncs the config.
references: CWE-287: Improper Authentication
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - IPsec VPN > Authentication (certificate/signature) and Certificate management
Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide - use certificate-based VPN authentication
NIST SP 800-77 Rev.1 (certificate vs pre-shared-key authentication)
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - IPsec VPN section 5.2; PCI DSS v4.0 Req 8.3 (strong authentication)

FORTIOS-IPSEC-010.risk: The phase-2 (ESP/IPsec SA) proposal offers a weak encryption algorithm (scanner flags des, 3des, rc4, null, rc2, idea, seed, or aria128). Because phase-2 protects the actual data payload, a weak or null cipher here directly exposes the traffic: an attacker capturing the ESP stream can exploit 64-bit block ciphers (Sweet32), single-DES key exhaustion, or - with null encryption - read plaintext outright, and can force a downgrade to the weakest offered transform. Business impact: direct confidentiality breach of all data carried by the tunnel.
steps: List the phase-2 proposal: 'get vpn ipsec phase2-interface <name> | grep proposal'.
Agree strong transforms with the peer (both ends must share one).
Apply an AES/AEAD-only proposal: 'config vpn ipsec phase2-interface' / 'edit <name>' / 'set proposal aes256-sha256 aes256gcm' / 'next' / 'end'.
Remove any DES/3DES/null transform - no weak fallback.
Update the remote peer's phase-2 proposal to match.
Flush the phase-2 SA: 'diagnose vpn tunnel flush <name>'.
Verify the negotiated cipher on the live SA: 'diagnose vpn tunnel list name <name>' (check the ESP enc= field).
Repeat for all phase-2 selectors under the tunnel.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-010.gui: FortiGate: VPN > IPsec Tunnels > [tunnel] > Edit > Phase 2 Selectors > [selector] > Advanced > Encryption = AES256 / AES256GCM, Authentication = SHA256; delete any DES/3DES/NULL row. FortiManager: VPN Manager > IPsec VPN > phase-2 selector > Advanced > Proposal > Install.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-010.cli:
config vpn ipsec phase2-interface
  edit <phase2-name>
    set proposal aes256-sha256 aes256gcm
  next
end
FORTIOS-IPSEC-010.verify: get vpn ipsec phase2-interface <name> | grep proposal   -> proposal: aes256-sha256 aes256gcm   (diagnose vpn tunnel list name <name> shows enc=aes256/aes-gcm, not des/3des/null)
FORTIOS-IPSEC-010.rollback: Re-add the previous proposal, e.g. 'set proposal 3des-sha1 aes256-sha256'. Reverting restores the weak-cipher/downgrade exposure of the data payload; only for a legacy peer that cannot be upgraded.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-010.impact: Disruptive to the affected phase-2 SA only; no reboot. The selector renegotiates and briefly drops traffic; it stays down if the peer offers no matching transform. Coordinate with the peer. No effect on SSL-VPN, admin or HA sessions; HA syncs automatically.
references: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - IPsec VPN > Phase 2 proposal / ESP algorithms
RFC 8221 - Cryptographic Algorithm Implementation Requirements for ESP and AH (3DES/DES/NULL deprecated)
Sweet32 (CVE-2016-2183); NIST SP 800-77 Rev.1
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - IPsec VPN section 5.2; PCI DSS v4.0 Req 4.2.1

FORTIOS-IPSEC-011.risk: The phase-2 key lifetime exceeds 43200 seconds (12 hours). A long phase-2 lifetime means the ESP data key is refreshed infrequently, so a compromised session key protects more data and remains valid longer, and (combined with weak or disabled PFS) enlarges the amount of recorded traffic an attacker can decrypt from a single key recovery. Business impact: extended exposure window for data-in-transit key compromise and weaker cryptographic hygiene on the payload-protecting SA.
steps: Read the value: 'get vpn ipsec phase2-interface <name> | grep keylife'.
Choose a shorter lifetime (1-8 hours is typical; must remain less than the phase-1 keylife).
Set the type and seconds: 'config vpn ipsec phase2-interface' / 'edit <name>' / 'set keylife-type seconds' / 'set keylifeseconds 3600' / 'next' / 'end' (valid range 120-172800).
If a byte-based lifetime is in use, review 'keylifekbs' as well; 'keylife-type both' rekeys on whichever limit is hit first.
Peers rekey on their own timers so exact match is not required, but keeping both ends similar avoids asymmetric rekeys.
The change applies at the next rekey; to force it now, flush: 'diagnose vpn tunnel flush <name>'.
Verify: 'get vpn ipsec phase2-interface <name> | grep keylifeseconds' shows 3600.
Repeat for other phase-2 selectors.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-011.gui: FortiGate: VPN > IPsec Tunnels > [tunnel] > Edit > Phase 2 Selectors > [selector] > Advanced > Key Lifetime = Seconds, value 3600. FortiManager: VPN Manager > IPsec VPN > phase-2 selector > Advanced > Key Lifetime > Install.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-011.cli:
config vpn ipsec phase2-interface
  edit <phase2-name>
    set keylife-type seconds
    set keylifeseconds 3600
  next
end
FORTIOS-IPSEC-011.verify: get vpn ipsec phase2-interface <name> | grep keylifeseconds   -> keylifeseconds: 3600
FORTIOS-IPSEC-011.rollback: Restore the previous value, e.g. 'set keylifeseconds 43200'. Reverting lengthens the data-key cryptoperiod; undesirable but harmless to connectivity.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-011.impact: Non-disruptive if applied without flushing - the new lifetime governs the next rekey only; no reboot and no immediate teardown. Flushing to apply immediately causes a brief phase-2 renegotiation. Note that a very short lifetime increases rekey (CPU) frequency slightly. No effect on SSL-VPN, admin or HA; HA syncs automatically.
references: CWE-326: Inadequate Encryption Strength
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - IPsec VPN > Phase 2 key lifetime (keylife-type / keylifeseconds)
NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev.5 - Key Management (cryptoperiods)
NIST SP 800-77 Rev.1, Guide to IPsec VPNs
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - IPsec VPN section 5.2

FORTIOS-IPSEC-012.risk: Anti-replay protection is disabled on the phase-2 SA, so the FortiGate does not enforce the ESP sequence-number sliding window. An on-path attacker can capture legitimate encrypted packets and re-inject (replay) them, causing duplicate transactions, state corruption in downstream applications, or resource consumption - without ever needing to decrypt the traffic. For protocols where replays are meaningful (financial transactions, control commands, authentication tokens tunneled over the VPN) this can drive fraudulent or disruptive actions. Business impact: transaction integrity and availability risk on data traversing the tunnel.
steps: Check the setting: 'get vpn ipsec phase2-interface <name> | grep replay'.
Re-enable anti-replay: 'config vpn ipsec phase2-interface' / 'edit <name>' / 'set replay enable' / 'next' / 'end'.

Identify why it was disabled - it is usually turned off to work around out-of-order delivery (e.g. certain QoS, per-packet load-balancing, or multipath designs); fix the underlying reordering instead of leaving replay off.
Ensure the remote peer also has anti-replay enabled.
Where legitimate reordering exists, prefer keeping replay enabled and addressing the path (single-path/queue ordering) rather than disabling protection.
Renegotiate the phase-2 SA to apply: 'diagnose vpn tunnel flush <name>'.
Verify config and runtime: 'get vpn ipsec phase2-interface <name> | grep replay' and 'diagnose vpn tunnel list name <name>'.
Repeat for all phase-2 selectors.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-012.gui: Anti-replay is enabled by default and is configured via CLI (config vpn ipsec phase2-interface > set replay enable); it is not exposed as a toggle in the standard VPN > IPsec Tunnels editor, so use the CLI (FortiGate: Dashboard > ... > CLI Console, or SSH). FortiManager: apply via the phase-2 object's CLI configuration / CLI-only template under VPN Manager or Device Manager, then Install.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-012.cli:
config vpn ipsec phase2-interface
  edit <phase2-name>
    set replay enable
  next
end
FORTIOS-IPSEC-012.verify: get vpn ipsec phase2-interface <name> | grep replay   -> replay: enable
FORTIOS-IPSEC-012.rollback: Disable again with 'set replay disable'. Reverting removes anti-replay protection and re-opens the packet-replay risk; only acceptable as a temporary workaround for a genuine packet-reordering path, and should be paired with compensating controls.
FORTIOS-IPSEC-012.impact: Low impact, effectively non-disruptive - toggling replay does not require the SA to renegotiate the algorithms, though flushing to apply immediately causes a brief phase-2 rebuild. No reboot; no effect on SSL-VPN, admin or HA. Caveat: if the tunnel path genuinely reorders packets, enabling replay may cause some legitimate packets to be dropped until the reordering is fixed.
references: CWE-294: Authentication Bypass by Capture-replay
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - IPsec VPN > Phase 2 (replay detection)
Fortinet Knowledge Base - IPsec anti-replay and out-of-order packet handling
RFC 4303 - IP Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) anti-replay / sequence numbers
NIST SP 800-77 Rev.1; CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - IPsec VPN section 5.2

FORTIOS-LOG-001.risk: With no FortiAnalyzer (FAZ) or other centralized collector, all logs live only in the FortiGate's local disk/memory buffer, which rolls over in hours-to-days under load and is fully controlled by anyone who compromises the device. An attacker who gains admin access (or exploits a FortiOS CVE) can wipe local logs with a single 'execute log-report reset' / factory reset, destroying the entire forensic trail of their intrusion. Business impact: inability to perform incident response, breach scope determination, or meet PCI-DSS 10 / HIPAA 164.312(b) audit-retention obligations, and no cross-device correlation for threat hunting.
steps: On the FortiAnalyzer, pre-authorize the FortiGate: FAZ GUI Device Manager > Add Device (or accept the unregistered device once it connects). Note the FAZ IP reachable from the FortiGate.
On the FortiGate, enable and point the FAZ logging feature: 'config log fortianalyzer setting' then 'set status enable' and 'set server <FAZ-IP>'.
Select what is uploaded and how fast: 'set upload-option realtime' (or 'store-and-upload' for low-bandwidth links).
Bind logging to the correct egress/management interface if needed: 'set source-ip <fgt-mgmt-ip>' and 'set interface-select-method sdwan|specify' as applicable, then 'end'.
Choose which logs to send with the log filter: 'config log fortianalyzer filter' / 'set severity information' (or higher) / 'set forward-traffic enable' / 'set local-traffic enable' / 'end'.
Approve the device on the FAZ side (Device Manager > Unregistered Devices > Authorize) so logs are accepted into an ADOM.
Validate the tunnel: 'execute log fortianalyzer test-connectivity' and confirm the FortiGate shows as connected in FAZ Device Manager.
(Optional resilience) Configure a second collector via 'config log fortianalyzer2 setting' / 'config log fortianalyzer3 setting'.
Confirm logs are actually arriving in FAZ Log View and set up retention/archive policies on the FAZ.
Save/backup the config once verified: 'execute backup config ...'.
FORTIOS-LOG-001.gui: FortiGate: Log & Report > Log Settings > Remote Logging and Archiving > enable 'Send logs to FortiAnalyzer/FortiManager', enter the FAZ IP and click 'Test Connectivity'. FortiAnalyzer side: Device Manager > authorize the FortiGate. FortiManager: Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > System Templates > Log Settings widget (push FAZ target to managed FortiGates).
FORTIOS-LOG-001.cli:
config log fortianalyzer setting
  set status enable
  set server <FAZ-IP>
end
FORTIOS-LOG-001.verify: get log fortianalyzer setting  -> status : enable and server : <FAZ-IP>; then 'execute log fortianalyzer test-connectivity' -> shows 'Connection: allow / Registration: registered' and a non-zero received rate.
FORTIOS-LOG-001.rollback: config log fortianalyzer setting / set status disable / end. Breaks: centralized log collection, FAZ reports, cross-device correlation and long-term retention immediately stop; the FortiGate falls back to volatile local logging only.
FORTIOS-LOG-001.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot; does not drop SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA/admin sessions. Adds outbound log traffic (TCP/514 OFTP by default) and CPU/disk load proportional to log volume; size the FAZ and WAN link accordingly. No maintenance window required.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring - 6.2.2 Centralized Logging and Reporting (tool COMPLIANCE_MAP: 6.1.1)
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Configuring logging to FortiAnalyzer
FortiAnalyzer Administration Guide - Device Manager: adding and authorizing devices
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 10.1/10.2, NIST SP 800-53 AU-2 / AU-3 / AU-4 / AU-9, HIPAA 164.312(b)
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-LOG-002.risk: Syslog forwarding to a SIEM is disabled, so security telemetry never leaves the appliance. Detections that depend on real-time SIEM correlation (brute-force, VPN abuse, policy violations, IPS hits) simply do not fire, and an attacker who roots the FortiGate can erase the only copy of the evidence. Business impact: blind SOC, failed PCI-DSS 10.5.3 (prompt back-up of logs to a central server) and undermined incident response and legal hold.
steps: Confirm the SIEM/syslog collector IP, transport (prefer TCP/TLS), and listening port; ensure a firewall path exists from the FortiGate's egress interface.
Enable primary syslog: 'config log syslogd setting' / 'set status enable' / 'set server <syslog-IP-or-FQDN>'.
Set reliable transport instead of lossy UDP: 'set mode reliable' (TCP); for encrypted transport also 'set enc-algorithm high' and 'set ssl-min-proto-version TLSv1-2'.
Set the destination port and facility to match the collector: 'set port 514' (or the TLS port) and 'set facility local7'.
Pick a parser-friendly format: 'set format rfc5424' (or 'cef' for CEF-based SIEMs), then 'end'.
Scope which logs forward: 'config log syslogd filter' / 'set severity information' / 'set forward-traffic enable' / 'set local-traffic enable' / 'end'.
If logging must egress a specific interface/VDOM: 'set source-ip <mgmt-ip>' and 'set interface-select-method specify' / 'set interface <mgmt-if>'.
Verify traffic on the wire: 'diagnose sniffer packet any "host <syslog-IP> and port 514" 4' and confirm packets leave the box.
Confirm the SIEM is receiving and parsing FortiGate events.
Document and back up the configuration.
FORTIOS-LOG-002.gui: FortiGate: syslog forwarding has no standard GUI page in FortiOS 7.x - configure it via CLI ('config log syslogd setting'); the Log & Report > Log Settings page primarily exposes FortiAnalyzer/FortiManager and FortiGate Cloud logging. FortiManager: Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > System Templates (Log Settings) or a CLI Template to push syslog config fleet-wide.
FORTIOS-LOG-002.cli:
config log syslogd setting
  set status enable
  set server <syslog-IP>
end
FORTIOS-LOG-002.verify: get log syslogd setting  -> status : enable and server : <syslog-IP>; corroborate delivery with 'diagnose sniffer packet any "host <syslog-IP> and port 514" 4' showing outbound packets and events landing in the SIEM.
FORTIOS-LOG-002.rollback: config log syslogd setting / set status disable / end. Breaks: SIEM ingestion of firewall logs stops immediately; any detections/alerts keyed on FortiGate syslog go dark.
FORTIOS-LOG-002.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot and no session drops. Adds outbound syslog traffic; UDP is fire-and-forget while reliable/TLS opens a persistent TCP session to the collector. No maintenance window required.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring - 6.2.2 Centralized Logging and Reporting (tool COMPLIANCE_MAP: 6.1.2)
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Configuring syslog settings
FortiOS Hardening Guide - Logging and reporting recommendations
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 10.5.3, NIST SP 800-53 AU-6 / AU-9, SOC 2 CC7.2
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-LOG-003.risk: The local log disk is set to 'overwrite', so when it fills the FortiGate silently discards the oldest records to make room for new ones. An attacker who understands this can generate a flood of noise (port scans, junk sessions) to roll the disk and push the evidence of their real actions off the end of the buffer - a low-effort anti-forensics technique. Business impact: loss of forensic history exactly when an incident is escalating, and gaps that violate audit-retention requirements.
steps: Confirm whether centralized logging (FAZ/syslog) is already in place; disk should be a buffer, not the system of record. If not, remediate FORTIOS-LOG-001/002 first.
Review current disk behavior: 'get log disk setting' and note diskfull, log-quota and the warning thresholds.
Change the full-disk action so logging halts instead of destroying history: 'config log disk setting' / 'set diskfull nolog' / 'end'.
Set early-warning thresholds so operators are alerted before the disk fills: 'set full-first-warning-threshold 75' / 'set full-second-warning-threshold 90' / 'set full-final-warning-threshold 95'.
Cap and schedule rolling so archives are uploaded before overwrite pressure builds: 'set roll-schedule daily' / 'set log-quota <MB>' as appropriate for the disk.
Ensure logs are offloaded (FAZ/syslog upload) so a 'nolog' stop is only a fallback, not a routine event.
Wire the disk-usage warning to an alert (see FORTIOS-LOG-014 alertemail 'log-disk-usage-warning', or an automation stitch) so a filling disk is actioned.
Monitor disk utilization: 'diagnose sys logdisk usage'.
Re-check the setting after a log-heavy day to confirm behavior.
Document the retention/offload design.
FORTIOS-LOG-003.gui: FortiGate: Log & Report > Log Settings (Local Log / Disk section) exposes disk logging and quotas; the diskfull action and warning thresholds are set via CLI ('config log disk setting'). FortiManager: push via Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > CLI Template.
FORTIOS-LOG-003.cli:
config log disk setting
  set diskfull nolog
  set full-first-warning-threshold 75
  set full-second-warning-threshold 90
  set full-final-warning-threshold 95
end
FORTIOS-LOG-003.verify: get log disk setting | grep diskfull  -> diskfull : nolog. (Monitor headroom with 'diagnose sys logdisk usage'.)
FORTIOS-LOG-003.rollback: config log disk setting / set diskfull overwrite / end. Breaks/tradeoff: reverting restores the anti-forensics exposure; conversely note that 'nolog' means new events are NOT written once the disk is full, so a full disk without offload creates a logging gap - always pair with FAZ/syslog offload and disk-usage alerting.
FORTIOS-LOG-003.impact: Non-disruptive to traffic. No reboot, no VPN/HA/admin session impact. Behavioral tradeoff only: on a full disk, logging stops rather than overwrites. No maintenance window. NOTE: the scanner's REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-LOG-003'] is mis-mapped (it contains 'set fwpolicy-implicit-log enable', the fix for rule LOG-004); the CLI above is the correct fix for the disk-full-overwrite finding.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring (tool COMPLIANCE_MAP: 6.1.3)
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Configuring disk logging and log quotas
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 10.2 / 10.5.1, NIST SP 800-53 AU-4 (Audit Storage Capacity) / AU-5 / AU-11 / AU-12
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-LOG-004.risk: Traffic dropped by the implicit deny (default-deny) policy is not logged, so the most security-relevant events on a firewall - packets that hit no allow rule - are invisible. Reconnaissance sweeps, lateral-movement probes to closed ports, exfiltration attempts to blocked destinations and misconfigured-app noise all disappear. Business impact: no visibility into denied/anomalous flows, crippled threat hunting and incident scoping, and a gap against PCI-DSS 10 audit requirements.
steps: Review current global log settings: 'get log setting' and locate fwpolicy-implicit-log / fwpolicy6-implicit-log.
Enable IPv4 implicit-deny logging: 'config log setting' / 'set fwpolicy-implicit-log enable'.
Enable IPv6 implicit-deny logging too: 'set fwpolicy6-implicit-log enable', then 'end'.
Ensure the events have somewhere to go (FAZ/syslog) so denied-traffic volume does not just churn local disk - see FORTIOS-LOG-001/002.
(Recommended) Also enable per-policy logging on production allow rules so both allowed and denied flows are covered: 'config firewall policy' / 'edit <id>' / 'set logtraffic all' / 'next' / 'end'.
Optionally reduce noise by writing explicit deny policies with logging for known-bad or chatty sources instead of relying solely on the implicit rule.
Confirm deny events now appear: 'execute log filter category 0' (traffic) then 'execute log display' and look for action=deny / policyid=0 entries.
Watch log rate to size the collector: 'diagnose test application miglogd 6' (log statistics).
Validate the same in the SIEM/FAZ.
Document and back up the config.
FORTIOS-LOG-004.gui: FortiGate: Log & Report > Log Settings governs local/remote log behavior; the implicit-deny logging toggle is a global setting configured via CLI ('config log setting / set fwpolicy-implicit-log enable'). Per-policy logging: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit policy > Logging Options > 'All Sessions'. FortiManager: Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > CLI Template.
FORTIOS-LOG-004.cli:
config log setting
  set fwpolicy-implicit-log enable
  set fwpolicy6-implicit-log enable
end
FORTIOS-LOG-004.verify: get log setting | grep fwpolicy-implicit-log  -> fwpolicy-implicit-log : enable (and fwpolicy6-implicit-log : enable). Confirm deny events with 'execute log filter category 0' (traffic) then 'execute log display'.
FORTIOS-LOG-004.rollback: config log setting / set fwpolicy-implicit-log disable / set fwpolicy6-implicit-log disable / end. Breaks: denied/anomalous traffic becomes invisible again; any SIEM detections keyed on action=deny/policyid=0 stop.
FORTIOS-LOG-004.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot, no session drops. Can significantly increase log volume on Internet-facing interfaces (scan/backscatter noise) - ensure FAZ/syslog and disk quotas are sized. No maintenance window. NOTE: the scanner's REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-LOG-004'] is mis-mapped (it contains 'set enc-algorithm high', the fix for rule LOG-005); the CLI above is the correct fix for the implicit-deny-logging finding, and it matches the (mislabeled) REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-LOG-003'] value.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 4 Firewall Policies / Section 6 Logging (tool COMPLIANCE_MAP: 6.1.4)
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Global log settings (fwpolicy-implicit-log)
FortiOS Administration Guide - Firewall Policy logging options (logtraffic all)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 10.2 / 10.5.1, NIST SP 800-53 AU-2 / AU-9 / AU-12
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-LOG-005.risk: FortiAnalyzer logging is enabled but the OFTP transport encryption (enc-algorithm) is default/low/disabled, so logs traverse the network weakly protected or in cleartext. An adversary positioned on the management/log path can sniff sensitive data carried in logs (usernames, source/destination IPs, URLs, VPN identities, admin activity) or tamper with the stream. Business impact: exposure of security telemetry, loss of log integrity/chain-of-custody, and non-compliance with encryption-in-transit mandates (PCI-DSS 4, HIPAA 164.312(e)).
steps: Confirm FAZ logging is enabled and connected first (FORTIOS-LOG-001).
Review current encryption/transport: 'get log fortianalyzer setting' and note enc-algorithm, reliable and ssl-min-proto-version.
Enforce strong cipher suites on the OFTP tunnel: 'config log fortianalyzer setting' / 'set enc-algorithm high'.
Encryption requires the reliable (SSL/OFTP) channel, so also set: 'set reliable enable'.
Pin a modern TLS floor if supported on your build: 'set ssl-min-proto-version TLSv1-2', then 'end'.
On the FortiAnalyzer side, ensure the device/ADOM is authorized and that encrypted OFTP is accepted (it is negotiated automatically) so the tunnel re-establishes.
Re-establish and test the tunnel: 'execute log fortianalyzer test-connectivity'.
Verify logs still flow into FAZ Log View after the cipher change.
Apply the same to fortianalyzer2/3 collectors if configured.
Document and back up the configuration.
FORTIOS-LOG-005.gui: FortiGate: log encryption to FortiAnalyzer (enc-algorithm) is configured via CLI under 'config log fortianalyzer setting'; the Log & Report > Log Settings page only exposes the FAZ target, not the cipher strength. FortiManager: Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > CLI Template to enforce enc-algorithm high fleet-wide.
FORTIOS-LOG-005.cli:
config log fortianalyzer setting
  set reliable enable
  set enc-algorithm high
  set ssl-min-proto-version TLSv1-2
end
FORTIOS-LOG-005.verify: get log fortianalyzer setting | grep enc-algorithm  -> enc-algorithm : high (and reliable : enable). Confirm connectivity with 'execute log fortianalyzer test-connectivity'.
FORTIOS-LOG-005.rollback: config log fortianalyzer setting / set enc-algorithm low / end (optionally 'set reliable disable'). Breaks: log traffic to FAZ reverts to weak/cleartext transport, re-exposing telemetry to interception/tampering.
FORTIOS-LOG-005.impact: Non-disruptive to firewall traffic. No reboot, no VPN/HA/admin session drops. The FAZ OFTP session briefly re-negotiates ciphers; slight CPU overhead for encryption. No maintenance window (do off-peak if log volume is very high). NOTE: not in COMPLIANCE_MAP; the (mislabeled) REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-LOG-004'] holds this same 'set enc-algorithm high' fix.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring - 6.2.1 Encrypt Log Transmission to FortiAnalyzer / FortiManager
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > FortiAnalyzer settings (reliable, enc-algorithm, ssl-min-proto-version)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 4.1/4.2, NIST SP 800-53 SC-8 / SC-13 / AU-9, HIPAA 164.312(e)(1)
CWE-319 Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information

FORTIOS-LOG-006.risk: FortiAnalyzer logging uses non-reliable (UDP-style) delivery, so under CPU pressure, link congestion, or a burst of events the FortiGate silently drops log records with no retransmission. During exactly the moments that matter most - an active attack generating a spike of IPS/traffic logs - the record can be incomplete. Business impact: gaps in the audit trail, unreliable detections and reporting, and inability to prove completeness of logs for compliance.
steps: Confirm FAZ logging is enabled (FORTIOS-LOG-001) and reachable.
