Review current transport: 'get log fortianalyzer setting | grep reliable'.
Enable reliable (OFTP over TCP) delivery: 'config log fortianalyzer setting' / 'set reliable enable'.
Pair reliability with encryption for defense in depth: 'set enc-algorithm high' (see FORTIOS-LOG-005), then 'end'.
Tune keepalive/failover so a stalled collector is detected: 'set monitor-keepalive-period 5' and 'set monitor-failure-retry-period 5' as supported.
Consider buffering to ride out short outages: 'set upload-option store-and-upload' (or keep realtime for lowest latency).
Re-test the tunnel: 'execute log fortianalyzer test-connectivity'.
Verify no drops under load: 'diagnose test application miglogd 6' and check FAZ received rate vs. sent.
Apply to fortianalyzer2/3 if used.
Document and back up the configuration.
FORTIOS-LOG-006.gui: FortiGate: reliable delivery to FortiAnalyzer is a CLI setting ('config log fortianalyzer setting / set reliable enable'); it is not exposed on the Log & Report > Log Settings page. FortiManager: Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > CLI Template.
FORTIOS-LOG-006.cli:
config log fortianalyzer setting
  set reliable enable
end
FORTIOS-LOG-006.verify: get log fortianalyzer setting | grep reliable  -> reliable : enable. Cross-check that FAZ received log rate matches the FortiGate send rate under load.
FORTIOS-LOG-006.rollback: config log fortianalyzer setting / set reliable disable / end. Breaks: log delivery reverts to best-effort transport that can silently drop records under load.
FORTIOS-LOG-006.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot, no session drops. Reliable mode opens a persistent TCP OFTP session and adds minor overhead vs. UDP-style delivery; ensure a firewall path allows the TCP log port. No maintenance window. NOTE: not present in COMPLIANCE_MAP or REMEDIATION_COMMANDS - CLI authored here.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring (reliable/centralized logging)
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > FortiAnalyzer settings (reliable delivery, upload-option)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 10.5.3, NIST SP 800-53 AU-4 / AU-9 / AU-12
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-LOG-007.risk: Syslog is forwarding over UDP, which is both unreliable (no retransmission - logs are dropped under congestion or during log storms) and unencrypted (records travel in cleartext). An attacker on the log path can passively read sensitive fields (users, IPs, URLs, admin actions) or actively spoof/forge syslog packets to poison the SIEM with false events, and can drop the packets that would reveal their activity. Business impact: interceptable/forgeable audit trail, missed detections, and failure of encryption-in-transit and log-integrity controls.
steps: Confirm the syslog collector supports TCP and, ideally, TLS on a known port.
Review current transport: 'get log syslogd setting' and note mode, enc-algorithm and port.
Switch from UDP to reliable TCP transport: 'config log syslogd setting' / 'set mode reliable'.
Add TLS encryption on top of reliable transport: 'set enc-algorithm high' and 'set ssl-min-proto-version TLSv1-2'.
Set the correct TLS/TCP port and (if mutual TLS) certificate: 'set port <tls-port>' / 'set certificate <local-cert>', then 'end'.
If the collector requires a specific source: 'set source-ip <mgmt-ip>'.
Confirm the encrypted session establishes: 'diagnose sniffer packet any "host <syslog-IP> and port <tls-port>" 4' should show a TCP/TLS handshake, not cleartext syslog.
Verify the SIEM decrypts and parses the events.
Apply equivalent settings to syslogd2/3/4 collectors if configured.
Document and back up the configuration.
FORTIOS-LOG-007.gui: FortiGate: syslog transport is CLI-only ('config log syslogd setting' - server, port, mode reliable/TLS, enc-algorithm, certificate); there is no syslog page in the Log & Report > Log Settings GUI in FortiOS 7.x. FortiManager: Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > System Templates (Log Settings) or CLI Template.
FORTIOS-LOG-007.cli:
config log syslogd setting
  set mode reliable
  set enc-algorithm high
  set ssl-min-proto-version TLSv1-2
end
FORTIOS-LOG-007.verify: get log syslogd setting | grep mode  -> mode : reliable (and enc-algorithm : high). Confirm with 'diagnose sniffer packet any "host <syslog-IP> and port <tls-port>"' that a TLS handshake occurs rather than plaintext UDP/514.
FORTIOS-LOG-007.rollback: config log syslogd setting / set mode udp / set enc-algorithm disable / end. Breaks/tradeoff: reverts to lossy cleartext transport; also, if the collector is not listening on TCP/TLS after you switch to reliable, log delivery stops until the server side is corrected - validate the collector first.
FORTIOS-LOG-007.impact: Non-disruptive to firewall traffic. No reboot, no VPN/HA/admin session drops. Reliable/TLS opens a persistent encrypted TCP session (minor CPU for crypto) and requires the collector to listen on TCP/TLS. No maintenance window. NOTE: not present in COMPLIANCE_MAP or REMEDIATION_COMMANDS - CLI authored here.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring - 6.2.1 Encrypt Log Transmission
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Syslog settings (mode reliable, enc-algorithm, ssl-min-proto-version)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 4.1 / 10.5.3, NIST SP 800-53 SC-8 / AU-9
CWE-319 Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information

FORTIOS-LOG-008.risk: Syslog forwarding is administratively enabled but no destination server is set, so the feature is a no-op: the FortiGate believes it is shipping logs while nothing is actually delivered to any collector. This is a dangerous false sense of security - operators assume the SIEM has the data during an incident and discover the gap only when they go looking for evidence. Business impact: silent loss of the entire remote audit trail and failed compliance despite an 'enabled' logging control.
steps: Identify the correct syslog/SIEM collector IP or FQDN and its transport/port.
Review the current (empty) config: 'get log syslogd setting'.
Set the destination so the enabled feature actually delivers: 'config log syslogd setting' / 'set status enable' / 'set server <syslog-IP-or-FQDN>'.
Set port and reliable/encrypted transport (see FORTIOS-LOG-007): 'set port 514' / 'set mode reliable' / 'set enc-algorithm high', then 'end'.
Configure the forwarding filter so meaningful events ship: 'config log syslogd filter' / 'set severity information' / 'end'.
If the collector expects a specific source or egress interface: 'set source-ip <mgmt-ip>' / 'set interface-select-method specify'.
Prove delivery on the wire: 'diagnose sniffer packet any "host <syslog-IP> and port 514" 4'.
Confirm events appear in the SIEM/collector.
Add a secondary collector for resilience (FORTIOS-LOG-016).
Document and back up the configuration.
FORTIOS-LOG-008.gui: FortiGate: syslog is configured via CLI ('config log syslogd setting' - set status/server/port); the Log & Report > Log Settings GUI page does not expose a syslog server field in FortiOS 7.x. FortiManager: Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > System Templates (Log Settings) or CLI Template to populate the server centrally.
FORTIOS-LOG-008.cli:
config log syslogd setting
  set status enable
  set server <syslog-IP-or-FQDN>
  set port 514
  set mode reliable
end
FORTIOS-LOG-008.verify: get log syslogd setting | grep server  -> server : <syslog-IP-or-FQDN> (and status : enable). Confirm packets leave via 'diagnose sniffer packet any "host <syslog-IP> and port 514" 4'.
FORTIOS-LOG-008.rollback: config log syslogd setting / set status disable / end (or unset server). Breaks: remote syslog delivery stops; the box returns to local-only logging.
FORTIOS-LOG-008.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot, no session drops. Adds outbound syslog traffic to the newly-defined collector. No maintenance window. NOTE: not present in COMPLIANCE_MAP or REMEDIATION_COMMANDS - CLI authored here.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring - 6.2.2 Centralized Logging and Reporting
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Syslog settings (server, port, mode)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 10.5.3, NIST SP 800-53 AU-6 / AU-9
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-LOG-009.risk: Logging of allowed local-in traffic (packets destined to the FortiGate itself - administrative HTTPS/SSH, VPN listeners, SNMP, FortiGuard) is disabled, so successful connections to the appliance's own services are not recorded. When an attacker authenticates to the management plane or VPN portal after credential theft or a device CVE, there is no local-in log of that successful access. Business impact: no audit trail of who reached the firewall's own services, hampering detection of management-plane compromise and admin accountability.
steps: Review current global log settings: 'get log setting | grep local-in'.
Enable logging of permitted local-in traffic: 'config log setting' / 'set local-in-allow enable', then 'end'.
Pair it with denied local-in logging for full coverage (FORTIOS-LOG-010): 'set local-in-deny-unicast enable' / 'set local-in-deny-broadcast enable'.
Ensure local-in policies exist so management access is explicitly controlled and thus meaningfully logged: 'config firewall local-in-policy' (restrict admin/VPN services to trusted sources).
Make sure the events reach FAZ/syslog (FORTIOS-LOG-001/002) rather than only local disk.
Generate a test admin/VPN connection to the box.
Confirm the event was captured: 'execute log filter category 0' (traffic) then 'execute log display' and look for local-in entries.
Watch volume, since management-plane scanning can be noisy: 'diagnose test application miglogd 6'.
Validate the same in the SIEM.
Document and back up the configuration.
FORTIOS-LOG-009.gui: FortiGate: local-in logging is a global CLI setting ('config log setting / set local-in-allow enable'); it is not on the Log Settings GUI page. Local-in policies that govern management access are under Policy & Objects > Local In Policy (where available) or CLI 'config firewall local-in-policy'. FortiManager: CLI Template.
FORTIOS-LOG-009.cli:
config log setting
  set local-in-allow enable
end
FORTIOS-LOG-009.verify: get log setting | grep local-in-allow  -> local-in-allow : enable. Confirm capture by connecting to the mgmt interface and checking 'execute log display' for local-in entries.
FORTIOS-LOG-009.rollback: config log setting / set local-in-allow disable / end. Breaks: successful connections to the FortiGate's own services stop being logged, reducing management-plane visibility.
FORTIOS-LOG-009.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot, no session drops. Minor increase in log volume (management/service traffic to the box). No maintenance window. NOTE: not present in COMPLIANCE_MAP or REMEDIATION_COMMANDS - CLI authored here.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring / management-plane logging
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Global log settings (local-in-allow); Local-in policy
FortiOS Hardening Guide - Restrict and log administrative/management access
NIST SP 800-53 AU-2 / AC-17 / SI-4, CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-LOG-010.risk: Logging of denied local-in unicast traffic is disabled, so probes and attacks aimed directly at the firewall's own services (admin GUI/SSH scanning, VPN portal enumeration, SNMP/FortiGuard port sweeps) are dropped without a trace. This is precisely the reconnaissance that precedes management-plane exploitation of FortiOS CVEs; without these logs, the early-warning signal of someone attacking the appliance itself is lost. Business impact: no detection of firewall-targeted recon/attacks and no evidence for incident scoping when a device CVE is exploited.
steps: Review current settings: 'get log setting | grep local-in-deny'.
Enable logging of denied unicast local-in traffic: 'config log setting' / 'set local-in-deny-unicast enable'.
Also log denied broadcast local-in traffic for completeness: 'set local-in-deny-broadcast enable', then 'end'.
Enable allowed local-in logging too (FORTIOS-LOG-009) so both sides are visible: 'set local-in-allow enable'.
Tighten local-in policies so only trusted sources can even reach admin/VPN services - denied attempts then log meaningfully: 'config firewall local-in-policy' with restrictive src.
Forward these events to FAZ/syslog (FORTIOS-LOG-001/002) and build a SIEM rule for repeated denied local-in hits.
Generate a blocked test connection to a closed service on the box.
Confirm capture: 'execute log filter category 0' (traffic) then 'execute log display' for action=deny local-in entries.
Validate in the SIEM/FAZ.
Document and back up the configuration.
FORTIOS-LOG-010.gui: FortiGate: denied local-in logging is a global CLI setting ('config log setting / set local-in-deny-unicast enable'); not exposed on the Log Settings page. Local-in policies: Policy & Objects > Local In Policy or CLI 'config firewall local-in-policy'. FortiManager: CLI Template.
FORTIOS-LOG-010.cli:
config log setting
  set local-in-deny-unicast enable
  set local-in-deny-broadcast enable
end
FORTIOS-LOG-010.verify: get log setting | grep local-in-deny-unicast  -> local-in-deny-unicast : enable. Confirm with a blocked probe to the box and 'execute log display'.
FORTIOS-LOG-010.rollback: config log setting / set local-in-deny-unicast disable / set local-in-deny-broadcast disable / end. Breaks: firewall-targeted recon/attack attempts become invisible again.
FORTIOS-LOG-010.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot, no session drops. On Internet-facing interfaces this can generate high log volume (constant scanning); size FAZ/syslog and disk accordingly. No maintenance window. NOTE: not present in COMPLIANCE_MAP or REMEDIATION_COMMANDS - CLI authored here.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring / management-plane logging
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Global log settings (local-in-deny-unicast/-broadcast)
FortiOS Hardening Guide - Log and restrict access to the management plane
NIST SP 800-53 AU-2 / SI-4 / SC-7, CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-LOG-011.risk: System event logging is disabled, so the FortiGate does not record its own operational and security events: configuration changes, admin logins/logouts, firmware changes, HA transitions, interface up/down, and system alerts. An attacker who gains admin access can add accounts, alter policies, disable protections and pivot - with zero record of any of it. Business impact: no admin accountability, no change-tracking, blind incident response, and direct failure of PCI-DSS 10.2 audit-trail requirements.
steps: Review current event filter: 'get log eventfilter' (or 'show log eventfilter').
Enable the master event logging switch: 'config log eventfilter' / 'set event enable'.
Turn on the key security subtypes: 'set system enable' / 'set vpn enable' / 'set user enable' / 'set router enable' / 'set ha enable', then 'end'.
Enable additional relevant subtypes for your deployment (e.g. 'set wireless-activity enable', 'set security-rating enable', 'set connector enable', 'set sdwan enable').
Ensure these events are forwarded (FAZ/syslog) not only stored locally - see FORTIOS-LOG-001/002.
Generate a test event (e.g. make a benign config change or log in as admin).
Confirm capture: 'execute log filter category 1' (event; run 'execute log filter category ?' to list categories) then 'execute log display' and look for the system/admin event.
Build SIEM detections on admin-login and config-change event IDs.
Verify in FAZ Log View / SIEM.
Document and back up the configuration.
FORTIOS-LOG-011.gui: FortiGate: Log & Report > Log Settings > Event Logging > select 'All' (or 'Customize' and enable System activity event, VPN, User, Router, HA, etc.). FortiManager: Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > System Templates (Log Settings) or CLI Template.
FORTIOS-LOG-011.cli:
config log eventfilter
  set event enable
  set system enable
  set vpn enable
  set user enable
  set ha enable
end
FORTIOS-LOG-011.verify: get log eventfilter | grep event  -> event : enable. Confirm capture with 'execute log filter category 1' (event) then 'execute log display' after generating an admin/config event.
FORTIOS-LOG-011.rollback: config log eventfilter / set event disable / end. Breaks: all system/event logging (config changes, admin logins, HA, VPN, user) stops - a major visibility and compliance regression.
FORTIOS-LOG-011.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot, no session drops. Modest, mostly low-rate event volume. No maintenance window. NOTE: not present in COMPLIANCE_MAP or REMEDIATION_COMMANDS - CLI authored here.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring - 6.1 Enable Event Logging
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Event log filters (config log eventfilter)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 10.2 (admin actions, config changes), NIST SP 800-53 AU-2 / AU-3 / AU-12
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-LOG-012.risk: VPN event logging is disabled, so IPsec/SSL-VPN tunnel negotiations, phase1/phase2 status, login successes/failures and disconnects are not recorded. Remote-access VPN is a top initial-access vector (credential stuffing, exploited VPN CVEs); without VPN event logs, brute-force campaigns and anomalous logins from new geographies go undetected, and post-incident you cannot tell which accounts or tunnels were used. Business impact: undetected VPN account compromise and no forensic trail for the most commonly abused entry point.
steps: Review the event filter: 'get log eventfilter | grep vpn'.
Ensure the master switch is on: 'config log eventfilter' / 'set event enable'.
Enable VPN event logging: 'set vpn enable', then 'end'.
Also enable user-auth events (FORTIOS-LOG-013) so VPN login attempts and failures are fully captured: 'set user enable'.
Forward VPN events to FAZ/syslog (FORTIOS-LOG-001/002) for correlation and geovelocity/brute-force detection.

VPN portal login successes/failures are captured via the event log (vpn + user subtypes) and forward-traffic logs - ensure both subtypes are enabled and forwarded rather than relying on any per-portal toggle.
Trigger a test VPN login (success and failure).
Confirm capture: 'execute log filter category 1' (event) then 'execute log display' for VPN events (or IPsec/SSL-VPN specific IDs).
Build SIEM alerts for repeated VPN auth failures / impossible travel.
Document and back up the configuration.
FORTIOS-LOG-012.gui: FortiGate: Log & Report > Log Settings > Event Logging > enable 'VPN activity event' (Customize). FortiManager: Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > System Templates (Log Settings) or CLI Template.
FORTIOS-LOG-012.cli:
config log eventfilter
  set event enable
  set vpn enable
end
FORTIOS-LOG-012.verify: get log eventfilter | grep vpn  -> vpn : enable. Confirm with a test tunnel/portal login: 'execute log filter category 1' (event) then 'execute log display'.
FORTIOS-LOG-012.rollback: config log eventfilter / set vpn disable / end. Breaks: VPN tunnel and login events stop logging, removing visibility into the top remote-access attack surface.
FORTIOS-LOG-012.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot; does NOT drop existing SSL-VPN/IPsec sessions (this only changes what is logged, not VPN operation). Low log volume. No maintenance window. NOTE: not present in COMPLIANCE_MAP or REMEDIATION_COMMANDS - CLI authored here.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring - 6.1 Enable Event Logging (VPN)
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Event log filters (vpn); VPN event logging
FortiOS Hardening Guide - Monitor and log VPN access
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 10.2 / 8.3, NIST SP 800-53 AU-2 / AC-17 / SI-4, MITRE ATT&CK T1133 External Remote Services
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-LOG-013.risk: User authentication event logging is disabled, so login attempts and authentication failures (admin logins, firewall-auth users, VPN/2FA challenges, LDAP/RADIUS/SSO auth) are not recorded. This directly blinds brute-force and password-spray detection: an attacker can hammer credentials or ride a stolen SSO session with no failed-auth trail to trigger lockout analytics or SOC alerts. Business impact: undetected credential attacks, no accountability for who authenticated, and failure of PCI-DSS/NIST authentication-monitoring controls.
steps: Review the event filter: 'get log eventfilter | grep user'.
Ensure the master switch is on: 'config log eventfilter' / 'set event enable'.
Enable user authentication event logging: 'set user enable', then 'end'.
Enable VPN events too (FORTIOS-LOG-012) since most user auth arrives via VPN: 'set vpn enable'.
Ensure admin-login accountability is captured and consider tightening lockout (see admin lockout settings) so failures both log and throttle.
Forward auth events to FAZ/syslog (FORTIOS-LOG-001/002) and build brute-force/spray detections there.
Trigger a failed and a successful authentication as a test.
Confirm capture: 'execute log filter category 1' (event) then 'execute log display' for user/auth events.
Validate the SIEM correlation rule fires on repeated failures.
Document and back up the configuration.
FORTIOS-LOG-013.gui: FortiGate: Log & Report > Log Settings > Event Logging > enable 'User activity event' (Customize). FortiManager: Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > System Templates (Log Settings) or CLI Template.
FORTIOS-LOG-013.cli:
config log eventfilter
  set event enable
  set user enable
end
FORTIOS-LOG-013.verify: get log eventfilter | grep user  -> user : enable. Confirm with a failed+successful auth: 'execute log filter category 1' (event) then 'execute log display'.
FORTIOS-LOG-013.rollback: config log eventfilter / set user disable / end. Breaks: authentication successes/failures stop logging, disabling brute-force/spray detection and auth accountability.
FORTIOS-LOG-013.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot, no session drops. Low-to-moderate log volume depending on auth activity. No maintenance window. NOTE: not present in COMPLIANCE_MAP or REMEDIATION_COMMANDS - CLI authored here.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring - 6.1 Enable Event Logging (user)
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Event log filters (user); authentication logging
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 8.3 / 10.2, NIST SP 800-53 AU-2 / AC-7 / IA-5, MITRE ATT&CK T1110 Brute Force
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-LOG-014.risk: No alert-email recipients are configured, so the FortiGate cannot push out-of-band notifications for critical events (admin logins, config changes, HA failover, IPS/AV hits, VPN auth failures, disk-full, license/FortiGuard expiry). If the SIEM pipeline is down or an attacker disables central logging, no human is notified and an incident can smolder undetected for days. Business impact: delayed incident detection/response, missed HA and capacity failures, and a gap in the 'timely alerting' expectation of monitoring frameworks.
steps: Configure the SMTP relay the FortiGate will use: 'config system email-server' / 'set server <smtp-host>' / 'set port 465' / 'set security smtps' / 'set authenticate enable' / 'set username <smtp-user>' / 'set password <smtp-pass>' / 'end'.
Set the alert-email sender and recipients: 'config alertemail setting' / 'set username <from-address>' / 'set mailto1 soc@example.com' (add mailto2/mailto3 as needed).
Choose the triggering model: 'set filter-mode category' (per-event-category alerts) and 'set email-interval 5'.

Enable the security-relevant categories: 'set admin-login-logs enable' / 'set configuration-changes-logs enable' / 'set HA-logs enable' / 'set IPS-logs enable' / 'set firewall-authentication-failure-logs enable' / 'set sslvpn-authentication-errors-logs enable' / 'set log-disk-usage-warning enable', then 'end'.
Ensure DNS and a firewall path to the SMTP relay exist from the FortiGate.
Send a test: trigger a benign event (e.g. admin login) or use the SMTP test where available and confirm mail is received.
Verify SPF/relay acceptance so alerts are not silently dropped by the mail gateway.
Consider pairing with an automation stitch (FORTIOS-LOG-015) for richer/webhook alerting.
Confirm recipients actually receive and monitor the mailbox.
Document and back up the configuration.
FORTIOS-LOG-014.gui: FortiGate: SMTP relay at System > Settings > Email Service (server/port/auth). Alert-email recipients and event categories are configured via CLI ('config alertemail setting') in current FortiOS. FortiManager: Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > CLI Template.
FORTIOS-LOG-014.cli:
config system email-server
  set server <smtp-host>
  set port 465
  set security smtps
  set authenticate enable
  set username <smtp-user>
  set password <smtp-pass>
end
config alertemail setting
  set username <from-address>
  set mailto1 <recipient@example.com>
  set filter-mode category
  set admin-login-logs enable
  set configuration-changes-logs enable
  set HA-logs enable
  set log-disk-usage-warning enable
end
FORTIOS-LOG-014.verify: get alertemail setting | grep mailto1  -> mailto1 : <recipient@example.com>; then generate a triggering event (admin login) and confirm the alert email is received. Check SMTP reachability with 'diagnose sniffer packet any "host <smtp-host> and port 465" 4'.
FORTIOS-LOG-014.rollback: config alertemail setting / unset mailto1 / end (and optionally clear 'config system email-server'). Breaks: out-of-band email alerting for critical events stops; only central logging (if any) remains.
FORTIOS-LOG-014.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot, no session drops. Requires reachable SMTP relay and correct credentials; misconfiguration only affects alert delivery, not traffic. No maintenance window. NOTE: not present in COMPLIANCE_MAP or REMEDIATION_COMMANDS - CLI authored here.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring (alerting/notification)
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Alert email; System > Settings > Email service (config system email-server, config alertemail setting)
NIST SP 800-53 AU-5 / IR-4 / IR-5 / SI-4, PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 10.7
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-LOG-015.risk: No FortiOS automation triggers/stitches are configured, so the firewall has no automated response to security events - an admin login from an unexpected source, an IPS critical alert, a repeated VPN auth failure or an HA failover all pass without any triggered notification or containment action. This lengthens mean-time-to-respond and forces every response to be fully manual, which fails during off-hours and fast-moving intrusions. Business impact: slower detection/containment, higher breach dwell time, and no self-defending guardrails.
steps: Design a minimum set of stitches: admin-login alert, IPS/critical-event alert, and HA-failover notification.
Create a trigger, e.g. admin login: 'config system automation-trigger' / 'edit admin-login-trigger' / 'set trigger-type event-based' / 'set event-type event-log' / 'set logid 32001' / 'next' / 'end'.

Create an action (email/webhook/notification): 'config system automation-action' / 'edit notify-soc' / 'set action-type email' / 'set email-to soc@example.com' / 'set email-subject "FortiGate admin login"' / 'next' / 'end'.

Bind them into an enabled stitch (FortiOS 7.2+ uses a 'config actions' sub-block): 'config system automation-stitch' / 'edit admin-login-alert' / 'set trigger admin-login-trigger' / 'set status enable' / 'config actions' / 'edit 1' / 'set action notify-soc' / 'next' / 'end' / 'next' / 'end'.
Add high-value triggers: an IPS-signature/severity trigger (trigger-type event-based, appropriate event-type/logid) and an HA-failover trigger (event-type ha-failover).
For containment, consider an action-type that quarantines a source or runs a CLI/webhook (e.g. to a SOAR) where policy allows.
Ensure the SMTP relay (FORTIOS-LOG-014) or webhook endpoint is reachable so actions can fire.
Test the stitch: 'diagnose automation test <stitch-name>' (or trigger the real event) and confirm the action executes.
Review execution history in Security Fabric > Automation.
Document and back up the configuration.
FORTIOS-LOG-015.gui: FortiGate: Security Fabric > Automation > create Triggers, Actions and Stitches (New Automation Stitch), then enable. FortiManager: Fabric/Automation is largely per-device; push via Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > CLI Template.
FORTIOS-LOG-015.cli:
config system automation-trigger
  edit "admin-login-trigger"
    set trigger-type event-based
    set event-type event-log
    set logid 32001
  next
end
config system automation-action
  edit "notify-soc"
    set action-type email
    set email-to "soc@example.com"
    set email-subject "FortiGate admin login"
  next
end
config system automation-stitch
  edit "admin-login-alert"
    set status enable
    set trigger "admin-login-trigger"
    config actions
      edit 1
        set action "notify-soc"
      next
    end
  next
end
FORTIOS-LOG-015.verify: get system automation-stitch  -> lists 'admin-login-alert' with status : enable; then 'diagnose automation test admin-login-alert' (or trigger the event) and confirm the action fires and appears in Security Fabric > Automation history.
FORTIOS-LOG-015.rollback: config system automation-stitch / delete admin-login-alert / end (and remove the trigger/action). Breaks: the automated notification/response for that event stops; response reverts to fully manual.
FORTIOS-LOG-015.impact: Non-disruptive by default (email/notification actions). No reboot, no session drops. Caution: containment-style actions (quarantine host, run CLI script) can affect traffic/users if trigger conditions are too broad - scope triggers tightly and test first. No maintenance window for notification-only stitches. NOTE: not present in COMPLIANCE_MAP or REMEDIATION_COMMANDS - CLI authored here.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring (automated alerting/response)
FortiOS Administration Guide - Automation (Security Fabric > Automation; config system automation-trigger/action/stitch)
NIST SP 800-53 IR-4 / IR-5 / SI-4 / AU-6, SOC 2 CC7.3 / CC7.4
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-LOG-016.risk: Primary syslog forwarding is enabled but there is no secondary (syslogd2) collector, creating a single point of failure in the audit pipeline. If the sole SIEM/collector is down for maintenance, is network-partitioned, or is deliberately targeted (an attacker DoSes or blocks the collector before acting), logs generated during that window are lost with no fallback destination. Business impact: audit-trail gaps during outages/attacks and reduced assurance of log completeness for compliance and forensics.
steps: Provision or identify a second, independent syslog/SIEM collector (ideally different host/site/path from the primary).
Configure the secondary destination: 'config log syslogd2 setting' / 'set status enable' / 'set server <secondary-syslog-IP>'.
Match reliable/encrypted transport to the primary: 'set mode reliable' / 'set enc-algorithm high' / 'set port <port>', then 'end'.
Set the secondary forwarding filter: 'config log syslogd2 filter' / 'set severity information' / 'end'.
If needed, add syslogd3/syslogd4 for additional redundancy the same way.
Confirm a firewall/routing path from the FortiGate egress to the secondary collector.
Verify delivery to the second collector: 'diagnose sniffer packet any "host <secondary-syslog-IP> and port <port>" 4' and check events arrive.
Simulate a primary-collector outage and confirm the secondary still receives logs.
Confirm both collectors are monitored/alerting.
Document and back up the configuration.
FORTIOS-LOG-016.gui: FortiGate: syslog servers (primary syslogd and secondary syslogd2/3/4) are configured via CLI ('config log syslogd2 setting'); the Log Settings GUI does not expose multiple syslog servers in FortiOS 7.x. FortiManager: Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > CLI Template to push the redundant collector fleet-wide.
FORTIOS-LOG-016.cli:
config log syslogd2 setting
  set status enable
  set server <secondary-syslog-IP>
  set mode reliable
end
FORTIOS-LOG-016.verify: get log syslogd2 setting | grep status  -> status : enable (and server : <secondary-syslog-IP>). Confirm delivery via 'diagnose sniffer packet any "host <secondary-syslog-IP> and port <port>" 4' and events landing in the second collector.
FORTIOS-LOG-016.rollback: config log syslogd2 setting / set status disable / end. Breaks: log redundancy is removed; the pipeline returns to a single point of failure (primary syslog only).
FORTIOS-LOG-016.impact: Non-disruptive. No reboot, no session drops. Adds a second outbound syslog stream (roughly doubles log egress bandwidth to the collectors). No maintenance window. NOTE: not present in COMPLIANCE_MAP or REMEDIATION_COMMANDS - CLI authored here.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark, Section 6 Logging and Monitoring - 6.2.2 Centralized Logging and Reporting (resilient/redundant logging)
FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Multiple syslog servers (syslogd2/3/4)
NIST SP 800-53 AU-4 / AU-9 / CP-9, PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 10.5.3
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-LOG-017.risk: Logs are shipped to FortiAnalyzer/syslog over unencrypted UDP. Log records frequently contain usernames, source IPs, URLs, policy names and other sensitive detail an on-path eavesdropper can read; UDP also gives no delivery guarantee, so records can be silently lost - undermining both confidentiality and the integrity of the audit trail used for incident response.
steps: Check current transport: show log fortianalyzer setting (or show log syslogd setting) - look for reliable and enc-algorithm.
For FortiAnalyzer, enable reliable (OFTP/TCP) delivery and TLS encryption: config log fortianalyzer setting / set status enable / set reliable enable / set enc-algorithm high / end.
For syslog, use TLS transport: config log syslogd setting / set mode reliable / set transport tcp / end (terminate at a TLS-capable collector).
Ensure the FortiAnalyzer/collector is reachable on the encrypted port (TCP/514 for OFTP) through intervening firewalls.
Confirm logs are arriving on the collector after the change.
Re-run the scanner to confirm encrypted transport.
FORTIOS-LOG-017.gui: Log & Report > Log Settings (or Security Fabric > Fortinet Analyzer): configure FortiAnalyzer with reliable/encrypted transport; for syslog, configure a reliable/TLS server.
FORTIOS-LOG-017.cli:
config log fortianalyzer setting
  set status enable
  set reliable enable
  set enc-algorithm high
end
FORTIOS-LOG-017.verify: show log fortianalyzer setting  ->  set reliable enable and set enc-algorithm high; confirm logs appear on FortiAnalyzer and diagnose test application miglogd shows an active encrypted session.
FORTIOS-LOG-017.rollback: config log fortianalyzer setting / set enc-algorithm disable / set reliable disable / end (reverts to UDP). This re-exposes log data in cleartext; keep encryption enabled.
FORTIOS-LOG-017.impact: Minimal, no reboot. Switching to reliable/TLS may add slight CPU/latency and requires the collector to accept TCP/TLS; a brief gap in log delivery can occur during the transport switch. No effect on firewalling.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - encrypt log transport (CIS 6.x)
Fortinet Administration Guide - Logging to FortiAnalyzer: reliable delivery & encryption
CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
PCI-DSS 10.5, NIST SP 800-53 AU-9, SC-8

FORTIOS-LOG-018.risk: No automation stitches are configured, so the firewall detects security events but cannot respond to them automatically. Without automated response (quarantine a compromised host, alert on a critical IPS/AV event, ban an attacker IP), containment depends entirely on a human noticing a log entry - lengthening attacker dwell time in the crucial early minutes of an incident.
steps: Define a trigger for a high-value event: config system automation-trigger / edit critical-ips / set event-type ips-logs / next / end (or an IOC/EventFilter trigger).
Define an action: config system automation-action / edit notify-soc / set action-type email / set email-to soc@example.com / next / end (or quarantine-forticlient / webhook / cli-script / ban-ip).
Bind them into a stitch: config system automation-stitch / edit ips-alert / set trigger critical-ips / set action notify-soc / set status enable / next / end.
Add high-value stitches: alert on Critical IPS/AV detections, auto-ban brute-force sources, and notify on admin config changes.
Test with a benign event and confirm the action fires (Log & Report > Automation, or the action target).
Re-run the scanner to confirm stitches exist.
FORTIOS-LOG-018.gui: Security Fabric > Automation: create a Trigger (e.g. Compromised Host, IPS/AV event), an Action (Quarantine, Email, Webhook, CLI script), and a Stitch linking them; enable it.
FORTIOS-LOG-018.cli:
config system automation-trigger
  edit "critical-ips"
    set event-type ips-logs
  next
end
config system automation-action
  edit "notify-soc"
    set action-type email
    set email-to "soc@example.com"
  next
end
config system automation-stitch
  edit "ips-alert"
    set trigger "critical-ips"
    set action "notify-soc"
    set status enable
  next
end
FORTIOS-LOG-018.verify: show system automation-stitch  ->  at least one stitch with status enable linking a trigger and action; trigger a test event and confirm the action fires.
FORTIOS-LOG-018.rollback: config system automation-stitch / delete <name> / end (and remove the trigger/action). Removing stitches only disables automated response; it does not affect other functions.
FORTIOS-LOG-018.impact: Non-disruptive to configure, no reboot. Be deliberate with active actions (quarantine/ban-ip) - a false-positive trigger could quarantine a legitimate host; start with email/webhook alerts, validate, then enable enforcing actions.
references: Fortinet Administration Guide - Security Fabric Automation (triggers, actions, stitches)
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - monitoring & response
CWE-778: Insufficient Logging
NIST SP 800-53 IR-4, SI-4, AU-6

FORTIOS-NET-001.risk: With no DoS policy on Internet-facing interfaces, a volumetric or protocol-anomaly flood (SYN/UDP/ICMP flood, port scan, session sweep) from a botnet can exhaust the FortiGate session table, CPU, and memory, driving the device into conserve mode and dropping all traffic. An attacker can knock the SSL-VPN gateway and published VIP services offline and hide a follow-on intrusion in the noise. Business impact: full site/Internet outage, SLA breach, and loss of security telemetry during the flood.
steps: Baseline normal traffic first: run 'diagnose sys session stat' and 'get system performance status' to capture legitimate sessions/sec before setting thresholds.
Enter DoS policy config: config firewall DoS-policy.
Bind a policy to the WAN interface: edit 1 / set interface "wan1" / set srcaddr "all" / set dstaddr "all" / set service "ALL".

Enable L3/L4 anomaly sensors under 'config anomaly' (tcp_syn_flood, udp_flood, icmp_flood, tcp_port_scan, ip_dst_session), e.g. edit "tcp_syn_flood" / set status enable / set action proxy / set threshold 2000.
Use action 'proxy' for tcp_syn_flood (SYN-proxy) and 'block' for the others, and add 'set log enable' on each anomaly.
Exit with end (anomaly) then next / end to save the policy.
Repeat for every Internet- and DMZ-facing interface (wan2, VPN tunnel interfaces, etc.).

Monitor per-anomaly hit counters with 'diagnose ips anomaly list' (DoS anomalies are processed by the IPS engine) and review the policy via 'get firewall DoS-policy'; raise thresholds if legitimate high-rate traffic (backup, replication) is being dropped.
FORTIOS-NET-001.gui: FortiGate: Policy & Objects > DoS Policy > Create New (enable IPv4/IPv6 DoS Policy under System > Feature Visibility if hidden); set Incoming Interface, Source/Destination = all, Service = ALL, then enable the L3/L4 anomalies with per-anomaly Threshold and Action. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > DoS Policy (per-ADOM), then Install to the device.
FORTIOS-NET-001.cli:
config firewall DoS-policy
  edit 1
    set status enable
    set interface <wan-interface>
  next
end
FORTIOS-NET-001.verify: get firewall DoS-policy  -> at least one policy present with 'interface: wan1' and enabled anomalies; diagnose ips anomaly list  -> shows per-anomaly hit counters. Expected: non-empty DoS policy list (was empty before).
FORTIOS-NET-001.rollback: config firewall DoS-policy / delete 1 / end. Removing DoS policies restores prior behaviour (no anomaly dropping). Only roll back if a too-tight threshold is dropping legitimate high-rate apps; prefer raising the threshold instead.
FORTIOS-NET-001.impact: Non-disruptive to add when thresholds sit above the measured baseline. DoS policies are evaluated ahead of the firewall policy lookup, so an over-tight threshold can drop legitimate bursts. No reboot, no SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA or admin session drop. Recommend enabling with monitoring before tightening.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP FORTIOS-NET-001 -> CIS 9.1.1)
PCI-DSS v4.0 req 11.4; NIST SP 800-53 SC-5 (Denial-of-Service Protection)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'DoS policies' / 'DoS protection'
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' best-practices guide
CWE-400 (Uncontrolled Resource Consumption)

FORTIOS-NET-002.risk: A tcp_syn_flood anomaly threshold above 10,000 pps means SYN-proxy/blocking never engages until the flood is already very large, by which time the session table or a backend server may be exhausted. A moderate spoofed-SYN flood of a few thousand pps sails under the limit, exhausting half-open connections on published servers and denying service while evading the DoS sensor. Business impact: degraded or unavailable public services with no automated mitigation.
steps: Read the current threshold from the finding and compare against measured peak SYN rate: diagnose sys session stat.
config firewall DoS-policy / edit <policyid>.
config anomaly / edit "tcp_syn_flood".
set status enable.
set action proxy (engages the SYN proxy rather than merely logging).
set threshold 2000 (tune 2000-5000 to sit just above legitimate peak).
set log enable to record flood events.
Exit: end / next / end.
Review 'diagnose ips anomaly list' counters over a full business cycle and adjust.
FORTIOS-NET-002.gui: FortiGate: Policy & Objects > DoS Policy > edit the policy > tcp_syn_flood > set Threshold, Action = Proxy, Logging = enable. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > DoS Policy.
FORTIOS-NET-002.cli:
config firewall DoS-policy
    edit <policyid>
        config anomaly
            edit "tcp_syn_flood"
                set status enable
                set action proxy
                set threshold 2000
            next
        end
    next
end
FORTIOS-NET-002.verify: show firewall DoS-policy <policyid>  -> under anomaly tcp_syn_flood: 'threshold: 2000', 'action: proxy', 'status: enable'.
FORTIOS-NET-002.rollback: Raise the threshold back or 'set status disable' under the anomaly. Rollback reduces flood protection. Watch connection-heavy legitimate apps (load balancers, large NAT pools) that a too-low threshold could trip - symptom is new connections dropped and logged as an anomaly.
FORTIOS-NET-002.impact: Non-disruptive; the change is live per-packet. No reboot, no session/VPN/HA drop. A too-aggressive threshold can drop legitimate SYNs, so tune during a monitored window.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP FORTIOS-NET-002 -> CIS 9.1.2)
PCI-DSS v4.0 req 2.2.2; NIST SP 800-53 CM-7
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'DoS policies' (SYN flood / SYN proxy)
CWE-400 (Uncontrolled Resource Consumption)

FORTIOS-NET-003.risk: A udp_flood anomaly threshold set too high lets reflection/amplification floods (DNS, NTP, memcached, chargen) and random-port UDP storms saturate the WAN and consume the session table before the sensor triggers. Because UDP is connectionless and easily spoofed, this is cheap for attackers, and a link-saturating flood also drowns legitimate VoIP/DNS traffic. Business impact: WAN saturation, VoIP/DNS outage, and session-table exhaustion.
steps: Baseline UDP pps per destination via 'diagnose sys session stat' and FortiView UDP sessions.
config firewall DoS-policy / edit <policyid>.
config anomaly / edit "udp_flood".
set status enable.
set action block.
set threshold 2000 (tune 2000-5000, just above legitimate UDP peak).
set log enable.
Exit: end / next / end.
Verify per-anomaly counters with 'diagnose ips anomaly list' and re-tune after a full business cycle.
FORTIOS-NET-003.gui: FortiGate: Policy & Objects > DoS Policy > edit the policy > udp_flood > set Threshold, Action = Block, Logging = enable. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > DoS Policy.
FORTIOS-NET-003.cli:
config firewall DoS-policy
    edit <policyid>
        config anomaly
            edit "udp_flood"
                set status enable
                set action block
                set threshold 2000
            next
        end
    next
end
FORTIOS-NET-003.verify: show firewall DoS-policy <policyid>  -> under anomaly udp_flood: 'threshold: 2000', 'action: block', 'status: enable'.
FORTIOS-NET-003.rollback: Raise the threshold or 'set status disable'. Rollback reduces flood protection. Watch legitimate high-volume UDP (streaming, backup-over-UDP, RTP media) that a too-low threshold could clip.
FORTIOS-NET-003.impact: Non-disruptive; live per-packet. No reboot, no session/VPN/HA drop. Tune to avoid clipping legitimate UDP bursts. NOTE: the existing REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-NET-003'] value is a mislabeled 'config system ntp' block that does NOT match this UDP-flood finding; the correct DoS udp_flood CLI above was authored instead of reusing it.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP FORTIOS-NET-003 -> CIS 9.1.3)
PCI-DSS v4.0 req 2.2.2, 10.6; NIST SP 800-53 AU-8
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'DoS policies' (UDP flood)
CWE-400 (Uncontrolled Resource Consumption)

FORTIOS-NET-004.risk: An icmp_flood threshold above ~1,000 pps lets ping floods, smurf-style traffic, and ICMP-based reconnaissance proceed unthrottled, wasting CPU and bandwidth and leaving room for covert ICMP tunneling of data. Severity is lower because ICMP is easily rate-limited, but an unbounded threshold weakens defence-in-depth. Business impact: bandwidth/CPU waste and a potential covert exfiltration/recon channel.
steps: Baseline legitimate ICMP (monitoring pings, PMTUD) rate on the interface.
config firewall DoS-policy / edit <policyid>.
config anomaly / edit "icmp_flood".
set status enable.
set action block.
set threshold 250 (250-500 pps typical).
set log enable.
Exit: end / next / end and confirm monitoring pings still succeed (review counters with 'diagnose ips anomaly list').
FORTIOS-NET-004.gui: FortiGate: Policy & Objects > DoS Policy > edit the policy > icmp_flood > set Threshold, Action = Block. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > DoS Policy.
FORTIOS-NET-004.cli:
config firewall DoS-policy
    edit <policyid>
        config anomaly
            edit "icmp_flood"
                set status enable
                set action block
                set threshold 250
            next
        end
    next
end
FORTIOS-NET-004.verify: show firewall DoS-policy <policyid>  -> under anomaly icmp_flood: 'threshold: 250', 'action: block', 'status: enable'.
FORTIOS-NET-004.rollback: Raise the threshold or disable the anomaly. Watch NMS/latency probes that rely on high-rate ICMP - they may see drops if the threshold is set below their probe rate.
FORTIOS-NET-004.impact: Non-disruptive; live per-packet. No reboot, no session/VPN/HA drop.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - DoS/anomaly protection (same family as scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP FORTIOS-NET-001..003; NET-004 itself is not in the scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'DoS policies' (ICMP flood)
CWE-400 (Uncontrolled Resource Consumption)

FORTIOS-NET-005.risk: DHCP relay on an Internet-facing interface forwards DHCP (UDP 67/68) between the WAN and internal DHCP servers. An attacker on the WAN can send crafted DHCP requests to exhaust the internal pool (DHCP starvation) or attempt to inject rogue option data (e.g., malicious default-gateway/DNS via options 3/6), enabling man-in-the-middle of internal clients. Business impact: internal addressing disruption and redirection of clients to attacker infrastructure.
steps: Identify the WAN interface named in the finding.
Confirm DHCP relay is not legitimately required on that interface.
config system interface / edit "wan1".
set dhcp-relay-service disable.
If relay is genuinely needed for an internal segment, configure it on the correct internal interface instead of the WAN.
next / end.
Ensure no firewall policy permits DHCP from WAN to the internal DHCP server.
Confirm no downstream clients depended on relay via this interface.
FORTIOS-NET-005.gui: FortiGate: Network > Interfaces > edit the WAN interface > disable 'DHCP Relay' (Networked Devices / DHCP section). FortiManager: Device Manager > device > Network > Interfaces, or a Provisioning/CLI template.
FORTIOS-NET-005.cli:
config system interface
    edit "wan1"
        set dhcp-relay-service disable
    next
end
FORTIOS-NET-005.verify: show system interface wan1 | grep dhcp-relay  -> 'set dhcp-relay-service disable' (or the field absent, which also means relay is off); get system interface wan1  -> dhcp-relay-service: disable.
FORTIOS-NET-005.rollback: config system interface / edit "wan1" / set dhcp-relay-service enable / set dhcp-relay-ip "<server>" / end. Only re-enable if a documented downstream segment requires relay via this interface; nothing internal breaks by leaving it disabled on a WAN port.
FORTIOS-NET-005.impact: Non-disruptive on a true WAN interface (WAN should not be serving DHCP). No reboot, no session/VPN/HA drop. If the interface genuinely relayed for a connected client segment, those clients lose DHCP - validate before disabling.
references: Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'DHCP relay'
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' (disable unused services on WAN interfaces)
PCI-DSS v4.0 req 1.4 (network access controls); NIST SP 800-53 AC-4
CWE-284 (Improper Access Control)

FORTIOS-NET-006.risk: When the FortiGate resolves names using DNS servers inherited from the ISP (via DHCP/PPPoE) instead of a trusted, filtering resolver, lookups are neither validated nor filtered for malicious domains. An attacker controlling or spoofing the upstream resolver can steer FortiGuard/update/logging and user traffic to malicious IPs (DNS-based redirection). Business impact: exposure to DNS poisoning and loss of malicious-domain filtering. SEMANTIC CAVEAT: in FortiOS, 'set dns-server-override enable' actually means the interface MAY use DNS learned via DHCP/PPPoE (the ISP) to override the system DNS; 'disable' prevents ISP-learned DNS from overriding your configured resolvers. The scanner flags 'disable', so the robust fix is to pin trusted FortiGuard DNS at the system level plus a DNS Filter profile, and set the interface flag to match your design.
steps: Pin trusted system resolvers (FortiGuard DNS): config system dns / set primary 96.45.45.45 / set secondary 96.45.46.46 / end.
Apply a DNS Filter security profile (FortiGuard botnet/malicious-domain category blocking) to Internet-bound firewall policies.
Review current WAN DNS behaviour: show system interface wan1 | grep dns.

To satisfy this specific check (it flags dns-server-override=disable), set the interface flag per your design: config system interface / edit "wan1" / set dns-server-override enable / next / end - understanding this permits ISP-learned DNS to override; static FortiGuard DNS remains the authoritative control.
Optionally add encrypted DNS to the trusted resolver (see FORTIOS-NET-017).
Validate the resolution path: diagnose test application dnsproxy 3.
Confirm malicious-domain filtering with a test lookup against a known-blocked domain.
FORTIOS-NET-006.gui: FortiGate: Network > DNS (set FortiGuard primary/secondary DNS); Network > Interfaces > WAN > 'Override internal DNS' / DNS server override toggle; Security Profiles > DNS Filter applied via Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy. FortiManager: Provisioning Templates (System/DNS) and Policy & Objects (DNS Filter).
FORTIOS-NET-006.cli:
config system dns
    set primary 96.45.45.45
    set secondary 96.45.46.46
end
config system interface
    edit "wan1"
        set dns-server-override enable
    next
end
FORTIOS-NET-006.verify: get system interface wan1 | grep dns-server-override  -> 'dns-server-override: enable'; get system dns  -> primary/secondary set to the trusted FortiGuard resolvers.
FORTIOS-NET-006.rollback: config system interface / edit "wan1" / set dns-server-override disable / end. Reverting only changes whether ISP-learned DNS can override system DNS; the trusted static system DNS entries remain and continue to protect resolution.
FORTIOS-NET-006.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot, no session/VPN/HA drop. Changing resolvers briefly re-resolves cached names. (Low severity - a secondary/defence-in-depth control.)
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - DNS server configuration recommendation (configure a trusted/filtering resolver; NET-006 is not in the scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP, so no specific numbered CIS control is cited)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'DNS' and 'DNS Filter'
RFC 1034/1035 (DNS); RFC 7858 (DNS over TLS) for the encrypted-DNS companion control (see NET-017)
CWE-350 (Reliance on Reverse DNS Resolution for a Security-Sensitive Action)

FORTIOS-NET-007.risk: With src-check (unicast Reverse Path Forwarding / anti-spoofing) disabled on a WAN or DMZ interface, the FortiGate accepts packets whose source addresses are not routable back through the receiving interface. Attackers can spoof internal/trusted source IPs to bypass source-address firewall rules, launch reflection/amplification DDoS using your network as a source, or evade attribution. Business impact: source-based policy bypass, participation in DDoS reflection, and defeated attribution.
steps: Confirm the interface is not part of an intentional asymmetric-routing design (strict uRPF can break asymmetric paths).
config system interface / edit "wan1".
set src-check enable.
next / end.
Repeat for every WAN and DMZ interface flagged.
For multihomed/SD-WAN designs, validate routing symmetry first; if strict RPF would drop legitimate asymmetric return traffic, resolve routing before enabling.
Test with a spoofed-source packet and confirm the drop via 'diagnose debug flow' or session logs.
FORTIOS-NET-007.gui: src-check is CLI-only in most FortiOS builds (not a standard GUI interface toggle). Use Dashboard > CLI Console (or configure Network > Interfaces via CLI). FortiManager: CLI Template / Provisioning Template applied to the device.
FORTIOS-NET-007.cli:
config system interface
    edit "wan1"
        set src-check enable
    next
end
FORTIOS-NET-007.verify: show system interface wan1 | grep src-check  -> 'set src-check enable'; get system interface wan1  -> src-check: enable.
FORTIOS-NET-007.rollback: config system interface / edit "wan1" / set src-check disable / end. Only disable if a documented asymmetric-routing requirement exists (traffic in one interface, out another); otherwise legitimate return paths may be dropped.
FORTIOS-NET-007.impact: Potentially disruptive in asymmetric-routing / multihoming / SD-WAN scenarios where return traffic legitimately arrives on a different interface - strict RPF can drop it. Non-disruptive otherwise. No reboot, no session/VPN/HA drop. Test in a maintenance window if asymmetric routing is possible.
references: BCP 38 / RFC 2827 (Network Ingress Filtering); RFC 3704 / BCP 84 (Ingress Filtering for Multihomed Networks)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'Reverse path forwarding' / interface 'src-check'
NIST SP 800-53 SC-7 (Boundary Protection); PCI-DSS v4.0 req 1.4
CWE-290 (Authentication Bypass by Spoofing)

FORTIOS-NET-008.risk: With tcp-session-without-syn enabled, the FortiGate builds state for TCP flows for which it never observed the three-way-handshake SYN (mid-stream pickup). An attacker can inject packets into, or cause creation of, TCP sessions that bypass stateful inspection and evade some IPS/policy checks, undermining the stateful security model. Business impact: firewall state created by unsolicited packets, weakening inspection and aiding evasion.
steps: Confirm there is no legitimate need for mid-session pickup (rarely required outside specific asymmetric / external-load-balancer designs).
If VDOMs are enabled, enter the VDOM: config vdom / edit root, then config system settings. If VDOMs are disabled: config system settings.
set tcp-session-without-syn disable.
end (and 'end' again to exit config vdom if used).
If a single policy legitimately needs mid-stream pickup, scope it per-policy with 'set tcp-session-without-syn' inside that firewall policy instead of globally.
Monitor for any application that relied on mid-stream pickup after the change.
FORTIOS-NET-008.gui: CLI-only (config system settings, per-VDOM). Use Dashboard > CLI Console. FortiManager: CLI Template / Provisioning Template.
FORTIOS-NET-008.cli:
config system settings
    set tcp-session-without-syn disable
end
FORTIOS-NET-008.verify: get system settings | grep tcp-session-without-syn  -> 'tcp-session-without-syn: disable'. (Per-VDOM; run inside the VDOM if VDOMs are enabled.)
FORTIOS-NET-008.rollback: config system settings / set tcp-session-without-syn enable / end. Only needed for documented asymmetric-routing or external-load-balanced designs; watch for flows that legitimately start mid-stream being dropped after tightening.
FORTIOS-NET-008.impact: Can be disruptive in asymmetric-routing or external-load-balancer designs where the FortiGate may first see a non-SYN packet of an existing flow - those flows are dropped after disabling. Non-disruptive in standard inline deployments. No reboot; existing sessions are unaffected, only new non-SYN flows. NOTE: the scanner recommendation string says 'config system global', but the correct location in FortiOS is 'config system settings' (per-VDOM).
references: Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'TCP session settings' / 'Asymmetric routing and FortiGate'
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' (stateful inspection integrity)
NIST SP 800-53 SC-7; CWE-284 (Improper Access Control)

FORTIOS-NET-009.risk: If IPv6 is enabled (feature visibility/addressing) but no IPv6 firewall policy set exists, IPv6 becomes a blind spot. Although FortiOS applies an implicit IPv6 deny, the absence of explicit, logged IPv6 policies means IPv6-capable hosts can establish link-local/SLAAC connectivity that is neither inspected nor logged, and any later-added permit policy would lack UTM. Attackers routinely use IPv6 as an unmonitored path to bypass IPv4-only controls. Business impact: an uninspected, unlogged protocol path and reduced visibility.
steps: Decide whether IPv6 is required. If not, disable IPv6 addressing to shrink attack surface.

If required, create explicit IPv6 policies with the same segmentation and UTM as IPv4: config firewall policy6 / edit 1 / set srcintf/dstintf/srcaddr/dstaddr/service/schedule/action, add 'set logtraffic all' and UTM profiles (av/ips/webfilter).
Add a final explicit deny-with-logging IPv6 policy for visibility.
Remove IPv6 addresses from interfaces that do not need them.
If IPv6 is entirely unused, hide it: config system global / set gui-ipv6 disable / end (note: this only hides GUI options; it does not by itself stop IPv6 traffic).
Verify policy hit counts and that IPv6 traffic is now logged.
FORTIOS-NET-009.gui: FortiGate: enable IPv6 under System > Feature Visibility, then Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > create IPv6 policies with security profiles and logging; or disable IPv6 visibility if unused. FortiManager: Policy & Objects (IPv6 policy package), then install.
FORTIOS-NET-009.cli:
config firewall policy6
    edit 1
        set name "IPv6-Internal-to-WAN"
        set srcintf "internal"
        set dstintf "wan1"
        set srcaddr "all"
        set dstaddr "all"
        set action accept
        set schedule "always"
        set service "ALL"
        set logtraffic all
    next
end
FORTIOS-NET-009.verify: show firewall policy6  -> at least one policy present; get firewall policy6  -> non-empty. If disabling instead: get system global | grep gui-ipv6  -> gui-ipv6: disable.
FORTIOS-NET-009.rollback: config firewall policy6 / delete 1 / end removes the added policy (implicit IPv6 deny resumes). Disabling gui-ipv6 only hides GUI options and does not remove interface IPv6 addresses.
FORTIOS-NET-009.impact: Non-disruptive to add permit policies matching current needs; adding only a deny-all could block IPv6 apps - inventory IPv6 usage first. No reboot. Disabling gui-ipv6 is cosmetic (GUI visibility) and does not stop IPv6 traffic on its own.
references: Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'IPv6 policies' and 'Feature visibility'
NIST SP 800-119 (Guidelines for the Secure Deployment of IPv6); NIST SP 800-53 SC-7
PCI-DSS v4.0 req 1.2/1.4 (network controls apply to all protocols)
CWE-284 (Improper Access Control)

FORTIOS-NET-010.risk: With LLDP reception enabled globally, the FortiGate ingests Link Layer Discovery Protocol advertisements on all interfaces, including untrusted ones. An attacker on an adjacent segment can learn topology (neighbor device/port IDs, management addresses, VLANs) to map the network, and can craft LLDP frames to influence discovery. Business impact: information disclosure that aids lateral-movement planning. (Low severity - requires local link adjacency.)
steps: Determine where LLDP is genuinely needed (typically only internal switch-facing / FortiLink ports).
Disable global reception: config system global / set lldp-reception disable / end.
Optionally disable global transmission too: set lldp-transmission disable (global), then selectively re-enable per trusted interface.
Re-enable LLDP only on trusted interfaces: config system interface / edit <trusted-iface> / set lldp-reception enable (and lldp-transmission as needed) / next / end.
Keep LLDP enabled on FortiLink ports so FortiSwitch/FortiAP discovery is unaffected.
Confirm WAN/DMZ interfaces no longer process LLDP.
FORTIOS-NET-010.gui: Global LLDP is CLI-managed (config system global). Per-interface LLDP appears under Network > Interfaces in some builds. Use Dashboard > CLI Console. FortiManager: CLI / Provisioning Template.
FORTIOS-NET-010.cli:
config system global
    set lldp-reception disable
end
FORTIOS-NET-010.verify: get system global | grep lldp-reception  -> 'lldp-reception: disable'.
FORTIOS-NET-010.rollback: config system global / set lldp-reception enable / end. Watch FortiLink/FortiSwitch/topology-discovery features that rely on LLDP - retain LLDP on those internal interfaces via a per-interface override.
FORTIOS-NET-010.impact: Non-disruptive to routed/forwarded traffic; no reboot, no session/VPN/HA drop. May reduce Security Fabric physical-topology detail and FortiSwitch discovery on interfaces where LLDP is turned off - keep it on FortiLink ports.
references: IEEE 802.1AB (LLDP)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'LLDP' (global and per-interface)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' (disable unneeded discovery protocols)
CWE-200 (Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor)

FORTIOS-NET-011.risk: A BGP neighbor without TCP MD5 (or later) authentication can be spoofed. An attacker able to reach the BGP TCP session (or positioned on-path) can reset the session (TCP RST attack) or, worse, establish a rogue peering to inject bogus routes - hijacking or blackholing prefixes and redirecting traffic through attacker infrastructure for interception. Business impact: route hijack/blackhole, traffic interception, and WAN outage.
steps: Coordinate the shared MD5 key with the peer administrator - it must match on both ends.
config router bgp.
config neighbor / edit "<neighbor-ip>".
set password <strong-shared-secret>.
end / end.
Have the peer apply the identical key in the same maintenance window (the session resets when auth is applied).
Add further hardening: prefix-lists/route-maps to constrain accepted prefixes, TTL security / GTSM where supported, and max-prefix limits.
Confirm the session re-establishes with authentication.
FORTIOS-NET-011.gui: FortiGate: Network > BGP supports neighbor settings in newer builds, but the MD5 password is typically set via the CLI Console. FortiManager: Device Manager routing / CLI / Provisioning Template.
FORTIOS-NET-011.cli:
config router bgp
    config neighbor
        edit "<neighbor-ip>"
            set password <strong-shared-secret>
        next
    end
end
FORTIOS-NET-011.verify: get router info bgp neighbors <neighbor-ip>  -> session Established with MD5/authentication in effect; show router bgp  -> the neighbor 'password' field is set (stored encrypted).
FORTIOS-NET-011.rollback: config router bgp / config neighbor / edit "<neighbor-ip>" / unset password / end / end. Removing auth drops protection and resets the session again; the peer must also remove its key or the session stays down on mismatch.
FORTIOS-NET-011.impact: Disruptive to that BGP session at change time - applying or removing an MD5 key resets the TCP session and causes brief route reconvergence/traffic loss for prefixes learned via that peer. Schedule a maintenance window and coordinate both ends. No device reboot; other sessions unaffected.
references: RFC 2385 (TCP MD5 Signature Option); RFC 7454 / BCP 194 (BGP Operations and Security)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'BGP' (neighbor authentication / password)
NIST SP 800-53 SC-7, IA-3; CWE-306 (Missing Authentication for Critical Function)

FORTIOS-NET-012.risk: An OSPF interface without authentication accepts Hello/LSA packets from any adjacent device. An attacker on the segment can form a rogue adjacency and inject false LSAs to reroute or blackhole traffic, or trigger continuous SPF recomputation (DoS). Because OSPF trusts unauthenticated neighbors on the link, this enables man-in-the-middle and routing disruption. Business impact: internal traffic redirection/interception and routing instability.
steps: Choose the auth type - prefer cryptographic md5 over cleartext 'text' auth (FortiOS ospf-interface authentication options are none, text, or md5).
config router ospf / config ospf-interface / edit "<name>".
set authentication md5.
config md5-keys / edit 1 / set key-string <shared-secret> / next / end.
next / end / end.
Apply the identical key and type on every router on that segment (a mismatch drops the adjacency).
For area-wide consistency, also consider configuring area authentication.
Verify the adjacency re-forms with authentication.
FORTIOS-NET-012.gui: FortiGate: Network > OSPF (interface list) in newer builds; the MD5 key is usually configured via the CLI Console. FortiManager: CLI / Provisioning Template.
FORTIOS-NET-012.cli:
config router ospf
    config ospf-interface
        edit "<name>"
            set authentication md5
            config md5-keys
                edit 1
                    set key-string <shared-secret>
                next
            end
        next
    end
end
FORTIOS-NET-012.verify: get router info ospf interface <iface>  -> shows 'Message digest authentication enabled' (or the configured auth type); show router ospf  -> the interface shows 'set authentication md5' with md5-keys.
FORTIOS-NET-012.rollback: config router ospf / config ospf-interface / edit "<name>" / set authentication none / end / end. Reverting removes neighbor authentication and flaps the adjacency; all routers on the link must use matching settings.
FORTIOS-NET-012.impact: Disruptive to OSPF adjacencies on the affected interface at change time - the adjacency drops and re-forms, causing brief reconvergence. Coordinate all neighbors and use a maintenance window. No device reboot.
references: RFC 2328 (OSPFv2, authentication); RFC 5709 (OSPFv2 HMAC-SHA Cryptographic Authentication)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'OSPF' (interface authentication / md5-keys)
NIST SP 800-53 SC-7, IA-3; CWE-306 (Missing Authentication for Critical Function)

FORTIOS-NET-013.risk: A default SNMP community string ('public'/'private') is world-known. Any attacker who can reach UDP 161 can read the full device configuration, interface/routing/ARP tables, and system inventory via SNMP GET/WALK - and with a writable 'private' community potentially SET/modify - providing complete reconnaissance and possibly write access. Business impact: full configuration disclosure and a foothold for targeted attack; critical if SNMP is reachable from untrusted networks.
steps: Identify the offending community by ID from the finding.
Preferred: migrate to SNMPv3 (see FORTIOS-NET-015) and delete v1/v2c communities. To immediately remediate the default string: config system snmp community / edit <id>.
set name "<unique-non-default-string>" (long, random).
Restrict querying hosts: config hosts / edit 1 / set ip <mgmt-host>/32 / next / end.
Disable SNMPv1: set query-v1-status disable / set trap-v1-status disable (see FORTIOS-NET-014).
next / end.
Ensure SNMP is not permitted on WAN - remove 'snmp' from the interface allowaccess on untrusted interfaces.
Verify no default community remains and access is source-restricted.
FORTIOS-NET-013.gui: FortiGate: System > SNMP > SNMP v1/v2c Communities > edit/delete the community, set Hosts (allowed source), disable unused versions; prefer creating an SNMP v3 user. FortiManager: Device Manager > System > SNMP or CLI / Provisioning Template.
FORTIOS-NET-013.cli:
config system snmp community
    edit <id>
        set name "<unique-non-default-string>"
        config hosts
            edit 1
                set ip <mgmt-host>/32
            next
        end
        set query-v1-status disable
        set trap-v1-status disable
    next
end
FORTIOS-NET-013.verify: show system snmp community  -> no community named 'public' or 'private'; get system snmp community  -> new name with restricted hosts. From an untrusted host, 'diagnose sniffer packet any "udp port 161" 4' should show no successful SNMP response.
FORTIOS-NET-013.rollback: config system snmp community / edit <id> / set name "public" / end (NOT recommended). Reverting reintroduces the default string. If the NMS breaks after the change, update the NMS with the new community/host rather than rolling back.
FORTIOS-NET-013.impact: Low disruption if the NMS is updated in tandem; polls using the old community fail until the NMS is reconfigured. No reboot, no session/VPN/HA drop. Coordinate with the monitoring team.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - SNMP (delete default community / SNMPv3-only recommendation)
RFC 3410-3418 (SNMPv3 framework)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'SNMP'
CWE-1188 (Insecure Default Initialization of Resource); CWE-798 (Use of Hard-coded Credentials)

FORTIOS-NET-014.risk: SNMPv1 sends the community string and all polled data in cleartext with no real authentication. An attacker able to sniff the management path (or positioned on-path) captures the community and reads device data, or spoofs SNMP responses. Business impact: credential/configuration disclosure over the wire and susceptibility to spoofed management data.
steps: Confirm the NMS supports SNMPv3 (virtually all modern tools do).
config system snmp community / edit <id>.
set query-v1-status disable.
set trap-v1-status disable.
If SNMPv3 is deployed, also disable v2c: set query-v2c-status disable / set trap-v2c-status disable.
next / end.
Create an SNMPv3 auth-priv user (see FORTIOS-NET-015) and point the NMS at it.
Verify v1 is off and polling now uses v3.
FORTIOS-NET-014.gui: FortiGate: System > SNMP > edit the community > disable SNMP v1 queries/traps; create an SNMP v3 user. FortiManager: Device Manager > System > SNMP / CLI Template.
FORTIOS-NET-014.cli:
config system snmp community
    edit <id>
        set query-v1-status disable
        set trap-v1-status disable
    next
end
FORTIOS-NET-014.verify: show system snmp community <id>  -> 'set query-v1-status disable' and 'set trap-v1-status disable'; get system snmp community  -> query-v1-status: disable.
FORTIOS-NET-014.rollback: config system snmp community / edit <id> / set query-v1-status enable / end. Re-enabling exposes cleartext SNMPv1. If the NMS lost visibility, migrate it to SNMPv3 instead of rolling back.
FORTIOS-NET-014.impact: Low disruption provided the NMS is moved to SNMPv3/v2c beforehand; any tool still polling v1 loses data until reconfigured. No reboot, no session/VPN/HA drop.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - SNMP ('only SNMPv3 enabled')
RFC 3414 (SNMPv3 User-based Security Model)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'SNMP'
CWE-319 (Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information)

FORTIOS-NET-015.risk: An SNMPv3 user configured at no-auth-no-priv provides SNMPv3 transport but none of its security - queries are unauthenticated and unencrypted, equivalent to SNMPv1/v2c exposure. An attacker reaching UDP 161 can read device data without credentials and eavesdrop on responses. Business impact: configuration/inventory disclosure despite nominally 'using SNMPv3'.
steps: config system snmp user / edit "<name>".
set security-level auth-priv.
set auth-proto sha256 (avoid md5/sha1).
set auth-pwd <strong-auth-password>.
set priv-proto aes256.
set priv-pwd <strong-priv-password>.
Restrict source/notify hosts and ensure SNMP is not in the WAN interface allowaccess.
next / end; load the auth/priv credentials into the NMS.
Verify the user is auth-priv and polling works encrypted.
FORTIOS-NET-015.gui: FortiGate: System > SNMP > SNMP v3 > edit/create user > Security Level = Authentication and Private (authPriv), Authentication = SHA256, Privacy = AES256, set passwords and Hosts. FortiManager: Device Manager > System > SNMP / CLI Template.
FORTIOS-NET-015.cli:
config system snmp user
    edit "<name>"
        set security-level auth-priv
        set auth-proto sha256
        set auth-pwd <strong-auth-password>
        set priv-proto aes256
        set priv-pwd <strong-priv-password>
    next
end
FORTIOS-NET-015.verify: show system snmp user <name>  -> 'set security-level auth-priv', 'set auth-proto sha256', 'set priv-proto aes256'; get system snmp user  -> security-level: auth-priv.
FORTIOS-NET-015.rollback: config system snmp user / edit "<name>" / set security-level no-auth-no-priv / end. Reverting removes SNMP authentication/encryption. If the NMS fails to poll, load the auth/priv keys into the NMS rather than downgrading.
FORTIOS-NET-015.impact: Low disruption once the NMS holds the auth/priv keys; polling fails until the NMS is updated. No reboot, no session/VPN/HA drop.
references: RFC 3414 (SNMPv3 USM); RFC 3826 (AES Cipher in the SNMP USM)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'SNMP' (SNMPv3 users, auth-priv)
NIST SP 800-53 IA-3, SC-8; CWE-306 (Missing Authentication for Critical Function)

FORTIOS-NET-016.risk: Without NTP authentication, an on-path or spoofing attacker can inject false time via forged NTP responses. Skewed clocks break log time-correlation and SIEM ordering, can invalidate or wrongly extend certificate validity windows, disrupt time-based OTP/MFA and Kerberos, and interfere with scheduled policies. Business impact: corrupted forensic timeline, TLS/certificate failures, and authentication breakage.
steps: Use trusted internal NTP sources where possible and obtain the shared key from the NTP server admin.
config system ntp / set type custom.
config ntpserver / edit 1 / set server "<ntp-server-ip>".
set authentication enable.
set key-type SHA256 (avoid MD5).
set key <shared-secret> and set key-id <id> (matching the server).
next / end / end.
Repeat for each configured NTP server so reachable ones are authenticated.
Verify time sync and that authentication is in effect.
FORTIOS-NET-016.gui: FortiGate: System > Settings > System Time > NTP - select 'Specify' (custom server); the NTP authentication key/key-type/key-id are typically set via the CLI Console. FortiManager: Provisioning Template (System) / CLI Template.
FORTIOS-NET-016.cli:
config system ntp
    set type custom
    config ntpserver
        edit 1
            set server "<ntp-server-ip>"
            set authentication enable
            set key-type SHA256
            set key <shared-secret>
            set key-id 1
        next
    end
end
FORTIOS-NET-016.verify: diagnose sys ntp status  -> server reachable and synchronized; show system ntp  -> per-server 'set authentication enable' and 'set key-type SHA256'.
FORTIOS-NET-016.rollback: config system ntp / config ntpserver / edit 1 / set authentication disable / end / end. Reverting removes NTP auth; time sync continues but becomes spoofable. If sync breaks after enabling (key mismatch), correct the key/key-id/key-type to match the server rather than disabling.
FORTIOS-NET-016.impact: Low disruption; a key/key-id mismatch with the server stops sync for that server (it falls back to other sources) - validate the key. No reboot, no session/VPN/HA drop, though a large one-time time correction can momentarily affect log timestamps and certificate checks.
references: RFC 5905 (NTPv4, symmetric-key authentication)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'Configuring NTP'
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - system time / NTP configuration
CWE-345 (Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity)

FORTIOS-NET-017.risk: With the system DNS protocol set to cleartext (UDP 53), all FortiGate name resolution - including FortiGuard/update lookups and, where the FortiGate is the resolver for clients, user lookups - is exposed to passive eavesdropping and active on-path manipulation (DNS spoofing/poisoning), letting an attacker redirect traffic to malicious hosts. Business impact: DNS-based redirection/MITM and disclosure of internal lookup patterns.
steps: Choose an encrypted-DNS resolver (FortiGuard supports DoT) and confirm outbound TCP 853 (DoT) is permitted through any upstream firewall.
config system dns.
set protocol dot (DNS over TLS; use 'doh' for DNS over HTTPS where supported).
set primary 96.45.45.45 / set secondary 96.45.46.46 (FortiGuard resolvers).
set server-hostname "globalsdns.fortinet.net" (for DoT certificate/SNI validation).
end.
Optionally pair with a DNS Filter security profile for malicious-domain blocking.
Verify resolution succeeds over the encrypted channel.
FORTIOS-NET-017.gui: FortiGate: Network > DNS > set DNS Protocol to 'DNS over TLS' (or 'DNS over HTTPS'), set primary/secondary and the Server hostname. FortiManager: Provisioning Template (DNS) / CLI Template.
FORTIOS-NET-017.cli:
config system dns
    set protocol dot
    set primary 96.45.45.45
    set secondary 96.45.46.46
    set server-hostname "globalsdns.fortinet.net"
end
FORTIOS-NET-017.verify: get system dns | grep protocol  -> 'protocol: dot'; diagnose test application dnsproxy 3  -> shows the DoT servers in use and successful resolution.
FORTIOS-NET-017.rollback: config system dns / set protocol cleartext / end. Reverting returns to plaintext DNS. If TCP 853 is blocked upstream or the server-hostname/cert cannot be validated, resolution may fail - fix connectivity/cert rather than reverting where possible.
FORTIOS-NET-017.impact: Non-disruptive if outbound DoT (TCP 853) is allowed and the resolver is reachable; if 853 is blocked or the resolver is unreachable, name resolution can fail - test first. No reboot; brief re-resolution of cached names.
references: RFC 7858 (DNS over TLS); RFC 8484 (DNS over HTTPS)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'DNS over TLS / HTTPS'
