CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - DNS server configuration recommendation (encrypted-DNS / trusted resolver; NET-017 is not in the scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP, so no specific numbered CIS control is cited)
CWE-319 (Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information)

FORTIOS-NET-018.risk: Same core exposure as FORTIOS-NET-007: source IP validation (src-check / uRPF anti-spoofing) disabled on a WAN or DMZ interface lets spoofed-source packets traverse the firewall, enabling source-based policy bypass, reflection/amplification abuse, and attribution evasion. This finding is raised per external interface during the interface anti-spoofing pass. Business impact: spoofed-traffic acceptance, defeated source-address controls, and potential participation in DDoS reflection.
steps: Confirm the interface is not part of an intentional asymmetric-routing design.
config system interface / edit "<interface>".
set src-check enable.
next / end.
Apply to every WAN/DMZ interface named in findings.
For SD-WAN/multihomed setups, validate routing symmetry before enabling to avoid dropping legitimate return traffic.
Test with a spoofed-source packet and confirm it is dropped (diagnose debug flow).
FORTIOS-NET-018.gui: src-check is CLI-only in most FortiOS builds; use Dashboard > CLI Console (or configure Network > Interfaces via CLI). FortiManager: CLI / Provisioning Template.
FORTIOS-NET-018.cli:
config system interface
    edit "<interface>"
        set src-check enable
    next
end
FORTIOS-NET-018.verify: show system interface <interface> | grep src-check  -> 'set src-check enable'; get system interface <interface>  -> src-check: enable.
FORTIOS-NET-018.rollback: config system interface / edit "<interface>" / set src-check disable / end. Only revert for a documented asymmetric-routing need; disabling re-permits spoofed packets.
FORTIOS-NET-018.impact: Potentially disruptive with asymmetric routing / multihoming / SD-WAN where return traffic arrives on a different interface - strict RPF can drop it. Otherwise non-disruptive. No reboot, no session/VPN/HA drop. Test in a maintenance window if asymmetric paths are possible. NOTE: this control overlaps FORTIOS-NET-007 (same src-check setting, raised by a different scan pass).
references: BCP 38 / RFC 2827 (Network Ingress Filtering); RFC 3704 / BCP 84 (Multihomed Ingress Filtering)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'Reverse path forwarding' / interface 'src-check'
NIST SP 800-53 SC-7; PCI-DSS v4.0 req 1.4
CWE-290 (Authentication Bypass by Spoofing)

FORTIOS-OBJECT-001.risk: Address objects defined but not referenced by any policy or used address group are orphaned clutter. They lengthen audits, obscure which objects are actually enforced, and are a hazard because a future rule author may reuse a stale, over-broad object by mistake.
steps: Review the listed unused address objects.

Confirm each is not referenced elsewhere (VPN phase2 selectors, routing, VIPs, proxy) before deleting - the scanner only checks firewall policies and address groups: diagnose sys cmdb refcnt <object> or GUI 'Where Used'.
Delete confirmed-orphan objects: config firewall address / delete <name> / end.
Adopt a naming convention and periodic object review to prevent re-accumulation.
FORTIOS-OBJECT-001.gui: Policy & Objects > Addresses: use the right-click 'Where Used' / reference count; delete objects with zero references.
FORTIOS-OBJECT-001.verify: diagnose sys cmdb refcnt <object>  ->  reference count 0 before deletion; get firewall address | grep <name>  ->  gone afterwards.
FORTIOS-OBJECT-001.rollback: Re-create the object from your config backup. Deleting an object that IS referenced elsewhere will fail or break that feature - always check references first.
FORTIOS-OBJECT-001.impact: Non-disruptive when the object is genuinely unreferenced (FortiOS blocks deletion of referenced objects). No reboot.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - object hygiene
Fortinet Administration Guide - 'Where Used' / cmdb refcnt
NIST SP 800-53 CM-7, PCI-DSS 1.1.7
CWE-1164: Irrelevant Code

FORTIOS-OBJECT-002.risk: Custom service objects defined but not referenced by any policy or service group are orphaned clutter that reduces audit clarity and risks a stale, over-broad service being reused in a new rule.
steps: Review the listed unused custom services.
Confirm each is not referenced by a service group or policy: GUI 'Where Used' or diagnose sys cmdb refcnt.
Delete confirmed-orphan services: config firewall service custom / delete <name> / end.
Standardise on the built-in services where possible and review custom services periodically.
FORTIOS-OBJECT-002.gui: Policy & Objects > Services: check reference count / 'Where Used', delete unreferenced custom services.
FORTIOS-OBJECT-002.cli:
config firewall service custom
  delete <service-name>
end
FORTIOS-OBJECT-002.verify: diagnose sys cmdb refcnt <service>  ->  0; the object no longer appears in show firewall service custom.
FORTIOS-OBJECT-002.rollback: Re-create from your config backup. FortiOS prevents deletion of a referenced service, so accidental breakage is unlikely.
FORTIOS-OBJECT-002.impact: Non-disruptive for genuinely-unreferenced services; no reboot.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - object hygiene
Fortinet Administration Guide - service objects / cmdb refcnt
NIST SP 800-53 CM-7, PCI-DSS 1.1.7
CWE-1164: Irrelevant Code

FORTIOS-OBJECT-003.risk: Security profiles (AntiVirus, IPS, Web Filter, Application Control, DNS Filter, DLP) that are defined but applied to no firewall policy provide zero protection and obscure which controls are actually enforced. Teams often believe a profile is protecting traffic when in fact it is attached to nothing.
steps: Review the listed unapplied profiles by type.
For each, decide: apply it to the relevant allow policies, or delete it if superseded.
Apply a profile: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set ips-sensor <name> (etc.) / next / end.
Ensure every internet-facing allow policy carries AV, IPS, Web Filter, App Control and SSL inspection.
Delete obsolete profiles once nothing references them.
Re-run the scanner to confirm coverage improved.
FORTIOS-OBJECT-003.gui: Security Profiles > (AntiVirus / IPS / Web Filter / Application Control / DNS Filter / DLP): confirm each profile is applied to a policy under Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > Security Profiles; delete unused ones.
FORTIOS-OBJECT-003.cli:
config firewall policy
  edit <policyid>
    set utm-status enable
    set av-profile <name>
    set ips-sensor <name>
    set webfilter-profile <name>
    set application-list <name>
    set ssl-ssh-profile deep-inspection
  next
end
FORTIOS-OBJECT-003.verify: show firewall policy | grep -E 'av-profile|ips-sensor|webfilter-profile'  ->  the profile now appears on the intended policies; the scanner no longer lists it as unused.
FORTIOS-OBJECT-003.rollback: Remove the profile binding from the policy (unset av-profile, etc.) or re-create a deleted profile from backup.
FORTIOS-OBJECT-003.impact: Applying UTM inspection adds CPU and may block previously-allowed malicious/undesired content (the intended effect); test on a pilot policy first, especially SSL deep inspection which requires the CA cert to be trusted by clients. No reboot.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - security profiles applied to policies (section 4.2)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' - apply UTM to all policies
NIST SP 800-53 SI-3 & SI-4, PCI-DSS 5.x / 11.x
CWE-693: Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-POLICY-001.risk: Disabled firewall policies accumulate as configuration debt. An operator troubleshooting an outage, or an attacker who has gained admin/write access, can re-enable a stale 'permit any' or legacy rule in one click and instantly re-open a path that was intentionally closed, with no change-control trail. Orphaned rules also hide shadowed/duplicate logic that leads to mis-ordered policy evaluation and unexpected allows.
steps: Inventory disabled rules: run `show firewall policy | grep -f "set status disable"` (the `-f` flag prints the full config block for each match) to list every disabled policy with its src/dst/service.
For each disabled policy, decide keep-with-justification or delete. Cross-check against the change ticket that disabled it.
If the rule is obsolete, delete it: `config firewall policy` then `delete <policy-id>` then `end`.

If it must be retained (seasonal/DR rule), document why using the comments field: `config firewall policy` / `edit <policy-id>` / `set comments "Disabled 2026-07-07 per CHG-1234, DR-only"` / `next` / `end`.
Confirm the deletion did not break policy ordering by reviewing `show firewall policy` and checking that intended traffic still matches an active allow rule.
Export the running config as a rollback point before mass cleanup: admin account menu (top-right) > Configuration > Backup (or `execute backup config <dest>`).
Commit the change and record the policy IDs removed in the change record.
Re-run the scanner to confirm FORTIOS-POLICY-001 no longer fires for cleaned rules.
FORTIOS-POLICY-001.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy — filter the 'Status' column for Disabled, then right-click > Delete (or Edit > add a comment). FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Policy Packages > <package> > IPv4/Consolidated Policy, filter by Status, then Delete and Install.
FORTIOS-POLICY-001.cli:
config firewall policy
    delete <policy-id>
end

# To retain but document instead of delete:
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set comments "Disabled 2026-07-07 per CHG-1234 - retained for DR"
    next
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-001.verify: show firewall policy | grep -f "set status disable"  -> returns no output once all disabled rules are removed (a documented, retained rule will still appear but now carries a 'set comments' line). Alternatively re-run the scanner and confirm 0 FORTIOS-POLICY-001 findings.
FORTIOS-POLICY-001.rollback: Restore the pre-change config backup (admin account menu (top-right) > Configuration > Restore, or `execute restore config <src>`), or manually re-create the deleted policy from the exported config block. Watch for policy-ID reuse: a re-created rule may land at the wrong position in the sequence and shadow/bypass other rules, so re-verify ordering with `show firewall policy`.
FORTIOS-POLICY-001.impact: Non-disruptive. Deleting an already-disabled policy has no effect on live traffic (the rule was not being evaluated). No reboot, no session drop, no VPN/HA impact. No maintenance window required.
references: Scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 4.1.1, PCI-DSS 1.2.1, NIST 800-53 AC-4, SOC 2 CC6.6
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Policy and Objects': Ensure that unused policies are reviewed regularly
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Firewall > Firewall policies (managing and ordering policies)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' recommended practices (policy hygiene / least privilege)
CWE-1078: Inappropriate Source Code Style or Formatting (per scanner: latent/unused configuration)

FORTIOS-POLICY-002.risk: An any-source-to-any-destination allow policy collapses network segmentation into a flat network. A single compromised host (phished workstation, exploited server, rogue IoT device) can then reach every subnet, pivot to domain controllers, databases and OT segments, and move laterally with no firewall enforcement between zones. This is a CRITICAL finding: it maps directly to MITRE ATT&CK T1190/T1021 and typically turns a contained breach into an enterprise-wide compromise, and it is a direct PCI-DSS segmentation failure that can invalidate cardholder-data scope reduction.
steps: Identify the flows the any-any rule was actually carrying before narrowing it: enable full logging first (`set logtraffic all`) and review Log & Report > Forward Traffic for the real source, destination and service tuples over a representative window.
Create explicit address objects for the legitimate endpoints: `config firewall address` / `edit "NET_APP_TIER"` / `set subnet 10.20.0.0 255.255.0.0` / `next` / `end`.
Create service objects (or use built-ins) for only the required ports (e.g. HTTPS, SQL).
Replace the wildcards on the policy with the specific objects using the canonical CLI block below.
Add security inspection while you are in the rule: `set utm-status enable`, `set ips-sensor "default"`, `set av-profile "default"`, and an `ssl-ssh-profile`.
Order the tightened rule correctly and keep an explicit logged deny beneath it.
Clear affected established sessions so they re-evaluate against the new rule: `diagnose sys session filter policy <policy-id>` then `diagnose sys session clear`.
Monitor Forward Traffic logs for legitimate flows now hitting the implicit deny and add narrowly-scoped rules for any valid business traffic you missed.
Re-run the scanner to confirm FORTIOS-POLICY-002 is cleared.
FORTIOS-POLICY-002.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit the policy > set Source and Destination to specific Address objects and Service to specific services (remove 'all'/'ALL'). FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Policy Packages > <package> > edit rule > replace all/ALL objects, then Install Wizard.
FORTIOS-POLICY-002.cli:
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set srcaddr <specific-address>
    set dstaddr <specific-address>
    set service <specific-service>
  next
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-002.verify: show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep -e srcaddr -e dstaddr -e service  -> expect `set srcaddr "NET_..."`, `set dstaddr "NET_..."`, `set service "HTTPS" ...` and NOT `set srcaddr "all"` / `set dstaddr "all"` / `set service "ALL"`.
FORTIOS-POLICY-002.rollback: Restore the config backup or re-add the broad objects (`set srcaddr all` / `set dstaddr all` / `set service ALL`). Reverting re-opens full lateral movement, so treat rollback as an emergency-only action and re-tighten as soon as the legitimate flow list is corrected.
FORTIOS-POLICY-002.impact: Potentially disruptive to traffic that was implicitly relying on the wildcard. No reboot and no HA/VPN/admin-session impact, but any legitimate flow not covered by the new narrow objects will be dropped on its next new session (and immediately if you run `diagnose sys session clear`). Perform during a maintenance window and stage with logging first.
references: Scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 4.1.2, PCI-DSS 1.2.1, NIST 800-53 AC-4/SC-7, SOC 2 CC6.6
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Policy and Objects' (least-privilege policy construction)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Firewall policies; Best Practices > Policy configuration
MITRE ATT&CK T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application), T1021 (Remote Services / lateral movement)
CWE-284: Improper Access Control

FORTIOS-POLICY-003.risk: An allow policy whose source is 'all' accepts sessions from any host, including the Internet or any untrusted VLAN, into the destination behind it. Attackers do not need to spoof or reach a trusted subnet; the rule invites them in. Combined with a permissive service this is a direct externally-exploitable ingress path (MITRE T1190/T1133) and defeats source-based zone trust.
steps: Determine the legitimate source ranges by enabling `set logtraffic all` and reviewing Forward Traffic logs for the actual client subnets hitting this policy.

Create precise source address objects: `config firewall address` / `edit "NET_TRUSTED_SRC"` / `set subnet 10.10.0.0 255.255.0.0` / `next` / `end` (use FQDN or Internet Service objects if the sources are dynamic/known SaaS).
Bind the specific object to the policy source, replacing 'all', with the CLI block below.
If several source subnets are valid, group them: `config firewall addrgrp` / `edit "GRP_TRUSTED_SRC"` / `set member "NET_A" "NET_B"` / `next` / `end` and reference the group.
Keep or add logging and UTM inspection on the same rule.
Clear existing sessions for this policy so they re-match: `diagnose sys session filter policy <policy-id>` then `diagnose sys session clear`.
Watch the implicit-deny logs for valid sources you missed and add them explicitly.
Re-run the scanner to confirm FORTIOS-POLICY-003 is resolved.
FORTIOS-POLICY-003.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit policy > Source > remove 'all', add specific Address/Address Group/Internet Service objects. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Policy Packages > edit rule Source, then Install.
FORTIOS-POLICY-003.cli:
config firewall address
    edit "NET_TRUSTED_SRC"
        set subnet 10.10.0.0 255.255.0.0
    next
end
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set srcaddr "NET_TRUSTED_SRC"
    next
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-003.verify: show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep srcaddr  -> expect `set srcaddr "NET_TRUSTED_SRC"` (or your group), NOT `set srcaddr "all"`.
FORTIOS-POLICY-003.rollback: Re-add the broad source (`set srcaddr all`) or restore the config backup. Reverting re-exposes the destination to any source; only do so if a critical legitimate flow was blocked, then immediately scope the source correctly.
FORTIOS-POLICY-003.impact: Potentially disruptive: legitimate clients whose subnet is not in the new source object are dropped on their next new session (immediately if you clear sessions). No reboot, no VPN/HA/admin impact. Stage with logging and schedule a short maintenance window. NOTE: the scanner's REMEDIATION_COMMANDS maps FORTIOS-POLICY-003 to `set logtraffic all`, which enables logging but does NOT remediate the wildcard source; the correct fix is the source-restriction CLI shown here (apply logging as a complementary control).
references: Scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 4.1.3, PCI-DSS 1.1.7, NIST 800-53 AU-3, SOC 2 CC7.2
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Policy and Objects' (restrict policy source/least privilege)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Firewall > Addresses and Firewall policies; Internet Service Database objects
MITRE ATT&CK T1133 (External Remote Services), T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application)
CWE-284: Improper Access Control

FORTIOS-POLICY-004.risk: A policy that permits the 'ALL' service opens every TCP/UDP port and protocol between the source and destination. Only the intended port (e.g. 443) is usually needed; the rest become a broad attack surface an adversary can use for lateral movement, C2 over non-standard ports (MITRE T1571), management-plane access, or exploiting an unpatched service that should never have been reachable. This is an explicit CIS FortiGate benchmark failure.
steps: Identify the ports actually used on this path from Forward Traffic logs (enable `set logtraffic all` first if needed).

Use built-in service objects where possible (HTTPS, HTTP, DNS, etc.) or create custom ones: `config firewall service custom` / `edit "APP-TCP-8443"` / `set protocol TCP/UDP/SCTP` / `set tcp-portrange 8443` / `next` / `end`.
Replace 'ALL' on the policy with the specific service(s) using the CLI block below.
If multiple services are legitimate, group them: `config firewall service group` / `edit "GRP_WEB"` / `set member "HTTPS" "HTTP"` / `next` / `end`.
Keep logging and UTM inspection enabled on the rule.
Clear matching sessions so they re-evaluate: `diagnose sys session filter policy <policy-id>` then `diagnose sys session clear`.
Review implicit-deny logs for any legitimate port you removed and add it explicitly and narrowly.
Re-run the scanner to confirm FORTIOS-POLICY-004 is cleared.
FORTIOS-POLICY-004.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit policy > Service > remove 'ALL', add specific service objects/groups. Service objects are managed under Policy & Objects > Services. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Policy Packages > edit rule Service, then Install.
FORTIOS-POLICY-004.cli:
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set service "HTTPS" "DNS"
    next
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-004.verify: show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep "set service"  -> expect the specific services (e.g. `set service "HTTPS" "DNS"`), NOT `set service "ALL"`.
FORTIOS-POLICY-004.rollback: Restore `set service ALL` or the config backup. Reverting re-opens all ports; only revert if a required service was inadvertently blocked, then re-scope to the specific ports immediately.
FORTIOS-POLICY-004.impact: Potentially disruptive: any protocol/port previously flowing through this rule that is not in the new service set is dropped on the next new session (immediately if sessions are cleared). No reboot, no HA/VPN/admin-session impact. Stage with logging and use a maintenance window.
references: Scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS 4.1.4, PCI-DSS 1.2.1, NIST 800-53 SC-7, SOC 2 CC6.6
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Policy and Objects': Ensure that policies do not use "ALL" as Service
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Firewall > Services; Firewall policies
MITRE ATT&CK T1571 (Non-Standard Port), T1021 (Remote Services)
CWE-284: Improper Access Control

FORTIOS-POLICY-005.risk: An allow policy with logging disabled creates a forensic blind spot: sessions permitted by that rule leave no record on the FortiGate, FortiAnalyzer or SIEM. After a breach there is no way to reconstruct what an attacker accessed through that path, no data to feed detections, and a direct PCI-DSS Requirement 10 / audit-trail failure. Attackers specifically favour paths with no logging (MITRE T1562.001 - Impair Defenses).
steps: Confirm current setting: `show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep logtraffic`.
Enable full session logging on the policy using the CLI block below (`set logtraffic all`).
For high-value rules also log session start, not just close: `config firewall policy` / `edit <policy-id>` / `set logtraffic-start enable` / `next` / `end`.

Verify logs are actually landing on the collector: enable a FortiAnalyzer/syslog target (`config log fortianalyzer setting` / `set status enable` / `set server <FAZ-IP>` / `end`) if not already configured.
Confirm disk logging is enabled if used: `config log disk setting` / `set status enable` / `end`.
Generate test traffic that matches the policy and confirm entries appear in Log & Report > Forward Traffic.
Repeat for every allow policy the scanner flagged.
Re-run the scanner to confirm FORTIOS-POLICY-005 is resolved.
FORTIOS-POLICY-005.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit policy > Logging Options > 'Log Allowed Traffic' = All Sessions. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Policy Packages > edit rule > Logging Options, then Install.
FORTIOS-POLICY-005.cli:
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set logtraffic all
    next
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-005.verify: show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep logtraffic  -> expect `set logtraffic all` (or `utm` at minimum). Then confirm real events in Log & Report > Forward Traffic, or CLI `execute log filter category 0` / `execute log display`.
FORTIOS-POLICY-005.rollback: Set `set logtraffic disable` (or `utm` to reduce volume) on the policy. Reverting removes visibility for that path; the only 'break' is increased log volume when enabled, so rollback is rarely warranted except for extreme log-rate/licensing constraints.
FORTIOS-POLICY-005.impact: Non-disruptive to traffic; no reboot, no session drop. Side effect is increased log volume and disk/FortiAnalyzer/SIEM storage and possible FortiAnalyzer GB/day licensing consumption. Use `logtraffic utm` on very chatty rules if volume is a concern.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Policy and Objects': Ensure logging is enabled on all firewall policies
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Logging concepts; Configuring logging
PCI-DSS Requirement 10 (audit trails); NIST 800-53 AU-2/AU-12
MITRE ATT&CK T1562.001 (Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools/Logging)
CWE-778: Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-POLICY-006.risk: An allow policy with no UTM/security profiles passes traffic through the firewall completely uninspected: no antivirus, no IPS, no web filtering, no application control, no DNS filtering. Malware downloads, exploit attempts against internal servers (MITRE T1190), phishing payloads and C2 traffic all traverse unchecked. For an Internet-facing or server-facing rule this is effectively running the firewall as a plain router, hence the CRITICAL rating.
steps: Confirm no profile is attached: `show firewall policy <policy-id>` and check for absence of av-profile/ips-sensor/webfilter-profile/application-list.
Ensure UTM processing is on: `config firewall policy` / `edit <policy-id>` / `set utm-status enable`.

Attach the core inspection profiles (start with the built-in 'default' profiles, then tune): `set av-profile "default"`, `set ips-sensor "default"`, `set application-list "default"`, `set webfilter-profile "default"`, `set dnsfilter-profile "default"`.

Attach an SSL/SSH inspection profile so the UTM engines can see inside TLS: `set ssl-ssh-profile "certificate-inspection"` (use "deep-inspection" where client CA distribution is in place). Then `next` / `end`.
For Internet-facing web servers, prefer a tuned IPS sensor and an antivirus profile with outbreak-prevention rather than defaults.
Validate the profiles exist and are licensed (FortiGuard AV/IPS subscription active): `get system fortiguard`.
Test representative traffic (e.g. EICAR test file) and confirm the AV/IPS logs populate in Log & Report > Security Events.
Repeat for every flagged policy; re-run the scanner to confirm FORTIOS-POLICY-006 is cleared.
FORTIOS-POLICY-006.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit policy > Security Profiles section > toggle on AntiVirus, IPS, Web Filter, Application Control, DNS Filter and select an SSL/SSH Inspection profile. Profiles are built under Security Profiles > *. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Policy Packages > edit rule > Security Profiles, then Install.
FORTIOS-POLICY-006.cli:
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set utm-status enable
        set av-profile "default"
        set ips-sensor "default"
        set application-list "default"
        set webfilter-profile "default"
        set dnsfilter-profile "default"
        set ssl-ssh-profile "certificate-inspection"
    next
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-006.verify: show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep -e utm-status -e profile -e ips-sensor -e application-list  -> expect `set utm-status enable` plus the attached profiles. Confirm FortiGuard is reachable/licensed with `get system fortiguard`.
FORTIOS-POLICY-006.rollback: Remove profiles with `unset av-profile` (repeat per profile) and `set utm-status disable`, or restore the config backup. Rollback removes inspection and re-exposes the path; the main risk when enabling is added latency/CPU and deep-inspection breaking cert-pinned apps, so tune rather than disable.
FORTIOS-POLICY-006.impact: Adds inspection latency and CPU/session-setup overhead; can reduce throughput on undersized units. Deep SSL inspection can break certificate-pinned apps (banking, some mobile/store apps) and requires the FortiGate CA on clients - certificate-inspection avoids that. No reboot; changes apply to new sessions. Test in a maintenance window on high-throughput or latency-sensitive rules.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Security Profiles' (apply AV/IPS/Web/App control)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Security Profiles (AntiVirus, IPS, Web Filter, Application Control, DNS Filter); applying profiles to policies
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' - enable security inspection on policies
MITRE ATT&CK T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application), T1071 (Application Layer Protocol / C2)
CWE-693: Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-POLICY-007.risk: Unnamed policies (identified only by numeric ID) are hard to audit, review and correlate with change tickets. During incident response or an audit, staff cannot quickly tell what a rule is for, which leads to mistakes: leaving a dangerous rule in place because its purpose is unclear, or deleting a needed one. This is a low-severity hygiene/manageability control that underpins reliable review of the higher-severity rules.
steps: List policies missing a name: review the scanner output for the flagged IDs, or in the GUI filter the Firewall Policy list where the Name column is blank.
Apply a descriptive, convention-based name to each: `config firewall policy` / `edit <policy-id>` / `set name "LAN_to_WAN_Web_Egress"` / `next` / `end`.
Adopt a naming standard (zone_to_zone_purpose) so rules are self-documenting and sortable.
Optionally add context in comments: `set comments "Owner: NetSec, CHG-1234"`.
Enforce naming going forward so new unnamed rules cannot be saved: `config system settings` / `set gui-allow-unnamed-policy disable` / `end`.
Repeat for all flagged policies.
Re-run the scanner to confirm FORTIOS-POLICY-007 is cleared.
FORTIOS-POLICY-007.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit policy > Name field. The enforcement toggle for named policies has no GUI control and is CLI-only (`config system settings / set gui-allow-unnamed-policy disable`). FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Policy Packages > edit rule Name, then Install.
FORTIOS-POLICY-007.cli:
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set name "LAN_to_WAN_Web_Egress"
    next
end

# Enforce named policies going forward:
config system settings
    set gui-allow-unnamed-policy disable
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-007.verify: show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep "set name"  -> expect `set name "LAN_to_WAN_Web_Egress"`. And `show system settings | grep gui-allow-unnamed-policy` -> `set gui-allow-unnamed-policy disable`.
FORTIOS-POLICY-007.rollback: Remove the name with `unset name`, or set `gui-allow-unnamed-policy enable` to allow unnamed rules again. Note: in NGFW policy-based mode names are mandatory and cannot be unset. Rollback has no traffic effect.
FORTIOS-POLICY-007.impact: Non-disruptive. Naming a policy does not change matching or forwarding behavior. No reboot, no session/VPN/HA impact, no maintenance window.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Policy and Objects' (policy documentation/manageability)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Firewall policies (Name field; NGFW policy-based mode requires names)
Fortinet CLI Reference - config system settings > gui-allow-unnamed-policy
CWE-1078: Inappropriate Source Code Style or Formatting (per scanner: manageability weakness)

FORTIOS-POLICY-008.risk: A deny policy that does not log dropped traffic silently discards blocked connection attempts. That means reconnaissance, port scans, brute-force attempts and blocked exfiltration produce no telemetry, so SOC detections and threat hunts are blind to attacks the firewall is actually stopping. You lose the single richest signal of active targeting (MITRE T1046/T1595) and fail PCI-DSS Req 10 logging expectations for denied traffic.
steps: Confirm the deny rule is not logging: `show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep logtraffic`.
Enable logging on the deny policy with the CLI block below (`set logtraffic all`).
Also enable logging of the global implicit-deny so all silently-dropped traffic is captured: `config log setting` / `set fwpolicy-implicit-log enable` / `end`.
Ensure a log destination (FortiAnalyzer/syslog/disk) is configured and reachable so denied-traffic events are retained.
Generate a test blocked connection and confirm it appears in Log & Report > Forward Traffic with action=deny.
Consider alerting: build a FortiAnalyzer/SIEM rule on spikes of denied traffic per source.
Repeat for all flagged deny policies.
Re-run the scanner to confirm FORTIOS-POLICY-008 is cleared.
FORTIOS-POLICY-008.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit the deny policy > Logging Options > enable 'Log Violation Traffic' (Log Allowed Traffic is not applicable to deny). Implicit deny logging: Log & Report settings / CLI. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > edit rule > Logging Options, then Install.
FORTIOS-POLICY-008.cli:
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set logtraffic all
    next
end

# Log the global implicit-deny as well:
config log setting
    set fwpolicy-implicit-log enable
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-008.verify: show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep logtraffic  -> expect `set logtraffic all`. And `show log setting | grep fwpolicy-implicit-log` -> `set fwpolicy-implicit-log enable`.
FORTIOS-POLICY-008.rollback: Set `set logtraffic disable` on the policy and/or `set fwpolicy-implicit-log disable`. The only effect of enabling is added log volume, so rollback is rarely justified; if needed, scope logging to the noisiest rules instead.
FORTIOS-POLICY-008.impact: Non-disruptive to traffic; no reboot or session impact. Increases log volume (denied-traffic and implicit-deny logs can be high on Internet-facing interfaces) and consumes FortiAnalyzer/SIEM storage and licensing. Monitor log rates after enabling.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Policy and Objects' / 'Log and Report' (log denied traffic; implicit-deny logging)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Configuring logging; config log setting fwpolicy-implicit-log
PCI-DSS Requirement 10; NIST 800-53 AU-2/AU-12
MITRE ATT&CK T1595 (Active Scanning), T1046 (Network Service Discovery)
CWE-778: Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-POLICY-009.risk: An allow policy whose destination is 'all' lets the permitted source reach any host in any direction. On an ingress rule it exposes the entire internal estate to a single trusted source; on an egress rule it allows a compromised host to talk to any Internet destination, enabling C2 and data exfiltration (MITRE T1041/T1048). It defeats destination-based segmentation and turns a scoped rule into an open conduit.
steps: Determine the real destinations from Forward Traffic logs (enable `set logtraffic all` first if needed).
Create destination address objects for the legitimate targets: `config firewall address` / `edit "SRV_APP01"` / `set subnet 10.20.30.40 255.255.255.255` / `next` / `end`.
Replace 'all' with the specific destination using the CLI block below.

For Internet egress where explicit IPs are impractical, scope by FQDN or Internet Service (ISDB) objects instead of 'all': `set internet-service enable` / `set internet-service-name "Microsoft-Office365"` (with srcaddr set and dstaddr omitted for ISDB rules).
Keep logging and UTM inspection on the rule.
Clear matching sessions so they re-evaluate: `diagnose sys session filter policy <policy-id>` then `diagnose sys session clear`.
Review implicit-deny logs for valid destinations you missed and add them narrowly.
Re-run the scanner to confirm FORTIOS-POLICY-009 is cleared.
FORTIOS-POLICY-009.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit policy > Destination > remove 'all', add specific Address / FQDN / Internet Service objects. Address and Internet Service objects are under Policy & Objects. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > edit rule Destination, then Install.
FORTIOS-POLICY-009.cli:
config firewall address
    edit "SRV_APP01"
        set subnet 10.20.30.40 255.255.255.255
    next
end
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set dstaddr "SRV_APP01"
    next
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-009.verify: show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep dstaddr  -> expect `set dstaddr "SRV_APP01"` (or an FQDN/ISDB object), NOT `set dstaddr "all"`. For ISDB rules confirm with `... | grep internet-service`.
FORTIOS-POLICY-009.rollback: Re-add `set dstaddr all` or restore the config backup. Reverting re-opens the destination scope; only revert if a legitimate destination was blocked, then re-scope precisely (FQDN/ISDB) rather than reverting to 'all'.
FORTIOS-POLICY-009.impact: Potentially disruptive: destinations not covered by the new object are dropped on the next new session (immediately if sessions cleared). No reboot, no HA/VPN/admin impact. For broad egress rules, expect an iteration or two of adding missed FQDN/ISDB objects; stage with logging and a maintenance window.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Policy and Objects' (restrict policy destination / least privilege)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Firewall > Addresses (FQDN), Internet Service Database in firewall policies
MITRE ATT&CK T1041 (Exfiltration Over C2 Channel), T1048 (Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol)
CWE-284: Improper Access Control

FORTIOS-POLICY-010.risk: With no SSL/SSH inspection profile, the FortiGate cannot see inside TLS. The overwhelming majority of malware delivery, phishing, and C2 now rides HTTPS, so antivirus, IPS, web and application filtering only ever inspect the small remaining plaintext. Encrypted threats and encrypted exfiltration (MITRE T1573 - Encrypted Channel) pass straight through even when UTM profiles are attached.
steps: Confirm no SSL profile: `show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep ssl-ssh-profile`.
Decide inspection depth: 'certificate-inspection' (checks SNI/certificate only, no decryption, no client CA needed) versus 'deep-inspection' (full decrypt, requires the FortiGate CA on clients).
Apply the profile with the CLI block below. For flat/perimeter rules choose deep-inspection where client CA distribution is in place; otherwise start with certificate-inspection.

For deep inspection, distribute the FortiGate CA certificate to endpoints (GPO/MDM) so browsers do not throw certificate errors, and build exemptions for cert-pinned categories (banking, health, software updates) under the SSL/SSH profile's exempt list.
Ensure the UTM engines are actually attached (AV/IPS/webfilter) so the now-visible traffic is inspected.
Test HTTPS traffic and confirm inspection is occurring (e.g. blocked category page served over TLS, or AV catching an HTTPS EICAR download).
Repeat for all flagged policies.
Re-run the scanner to confirm FORTIOS-POLICY-010 is cleared.
FORTIOS-POLICY-010.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit policy > Security Profiles > SSL/SSH Inspection > select 'certificate-inspection' or 'deep-inspection' (custom profiles built under Security Profiles > SSL/SSH Inspection). FortiManager: Policy & Objects > edit rule > SSL/SSH Inspection, then Install.
FORTIOS-POLICY-010.cli:
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"
    next
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-010.verify: show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep ssl-ssh-profile  -> expect `set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"` (or "certificate-inspection"), NOT the field being absent/no-inspection.
FORTIOS-POLICY-010.rollback: Set `set ssl-ssh-profile "no-inspection"` or restore the backup. Rollback removes encrypted-traffic visibility. The main risk when enabling is deep inspection breaking cert-pinned applications - handle via the profile's exempt list rather than disabling inspection entirely.
FORTIOS-POLICY-010.impact: Certificate-inspection is low-impact and needs no client changes. Deep-inspection adds CPU/latency, requires the FortiGate CA on all clients, and will break certificate-pinned apps unless exempted. No reboot; applies to new sessions. Roll out deep inspection in a maintenance window with a pilot group.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Security Profiles' (SSL/SSH inspection)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Security Profiles > SSL/SSH inspection (certificate vs deep inspection; exemptions; CA deployment)
MITRE ATT&CK T1573 (Encrypted Channel), T1071.001 (Web Protocols / C2)
CWE-693: Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-POLICY-011.risk: A broad rule (any src/dst/service) with an 'always' schedule is the classic 'temporary rule that became permanent'. A firewall change made for a migration, vendor access or troubleshooting window was never removed, leaving a wide-open path running 24/7 with no expiry. Attackers benefit from exactly these forgotten, over-broad allowances (MITRE T1190/T1133), and they routinely survive audits because 'always' looks normal.
steps: Review the flagged rule and confirm whether it is genuinely required permanently or was meant to be temporary (check the change ticket / owner).
If it was temporary, remove it or replace its scope with least-privilege src/dst/service objects (see FORTIOS-POLICY-002/004 remediation).

If time-boxed access is the real requirement, create a bounded schedule instead of 'always'. One-time: `config firewall schedule onetime` / `edit "MAINT_WINDOW"` / `set start 00:00 2026/07/10` / `set end 06:00 2026/07/10` / `next` / `end`. Recurring: `config firewall schedule recurring` for defined days/hours.
Apply the schedule to the policy using the CLI block below.
Tighten the source/destination/service on the same rule so it is no longer broad.
Set a calendar reminder / ticket to review or delete the rule at the schedule's end.
Confirm the rule now denies traffic outside the window as expected.
Re-run the scanner to confirm FORTIOS-POLICY-011 is cleared.
FORTIOS-POLICY-011.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit policy > Schedule > select a one-time or recurring schedule (built under Policy & Objects > Schedules) instead of 'always'; also narrow Source/Destination/Service. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > edit rule Schedule, then Install.
FORTIOS-POLICY-011.cli:
config firewall schedule onetime
    edit "MAINT_WINDOW"
        set start 00:00 2026/07/10
        set end 06:00 2026/07/10
    next
end
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set schedule "MAINT_WINDOW"
    next
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-011.verify: show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep schedule  -> expect `set schedule "MAINT_WINDOW"` (not `always`). Confirm the schedule window with `show firewall schedule onetime MAINT_WINDOW`.
FORTIOS-POLICY-011.rollback: Set `set schedule always` again (or restore the backup). Watch for the opposite failure mode: an over-tight schedule will DENY traffic outside its window, so if a flow really is needed 24/7, revert to 'always' but keep the src/dst/service tightened rather than leaving it broad.
FORTIOS-POLICY-011.impact: Applying a time-limited schedule is disruptive by design outside the window: traffic is denied when the schedule is inactive. If the rule actually carries production traffic around the clock this will cause an outage, so validate the true business hours first. No reboot, no session-clear needed for the schedule to take effect on new sessions.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Policy and Objects' (review broad/permanent rules; least privilege)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Firewall > Schedules (one-time and recurring); Firewall policies
MITRE ATT&CK T1133 (External Remote Services), T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application)
CWE-284: Improper Access Control

FORTIOS-POLICY-012.risk: A high ratio of disabled policies (the scanner flags >30% and >=5 disabled) signals poor policy lifecycle management. Large numbers of dormant rules make the policy base hard to audit, obscure shadowing/ordering problems, and give an attacker with write access many pre-built dangerous rules to silently re-enable. It is a governance/hygiene indicator that the higher-severity findings are likely to hide in.
steps: Quantify: `show firewall policy | grep -f "set status disable"` to list all disabled rules and their scope.
For each, determine last-used data. Use the GUI hit-count columns (Bytes/First Used/Last Used) or FortiManager Policy Hit Count to see which were ever used before being disabled.
Delete rules that are obsolete: `config firewall policy` / `delete <policy-id>` / `end` (repeat).
For any disabled rule that must stay, document it with `set comments` so it is not counted as unmanaged clutter.
Back up the config before bulk deletion (admin account menu (top-right) > Configuration > Backup or `execute backup config <dest>`).
Establish a recurring policy-review process (e.g. quarterly) so the disabled ratio stays low.
Re-verify the ratio and re-run the scanner to confirm FORTIOS-POLICY-012 no longer fires.
FORTIOS-POLICY-012.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > filter Status = Disabled, review Last Used, then Delete stale rules. Hit counters: enable the Bytes/Last Used columns. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Policy Packages, use Policy Hit Count to identify unused rules, clean up and Install.
FORTIOS-POLICY-012.cli:
# Review then remove obsolete disabled rules one by one:
config firewall policy
    delete <policy-id>
end

# Retain-but-document any that must stay disabled:
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set comments "Retained disabled per CHG-XXXX - review 2026-Q4"
    next
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-012.verify: After cleanup: `show firewall policy | grep -c "set status disable"` and compare to total policy count so the disabled ratio is under threshold (<30% and <5). Re-run the scanner and confirm 0 FORTIOS-POLICY-012 findings.
FORTIOS-POLICY-012.rollback: Restore the config backup to recover deleted rules, or re-create them from the exported config. Re-created rules may take new policy IDs and land at the wrong position, so re-verify ordering with `show firewall policy` after any restore.
FORTIOS-POLICY-012.impact: Non-disruptive: only already-disabled (non-forwarding) rules are removed, so live traffic is unaffected. No reboot, no session/VPN/HA impact. No maintenance window required, though a config backup beforehand is prudent.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Policy and Objects': Ensure that unused policies are reviewed regularly
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Firewall policies (policy hit count / usage); FortiManager Policy Hit Count
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' - regular policy review
CWE-1078: Inappropriate Source Code Style or Formatting (per scanner: manageability weakness)

FORTIOS-POLICY-013.risk: A Virtual IP configured without port forwarding maps ALL ports of the external (public) address to the internal server. Instead of exposing only, say, TCP/443, the VIP publishes every service the host runs - RDP, SMB, database, management interfaces - to the Internet. A single vulnerable ancillary service then becomes an externally reachable entry point (MITRE T1190), which is exactly how many perimeter breaches and ransomware intrusions start.
steps: Identify the VIP and what it currently exposes: `show firewall vip <vip-name>`; if `set portforward` is absent/disable, all ports are mapped.
Determine the single service/port the published host legitimately needs (e.g. TCP/443 for a web app).
Enable port forwarding and restrict to that port using the CLI block below (set protocol, extport, mappedport).
If the host needs more than one specific port, create additional VIPs (or use a VIP group) rather than reverting to all-port mapping.
Ensure the firewall policy that references this VIP as its destination is also scoped (specific service, UTM profiles, logging).
Verify external reachability: from outside, confirm only the intended port answers and previously-exposed ports (e.g. RDP/SMB) are now blocked.
Repeat for every VIP the scanner flagged.
Re-run the scanner to confirm FORTIOS-POLICY-013 is cleared.
FORTIOS-POLICY-013.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Virtual IPs > edit the VIP > enable 'Port Forwarding', set Protocol, External Service Port and Map to Port. Then confirm the consuming Firewall Policy is scoped. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > Virtual IPs, edit and Install.
FORTIOS-POLICY-013.cli:
config firewall vip
    edit "VIP_WEB01"
        set extip 203.0.113.10
        set mappedip 10.20.30.40
        set portforward enable
        set protocol tcp
        set extport 443
        set mappedport 443
    next
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-013.verify: show firewall vip VIP_WEB01 | grep -e portforward -e extport -e mappedport  -> expect `set portforward enable`, `set extport 443`, `set mappedport 443`. Externally, only TCP/443 should respond.
FORTIOS-POLICY-013.rollback: Set `set portforward disable` (restores all-port mapping) or restore the config backup. Reverting re-exposes every port on the internal host to the Internet, so only revert if a required additional service was cut, and prefer adding a second scoped VIP instead.
FORTIOS-POLICY-013.impact: Potentially disruptive: any additional service that was reachable through the all-port VIP (and legitimately used) will stop working once forwarding is restricted to one port - identify all required ports first. No reboot; existing sessions may persist until cleared/expired. Schedule a brief maintenance window and test external access.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Policy and Objects' (restrict published services / least exposure)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Firewall > Virtual IPs (port forwarding; VIP configuration)
MITRE ATT&CK T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application), T1133 (External Remote Services)
CWE-284: Improper Access Control

FORTIOS-POLICY-014.risk: No egress filtering means outbound traffic from internal/LAN zones to the Internet is effectively unrestricted. A compromised host can then reach any external IP/port for command-and-control, tool download, and bulk data exfiltration (MITRE T1041/T1048/T1071) with nothing to block or even flag it. Egress control is the last-line containment that turns a workstation compromise into a stopped incident rather than a data breach.
steps: Confirm the gap: review LAN->WAN policies for any explicit deny; the scanner flags when none exists and there are >3 active policies.
Baseline legitimate outbound flows from Forward Traffic logs (which internal hosts talk to which external services/ports).
Build least-privilege outbound allow rules for required traffic (e.g. proxies, DNS to approved resolvers, business SaaS via FQDN/Internet Service objects) with UTM and logging enabled.
Add an explicit, logged catch-all egress deny beneath the allows so blocked outbound is visible (implicit deny alone is silent). Use the CLI block below and place it last in the LAN->WAN section.
Enable implicit-deny logging globally too: `config log setting` / `set fwpolicy-implicit-log enable` / `end`.
Verify ordering: the deny must sit AFTER the legitimate allow rules or it will block everything.
Monitor the egress-deny logs for a period and add narrowly-scoped allows for any valid business traffic that was blocked.
Re-run the scanner to confirm FORTIOS-POLICY-014 is cleared.
FORTIOS-POLICY-014.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > Create New > Incoming Interface = LAN/internal, Outgoing = wan1, Action = DENY, Service = ALL, enable Log Violation Traffic; position it below the outbound allow rules. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Policy Packages > add deny rule at the bottom of the egress section, then Install.
FORTIOS-POLICY-014.cli:
config firewall policy
    edit 0
        set name "LAN_to_WAN_DENY_ALL"
        set srcintf "internal"
        set dstintf "wan1"
        set srcaddr "all"
        set dstaddr "all"
        set action deny
        set schedule "always"
        set service "ALL"
        set logtraffic all
    next
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-014.verify: show firewall policy | grep -f "set action deny"  -> confirm a LAN->WAN deny rule exists and is positioned after the allow rules (`show firewall policy` to check order). Re-run the scanner to confirm the egress-filter finding is gone.
FORTIOS-POLICY-014.rollback: Delete the egress-deny rule (`config firewall policy` / `delete <id>` / `end`) or restore the backup. The critical risk is ORDERING: if the deny is placed above legitimate allow rules it will block all outbound traffic and cause an outage - fix by moving it (`move <id> after <allow-id>`) rather than deleting the control.
FORTIOS-POLICY-014.impact: Potentially highly disruptive if misordered or if allow rules are incomplete: legitimate outbound traffic not covered by an allow rule above the deny will be blocked. No reboot. Stage with logging, validate the allow set, and deploy the deny in a maintenance window; existing sessions persist until cleared/expired.
references: NIST 800-53 AC-4/SC-7 - egress/least-privilege outbound control (note: FORTIOS-POLICY-014 has no entry in the scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Policy and Objects' (outbound/egress filtering; implicit-deny logging)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Firewall policies (policy ordering, action deny); config log setting fwpolicy-implicit-log
MITRE ATT&CK T1041 (Exfiltration Over C2 Channel), T1048 (Exfil Over Alternative Protocol), T1071 (Application Layer Protocol)
CWE-284: Improper Access Control

FORTIOS-POLICY-015.risk: This is the fleet-level view of FORTIOS-POLICY-006: a large share of active policies (the finding reports N of M with a percentage) pass traffic with NO security profile attached at all - no AV, IPS, web filter, application control or DNS filter. Any traffic matching those rules bypasses every inspection engine, so malware, exploits and C2 flow freely across a big fraction of the rule base. A high percentage here indicates a systemic gap (often flat 'allow' rules built without a profile template) rather than a one-off, hence the HIGH severity.
steps: Get the list of offending policies from the scanner output / GUI, then confirm per policy with `show firewall policy <id>` (absence of av-profile/ips-sensor/webfilter-profile/application-list).

Standardize a security-profile group so every policy inherits a consistent baseline: `config firewall profile-group` / `edit "CORP_BASELINE"` / `set av-profile "default"` / `set ips-sensor "default"` / `set application-list "default"` / `set webfilter-profile "default"` / `set ssl-ssh-profile "certificate-inspection"` / `next` / `end`.

Apply the profile group to each flagged policy: `config firewall policy` / `edit <id>` / `set utm-status enable` / `set profile-type group` / `set profile-group "CORP_BASELINE"` / `next` / `end` (or attach individual profiles as in POLICY-006).
For bulk change across many rules use FortiManager: apply the profile group to the policy package and Install once, rather than editing each rule by hand.
Confirm FortiGuard AV/IPS licensing is active so profiles are effective: `get system fortiguard`.
Pilot on a subset, validate no legitimate app breakage (especially if using deep inspection), then roll out.
Re-run the scanner and confirm the N-without-profiles count drops to 0.
FORTIOS-POLICY-015.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > for each flagged rule enable Security Profiles (AV/IPS/Web/App/DNS + SSL/SSH). To standardize, build a profile group and reference it. FortiManager (recommended for scale): Policy & Objects > Policy Packages > apply profile group to rules > Install Wizard.
FORTIOS-POLICY-015.cli:
config firewall profile-group
    edit "CORP_BASELINE"
        set av-profile "default"
        set ips-sensor "default"
        set application-list "default"
        set webfilter-profile "default"
        set ssl-ssh-profile "certificate-inspection"
    next
end
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set utm-status enable
        set profile-type group
        set profile-group "CORP_BASELINE"
    next
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-015.verify: show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep -e utm-status -e profile-group  -> expect `set utm-status enable` and `set profile-group "CORP_BASELINE"` (or individual profiles). Re-run the scanner and confirm the aggregate count is 0.
FORTIOS-POLICY-015.rollback: Per policy: `set utm-status disable` / `unset profile-group` (or unset individual profiles), or restore the config backup. Rollback removes inspection across the fleet; when enabling, the risk is added latency and deep-inspection breaking cert-pinned apps - use certificate-inspection and profile exemptions rather than disabling.
FORTIOS-POLICY-015.impact: Adds inspection CPU/latency across many rules simultaneously - size the appliance and roll out in stages. Deep SSL inspection can break cert-pinned apps and needs the FortiGate CA on clients; certificate-inspection avoids that. No reboot; applies to new sessions. Use a maintenance window and a pilot group for a fleet-wide change.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Security Profiles' (apply inspection to all allow policies)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Security Profiles; Firewall > Profile groups; applying profiles at scale
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' - enable UTM inspection on policies
MITRE ATT&CK T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application), T1071 (Application Layer Protocol / C2)
CWE-693: Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-POLICY-016.risk: This is the fleet-level view of FORTIOS-POLICY-005: N of M active policies have traffic logging disabled, creating systemic monitoring blind spots. Across those rules there is no session telemetry for the SOC, no data for detections or threat hunts, and gaps in the audit trail required by PCI-DSS Req 10. Attackers deliberately traverse unlogged paths (MITRE T1562.001); a high count here means much of the estate's east-west/north-south traffic is effectively invisible.
steps: Get the flagged policies from the scanner output; confirm per rule with `show firewall policy <id> | grep logtraffic`.
Enable logging on each: `config firewall policy` / `edit <id>` / `set logtraffic all` / `next` / `end` (repeat), or push it in bulk via FortiManager to the whole policy package.
Enable global implicit-deny logging so dropped traffic is also captured: `config log setting` / `set fwpolicy-implicit-log enable` / `end`.

Confirm a log destination exists and is healthy (FortiAnalyzer/syslog/disk) so the additional volume is actually retained: `config log fortianalyzer setting` / `set status enable` / `set server <FAZ-IP>` / `end`.
Right-size retention/licensing on FortiAnalyzer/SIEM before enabling everywhere to avoid dropping logs (GB/day limits).
For extremely chatty rules where full logging is impractical, use `set logtraffic utm` as a minimum so security events are still captured.
Generate test traffic and confirm events populate in Log & Report > Forward Traffic.
Re-run the scanner and confirm the N-without-logging count drops to 0.
FORTIOS-POLICY-016.gui: FortiGate GUI: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > for each flagged rule > Logging Options > Log Allowed Traffic = All Sessions. FortiManager (recommended for scale): Policy & Objects > Policy Packages > set logging on rules > Install. Log destinations: Log & Report > Log Settings.
FORTIOS-POLICY-016.cli:
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set logtraffic all
    next
end

# And capture dropped traffic globally:
config log setting
    set fwpolicy-implicit-log enable
end
FORTIOS-POLICY-016.verify: show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep logtraffic  -> expect `set logtraffic all` (or `utm`) on each remediated rule; `show log setting | grep fwpolicy-implicit-log` -> `set fwpolicy-implicit-log enable`. Re-run the scanner and confirm the aggregate count is 0.
FORTIOS-POLICY-016.rollback: Per policy: `set logtraffic disable` (or `utm` to reduce volume), and/or `set fwpolicy-implicit-log disable`, or restore the backup. The only side effect of enabling is log volume, so rollback is rarely justified; instead throttle by using `utm` on the noisiest rules.
FORTIOS-POLICY-016.impact: Non-disruptive to traffic; no reboot or session impact. Fleet-wide enablement can substantially increase log volume and FortiAnalyzer/SIEM storage and licensing consumption - validate capacity first and stage the rollout. Monitor log rates after enabling.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Policy and Objects': Ensure logging is enabled on all firewall policies
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - Log and Report > Configuring logging; config log setting fwpolicy-implicit-log
PCI-DSS Requirement 10; NIST 800-53 AU-2/AU-12
MITRE ATT&CK T1562.001 (Impair Defenses: Disable/Modify Logging)
CWE-778: Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-PROFILE-001.risk: With no antivirus profile configured (or none attached to any policy), file transfers over HTTP/HTTPS/FTP/SMTP/IMAP/POP3 traverse the firewall uninspected. An attacker delivering a malicious payload via a drive-by download, a phishing attachment, or a compromised software update reaches the endpoint unscanned, leading to ransomware detonation, credential theft, or a beachhead for lateral movement. Business impact: full endpoint/server compromise and potential enterprise-wide ransomware event with no gateway control in the kill chain.
steps: Confirm the gap: run 'show antivirus profile' and 'show firewall policy | grep -e "edit " -e av-profile' to see that no AV profile exists or is bound to policies.
Ensure the AV/FortiGuard subscription is active and signatures are current: 'diagnose autoupdate versions' and 'execute update-now'.
Create a blocking AV profile: config antivirus profile / edit "block-malware" / set feature-set flow.

Set HTTP scanning to block: config http / set av-scan block / set outbreak-prevention block / end. HTTPS is covered by this same http handler once a deep-inspection ssl-ssh-profile decrypts it - the AV profile has no separate 'config https' section.
Add mail/file protocols: config smtp / set av-scan block / end, config pop3 / set av-scan block / end, config imap / set av-scan block / end, config ftp / set av-scan block / end.
Save the profile with 'next / end'.

Attach the profile to internet-facing policies together with an inspection profile: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set av-profile "block-malware" / set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection" / next / end.
Repeat the policy binding for every egress/ingress policy that carries user or server traffic.
Validate a controlled EICAR test download to confirm blocking, then review UTM logs.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-001.gui: Security Profiles > AntiVirus > Create New (build the profile), then Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit each policy > Security Profiles > enable AntiVirus + SSL/SSH Inspection. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > Security Profiles > AntiVirus, then attach in the policy package.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-001.cli:
config antivirus profile
    edit "block-malware"
        set feature-set flow
        config http
            set av-scan block
            set outbreak-prevention block
        end
        config ftp
            set av-scan block
        end
        config smtp
            set av-scan block
        end
        config pop3
            set av-scan block
        end
        config imap
            set av-scan block
        end
    next
end
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set utm-status enable
        set av-profile "block-malware"
        set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"
    next
end
# Note: HTTPS/SMTPS/etc. are scanned by the http/smtp handlers above once the
# deep-inspection ssl-ssh-profile decrypts them - the antivirus profile has no
# separate 'config https' (or ftps/imaps/pop3s/smtps) subsection.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-001.verify: show antivirus profile block-malware  -> shows 'config http' with 'set av-scan block'; and 'show firewall policy <id> | grep av-profile' -> set av-profile "block-malware". Live check: download the EICAR test file over HTTP/HTTPS and confirm a virus event in Log & Report > Security Events > AntiVirus.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-001.rollback: Detach with 'config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset av-profile / next / end' and optionally 'delete' the profile. Removing AV re-exposes the network to malware; only roll back if a false positive blocks a business-critical transfer, and prefer an exclusion over full removal.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-001.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot, and no admin/SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA session drops. Flow-based AV adds minimal latency; enabling it inspects new sessions only. Risk is false-positive blocking of legitimate downloads, so pilot on a test policy first.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4.2 Antivirus (apply antivirus profile to policies); scanner compliance map CIS 4.2.1
FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Antivirus > Configuring an antivirus profile
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide - Enable security profiles on policies
PCI-DSS v4.0 5.2 / 5.3 (anti-malware on all systems)
NIST SP 800-53 SI-3 Malicious Code Protection
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-PROFILE-002.risk: No IPS sensor exists (or none is applied to policies), so the FortiGate performs no virtual patching or exploit detection. Internet-facing services and internal servers are exposed to known CVE exploitation - SQL injection, RCE, deserialization, and worming attacks (e.g., Log4Shell, ProxyShell, and FortiOS-adjacent CVEs) reach targets with no signature-based blocking. Business impact: opportunistic and targeted exploitation of unpatched or slow-to-patch systems, breach, and lateral spread.
steps: Confirm the gap: 'show ips sensor' returns empty and 'show firewall policy | grep ips-sensor' shows no bindings.
Ensure IPS definitions are current: 'diagnose autoupdate versions' and 'execute update-now'.
Create a protective sensor: config ips sensor / edit "protect-all".
Add a high/critical block filter: config entries / edit 1 / set severity high critical / set status enable / set action block / next.
Add a medium filter using the default action: edit 2 / set severity medium / set status enable / set action default / next / end.
Save with 'next / end'.
Bind to internet-facing and inter-zone policies: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set ips-sensor "protect-all" / next / end.
For servers, consider a server-specific sensor scoped to the relevant OS/application signatures.
Watch Log & Report > Security Events > Intrusion Prevention for hits and tune false positives.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-002.gui: Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention > Create New (add signature/filter entries with Action=Block), then Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > enable IPS on each policy. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-002.cli:
config ips sensor
    edit "protect-all"
        config entries
            edit 1
                set severity high critical
                set status enable
                set action block
            next
            edit 2
                set severity medium
                set status enable
                set action default
            next
        end
    next
end
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set utm-status enable
        set ips-sensor "protect-all"
    next
end
FORTIOS-PROFILE-002.verify: show ips sensor protect-all  -> entries with 'set action block'; 'show firewall policy <id> | grep ips-sensor' -> set ips-sensor "protect-all". Live: confirm signatures are loaded with 'diagnose ips rule list' and watch hits under Log & Report > Security Events > Intrusion Prevention.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-002.rollback: Detach with 'config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset ips-sensor / next / end' or delete the sensor. Removing IPS re-exposes services to exploitation; if a signature false-positives, prefer setting that specific signature to monitor or using rate-based/exempt tuning instead of removing the sensor.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-002.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot, no admin/VPN/HA session drop. IPS inspection adds CPU load and slight latency; block mode can cause false positives against legitimate traffic - stage on a low-risk policy or start in monitor for a short baseline, then move to block.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4.1 Intrusion Prevention (IPS); scanner compliance map CIS 4.2.2
FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Intrusion prevention > IPS sensors
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
PCI-DSS v4.0 11.5.1 (IDS/IPS techniques)
NIST SP 800-53 SI-4 System Monitoring, SI-2 Flaw Remediation (virtual patching)
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-PROFILE-003.risk: No web filter profile exists, so outbound web browsing is uncategorized and unfiltered. Users reach phishing pages, malware-distribution and newly-registered domains, and command-and-control front-ends with no gateway block, and there is no content-category control for acceptable-use or data-exfiltration-to-web-mail concerns. Business impact: increased phishing/malware success rate, C2 egress, and lack of an outbound web control layer.
steps: Confirm the gap: 'show webfilter profile' is empty and no policy references a webfilter-profile.
Ensure the Web Filtering (FortiGuard URL) subscription is active: System > FortiGuard.
Create a profile: config webfilter profile / edit "corp-webfilter" / set feature-set flow.

Block security categories via FortiGuard: config ftgd-wf / config filters / edit 1 / set category 26 / set action block (Malicious Websites) / next / edit 2 / set category 61 / set action block (Phishing) / next / edit 3 / set category 86 / set action block (Newly Observed Domain) / next / end / end.
Ensure logging is enabled on the applying policy (set logtraffic all) so URL ratings, blocks, and monitored categories are recorded in Security Events.
Save with 'next / end'.

Bind to web-egress policies with deep SSL inspection: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set webfilter-profile "corp-webfilter" / set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection" / next / end.
Validate by browsing to a known-blocked test category and confirm the FortiGuard block page.
Note: confirm category IDs against your FortiOS version in the GUI category list, as IDs can vary by release.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-003.gui: Security Profiles > Web Filter > Create New > enable FortiGuard Category Based Filter and set Security-risk categories to Block; then Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > apply Web Filter + SSL/SSH Inspection. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Web Filter.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-003.cli:
config webfilter profile
    edit "corp-webfilter"
        set feature-set flow
        config ftgd-wf
            config filters
                edit 1
                    set category 26
                    set action block
                next
                edit 2
                    set category 61
                    set action block
                next
                edit 3
                    set category 86
                    set action block
                next
            end
        end
    next
end
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set utm-status enable
        set webfilter-profile "corp-webfilter"
        set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"
    next
end
FORTIOS-PROFILE-003.verify: show webfilter profile corp-webfilter  -> ftgd-wf filters present with action block; 'show firewall policy <id> | grep webfilter-profile'. Live: browse to a test URL in a blocked category and confirm the FortiGuard replacement/block page and a Web Filter log entry.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-003.rollback: Detach with 'config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset webfilter-profile / next / end' or delete the profile. Removing web filtering re-exposes users to phishing/malicious sites; prefer moving a specific over-blocked category to 'warning'/'monitor' or adding a URL exemption instead of removing the profile.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-003.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. HTTPS category filtering is only fully effective where deep SSL inspection is applied (else only SNI/certificate hostname is visible). Category blocks can affect legitimate business sites - tune per acceptable-use policy.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4 Security Profiles (Web Filter); scanner compliance map CIS 4.2.3
FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Web filter > FortiGuard category based filter
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
NIST SP 800-53 SC-7 Boundary Protection
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-PROFILE-004.risk: No application control list exists, so the firewall cannot identify or restrict Layer-7 applications regardless of port. Users and malware can tunnel over HTTP/HTTPS using proxy-avoidance tools, anonymizers (Tor/Ultrasurf), P2P, and unsanctioned remote-access apps, evading port-based policy. Business impact: shadow-IT and data-exfiltration channels, C2 over allowed ports, and no visibility into what applications actually traverse the firewall.
steps: Confirm the gap: 'show application list' is empty and no policy sets application-list.
Ensure the Application Control database is current: 'diagnose autoupdate versions'.
Create a list: config application list / edit "appctrl-baseline".

Block high-risk categories: config entries / edit 1 / set category <ids> / set action block / next / end (confirm the numeric category IDs in the GUI - e.g., P2P, Proxy, Botnet - as IDs vary by release).
Set the default handling for unmatched and unknown apps per policy: set other-application-action pass and set unknown-application-action pass (tighten later once visibility is established).
Save with 'next / end'.
Bind to policies: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set application-list "appctrl-baseline" / next / end.
Review Application Control logs to build an inventory, then progressively block additional unsanctioned apps.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-004.gui: Security Profiles > Application Control > Create New > add category/application overrides (Block high-risk), then Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > apply Application Control. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Application Control.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-004.cli:
config application list
    edit "appctrl-baseline"
        set other-application-action pass
        set unknown-application-action pass
        config entries
            edit 1
                set category 2 6 25
                set action block
            next
        end
    next
end
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set utm-status enable
        set application-list "appctrl-baseline"
    next
end
# Confirm the numeric category IDs against your FortiOS version in
# Security Profiles > Application Control (category view) - IDs vary by release.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-004.verify: show application list appctrl-baseline  -> entry with action block on high-risk categories; 'show firewall policy <id> | grep application-list'. Live: generate P2P/proxy traffic on a test host and confirm a block in Log & Report > Security Events > Application Control.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-004.rollback: Detach with 'config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset application-list / next / end' or delete the list. Removing app control restores unrestricted L7 apps; if a legitimate app is blocked, add an application override to allow it rather than removing the list.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-004.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. App identification adds CPU load; blocking categories can affect sanctioned SaaS or collaboration tools that share a category - start in monitor to baseline, then enforce.
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Application control > Application control profiles
CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4 Security Profiles (Application Control)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
NIST SP 800-53 CM-7 Least Functionality, SC-7 Boundary Protection
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-PROFILE-005.risk: No DLP sensor exists, so the FortiGate cannot detect or block sensitive data (PII, PCI cardholder data, PHI, source code, credentials) leaving over HTTP-POST, FTP, SMTP, or web-mail. An insider or compromised host can exfiltrate regulated data through the gateway with no content-aware control. Business impact: undetected data breach, regulatory penalties (PCI-DSS/HIPAA/GDPR), and no gateway evidence of exfiltration.
steps: Confirm the gap: 'show dlp sensor' (FortiOS 7.0/6.x) or 'show dlp profile' (7.2+) returns empty.
Define what to detect (credit-card, SSN, custom regex/keywords, fingerprints) with your compliance team.
Create a sensor (7.0/6.x): config dlp sensor / edit "block-pii".
Add a blocking filter for cardholder data: config filter / edit 1 / set proto smtp http-post ftp / set filter-by credit-card / set action block / next / end.
(FortiOS 7.2+) Instead define data types and use 'config dlp profile' referencing 'config dlp dictionary' / 'config dlp data-type'.
Save with 'next / end'.
Bind to egress policies: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set dlp-sensor "block-pii" (7.0) or set dlp-profile (7.2+) / set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection" / next / end.
Send a controlled test containing a synthetic card number and confirm the block/log in DLP events.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-005.gui: System > Feature Visibility > enable DLP (if hidden), then Security Profiles > Data Leak Prevention (DLP) > Create New; apply under Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > DLP.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-005.cli:
config dlp sensor
    edit "block-pii"
        config filter
            edit 1
                set proto smtp http-post ftp
                set filter-by credit-card
                set action block
            next
        end
    next
end
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set utm-status enable
        set dlp-sensor "block-pii"
        set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"
    next
end
# FortiOS 7.2+: use 'config dlp profile' + 'config dlp data-type' and 'set dlp-profile' on the policy.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-005.verify: show dlp sensor block-pii (or 'show dlp profile')  -> filter with action block; 'show firewall policy <id> | grep dlp'. Live: transmit a synthetic PAN over an inspected channel and confirm a DLP block in Log & Report > Security Events > DLP.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-005.rollback: Detach with 'config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset dlp-sensor (or unset dlp-profile) / next / end' or delete the sensor. Removing DLP re-exposes the org to silent data exfiltration; if false positives block legitimate transfers, narrow the pattern or set that filter to log-only rather than removing DLP.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-005.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Content inspection for DLP requires deep SSL inspection to see encrypted uploads and adds CPU/latency. Poorly-scoped patterns cause false positives - pilot in log-only, then move critical patterns to block.
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Data leak prevention (DLP) profiles and data types
CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4 Security Profiles (DLP)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
PCI-DSS v4.0 3.x (protect stored/transmitted account data)
NIST SP 800-53 AC-4 Information Flow Enforcement, SC-7 Boundary Protection; HIPAA 164.312(e)(1)
CWE-200 Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

FORTIOS-PROFILE-006.risk: No DNS filter profile exists, so DNS resolution is not inspected and malicious domains are not blocked at the DNS layer - the earliest and cheapest chokepoint in the kill chain. Malware performing C2 domain lookups, DGA beaconing, and phishing-link resolution proceeds unhindered, and DNS tunneling for exfiltration goes unseen. Business impact: C2 establishment, data exfiltration over DNS, and users resolving phishing/malware domains with no early block.
steps: Confirm the gap: 'show dnsfilter profile' is empty and no policy sets dnsfilter-profile.
Ensure the FortiGuard DNS/Web Filtering service is licensed and reachable (System > FortiGuard).
Create a profile: config dnsfilter profile / edit "block-malicious-dns".
Enable botnet C2 domain blocking: set block-botnet enable.
Block malicious FortiGuard DNS categories: config ftgd-dns / config filters / edit 1 / set category 26 / set action block / next / end / end.
Enable DNS query logging for visibility: set sdns-domain-log enable (and set block-action redirect if you want a block page).
Save with 'next / end'.
