Bind to egress policies: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set dnsfilter-profile "block-malicious-dns" / next / end. Ensure clients resolve through the FortiGate (or via FortiGate DNS/DoT).
Validate by resolving a known test-malicious domain and confirm it is blocked/redirected.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-006.gui: Security Profiles > DNS Filter > Create New > enable 'Redirect botnet C&C requests to Block Portal' and FortiGuard category blocking; then apply under Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > DNS Filter.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-006.cli:
config dnsfilter profile
    edit "block-malicious-dns"
        set block-botnet enable
        set sdns-domain-log enable
        config ftgd-dns
            config filters
                edit 1
                    set category 26
                    set action block
                next
            end
        end
    next
end
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set utm-status enable
        set dnsfilter-profile "block-malicious-dns"
    next
end
FORTIOS-PROFILE-006.verify: show dnsfilter profile block-malicious-dns  -> set block-botnet enable and ftgd-dns filters; 'show firewall policy <id> | grep dnsfilter-profile'. Live: resolve a test domain in a blocked category and confirm the DNS Filter block/redirect and log.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-006.rollback: Detach with 'config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset dnsfilter-profile / next / end' or delete the profile. Removing DNS filtering re-opens C2/phishing domain resolution; if a legitimate domain is blocked, add a domain-filter allow entry rather than removing the profile.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-006.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Effective only for DNS that egresses through the FortiGate - clients using external resolvers (8.8.8.8/DoH) bypass it, so also restrict outbound DNS in policy. Category/botnet blocking can affect a wrongly-rated domain; use domain-filter exceptions.
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > DNS filter > Botnet C&C / FortiGuard DNS categories
CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4.3 DNS Filter
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide - block DNS over external resolvers
NIST SP 800-53 SC-7 Boundary Protection, SI-4 System Monitoring
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-PROFILE-007.risk: An SSL/SSH inspection profile is set to certificate-inspection only (not deep/inspect-all), so the FortiGate sees only the certificate/SNI and cannot examine encrypted payloads. Because the overwhelming majority of web and malware traffic is TLS-encrypted, AV, IPS, web filter (URL path), DLP, and file filter are effectively blind on that traffic - malware download, C2, and data exfiltration hide inside TLS. Business impact: the entire UTM stack is bypassed for encrypted flows, which is most of the traffic.
steps: Prerequisite: build/deploy a trusted CA. Use the built-in Fortinet_CA_SSL or a private subordinate CA, and distribute that CA certificate to all managed clients (GPO/MDM) before enforcing, or users will see certificate errors.
Identify the profile: 'show firewall ssl-ssh-profile <name>' and check 'config ssl / set inspect-all' and per-protocol status.
Edit the profile: config firewall ssl-ssh-profile / edit "<name>".
Enable the deep-inspection catch-all: config ssl / set inspect-all deep-inspection / end.
Enable per-protocol deep inspection: config https / set ports 443 / set status deep-inspection / end (and ftps/imaps/pop3s/smtps as needed).
Set the re-sign CA: set server-cert-mode re-sign and set caname "Fortinet_CA_SSL" (or your private CA).
Add exemptions for pinned/regulated categories (banking, health, and cert-pinned apps) via the profile's SSL exemption settings to avoid breakage.
Save with 'next / end' and apply the profile to the target policies.
Pilot on a small user group, validate no cert warnings, then roll out.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-007.gui: Security Profiles > SSL/SSH Inspection > edit/create a profile > select 'Full SSL Inspection (deep inspection)', choose the CA certificate, add exemptions (Reputable Websites/categories); apply under Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > SSL/SSH Inspection.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-007.cli:
config firewall ssl-ssh-profile
    edit "<profile>"
        set server-cert-mode re-sign
        set caname "Fortinet_CA_SSL"
        config ssl
            set inspect-all deep-inspection
        end
        config https
            set ports 443
            set status deep-inspection
        end
    next
end
# Then set on the policy: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set ssl-ssh-profile "<profile>" / next / end
# Distribute the CA (Fortinet_CA_SSL or your private CA) to client trust stores first.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-007.verify: show firewall ssl-ssh-profile <profile> | grep -e inspect-all -e status  -> inspect-all deep-inspection / status deep-inspection. Live: browse an HTTPS site through the policy and confirm the served leaf certificate is signed by the FortiGate CA, and that AV/IPS now inspect the decrypted content.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-007.rollback: Revert with 'config firewall ssl-ssh-profile / edit <profile> / config ssl / set inspect-all certificate-inspection / end / config https / set status certificate-inspection / end / next / end', or switch the policy back to the built-in 'certificate-inspection' profile. Reverting restores blind spots on encrypted traffic; use targeted exemptions instead of full rollback when a specific app breaks.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-007.impact: No reboot and no admin/SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA session drops, but this is operationally impactful: clients that do not trust the FortiGate CA get TLS errors, and certificate-pinned apps (some banking/O365/mobile apps) break unless exempted. Deep inspection also increases CPU/latency. Deploy the CA first and roll out in a maintenance window / phased pilot.
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > SSL & SSH Inspection > Full (deep) inspection; Deploying SSL inspection using a CA
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide - SSL inspection and CA distribution
CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4 Security Profiles (SSL/SSH inspection)
NIST SP 800-53 SC-7 Boundary Protection, SI-4 System Monitoring
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-PROFILE-008.risk: The SSL/SSH inspection profile allows connections to servers presenting invalid certificates (invalid-server-cert=allow, i.e. expired, self-signed, untrusted, or hostname-mismatched). This defeats TLS server authentication: an attacker performing an on-path (MitM) attack with a forged or invalid certificate is permitted through, enabling interception and credential theft on 'secure' connections. Business impact: TLS MitM/interception of user and application traffic, credential and session-token theft.
steps: Identify the profile: 'show firewall ssl-ssh-profile <name>' and review certificate-validation handling (invalid/untrusted/expired/revoked server-cert).
Edit the profile: config firewall ssl-ssh-profile / edit "<name>".

On modern FortiOS, set per-protocol certificate handling to block: config https / set untrusted-server-cert block / set expired-server-cert block / set revoked-server-cert block / set cert-validation-failure block / set cert-validation-timeout block / end.
On FortiOS releases that expose the single legacy key, set: config ssl / set invalid-server-cert block / end.
Repeat for other inspected protocols (ftps, imaps, pop3s, smtps) as applicable - the same keys exist under each protocol subsection.
Keep sni-server-cert-check enabled (default 'enable'/'strict') so SNI/host mismatches are caught.
Save with 'next / end' and confirm the profile is applied to the intended policies.
Test against a site with a deliberately invalid/expired certificate and confirm the connection is blocked.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-008.gui: Security Profiles > SSL/SSH Inspection > edit <profile> > under the server-certificate SSL handshake handling, set the action for invalid/untrusted/expired/revoked server certificates to Block. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > SSL/SSH Inspection.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-008.cli:
config firewall ssl-ssh-profile
    edit "<profile>"
        config https
            set untrusted-server-cert block
            set expired-server-cert block
            set revoked-server-cert block
            set cert-validation-failure block
            set cert-validation-timeout block
        end
    next
end
# On FortiOS versions exposing the legacy single key, the equivalent is:
#   config firewall ssl-ssh-profile / edit "<profile>" / config ssl / set invalid-server-cert block / end / next / end
FORTIOS-PROFILE-008.verify: show firewall ssl-ssh-profile <profile> | grep -e server-cert -e cert-validation  -> the invalid/untrusted/expired handling shows 'block'. Live: browse to a site with an expired/self-signed cert through the policy and confirm the FortiGate blocks it with a certificate-error block page.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-008.rollback: Revert the relevant keys to 'allow' (e.g. 'set untrusted-server-cert allow' or the legacy 'set invalid-server-cert allow'). Reverting re-enables MitM exposure on invalid certificates; if a legitimate internal server uses an untrusted internal CA, import that CA to the FortiGate trust store or add a narrow SSL exemption rather than allowing all invalid certs.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-008.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Enforcing block may break access to internal/legacy sites that use self-signed or private-CA certificates - remediate by trusting the internal CA or exempting that specific destination, not by globally allowing invalid certs.
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > SSL & SSH Inspection > Server certificate handling (untrusted/expired/revoked/invalid server cert)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4 Security Profiles (SSL/SSH inspection - certificate handling)
PCI-DSS v4.0 4.2.1 (strong cryptography and certificate validation)
NIST SP 800-53 SC-8 Transmission Confidentiality/Integrity, SC-23 Session Authenticity
CWE-295 Improper Certificate Validation

FORTIOS-PROFILE-009.risk: No email filter profile is configured, so if SMTP/IMAP/POP3 traffic transits the FortiGate it is not scanned for spam or phishing indicators, and known-bad senders/URLs are not tagged or blocked. Phishing and business-email-compromise lures reach mailboxes without a gateway anti-spam/anti-phishing layer. Business impact: increased phishing/BEC success (the top initial-access vector) where the FortiGate is in the mail path. Note this is LOW severity because many environments route mail through a dedicated secure email gateway.
steps: Determine whether SMTP/IMAP/POP3 actually transits this FortiGate; if a dedicated email security gateway handles mail, document that as a compensating control.
If mail transits the FortiGate, enable the feature: System > Feature Visibility > Email Filter (if hidden).
Create a profile: config emailfilter profile / edit "block-spam" / set feature-set flow.
Enable FortiGuard spam checks: set options spamfsurl spamfsip spamfschksum spamfsphish (URL, IP reputation, checksum, and phishing checks).
Configure SMTP handling: config smtp / set action discard (or tag) / set tag-type subject spaminfo / end; configure imap/pop3 with tag as appropriate.
Enable spam logging: set spam-log enable.
Save with 'next / end'.
Bind to the mail-handling policy: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set emailfilter-profile "block-spam" / next / end.
Monitor Email Filter logs and tune false positives.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-009.gui: System > Feature Visibility > enable Email Filter, then Security Profiles > Email Filter > Create New (enable FortiGuard spam filtering); apply under Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Email Filter.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-009.cli:
config emailfilter profile
    edit "block-spam"
        set feature-set flow
        set spam-log enable
        set options spamfsurl spamfsip spamfschksum spamfsphish
        config smtp
            set action discard
            set tag-type subject spaminfo
        end
    next
end
config firewall policy
    edit <policy-id>
        set utm-status enable
        set emailfilter-profile "block-spam"
    next
end
# Note: the SMTP 'discard' action and subject tagging are most reliable in
# proxy mode; if using feature-set flow and discard is unavailable, use 'set action tag'.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-009.verify: show emailfilter profile block-spam  -> options include spamfsurl/spamfsip and smtp action set; 'show firewall policy <id> | grep emailfilter-profile'. Live: send a GTUBE spam-test message through the mail policy and confirm it is tagged/discarded and logged under Security Events > Email Filter.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-009.rollback: Detach with 'config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset emailfilter-profile / next / end' or delete the profile. Removing email filtering re-exposes mail to spam/phishing at the gateway; if legitimate mail is discarded, switch SMTP action to 'tag' or add sender/domain allowlists rather than removing the profile.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-009.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Only relevant if mail transits this FortiGate; 'discard' can drop legitimate mail on a false positive - start with 'tag' and monitor, then tighten. Requires an active FortiGuard AntiSpam subscription for FortiGuard checks.
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Email filter > Email filter profiles / FortiGuard antispam
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4 Security Profiles (Email Filter / Antispam)
PCI-DSS v4.0 5.2
NIST SP 800-53 SI-8 Spam Protection, SI-3 Malicious Code Protection
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-PROFILE-010.risk: A file filter profile exists but has no rules, so it blocks no file types - dangerous executable and script types (exe, dll, scr, bat, vbs, ps1, js, lnk) pass regardless of their true type (file filter matches by real file type, not just extension). This lets malware droppers and script payloads through even when AV misses them. Business impact: delivery of executable/script malware and defeat of a defense-in-depth control that should hard-block dangerous file categories.
steps: Identify the empty profile: 'show file-filter profile <name>' shows 'config rules' with no entries.
Ensure File Filter is visible/enabled (System > Feature Visibility) and that you reference it within an AV/Web Filter/DLP profile as your FortiOS version requires.
Edit the profile: config file-filter profile / edit "<name>" / set feature-set flow.

Add a blocking rule for executables/scripts: config rules / edit "block-executables" / set protocol http ftp smtp imap pop3 / set direction any / set action block / set file-type "exe" "dll" "scr" "bat" "vbs" "ps1" "lnk" / next / end. Confirm each file-type name exists in your FortiOS file-type list (some, e.g. ps1/lnk, are version-dependent).
Add additional rules for other high-risk types (e.g., password-protected archives) if policy requires.
Save with 'next / end'.
Ensure the file filter is referenced by the security profile applied to the policy and that deep SSL inspection is on for HTTPS coverage.
Test by transferring a benign .exe and confirm it is blocked and logged.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-010.gui: System > Feature Visibility > enable File Filter (if hidden), then Security Profiles > File Filter > edit <profile> > add rules (Action=Block, select dangerous file types); ensure it is applied via the AntiVirus/Web Filter/DLP profile on the policy. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > File Filter.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-010.cli:
config file-filter profile
    edit "<profile>"
        set feature-set flow
        config rules
            edit "block-executables"
                set protocol http ftp smtp imap pop3
                set direction any
                set action block
                set file-type "exe" "dll" "scr" "bat" "vbs" "ps1" "lnk"
            next
        end
    next
end
FORTIOS-PROFILE-010.verify: show file-filter profile <profile> | grep -A5 'config rules'  -> at least one rule with action block and a file-type list. Live: download a benign .exe over an inspected policy and confirm a File Filter block event in Security Events.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-010.rollback: Remove the rule with 'config file-filter profile / edit <profile> / config rules / delete "block-executables" / end / next / end'. Removing rules re-allows dangerous file types; if a legitimate business transfer is blocked, narrow the rule (specific protocol/direction) or add a matching exception rather than clearing all rules.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-010.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Blocking file types can affect legitimate software distribution (e.g., IT pushing installers) - scope direction/protocol accordingly. HTTPS coverage requires deep SSL inspection.
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > File filter > File filter profiles and rules
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4 Security Profiles (File filtering)
NIST SP 800-53 SI-3 Malicious Code Protection, SC-18 Mobile Code
CWE-434 Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type

FORTIOS-PROFILE-011.risk: An ICAP profile is configured without preview mode, so the FortiGate forwards full request/response bodies to the external ICAP server (DLP/AV/CASB) for every transaction. Beyond added latency and ICAP-server load, this increases the chance of processing timeouts and reduces throughput, and can create a fail-open/fail-closed decision point under load. Business impact: performance degradation of inspected traffic and potential availability issues on the ICAP inspection path. This is LOW severity (a performance/robustness hardening item, not a direct exposure).
steps: Identify the profile: 'show icap profile <name>' and confirm 'set preview' is disable.
Confirm the external ICAP server supports preview (RFC 3507 Preview) before enabling.
Edit the profile: config icap profile / edit "<name>".
Enable preview: set preview enable.
Set a sensible preview data length so the ICAP server can decide on the first bytes: set preview-data-length 4 (per your ICAP server's Preview advertisement).
Confirm the request/response server and methods are correct (set request-server / set response-server / set request enable / set response enable).
Save with 'next / end'.
Validate that inspected traffic still works and measure latency improvement, and confirm the ICAP failure action (bypass vs block) matches policy.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-011.gui: Security Profiles > ICAP > edit <profile> > enable Preview and set the preview data length; ensure it is applied on the relevant policy. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > ICAP.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-011.cli:
config icap profile
    edit "<profile>"
        set preview enable
        set preview-data-length 4
    next
end
FORTIOS-PROFILE-011.verify: show icap profile <profile> | grep preview  -> set preview enable / set preview-data-length 4. Validate transactions still complete and observe reduced ICAP round-trips (the ICAP server logs show preview requests).
FORTIOS-PROFILE-011.rollback: Revert with 'config icap profile / edit <profile> / set preview disable / next / end'. Disabling preview returns to sending full bodies (higher latency/load); roll back only if the ICAP server mishandles preview or returns incorrect verdicts on previewed data.
FORTIOS-PROFILE-011.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Preview only works if the ICAP server advertises Preview support; if misconfigured it can cause the ICAP server to request the full body anyway (no benefit) - validate against the specific ICAP vendor.
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > ICAP > ICAP profiles (preview)
RFC 3507 - Internet Content Adaptation Protocol (ICAP), Message Preview
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
NIST SP 800-53 SI-4 System Monitoring
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-RULEBASE-001.risk: A shadowed (dead) policy can never match, because an earlier, broader policy is evaluated first and already covers all of its traffic. The dangerous case is an earlier ALLOW rule shadowing a later DENY rule: traffic the deny rule was meant to block is silently permitted, creating a false sense of security and a real exposure. Even in the benign direction it signals a mis-ordered rule-base that auditors and responders cannot reason about.
steps: Identify the shadowed policy ID and the earlier policy that covers it (both are named in the finding).
Decide the intent: should the shadowed rule's action actually take effect? If yes, it must sit ABOVE the covering rule.
In the GUI drag the shadowed policy above the covering policy, or via CLI: config firewall policy / move <shadowed-id> before <covering-id> / end.
Alternatively, if the shadowed rule is obsolete, remove it: config firewall policy / delete <shadowed-id> / end.
If the covering rule is over-broad (e.g. any-any-any), narrow it to specific objects so intended exceptions can follow it.
Re-run the scanner and confirm the shadow finding is cleared and traffic behaves as intended.
FORTIOS-RULEBASE-001.gui: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy: review the sequence column; drag the shadowed rule above the covering rule, or right-click > Delete if obsolete. FortiManager: Policy Package > reorder/remove, then install.
FORTIOS-RULEBASE-001.cli:
config firewall policy
  move <shadowed-policyid> before <covering-policyid>
end
# or, if the shadowed rule is obsolete:
# config firewall policy
#   delete <shadowed-policyid>
# end
FORTIOS-RULEBASE-001.verify: show firewall policy | grep -A2 'edit <shadowed-policyid>'  and confirm ordering with: get firewall policy  (sequence). Generate live traffic and check the session is matched by the intended policy: diagnose firewall iprope lookup ...
FORTIOS-RULEBASE-001.rollback: Re-run 'move' with the original ordering, or re-create the deleted policy from your config backup. Take a config backup (execute backup config flash) before reordering.
FORTIOS-RULEBASE-001.impact: Reordering/removing a policy changes traffic matching immediately and can allow or block flows that were previously handled differently. Non-disruptive to the device (no reboot) but test in a change window; on an HA cluster the change syncs to the peer.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - firewall rule-base review (CIS 4.1)
Fortinet Administration Guide - Firewall policy sequence / policy lookup
PCI-DSS 1.1.7 (review rule-sets at least every 6 months), NIST SP 800-53 AC-4
CWE-561: Dead Code / CWE-284: Improper Access Control

FORTIOS-RULEBASE-002.risk: A redundant (duplicate) policy matches only traffic already matched by an earlier policy with the same action, so it never changes the outcome. Redundant rules inflate the rule-base, slow audits and troubleshooting, and increase the chance that a future edit to one copy silently diverges from the other, creating an inconsistency.
steps: Identify the redundant policy and the earlier policy that already covers it (named in the finding).
Confirm they truly overlap (same or broader src/dst/service/interfaces and identical action).
Remove the redundant policy: config firewall policy / delete <redundant-id> / end.
If both exist for documentation reasons, consolidate their objects into a single well-named policy instead.
Re-run the scanner to confirm the redundancy is resolved.
FORTIOS-RULEBASE-002.gui: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy: locate the duplicate rule and Delete it, or merge its address/service objects into the retained rule. FortiManager: Policy Package cleanup, then install.
FORTIOS-RULEBASE-002.verify: get firewall policy | grep <redundant-policyid>  ->  no longer present; traffic still matches the retained policy (diagnose firewall iprope lookup).
FORTIOS-RULEBASE-002.rollback: Re-create the policy from your config backup (execute backup config flash beforehand).
FORTIOS-RULEBASE-002.impact: Non-disruptive if the retained policy genuinely covers the same traffic with the same action; no reboot. Verify object membership before deleting so you do not remove a rule that was subtly narrower.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - rule-base hygiene (CIS 4.1)
Fortinet Administration Guide - Firewall policy consolidation
PCI-DSS 1.1.7, NIST SP 800-53 CM-7 (least functionality)
CWE-1164: Irrelevant Code

FORTIOS-RULEBASE-SCORE.risk: The Policy Control Index is a 0-100 summary of firewall rule-base hygiene (higher is better). A low grade means the allow rule-base is permissive, poorly logged, under-protected by UTM, and/or cluttered with dead rules - the exact conditions in which a single mistake becomes an exposure and in which incidents are hard to detect and investigate. Rule-base misconfiguration, not firewall software, is the dominant cause of firewall-related breaches.
steps: Read the breakdown in the finding: any-any-any rules, broadly-permissive rules, logging coverage, UTM coverage, and shadowed/redundant counts.
Tighten permissive rules: replace 'all' source/destination and 'ALL' service with specific address and service objects (least privilege).
Enable traffic logging on every allow rule: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set logtraffic all / next / end.
Apply UTM security profiles (AV, IPS, Web Filter, App Control, SSL inspection) to every internet-facing allow rule.
Remove the shadowed and redundant rules flagged separately by the scanner.
Re-run the scanner and confirm the index moves toward grade A (>= 90).
FORTIOS-RULEBASE-SCORE.gui: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy: edit each broad/allow rule to add specific objects, logging, and UTM profiles; use the Policy sequence view to spot dead rules. FortiManager Policy Optimizer assists at scale.
FORTIOS-RULEBASE-SCORE.cli:
config firewall policy
  edit <policyid>
    set srcaddr <specific-address>
    set dstaddr <specific-address>
    set service <specific-service>
    set logtraffic all
    set av-profile default
    set ips-sensor default
    set ssl-ssh-profile deep-inspection
  next
end
FORTIOS-RULEBASE-SCORE.verify: Re-run the scanner: the Policy Control Index rises and the count of permissive / unlogged / unprotected rules falls. Spot-check with: show firewall policy | grep -E 'logtraffic|av-profile|srcaddr'.
FORTIOS-RULEBASE-SCORE.rollback: Revert individual policy edits from your config backup if a tightened rule breaks a required flow; loosen one object at a time rather than reverting logging/UTM.
FORTIOS-RULEBASE-SCORE.impact: Tightening rules can block flows that were implicitly allowed by an over-broad rule - stage changes and test. Enabling logging/UTM adds some CPU/logging load. No reboot; changes sync on HA.
references: Gartner/industry: the majority of firewall breaches stem from misconfiguration, not the firewall
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - firewall policies (section 4)
PCI-DSS 1.2.1 / 1.1.7, NIST SP 800-53 AC-4 & CM-7
CWE-1120: Excessive Code Complexity (rule-base complexity)

FORTIOS-SSLVPN-001.risk: The SSL-VPN daemon accepts TLS 1.1 / TLS 1.0 / SSLv3, which are deprecated and carry known cryptographic breaks (BEAST, POODLE, weak RC4/CBC constructions). An attacker positioned on-path (rogue Wi-Fi, ISP, compromised transit) can downgrade a remote-worker session to the weakest mutually-supported version and mount a padding-oracle or record-splitting attack to recover session cookies or credentials, then ride the authenticated VPN tunnel into the corporate LAN. Because SSL-VPN is Internet-facing, this is a direct, unauthenticated exposure of the crypto stack; it also fails PCI-DSS 4.x strong-cryptography requirements and undermines every downstream control that trusts the tunnel.
steps: Confirm the current minimum protocol: get vpn ssl settings | grep ssl-min-proto-ver (on 6.2+) or show vpn ssl settings (on 6.0/legacy, look at the sslv3/tlsv1-x toggles).
Notify remote users; very old endpoints (Windows 7 without TLS 1.2, legacy FortiClient) may lose access once weak versions are removed.
Enter SSL-VPN settings: config vpn ssl settings.
On FortiOS 6.2 and later set the floor to TLS 1.2: set ssl-min-proto-ver tls1-2 (use tls1-3 only if all clients support it).

On FortiOS 6.0/legacy where per-version toggles exist, disable the weak versions and enable strong ones: set sslv3 disable / set tlsv1-0 disable / set tlsv1-1 disable / set tlsv1-2 enable / set tlsv1-3 enable.
Also raise the device-wide floor so admin GUI and other TLS services match: config system global / set ssl-static-key-ciphers disable / set strong-crypto enable / end.
Commit with end.
Re-run get vpn ssl settings and confirm the minimum version, then test a FortiClient and browser (web-mode) login.
Validate externally with: nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p <sslvpn-port> <public-ip> (or openssl s_client -tls1_1 -connect <ip>:<port> should fail).
Record the change and monitor SSL-VPN auth logs for clients now failing to negotiate.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-001.gui: VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > (there is no direct min-TLS field in the GUI on most versions; use CLI). For the device-wide crypto floor: System > Settings, but enabling strong crypto (strong-crypto) is CLI-only. FortiManager: Device Manager > Device & Groups > <device> > install a CLI Template / CLI Configuration containing the 'config vpn ssl settings' block, then Install Wizard to push.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-001.cli:
config vpn ssl settings
  set sslv3 disable
  set tlsv1-0 disable
  set tlsv1-1 disable
  set tlsv1-2 enable
  set tlsv1-3 enable
end
# FortiOS 6.2+ equivalent single knob:
# config vpn ssl settings
#   set ssl-min-proto-ver tls1-2
# end
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-001.verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep ssl-min-proto-ver   -> ssl-min-proto-ver: tls1-2  (on 6.0/legacy: show vpn ssl settings shows 'set tlsv1-1 disable' and 'set tlsv1-2 enable'). External check: openssl s_client -tls1_1 -connect <ip>:<port> must fail to handshake.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-001.rollback: Revert with config vpn ssl settings / set ssl-min-proto-ver tls1-1 (or re-enable set tlsv1-1 enable). Rolling back re-opens the downgrade exposure. Only needed if a business-critical legacy endpoint cannot be upgraded; prefer isolating that endpoint instead of lowering the floor for everyone.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-001.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot, no dataplane restart). Existing established SSL-VPN sessions continue; only NEW negotiations are affected. Clients that can only speak TLS 1.0/1.1 will be unable to reconnect until upgraded. No effect on IPsec, HA, or admin sessions.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark 5.1.1 (per scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 4.2.1 (strong cryptography for transmission); mapped PCI 4.1
NIST SP 800-52 Rev.2 (TLS server config); NIST 800-53 SC-8
FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - SSL-VPN / 'Hardening your FortiGate' (SSL VPN best practices)
CWE-326: Inadequate Encryption Strength

FORTIOS-SSLVPN-002.risk: SSL-VPN is listening on a well-known default port (443 or 10443). Internet-wide scanners (Shodan, Censys) fingerprint the FortiGate SSL-VPN login page instantly on these ports, placing the device in mass target lists for credential-stuffing and, more importantly, for weaponized pre-auth SSL-VPN exploits (e.g. CVE-2022-42475, CVE-2023-27997, CVE-2024-21762). Default-port exposure does not itself grant access, but it maximizes discoverability and the speed at which an unpatched or weakly-authenticated gateway is found and hit. Port 443 also collides with any published HTTPS VIP, forcing awkward host-based routing.
steps: Confirm current port: get vpn ssl settings | grep '^port'.

Choose a non-standard high port that does not collide with other VIPs/services (avoid common alternates like 8443; prefer a less-common high port such as 20443) and open it inbound on the perimeter/upstream firewall.
Communicate the new port to users and update FortiClient EMS profiles / VPN bookmarks (URL becomes https://<fqdn>:<newport>).
Change the port: config vpn ssl settings / set port <new-port> / end.
Ensure the GUI admin port does not conflict: get system global | grep admin-sport (relocate admin-sport if it clashes).
Update any external DNAT / load-balancer / upstream ACL to forward the new port to the FortiGate.
Restrict source exposure further where feasible: bind SSL-VPN to specific source geographies/addresses via firewall local-in policy or 'source-address' in authentication rules.
Reconnect a test client on the new port and confirm login.
Re-run get vpn ssl settings | grep '^port' to confirm.
Monitor for lingering scan/hits on the old port and confirm they now fail.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-002.gui: VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > 'Listen on Port' field. FortiManager: Device Manager > Device & Groups > <device> > VPN SSL Settings (or a CLI Template with the 'config vpn ssl settings / set port' block), then Install Wizard.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-002.cli:
config vpn ssl settings
  set port <non-standard-port>
end
# Example: set port 20443  (ensure it does not collide with admin-sport or any VIP)
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-002.verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep '^port'   -> port: 20443  (any value other than 443/10443). Externally confirm the old port no longer answers and the new port serves the SSL-VPN login.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-002.rollback: config vpn ssl settings / set port 10443 / end. Rolling back restores default-port discoverability. Note: any client, bookmark, or EMS profile pinned to the new port must be updated again on rollback or users lose access.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-002.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot). Existing sessions on the old port drop when the listener moves; every client must reconnect using the new port and any FortiClient/EMS profile and upstream firewall/NAT rule must be updated first or remote access breaks. No effect on IPsec/HA/admin.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark 5.1.2 (per scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - SSL-VPN settings (Listen on Interface/Port)
Fortinet PSIRT SSL-VPN advisories: FG-IR-22-398 (CVE-2022-42475), FG-IR-23-097 (CVE-2023-27997), FG-IR-24-015 (CVE-2024-21762) - illustrate why minimizing SSL-VPN exposure matters
CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

FORTIOS-SSLVPN-003.risk: The SSL-VPN idle timeout exceeds 600 seconds, so an authenticated tunnel stays alive long after the user walks away. On a shared, kiosk, or stolen/unlocked laptop an attacker inherits a live, fully-authorized VPN session into the internal network without ever seeing a credential or MFA prompt - a classic session-hijack / unattended-session escalation. Long idle windows also increase the value of a stolen session cookie and give lateral-movement tooling more time before a session naturally expires.
steps: Check the current value: get vpn ssl settings | grep idle-timeout.
Decide a policy value (CIS/industry guidance is 300 seconds or less for remote-access idle).
Enter settings: config vpn ssl settings.
Set the idle timeout: set idle-timeout 300 (seconds; default 300).
Optionally cap total session length as well: set auth-timeout <seconds> to force periodic re-authentication.
Commit: end.

NOTE - the scanner's stored REMEDIATION_COMMANDS entry for FORTIOS-SSLVPN-003 is 'set reqclientcert enable', which is a DIFFERENT hardening (client-certificate requirement) and does NOT fix idle timeout; use 'set idle-timeout' as authored here. Requiring client certs (set reqclientcert enable) is a good complementary control but is not the remediation for this rule.
Verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep idle-timeout returns 300.
Log in with a test client, leave it idle past 5 minutes, and confirm the tunnel drops automatically (diagnose vpn ssl list shows the session removed).
Communicate the shorter timeout to users so expected re-logins are not raised as incidents.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-003.gui: VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > 'Idle Logout' > enable and set 'Inactive For' to 300 seconds. FortiManager: Device Manager > <device> > VPN SSL Settings, or a CLI Template with the 'config vpn ssl settings / set idle-timeout 300' block.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-003.cli:
config vpn ssl settings
  set idle-timeout 300
end
# NOTE: scanner REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-SSLVPN-003'] currently stores
# 'config vpn ssl settings / set reqclientcert enable' which is mismatched to this
# rule (that command enforces client certs, not idle timeout). The correct fix for
# 'idle timeout too long' is 'set idle-timeout 300' as shown above.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-003.verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep idle-timeout   -> idle-timeout: 300   (must be <= 600, ideally <= 300).
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-003.rollback: config vpn ssl settings / set idle-timeout <previous-value> / end. Increasing it again restores the unattended-session risk. Users on high-latency/intermittent links who complain of premature disconnects should be handled by keepalive tuning, not by widening the idle window.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-003.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot). Applies to new idle evaluations immediately; users idle beyond the new threshold will be logged out and must re-authenticate. No effect on active, in-use sessions, IPsec, HA, or admin.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark 5.1.3 (per scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 8.2.8 (idle session re-authentication after 15 min); mapped PCI 8.3.1
NIST 800-53 AC-11 (Session Lock) / AC-12 (Session Termination); mapped IA-2
FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - SSL-VPN settings (Idle Logout / idle-timeout, auth-timeout)
CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration

FORTIOS-SSLVPN-004.risk: SSL-VPN allows more than 5 failed logins before locking (login-attempt-limit too high). Combined with an Internet-facing login portal this invites automated password-spraying and credential-stuffing: attackers try common passwords across many accounts, and a permissive attempt limit means each account can absorb many guesses before block-out. Successful guessing yields a full VPN foothold; even unsuccessful spraying can trigger MFA-fatigue and lock legitimate users. This is one of the most common initial-access vectors against remote-access gateways.
steps: Check the current limit: get vpn ssl settings | grep login-attempt-limit.
Enter settings: config vpn ssl settings.
Lower the per-connection attempt ceiling: set login-attempt-limit 3 (default is 2; keep it <= 3).
Set/confirm the lockout duration applied after the limit is hit: set login-block-time 60 (seconds; increase to slow spraying further).
Commit: end.
Layer defense-in-depth: ensure MFA is enforced (see FORTIOS-SSLVPN-006) so guessed passwords alone are insufficient.
Restrict SSL-VPN source exposure with geo/IP local-in policy where feasible to shrink the attacker population.
Verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep login-attempt-limit returns 3.
Test: deliberately fail logins from a lab client and confirm block-out engages after 3 tries.
Enable/verify SSL-VPN authentication event logging and alerting on repeated failures (VPN event logs).
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-004.gui: There is no dedicated GUI field for login-attempt-limit; configure via CLI (VPN > SSL-VPN Settings does not expose it). FortiManager: push via a CLI Template / CLI Configuration containing the 'config vpn ssl settings' block, then Install Wizard.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-004.cli:
config vpn ssl settings
  set login-attempt-limit 3
  set login-block-time 60
end
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-004.verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep -E 'login-attempt-limit|login-block-time'   -> login-attempt-limit: 3  and  login-block-time: 60   (attempt-limit must be <= 5, recommended <= 3).
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-004.rollback: config vpn ssl settings / set login-attempt-limit <previous> / end. Raising the limit again re-widens the brute-force window. If legitimate users are being locked out, address root cause (password resets, MFA UX) rather than raising the limit.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-004.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot). Affects new login attempts immediately; a user who fat-fingers the password more than 3 times will be temporarily blocked for login-block-time. No effect on established sessions, IPsec, HA, or admin.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - SSL VPN authentication hardening (no explicit sub-section mapped in scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 8.3.4 (limit repeated access attempts)
NIST 800-53 AC-7 (Unsuccessful Logon Attempts)
FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - SSL-VPN settings (login-attempt-limit, login-block-time); 'Hardening your FortiGate'
CWE-307: Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts

FORTIOS-SSLVPN-005.risk: A portal enables split tunneling, so only corporate-bound traffic goes through the VPN while the client's general Internet traffic egresses locally, bypassing the FortiGate's web filter, IPS, AV, and DNS controls. A remote endpoint can be simultaneously connected to the corporate LAN and browsing a malicious/compromised site; malware pulled over the unfiltered local path can pivot straight through the tunnel into internal resources, and data can be exfiltrated over the local egress with no inspection or logging. Split tunneling also blinds the SOC to the endpoint's full traffic during the VPN session.
steps: Identify affected portals: get vpn ssl web portal <name> | grep split-tunneling (repeat per portal, or 'show vpn ssl web portal').
Decide policy: full-tunnel (all traffic inspected) is the secure default; if split tunneling must remain for bandwidth reasons, tightly scope it to defined destinations.
Enter the portal: config vpn ssl web portal / edit <portal-name>.
To force full tunneling, disable split: set split-tunneling disable.

Ensure a default route/gateway is pushed so full-tunnel clients have Internet via the FortiGate: verify the tunnel IP pool and that outbound firewall policy from ssl.root allows and inspects client traffic.

If split tunneling is a hard requirement, instead constrain it: set split-tunneling enable / set split-tunneling-routing-address <address-object> (only listed subnets use the tunnel) and document the exception.
Commit: next / end.
Confirm the FortiGate has an outbound policy from the SSL-VPN interface (ssl.root) to WAN with UTM inspection profiles for the now-full-tunnel traffic.
Verify: get vpn ssl web portal <name> | grep split-tunneling returns disable.
Test with a client - confirm Internet browsing now traverses and is logged by the FortiGate (check forward traffic logs).
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-005.gui: VPN > SSL-VPN Portals > edit <portal> > 'Enable Split Tunneling' (uncheck to force full tunnel; or choose 'Based on Policy Destination' / select routing addresses to scope it). FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > VPN > SSL-VPN Portals, or a per-device CLI Template.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-005.cli:
config vpn ssl web portal
  edit <portal-name>
    set split-tunneling disable
  next
end
# To scope instead of fully disable:
# config vpn ssl web portal
#   edit <portal-name>
#     set split-tunneling enable
#     set split-tunneling-routing-address <addr-object>
#   next
# end
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-005.verify: get vpn ssl web portal <portal-name> | grep split-tunneling   -> split-tunneling: disable   (or, if scoped, split-tunneling: enable with a non-empty split-tunneling-routing-address).
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-005.rollback: config vpn ssl web portal / edit <portal> / set split-tunneling enable / next / end. Re-enabling restores the inspection-bypass/exfil risk. Watch for user bandwidth complaints after moving to full-tunnel; if the FortiGate WAN uplink becomes a bottleneck, scope split tunneling to specific subnets rather than fully re-enabling.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-005.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot). Takes effect on the next client connection/reconnect for that portal. Full-tunnel routing pushes all client Internet traffic through the FortiGate - expect increased WAN utilization and dependency on the FortiGate's Internet path; existing sessions keep their prior routing until reconnect. No effect on IPsec/HA/admin.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - SSL VPN portal hardening (no explicit sub-section mapped in scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
NIST 800-53 SC-7 (Boundary Protection) / AC-4 (Information Flow Enforcement)
FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - SSL-VPN portals (split tunneling, split-tunneling-routing-address); 'Hardening your FortiGate'
CWE-284: Improper Access Control

FORTIOS-SSLVPN-006.risk: A VPN-associated user group has members but no verifiable MFA/second-factor enforcement. Remote access protected only by a username and password is the single highest-value target for attackers: a phished, reused, or infostealer-harvested password grants a full authenticated tunnel into the internal network with no additional challenge. Nearly every major VPN-related breach begins with valid but stolen single-factor credentials; without MFA there is no compensating control between a leaked password and internal network access.
steps: Identify the VPN group and members: show user group <group-name>.
Confirm how members authenticate (local users, LDAP, RADIUS): show user group <group-name> | grep -E 'member|match'.

For LOCAL users, enable a second factor per user: config user local / edit <user> / set two-factor fortitoken / set fortitoken <serial> / set email-to <addr> (or set two-factor email/sms) / next / end.
Assign/activate FortiTokens: config user fortitoken / edit <serial> / set status active / next / end (hardware or FortiToken Mobile).

For LDAP/AD back-ends, front authentication with FortiAuthenticator/RADIUS that enforces MFA, then point the group at it: config user radius / edit <radius-mfa> / ... / end and reference it in the group.

Bind the MFA-protected group to the SSL-VPN portal via authentication rules: config vpn ssl settings / config authentication-rule / edit 1 / set groups <group-name> / set portal <portal> / next / end.
Remove any local-password-only fallback path to the same portal.
Commit all blocks with end.
Verify a member now gets a token/OTP prompt at login (diagnose vpn ssl list confirms the session and the auth logs show two-factor).
Roll out to all VPN users and document token distribution/recovery procedures.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-006.gui: User & Authentication > User Definition > edit user > enable 'Two-factor Authentication' and assign a FortiToken; User & Authentication > User Groups to confirm membership; VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > Authentication/Portal Mapping to bind the group. RADIUS/FortiAuthenticator MFA under User & Authentication > RADIUS Servers. FortiManager: User & Device / RADIUS objects in Policy & Objects, plus per-device SSL-VPN authentication-rule via CLI Template.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-006.cli:
config user local
  edit <vpn-user>
    set type password
    set two-factor fortitoken
    set fortitoken <token-serial>
    set email-to <user-email>
  next
end
config vpn ssl settings
  config authentication-rule
    edit 1
      set groups <vpn-group-name>
      set portal <portal-name>
    next
  end
end
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-006.verify: show user local <vpn-user> | grep two-factor   -> set two-factor fortitoken   ; and at login the client is prompted for a FortiToken/OTP code. diagnose vpn ssl list shows the authenticated session only after the second factor.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-006.rollback: Remove the second factor: config user local / edit <user> / unset two-factor / next / end (and detach the authentication-rule if added). Rolling back reduces remote access to single-factor - a high-risk state. If token delivery fails for a user, issue a temporary token or use an emergency break-glass account with tight logging rather than disabling MFA group-wide.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-006.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot). New logins immediately require the second factor; users without a provisioned token cannot authenticate until enrolled, so stage token distribution before enforcing. Existing sessions are unaffected until they reconnect. No effect on IPsec/HA/admin sessions.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - remote access / two-factor authentication (mapped to auth controls; no explicit SSL-VPN-006 sub-section in scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 8.4.2 / 8.5 (MFA for all remote/network access)
NIST 800-53 IA-2(1)/IA-2(2) (MFA to privileged/non-privileged accounts)
FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - FortiToken / two-factor authentication; 'Hardening your FortiGate' (require MFA for SSL-VPN)
CWE-308: Use of Single-factor Authentication

FORTIOS-SSLVPN-007.risk: No tunnel-mode IP pool is configured, so tunnel-mode SSL-VPN clients may fail to receive a proper, controlled internal address - or fall back to an unplanned/overlapping range. Beyond the availability problem (clients can't route), the absence of a dedicated, documented client subnet undermines segmentation and policy control: firewall policies, logging, and threat attribution all rely on knowing that 'this address range == remote VPN users.' Without a distinct pool you cannot cleanly scope what VPN clients may reach, and you lose the ability to isolate/rate-limit that population.
steps: Confirm the gap: get vpn ssl settings | grep tunnel-ip-pools (empty means unset).
Choose a dedicated client subnet that does not overlap any internal LAN/DMZ (e.g. 10.212.134.0/24 style, non-conflicting).
Create the address-range object: config firewall address / edit SSLVPN_TUNNEL_POOL / set type iprange / set start-ip <first> / set end-ip <last> / next / end.
Assign it globally: config vpn ssl settings / set tunnel-ip-pools SSLVPN_TUNNEL_POOL / end.
Also assign it at the portal for tunnel mode: config vpn ssl web portal / edit <portal> / set tunnel-mode enable / set ip-pools SSLVPN_TUNNEL_POOL / next / end.
Ensure no route/subnet overlap: verify the pool subnet is unique across all interfaces and IPsec selectors.
Write firewall policy scoped to the pool as source (ssl.root -> internal) with appropriate destinations and UTM inspection.
Commit all blocks (end).
Verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep tunnel-ip-pools shows the object; connect a tunnel-mode client and confirm it receives an address in the pool (diagnose vpn ssl list).
Document the pool subnet in the network IPAM/segmentation records.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-007.gui: VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > 'Address Range' > choose 'Specify custom IP ranges' and select/create the pool; VPN > SSL-VPN Portals > edit <portal> > 'Tunnel Mode' > 'Source IP Pools'. Address object under Policy & Objects > Addresses. FortiManager: Policy & Objects for the address object and portal, per-device SSL-VPN settings via CLI Template.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-007.cli:
config firewall address
  edit SSLVPN_TUNNEL_POOL
    set type iprange
    set start-ip 10.212.134.100
    set end-ip 10.212.134.200
  next
end
config vpn ssl settings
  set tunnel-ip-pools SSLVPN_TUNNEL_POOL
end
config vpn ssl web portal
  edit <portal-name>
    set tunnel-mode enable
    set ip-pools SSLVPN_TUNNEL_POOL
  next
end
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-007.verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep tunnel-ip-pools   -> tunnel-ip-pools: SSLVPN_TUNNEL_POOL   ; connect tunnel-mode client, then diagnose vpn ssl list shows the client with an IP inside the configured range.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-007.rollback: Remove the assignment: config vpn ssl settings / unset tunnel-ip-pools / end (and unset ip-pools on the portal). Rolling back returns tunnel-mode clients to no/uncontrolled addressing. If the chosen pool overlaps an internal subnet you will cause routing conflicts - fix by re-scoping the pool, not by removing it.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-007.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot). New tunnel-mode connections pick up the pool on next connect; web-mode users are unaffected. Poorly chosen (overlapping) pool subnets can cause client routing problems - validate uniqueness first. No effect on IPsec/HA/admin.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - SSL VPN tunnel mode configuration (no explicit sub-section mapped in scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
NIST 800-53 SC-7 (Boundary Protection) / AC-4 (Information Flow)
FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - SSL-VPN tunnel mode / IP pools; SSL-VPN full tunnel setup guide
CWE-284: Improper Access Control

FORTIOS-SSLVPN-008.risk: No DNS servers are pushed to SSL-VPN clients, so connected clients keep resolving names via their local/ISP resolver. This causes DNS leakage (internal hostnames or the fact of corporate access are exposed to third-party resolvers), breaks split-horizon resolution (clients get external IPs for internal services and may bypass the VPN path), and removes the FortiGate/internal resolver's ability to apply DNS filtering, logging, and threat-intel blocking to the remote endpoint. An attacker running a rogue local resolver could also selectively poison responses for the VPN user.
steps: Confirm the gap: get vpn ssl settings | grep -E 'dns-server1|dns-server2'.
Identify the internal/corporate DNS resolvers (and internal DNS suffix) that VPN clients should use.
Enter settings: config vpn ssl settings.
Push primary and secondary DNS: set dns-server1 <internal-dns-1> / set dns-server2 <internal-dns-2>.
Push the internal search domain so short names resolve: set dns-suffix <corp.example.com>.
Optionally push WINS if legacy: set wins-server1 <ip> (only if required).
Commit: end.
If using full tunnel, ensure firewall policy permits client-to-DNS (UDP/TCP 53) to the internal resolvers.

Verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep dns-server shows the configured IPs; connect a client and run nslookup <internal-host> confirming it resolves via the pushed server (ipconfig /all on the tunnel adapter shows the corporate DNS).
Confirm DNS queries from the client now appear in internal DNS/DNS-filter logs (no leak to ISP resolver).
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-008.gui: VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > 'DNS Server' > Specify (enter Primary/Secondary DNS and DNS suffix). FortiManager: per-device SSL-VPN settings via CLI Template / Device Manager VPN SSL Settings.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-008.cli:
config vpn ssl settings
  set dns-server1 <internal-dns-1>
  set dns-server2 <internal-dns-2>
  set dns-suffix <corp.example.com>
end
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-008.verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep -E 'dns-server1|dns-server2'   -> dns-server1: 10.10.10.10  dns-server2: 10.10.10.11 . On a connected client, the VPN adapter's DNS matches these and nslookup of an internal-only record succeeds.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-008.rollback: config vpn ssl settings / unset dns-server1 / unset dns-server2 / unset dns-suffix / end. Rolling back reintroduces DNS-leak and split-horizon resolution problems. If the pushed servers are unreachable for full-tunnel clients, verify firewall policy to the DNS servers rather than removing the setting.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-008.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot). New client connections receive the DNS config on connect; existing sessions keep prior settings until reconnect. If the specified resolvers are unreachable from the client's tunnel path, name resolution can fail for VPN users - validate reachability. No effect on IPsec/HA/admin.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - SSL VPN client configuration (no explicit sub-section mapped in scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
NIST 800-53 SC-20/SC-21 (secure name resolution)
FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - SSL-VPN settings (DNS server, DNS suffix); SSL-VPN full tunnel guide
CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor (DNS leak)

FORTIOS-SSLVPN-009.risk: DTLS tunnel is disabled, so SSL-VPN tunnel-mode traffic runs over TCP-based TLS instead of UDP-based DTLS. This is primarily a performance/resilience issue rather than a direct security exposure: TLS-over-TCP inside a TCP tunnel suffers 'TCP meltdown' (nested retransmission) on lossy or high-latency links, degrading VoIP/RDP/real-time app performance for remote users. Poor VPN performance drives users toward risky workarounds (personal hotspots, unmanaged tools, disabling the VPN), which indirectly weakens the security posture. Severity is INFO.
steps: Confirm the current state: get vpn ssl settings | grep dtls-tunnel.
Confirm client compatibility: FortiClient tunnel mode supports DTLS; ensure the deployed FortiClient/EMS versions have DTLS enabled.
Ensure UDP for the SSL-VPN port is permitted end-to-end (perimeter firewall must allow UDP on the SSL-VPN port for DTLS, in addition to TCP).
Enter settings: config vpn ssl settings.
Enable DTLS: set dtls-tunnel enable.
Optionally tune the DTLS handshake fallback timeout: set dtls-hello-timeout <seconds> (leave default unless issues).
Commit: end.
On FortiClient, confirm 'Preferred DTLS Tunnel' is enabled in the VPN profile/EMS.
Verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep dtls-tunnel returns enable; reconnect a client and confirm the tunnel negotiates DTLS (client status / diagnose vpn ssl list).
Spot-check real-time app performance improvement on a lossy link.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-009.gui: No dedicated toggle in VPN > SSL-VPN Settings on many builds - configure via CLI. On FortiClient/EMS: VPN profile > 'Preferred DTLS Tunnel'. FortiManager: per-device SSL-VPN settings via CLI Template.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-009.cli:
config vpn ssl settings
  set dtls-tunnel enable
end
# Ensure upstream firewall permits UDP on the SSL-VPN port for DTLS.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-009.verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep dtls-tunnel   -> dtls-tunnel: enable . With a connected FortiClient, the session negotiates DTLS (UDP) when UDP is reachable; it falls back to TLS/TCP automatically otherwise.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-009.rollback: config vpn ssl settings / set dtls-tunnel disable / end. Rolling back forces all tunnel traffic back to TLS/TCP with the associated performance penalty on lossy links but no loss of confidentiality. Disable if a specific client/path has DTLS negotiation problems.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-009.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot). Applies on next client reconnect. Requires UDP on the SSL-VPN port to be open upstream; if UDP is blocked, clients silently fall back to TCP (no outage, just no benefit). No effect on IPsec/HA/admin. Informational/performance-oriented, not a confidentiality fix.
references: Fortinet Knowledge Base - SSL-VPN DTLS tunnel / performance tuning
FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - SSL-VPN settings (dtls-tunnel, dtls-hello-timeout); FortiClient EMS SSL-VPN profile
CWE-693: Protection Mechanism Failure (defense-in-depth/availability consideration)

FORTIOS-SSLVPN-010.risk: No cipher suites are explicitly banned for SSL-VPN, so the negotiation set can include weak or non-forward-secret suites (static RSA key exchange, 3DES, SHA-1-based MACs). An on-path attacker can force selection of the weakest mutually-supported suite to enable record-decryption, birthday-bound (Sweet32 on 3DES), or downgrade attacks; static-RSA suites also mean a single stolen server private key retroactively decrypts all captured sessions (no forward secrecy). For an Internet-facing remote-access service handling credentials and internal data, leaving weak suites negotiable is a meaningful confidentiality exposure.
steps: Check current bans: get vpn ssl settings | grep banned-cipher.
Review which suites are actually negotiated externally: nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p <sslvpn-port> <ip> to see 3DES/SHA1/static-RSA presence.
Enter settings: config vpn ssl settings.

Ban weak/non-PFS families using VALID FortiOS tokens (valid set: RSA DHE ECDHE DSS ECDSA AES AESGCM CAMELLIA 3DES SHA1 SHA256 SHA384 STATIC CHACHA20 ARIA AES128 AES256): set banned-cipher 3DES STATIC RSA (bans 3DES, static ciphers, and static-RSA key exchange to force ECDHE/DHE forward secrecy).

NOTE - the scanner's inline recommendation 'set banned-cipher RSA DES-CBC-SHA RC4 NULL' is NOT valid: DES-CBC-SHA, RC4, and NULL are not accepted banned-cipher tokens on FortiOS. Use only the tokens listed in step 4.
Pair with the TLS floor from FORTIOS-SSLVPN-001 (tls1-2 minimum) and device-wide set strong-crypto enable.
Commit: end.
Re-scan with nmap ssl-enum-ciphers and confirm 3DES/SHA1/static suites are gone and only strong PFS suites remain.
Test that current FortiClient and supported browsers still connect (avoid banning families your clients require, e.g. do not ban AES/AESGCM).
Document the banned list and re-test after each FortiOS upgrade (default suite set can change).
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-010.gui: No GUI field for banned-cipher - CLI only (VPN > SSL-VPN Settings does not expose it). FortiManager: per-device SSL-VPN settings via CLI Template / CLI Configuration, then Install Wizard.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-010.cli:
config vpn ssl settings
  set banned-cipher 3DES STATIC RSA
end
# Valid banned-cipher tokens: RSA DHE ECDHE DSS ECDSA AES AESGCM CAMELLIA 3DES
#   SHA1 SHA256 SHA384 STATIC CHACHA20 ARIA AES128 AES256
# Do NOT use 'RC4', 'NULL', or 'DES-CBC-SHA' - these are not accepted tokens.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-010.verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep banned-cipher   -> banned-cipher: 3DES STATIC RSA . External confirmation: nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p <port> <ip> no longer lists 3DES/SHA1/static-RSA suites.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-010.rollback: config vpn ssl settings / unset banned-cipher / end. Rolling back re-permits weak suites (Sweet32/no-PFS exposure). If a legacy client can no longer connect, remove only the specific family it needs (e.g. keep 3DES banned but do not ban RSA) rather than clearing the whole list.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-010.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot). Applies to new TLS negotiations immediately. Over-aggressive banning (e.g. banning AES or all RSA) can lock out clients - ban only weak/non-PFS families and test. Existing sessions continue until renegotiation. No effect on IPsec/HA/admin.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - SSL VPN cipher hardening (no explicit sub-section mapped in scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 4.2.1 (strong cryptography); Sweet32 (CVE-2016-2183, 3DES)
NIST SP 800-52 Rev.2 (approved cipher suites); NIST 800-53 SC-8/SC-13
FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - SSL-VPN settings (banned-cipher); 'Hardening your FortiGate'
CWE-326: Inadequate Encryption Strength

FORTIOS-SSLVPN-011.risk: A web-mode SSL-VPN portal does not enforce host checking, so any endpoint - a personal, unpatched, or already-compromised machine, or a public kiosk - can authenticate and reach internal resources through the browser portal. Without an endpoint-posture gate (AV present, firewall on, required registry/process, certificate) the FortiGate cannot distinguish a managed corporate laptop from an attacker's box holding stolen credentials. This is a direct path for infostealer-harvested or phished credentials to yield internal access from an untrusted device, and web-mode portals specifically have been targeted by SSL-VPN vulnerabilities and credential theft (e.g. CVE-2018-13379 harvested exactly these portal credentials).
steps: List web-mode portals and their host-check state: get vpn ssl web portal <name> | grep -E 'web-mode|host-check'.
Decide the posture requirement (AV running, firewall enabled, or a custom check / client certificate).
Enter the portal: config vpn ssl web portal / edit <portal>.
Enable host checking with a real value (valid values: none | av | fw | av-fw | custom): set host-check av-fw (requires AV and firewall on the endpoint).

NOTE - the scanner's inline recommendation 'set host-check enable' is NOT valid syntax; host-check takes none/av/fw/av-fw/custom, not enable. For a tailored check use set host-check custom / set host-check-policy <policy>.
For strongest assurance also require a client certificate globally: config vpn ssl settings / set reqclientcert enable / end (only managed devices with the issued cert can connect).
Consider disabling web mode entirely if only tunnel mode is needed (set web-mode disable) to shrink attack surface.
Commit: next / end.
Verify: get vpn ssl web portal <portal> | grep host-check returns av-fw; test from a compliant vs non-compliant endpoint and confirm the non-compliant one is denied.
Document the posture policy and review it after client AV/OS changes.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-011.gui: VPN > SSL-VPN Portals > edit <portal> > 'Enable Host Check' and choose AV / Firewall / Custom (and select the host-check policy). Client-cert requirement under VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > 'Require Client Certificate'. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > VPN > SSL-VPN Portals, plus per-device SSL-VPN settings via CLI Template.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-011.cli:
config vpn ssl web portal
  edit <portal-name>
    set host-check av-fw
  next
end
# Optional strong complement (managed devices only):
# config vpn ssl settings
#   set reqclientcert enable
# end
# Valid host-check values: none | av | fw | av-fw | custom (NOT 'enable').
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-011.verify: get vpn ssl web portal <portal-name> | grep host-check   -> host-check: av-fw . A non-compliant endpoint (no AV/FW) is blocked at connect; the SSL-VPN event log records the host-check failure.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-011.rollback: config vpn ssl web portal / edit <portal> / set host-check none / next / end (and unset reqclientcert if set). Rolling back re-admits untrusted endpoints. If legitimate users are blocked because their AV isn't detected, adjust the host-check policy/supported-AV list rather than disabling the check outright.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-011.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot). Enforced at next portal login. Endpoints failing the posture check (or lacking the required client cert) will be denied access - communicate requirements and validate detection for the corporate AV/EDR first to avoid locking out compliant users. No effect on IPsec/HA/admin.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - SSL VPN portal / endpoint compliance (no explicit sub-section mapped in scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
NIST 800-53 IA-3 (Device Identification/Authentication) / AC-19 (Access Control for Mobile Devices)
FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - SSL-VPN portal host check; VPN > SSL-VPN with client certificate (reqclientcert)
Fortinet PSIRT FG-IR-18-384 (CVE-2018-13379) - SSL-VPN web portal credential exposure
CWE-287: Improper Authentication

FORTIOS-SSLVPN-012.risk: A web-mode SSL-VPN portal exposes FTP in its allowed access protocols. FTP is cleartext (credentials and data unencrypted at the far side of the portal), unlogged at the file level by the FortiGate, and provides remote users a broad, hard-to-monitor file-transfer channel into/out of internal file servers - an easy exfiltration path and, inbound, a way to stage malware onto internal hosts. It also widens the portal's bookmarked-resource surface beyond what most remote-access use cases require.
steps: Identify portals with FTP allowed: get vpn ssl web portal <name> | grep allow-user-access.
Determine which protocols users genuinely need (typically rdp/ssh/https bookmarks, not ftp).
Enter the portal: config vpn ssl web portal / edit <portal>.
Re-set allow-user-access to the minimal required set, omitting ftp. Valid tokens: web ftp smb telnet ssh vnc rdp ping citrix portforward. Example: set allow-user-access web rdp ssh.
If internal file access is still required, use the portal's SMB/CIFS file-share widget (smb) rather than cleartext ftp: include smb instead of ftp.
Commit: next / end.
Review portal bookmarks and remove any predefined FTP bookmarks: config vpn ssl web portal / edit <portal> / config bookmark-group / edit <grp> / delete <ftp-bookmark> / end.
Verify: get vpn ssl web portal <portal> | grep allow-user-access no longer contains ftp.
Test the portal - confirm remaining needed protocols work and FTP is gone.
Log/monitor portal usage for attempts to reach removed protocols.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-012.gui: VPN > SSL-VPN Portals > edit <portal> > 'Predefined Bookmarks' / enabled applications - remove FTP; the allowed application set is controlled here. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > VPN > SSL-VPN Portals.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-012.cli:
config vpn ssl web portal
  edit <portal-name>
    set allow-user-access web rdp ssh
  next
end
# Valid allow-user-access tokens: web ftp smb telnet ssh vnc rdp ping citrix portforward
# Omit 'ftp' (and 'telnet') from the list; use 'smb' if internal file access is required.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-012.verify: get vpn ssl web portal <portal-name> | grep allow-user-access   -> allow-user-access: web rdp ssh   (must NOT include ftp).
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-012.rollback: config vpn ssl web portal / edit <portal> / set allow-user-access <previous-list-including-ftp> / next / end. Rolling back re-exposes the cleartext, poorly-audited FTP channel. If users legitimately need file transfer, add smb (the portal's file-share widget) instead of restoring ftp.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-012.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot). Takes effect on next portal session. Users who relied on the portal's FTP applet lose it and must use an approved alternative (SMB file-share widget) - confirm no production workflow depends on portal FTP before removing. No effect on IPsec/HA/admin.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - SSL VPN portal application restriction (no explicit sub-section mapped in scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
NIST 800-53 AC-4 (Information Flow) / SC-7 (Boundary Protection); PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 1.2.1
FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - SSL-VPN web portal (allow-user-access, bookmarks)
CWE-284: Improper Access Control

FORTIOS-SSLVPN-013.risk: A portal does not limit concurrent logins per user, so one account can hold many simultaneous SSL-VPN sessions. This masks credential sharing and account compromise: a stolen credential can be used by an attacker at the same time as the legitimate user without any conflict or alert, and a single leaked account can spawn dozens of tunnels for scanning, exfiltration, or resource exhaustion. It also complicates forensic attribution (which session was the user vs the attacker) and can breach per-seat licensing/usage policies.
steps: Check the current setting: get vpn ssl web portal <name> | grep limit-user-logins.
Confirm business need - most remote-access use cases require exactly one active session per user.
Enter the portal: config vpn ssl web portal / edit <portal>.
Enforce one session per user: set limit-user-logins enable.
Commit: next / end.
For users who legitimately need concurrent devices, create a separate portal with the exception documented rather than disabling the limit globally.
Ensure idle/auth timeouts (FORTIOS-SSLVPN-003) are also tight so stale sessions free up quickly.
Verify: get vpn ssl web portal <portal> | grep limit-user-logins returns enable.
Test: log in the same user from two devices and confirm the second is rejected or displaces the first.
Alert on repeated concurrent-login rejections in SSL-VPN logs (possible credential sharing/compromise).
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-013.gui: VPN > SSL-VPN Portals > edit <portal> > 'Limit Users to One SSL-VPN Connection at a Time' checkbox. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > VPN > SSL-VPN Portals.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-013.cli:
config vpn ssl web portal
  edit <portal-name>
    set limit-user-logins enable
  next
end
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-013.verify: get vpn ssl web portal <portal-name> | grep limit-user-logins   -> limit-user-logins: enable . A second concurrent login by the same user is denied (visible in SSL-VPN event logs / diagnose vpn ssl list shows a single session per user).
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-013.rollback: config vpn ssl web portal / edit <portal> / set limit-user-logins disable / next / end. Rolling back re-allows undetected concurrent/shared sessions. If a user genuinely needs multiple devices, move them to a dedicated portal with the exception documented instead of disabling globally.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-013.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot). Enforced at next login. Users who currently rely on multiple simultaneous sessions (e.g. laptop + phone) will be limited to one - identify such cases first. No effect on IPsec/HA/admin.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - SSL VPN portal session control (no explicit sub-section mapped in scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
NIST 800-53 AC-10 (Concurrent Session Control)
FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - SSL-VPN web portal (limit-user-logins)
CWE-307: Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts (scanner-assigned CWE; the control also relates to CWE-384 session-abuse/credential-sharing detection)

FORTIOS-SSLVPN-014.risk: HTTP/deflate compression is enabled on the SSL-VPN, combining compression with TLS. This is exactly the precondition for the CRIME (CVE-2012-4929) and BREACH (CVE-2013-3587) class of attacks: an attacker who can observe encrypted response sizes and induce chosen inputs can use compression-ratio side channels to incrementally recover secrets in the TLS stream (session tokens, CSRF tokens, portions of credentials) from the web-mode portal. Because the SSL-VPN portal is Internet-facing and authenticated, leaking a session token here can hand an attacker a live tunnel into the corporate network.
steps: Confirm compression is on: get vpn ssl settings | grep deflate-compression-level (a value > 0 means compression is active; the scanner also checks a legacy http-compression flag).
Enter settings: config vpn ssl settings.
Disable TLS-layer compression by setting the deflate level to zero: set deflate-compression-level 0.

NOTE - the scanner's inline recommendation 'set http-compression disable' does not map to a real field in 'config vpn ssl settings'; the actual control is deflate-compression-level (0 disables). Set it to 0.
Optionally set the deflate minimum data size high so nothing small is ever compressed: set deflate-min-data-size <large-value> (belt-and-suspenders; level 0 already disables).
Commit: end.
Confirm no portal-level compression is re-enabling it: review web portals for any compression/theme options.
Verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep deflate-compression-level returns 0.
Externally test that TLS compression is off (e.g. openssl s_client -connect <ip>:<port> reports 'Compression: NONE', or testssl.sh flags CRIME as not vulnerable).
Re-test after FortiOS upgrades in case defaults change.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-014.gui: No GUI toggle - configure via CLI (VPN > SSL-VPN Settings does not expose compression). FortiManager: per-device SSL-VPN settings via CLI Template / CLI Configuration, then Install Wizard.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-014.cli:
config vpn ssl settings
  set deflate-compression-level 0
end
# NOTE: 'set http-compression disable' (the scanner's inline text) is not a valid
# field here; deflate-compression-level 0 disables TLS/deflate compression.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-014.verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep deflate-compression-level   -> deflate-compression-level: 0 . External: openssl s_client -connect <ip>:<port> shows 'Compression: NONE' (testssl.sh reports CRIME: not vulnerable).
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-014.rollback: config vpn ssl settings / set deflate-compression-level <1-9> / end. Rolling back re-enables the CRIME/BREACH side-channel exposure for a marginal bandwidth gain - not recommended for an Internet-facing portal.
FORTIOS-SSLVPN-014.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot). Applies to new sessions. Disabling compression may slightly increase bandwidth for web-mode portal traffic but removes the side-channel; tunnel-mode throughput is unaffected. Existing sessions continue until reconnect. No effect on IPsec/HA/admin.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - SSL VPN transport hardening (no explicit sub-section mapped in scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
CRIME: CVE-2012-4929; BREACH: CVE-2013-3587 (TLS compression side-channel)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 4.2.1 (strong cryptography); NIST 800-53 SC-8
FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - SSL-VPN settings (deflate-compression-level, deflate-min-data-size)
CWE-326: Inadequate Encryption Strength

FORTIOS-SYS-001.risk: With the SIP ALG (sip-helper) enabled, the FortiGate transparently rewrites SIP/SDP payloads and dynamically opens RTP pinholes. An attacker on a VoIP-reachable segment can abuse these auto-created pinholes to bypass explicit firewall policy (unsolicited RTP/media injection, toll fraud, call hijack) and can crash or confuse the ALG with malformed SIP, causing dropped calls. Business impact: unreliable voice service plus an under-audited traffic path that circumvents your policy set.
steps: Confirm current state: get system settings | grep sip-helper
If VoIP traversal is genuinely required, prefer a controlled VoIP profile (Security Profiles > VoIP) over the global kernel ALG; otherwise proceed to disable.
Enter the settings context (per-VDOM): config system settings
Disable the helper: set sip-helper disable
Disable SIP NAT tracing: set sip-nat-trace disable
Keep the ALG off the kernel path: set default-voip-alg-mode proxy-based
Apply: end
Schedule and perform a reboot so the SIP session helper fully unloads (required by FortiOS for the change to take effect).
Re-test VoIP registration/call flow and confirm no unintended media pinholes remain.
FORTIOS-SYS-001.gui: No dedicated GUI toggle for the kernel SIP ALG. Manage VoIP inspection via Security Profiles > VoIP (voip profile) and Policy & Objects. FortiManager: push the `config system settings` block via Device Manager > Provisioning Templates > CLI Template (or Device DB > System > Settings).
FORTIOS-SYS-001.cli:
# NOTE: scanner REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-SYS-001'] returns the strong-crypto block (mis-keyed to an older control). The block below is the correct fix for the SIP-ALG finding.
config system settings
  set sip-helper disable
  set sip-nat-trace disable
  set default-voip-alg-mode proxy-based
end
# A reboot is required for the SIP session helper to fully unload.
FORTIOS-SYS-001.verify: get system settings | grep sip-helper  -> sip-helper : disable   (and: get system settings | grep sip-nat-trace -> sip-nat-trace : disable)
FORTIOS-SYS-001.rollback: config system settings / set sip-helper enable / end, then reboot. Re-enabling restores automatic SIP NAT traversal but reintroduces the dynamic-pinhole/policy-bypass exposure and potential VoIP interop bugs.
FORTIOS-SYS-001.impact: Requires a reboot to take full effect (maintenance window). Active SIP calls drop during the reboot. If VoIP relies on the ALG for NAT traversal, calls may fail until a VoIP profile/SIP session-helper is configured. Non-VoIP traffic is unaffected.
references: Fortinet Community KB — Technical Tip: Disabling the SIP ALG / SIP helper (config system settings set sip-helper disable)
FortiOS CLI Reference (7.x) — config system settings (sip-helper, sip-nat-trace, default-voip-alg-mode)
CWE-284: Improper Access Control
Scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP[FORTIOS-SYS-001]: CIS 3.1.1 / PCI-DSS 2.2.4 / NIST SC-13 / SOC2 CC6.1 (note: internal map is keyed to an older strong-crypto control)

FORTIOS-SYS-002.risk: ecmp-max-paths set far above the number of real equal-cost next hops (e.g. 255) can cause traffic to be hashed across paths that do not exist or that behave asymmetrically, producing unpredictable routing, flow-based inspection breakage (asymmetric return paths bypassing stateful inspection), and troubleshooting difficulty. An attacker can exploit asymmetric/looping paths to evade IPS/session tracking or to amplify resource use. Business impact: intermittent connectivity and weakened inspection integrity.
steps: Review current value: get system settings | grep ecmp-max-paths
Determine the real number of equal-cost paths in your topology (typically 2-8).
Enter settings context: config system settings
Set an appropriate bound: set ecmp-max-paths 4
Apply: end
Confirm route programming: get router info routing-table all (ensure ECMP next-hops are as expected).
Monitor sessions for asymmetry: diagnose sys session list (spot-check flows).
Document the chosen value against the network design.
