FORTIOS-SYS-002.gui: No dedicated GUI field; configure via CLI. FortiManager: push `config system settings` (ecmp-max-paths) via a CLI Template or Device DB > System > Settings.
FORTIOS-SYS-002.cli:
# NOTE: scanner REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-SYS-002'] returns a fds-statistics block (mis-keyed). Correct fix below.
config system settings
  set ecmp-max-paths 4
end
FORTIOS-SYS-002.verify: get system settings | grep ecmp-max-paths  -> ecmp-max-paths : 4
FORTIOS-SYS-002.rollback: config system settings / set ecmp-max-paths 255 / end (default). Raising it again only matters if you truly have that many equal-cost paths; otherwise it reintroduces the routing-unpredictability exposure.
FORTIOS-SYS-002.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Routing table is recomputed on apply. Only affects flows that were being spread across more paths than actually exist.
references: FortiOS CLI Reference (7.x) — config system settings (ecmp-max-paths, v4-ecmp-mode)
FortiOS Administration Guide — Equal Cost Multi-Path (ECMP) routing
CWE-400: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
Scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP[FORTIOS-SYS-002]: CIS 3.1.2 / PCI-DSS 2.2.4 / NIST SC-13 (internal map keyed to an older control)

FORTIOS-SYS-003.risk: With multicast-forward enabled, the FortiGate forwards multicast between segments. If no multicast application requires it, this needlessly widens the attack surface: it can leak internal discovery/streaming traffic across zones, enable multicast-based reconnaissance or amplification, and forward unvetted multicast that firewall policy may not fully constrain. Business impact: cross-segment exposure and wasted forwarding capacity for a feature you are not using.
steps: Determine whether any service (IPTV, PIM/multicast routing, market-data feeds) actually needs multicast forwarding.
Check current state: get system settings | grep multicast-forward
If not required, enter settings context: config system settings
Disable it: set multicast-forward disable
Apply: end
If multicast IS required, leave it enabled but scope it with multicast firewall policies (config firewall multicast-policy) rather than forwarding globally.
Validate that legitimate multicast apps still function (or are intentionally blocked).
Record the decision in the change log.
FORTIOS-SYS-003.gui: No simple GUI toggle for multicast-forward; use CLI. Multicast policies live under Policy & Objects (multicast policy, when visible). FortiManager: push via CLI Template / Device DB > System > Settings.
FORTIOS-SYS-003.cli:
# NOTE: scanner REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-SYS-003'] returns a pre-login-banner block (mis-keyed). Correct fix below.
config system settings
  set multicast-forward disable
end
FORTIOS-SYS-003.verify: get system settings | grep multicast-forward  -> multicast-forward : disable
FORTIOS-SYS-003.rollback: config system settings / set multicast-forward enable / end. Re-enabling restores multicast forwarding (needed for IPTV/PIM) but reopens the cross-segment exposure.
FORTIOS-SYS-003.impact: Disrupts any multicast application in use (streaming, IPTV, some routing/discovery protocols). No reboot. Non-disruptive if multicast is not used.
references: FortiOS CLI Reference (7.x) — config system settings (multicast-forward, multicast-ttl-notchange, multicast-skip-policy)
FortiOS Administration Guide — Multicast forwarding and multicast policies
CWE-284: Improper Access Control
Scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP[FORTIOS-SYS-003]: CIS 3.1.3 / PCI-DSS 2.2.2 / NIST AC-8 / SOC2 CC6.1 (internal map keyed to an older banner control)

FORTIOS-SYS-004.risk: allow-subnet-overlap permits interfaces to be configured with overlapping IP subnets. Overlapping subnets create routing ambiguity: return traffic and policy matching can resolve to the wrong interface, allowing security-policy bypass, traffic blackholing, or interception of flows intended for another segment. An attacker who can influence addressing (rogue DHCP, misconfig) may exploit the ambiguity to redirect or evade inspection. Business impact: unpredictable enforcement and hard-to-diagnose outages.
steps: Check state: get system settings | grep allow-subnet-overlap
Inventory interfaces for existing overlaps: show system interface (review the ip fields for overlapping ranges).
If overlaps exist, re-address the offending interfaces FIRST (the disable command is rejected while overlaps remain).
Enter settings context: config system settings
Disable overlap: set allow-subnet-overlap disable
Apply: end
Validate routing and policy hit counts after re-addressing.
Document the corrected addressing plan.
FORTIOS-SYS-004.gui: No dedicated GUI toggle; configure via CLI. Interface addressing is under Network > Interfaces. FortiManager: push via CLI Template / Device DB > System > Settings.
FORTIOS-SYS-004.cli:
# NOTE: scanner REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-SYS-004'] returns a post-login-banner block (mis-keyed). Correct fix below.
config system settings
  set allow-subnet-overlap disable
end
FORTIOS-SYS-004.verify: get system settings | grep allow-subnet-overlap  -> allow-subnet-overlap : disable
FORTIOS-SYS-004.rollback: config system settings / set allow-subnet-overlap enable / end. Only re-enable if a design genuinely requires overlapping subnets (rare); it reintroduces routing ambiguity.
FORTIOS-SYS-004.impact: Non-disruptive if no overlaps exist (no reboot). If overlaps are present, the command fails until you re-address interfaces, which is itself a disruptive change requiring a maintenance window.
references: FortiOS CLI Reference (7.x) — config system settings (allow-subnet-overlap)
FortiOS Administration Guide — Interface addressing and subnet configuration
CWE-284: Improper Access Control
Scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP[FORTIOS-SYS-004]: CIS 3.1.4 / PCI-DSS 2.2.2 / NIST AC-8 (internal map keyed to an older banner control)

FORTIOS-SYS-005.risk: This is an INFO/manageability finding, not a direct exploit. With Central NAT disabled, NAT is defined per-policy, which is harder to audit and easy to make inconsistent — orphaned or overly broad NAT rules can persist unnoticed, and inconsistent SNAT/DNAT can expose internal hosts or leak source addresses. Business impact: audit gaps and configuration drift that indirectly weaken egress/ingress control. Note: enabling Central NAT is an architectural preference, not a mandatory security fix.
steps: Confirm current model: get system settings | grep central-nat
Inventory existing policy-based NAT (nat enable, ippool, VIPs) before switching — enabling central-nat changes how NAT is expressed.
Plan migration of per-policy SNAT to Central SNAT policies and DNAT to VIPs; schedule a maintenance window.
Enter settings context: config system settings
Enable central NAT: set central-nat enable
Apply: end
Rebuild NAT under Policy & Objects > Central SNAT (config firewall central-snat-map) and validate DNAT via VIPs.
Test representative inbound and outbound flows and confirm NAT translations, then document the new model.
FORTIOS-SYS-005.gui: Enabling central-nat is a CLI action (config system settings). Once enabled, manage rules at Policy & Objects > Central SNAT. FortiManager: push via CLI Template / Device DB > System > Settings, then manage Central SNAT in Policy & Objects.
FORTIOS-SYS-005.cli:
# NOTE: scanner REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-SYS-005'] returns an admin-lockout block (mis-keyed). Correct fix below.
config system settings
  set central-nat enable
end
FORTIOS-SYS-005.verify: get system settings | grep central-nat  -> central-nat : enable
FORTIOS-SYS-005.rollback: config system settings / set central-nat disable / end. Reverting again re-migrates NAT back to per-policy and can drop translated sessions; both directions are disruptive, so decide once.
FORTIOS-SYS-005.impact: Potentially highly disruptive: switching the NAT model can drop NAT'd sessions and requires reconfiguring all NAT as Central SNAT policies / VIPs. Maintenance window required. No reboot. Because the finding is INFO, treat enablement as optional and design-driven.
references: FortiOS Administration Guide (7.x) — Central SNAT / Central NAT
FortiOS CLI Reference — config system settings (central-nat), config firewall central-snat-map
CWE-1078: Inappropriate Source Code Style or Formatting (per scanner classification; the practical issue is auditability/consistency)
Scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP[FORTIOS-SYS-005]: CIS 3.1.5 / PCI-DSS 8.1.6 / NIST AC-7 / HIPAA 164.312(a)(2)(i) (internal map keyed to an older lockout control)

FORTIOS-SYS-006.risk: Allowing unnamed firewall policies means rules can be created without a descriptive name, so change management and audit trails reference only numeric policy IDs. This makes it easy to introduce shadow/overly-permissive rules that reviewers overlook, and it slows incident response when an analyst must reason about an unlabeled ACCEPT rule. Business impact: weakened governance and higher chance an insecure policy survives review.
steps: Check current setting: get system settings | grep gui-allow-unnamed-policy
Enter settings context: config system settings
Require named policies: set gui-allow-unnamed-policy disable
Apply: end
Identify existing unnamed policies (review Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy for blank Name fields, or show firewall policy and look for entries with no 'set name').
Add meaningful names to existing policies: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set name "<purpose>" / next / end
Establish a naming convention and enforce it in change control.
Re-run the scan to confirm the finding clears.
FORTIOS-SYS-006.gui: System > Settings — the 'Allow Unnamed Policies' toggle in the Policy section (label/location varies slightly by version); when disabled the Policy editor requires a Name. FortiManager: push `config system settings` via CLI Template / Device DB.
FORTIOS-SYS-006.cli:
config system settings
  set gui-allow-unnamed-policy disable
end
FORTIOS-SYS-006.verify: get system settings | grep gui-allow-unnamed-policy  -> gui-allow-unnamed-policy : disable
FORTIOS-SYS-006.rollback: config system settings / set gui-allow-unnamed-policy enable / end. Re-enabling only relaxes a GUI guardrail; existing policies are unaffected.
FORTIOS-SYS-006.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot, no session impact. Existing unnamed policies keep forwarding; only new GUI policy creation is affected (will require a name).
references: FortiOS Administration Guide (7.x) — Firewall policy configuration / naming
FortiOS CLI Reference — config system settings (gui-allow-unnamed-policy)
CWE-1078: Inappropriate Source Code Style or Formatting (per scanner classification; practical issue is policy auditability)
Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide — administrative change management best practices

FORTIOS-SYS-007.risk: Without a pre-login banner, administrators authenticate with no legal warning that the system is restricted to authorized use. Beyond compliance exposure, the absence of a warning can undermine prosecution of unauthorized access and provides no deterrent to opportunistic login attempts. Business impact: legal/compliance gap (many regimes and CIS require an authorized-use notice) with no operational benefit to leaving it off.
steps: Check state: get system global | grep pre-login-banner
Obtain approved legal warning text from Legal/Compliance.
Enable the banner: config system global / set pre-login-banner enable / end
Set the banner text via the admin replacement message: config system replacemsg admin pre_admin-disclaimer-text
set buffer "This system is for authorized use only. Activity may be monitored and unauthorized access will be prosecuted."
Apply: end
Log out and confirm the disclaimer appears (and must be accepted) before the login prompt.
Record the approved text and effective date.
FORTIOS-SYS-007.gui: Banner enablement is CLI (config system global). Banner text: System > Replacement Messages > Administration > 'Pre-login Disclaimer Message'. FortiManager: push via CLI Template, or configure the replacement message in the ADOM.
FORTIOS-SYS-007.cli:
config system global
  set pre-login-banner enable
end
config system replacemsg admin pre_admin-disclaimer-text
  set buffer "This system is for authorized use only. Activity may be monitored and unauthorized access will be prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law."
end
FORTIOS-SYS-007.verify: get system global | grep pre-login-banner  -> pre-login-banner : enable   (then log in via SSH/HTTPS and confirm the disclaimer prompt)
FORTIOS-SYS-007.rollback: config system global / set pre-login-banner disable / end. Removes the warning; no functional impact but reopens the compliance gap.
FORTIOS-SYS-007.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot, no session drops. Affects only new administrator login sessions (a disclaimer that must be accepted).
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.3.0 — 2.1.1 'Ensure Pre-Login Banner is set'
FortiOS Administration Guide (7.x) — Replacement messages / pre-login disclaimer
FortiOS CLI Reference — config system global (pre-login-banner), config system replacemsg admin (pre_admin-disclaimer-text)
NIST SP 800-53 AC-8 (System Use Notification); CWE-1078 (per scanner classification)

FORTIOS-SYS-008.risk: Without a post-login banner, administrators receive no acknowledgement/notice after authenticating (e.g., monitoring notice, handling rules, or last-login reminders). This is primarily a compliance and operational-awareness gap; some standards expect a post-authentication notice. Business impact: minor security benefit but a real audit finding in regulated environments.
steps: Check state: get system global | grep post-login-banner
Obtain approved post-login notice text.
Enable: config system global / set post-login-banner enable / end
Set the text via the admin replacement message: config system replacemsg admin post_admin-disclaimer-text
set buffer "Access is logged and monitored. Handle all data per corporate policy."
Apply: end
Log in and confirm the notice appears after authentication.
Document the text and effective date.
FORTIOS-SYS-008.gui: Enablement is CLI (config system global). Text: System > Replacement Messages > Administration > 'Post-login Disclaimer Message'. FortiManager: push via CLI Template / configure the replacement message in the ADOM.
FORTIOS-SYS-008.cli:
config system global
  set post-login-banner enable
end
config system replacemsg admin post_admin-disclaimer-text
  set buffer "Access to this device is logged and monitored. Handle all data in accordance with corporate security policy."
end
FORTIOS-SYS-008.verify: get system global | grep post-login-banner  -> post-login-banner : enable   (then log in and confirm the post-auth notice)
FORTIOS-SYS-008.rollback: config system global / set post-login-banner disable / end. No functional impact; removes the post-login notice.
FORTIOS-SYS-008.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot, no session drops. Affects only new administrator sessions after authentication.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.3.0 — 2.1.2 'Ensure Post-Login-Banner is set'
FortiOS Administration Guide (7.x) — Replacement messages / post-login disclaimer
FortiOS CLI Reference — config system global (post-login-banner), config system replacemsg admin (post_admin-disclaimer-text)
NIST SP 800-53 AC-8; CWE-1078 (per scanner classification)

FORTIOS-SYS-009.risk: With global strong-crypto disabled, the FortiGate permits weak ciphers (DES, 3DES, RC4) and legacy protocols (SSLv3, TLS 1.0/1.1) for its own SSL/SSH management and other TLS services. A network-positioned attacker can downgrade admin/VPN sessions and exploit BEAST/POODLE/SWEET32-class weaknesses to recover credentials or session material. Business impact: interceptable administrative and VPN traffic and a direct failure of encryption-strength controls (PCI/HIPAA).
steps: Establish out-of-band access (console) in case a management client uses only weak TLS.
Back up config: execute backup config <dest>.
Check state: get system global | grep strong-crypto
Enable strong crypto: config system global / set strong-crypto enable / end
Confirm admin HTTPS/SSH still reachable from approved clients (older browsers/scripts may fail and must be updated).
Re-check dependent services (SSL-VPN, LDAPS, syslog-over-TLS) negotiate TLS 1.2+ ciphers.
Update any monitoring/automation still using weak TLS.
Re-run the scan to confirm strong-crypto is enforced.
FORTIOS-SYS-009.gui: No GUI toggle; strong-crypto is CLI-only (config system global). FortiManager: push via CLI Template / Device DB > System > Global.
FORTIOS-SYS-009.cli:
config system global
  set strong-crypto enable
end
FORTIOS-SYS-009.verify: get system global | grep strong-crypto  -> strong-crypto : enable
FORTIOS-SYS-009.rollback: config system global / set strong-crypto disable / end. Reverting restores weak-cipher support for legacy clients but reintroduces downgrade/interception exposure.
FORTIOS-SYS-009.impact: No reboot. Existing SSL/SSH management sessions negotiated with weak ciphers are renegotiated/dropped, and old clients (legacy browsers, scripts, monitoring using RC4/3DES/TLS1.0) may lose access — keep a console fallback. VPN/TLS services continue with strong ciphers.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.3.0 — 'Enable Global Strong Encryption'
Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide — Global commands for stronger and more secure encryption (strong-crypto)
FortiOS CLI Reference — config system global (strong-crypto, ssl-static-key-ciphers)
CWE-326: Inadequate Encryption Strength; NIST SP 800-52 Rev.2

FORTIOS-SYS-010.risk: With admin-lockout-threshold at 0, failed administrator logins are unlimited, so the management interface (HTTPS/SSH) is open to online brute-force and credential-stuffing. An attacker can iterate passwords indefinitely until a weak/reused admin credential is found, yielding full device compromise (policy changes, VPN pivot, log tampering). Business impact: high — direct path to administrative takeover of the firewall.
steps: Check state: get system global | grep admin-lockout-threshold
Ensure a break-glass local admin and out-of-band console access exist (lockout can affect legitimate admins).
Set a threshold: config system global / set admin-lockout-threshold 3
Set a meaningful lockout duration: set admin-lockout-duration 300
Apply: end
Restrict admin access to trusted hosts as defense-in-depth: config system admin / edit <admin> / set trusthost1 <mgmt-net/mask> / next / end
Ensure failed logins are logged/alerted (event log + FortiAnalyzer/syslog).
Test with an intentional bad password to confirm lockout triggers, then re-run the scan.
FORTIOS-SYS-010.gui: Threshold/duration are CLI-only (config system global). Trusted hosts: System > Administrators > [admin] > Restrict login to trusted hosts. FortiManager: push via CLI Template / Device DB > System > Global.
FORTIOS-SYS-010.cli:
config system global
  set admin-lockout-threshold 3
  set admin-lockout-duration 300
end
FORTIOS-SYS-010.verify: get system global | grep admin-lockout  -> admin-lockout-threshold : 3  /  admin-lockout-duration : 300
FORTIOS-SYS-010.rollback: config system global / set admin-lockout-threshold 0 / end (disables lockout). Reverting removes brute-force protection; instead of disabling, raise the threshold if legitimate lockouts occur.
FORTIOS-SYS-010.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot, no session drops. Operational caveat: a determined attacker (or a misconfigured monitor) can lock out admin accounts — retain break-glass/console access and consider trusted-host restrictions.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.3.0 — 2.2.2 'Ensure administrator password retries and lockout time are configured'
FortiOS CLI Reference — config system global (admin-lockout-threshold, admin-lockout-duration)
CWE-307: Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts
NIST SP 800-53 AC-7 (Unsuccessful Logon Attempts); PCI-DSS 8.3.4

FORTIOS-SYS-011.risk: A lockout is configured but the duration is under 300 seconds, so a locked account frees again quickly. An attacker running a slow/distributed brute-force can simply wait out short lockouts and continue guessing, defeating most of the throttling benefit. Business impact: brute-force protection is present but weak, leaving a viable path to admin credential compromise.
steps: Check current duration: get system global | grep admin-lockout-duration
Confirm the threshold is set (>0): get system global | grep admin-lockout-threshold
Raise the duration: config system global / set admin-lockout-duration 300
Optionally tighten the threshold: set admin-lockout-threshold 3
Apply: end
Verify a triggered lockout now persists for the full duration.
Ensure lockout events are logged/alerted for SOC visibility.
Re-run the scan to confirm the finding clears.
FORTIOS-SYS-011.gui: CLI-only (config system global). FortiManager: push via CLI Template / Device DB > System > Global.
FORTIOS-SYS-011.cli:
config system global
  set admin-lockout-duration 300
end
FORTIOS-SYS-011.verify: get system global | grep admin-lockout-duration  -> admin-lockout-duration : 300
FORTIOS-SYS-011.rollback: config system global / set admin-lockout-duration <previous> / end. Lowering it again shortens the brute-force pause and re-weakens protection.
FORTIOS-SYS-011.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot, no session drops. Only lengthens how long a locked-out admin must wait — retain break-glass/console access.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.3.0 — 2.2.2 'Ensure administrator password retries and lockout time are configured'
FortiOS CLI Reference — config system global (admin-lockout-duration)
CWE-307: Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts
NIST SP 800-53 AC-7

FORTIOS-SYS-012.risk: The admin HTTPS GUI accepts deprecated TLS (SSLv3 / TLS 1.0 / TLS 1.1). A man-in-the-middle can force a protocol downgrade and leverage POODLE, BEAST, or SWEET32 to recover the admin session cookie or credentials, leading to full firewall takeover. Because this is the management plane, successful interception is high-impact. Business impact: interceptable admin access plus a clear PCI/HIPAA encryption-strength violation.
steps: Establish console access as a fallback in case a management client only supports old TLS.
Check state: get system global | grep admin-https-ssl-versions
Restrict to modern TLS: config system global / set admin-https-ssl-versions tlsv1-2 tlsv1-3 / end
(Optional) enforce strong ciphers globally: set strong-crypto enable (see FORTIOS-SYS-009).
Confirm the GUI still loads from approved, up-to-date browsers.
Update any monitoring/automation that hit the GUI over TLS 1.0/1.1.
Externally validate: nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 443 <mgmt-ip> (expect only TLS 1.2/1.3).
Re-run the scan to confirm the finding clears.
FORTIOS-SYS-012.gui: No GUI toggle; admin-https-ssl-versions is CLI-only (config system global). FortiManager: push via CLI Template / Device DB > System > Global.
FORTIOS-SYS-012.cli:
config system global
  set admin-https-ssl-versions tlsv1-2 tlsv1-3
end
FORTIOS-SYS-012.verify: get system global | grep admin-https-ssl-versions  -> admin-https-ssl-versions : tlsv1-2 tlsv1-3   (externally: nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 443 <mgmt-ip> shows only TLSv1.2/1.3)
FORTIOS-SYS-012.rollback: config system global / set admin-https-ssl-versions tlsv1-1 tlsv1-2 tlsv1-3 (or prior value) / end. Re-adding old TLS restores legacy-client access but reopens downgrade/interception exposure.
FORTIOS-SYS-012.impact: No reboot. Admin HTTPS sessions negotiated over TLS 1.0/1.1 are dropped and legacy browsers/tools can no longer reach the GUI — keep console/SSH access available. Data-plane traffic unaffected.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark — 'Ensure management GUI listens on secure TLS version' (admin-https-ssl-versions)
FortiOS CLI Reference — config system global (admin-https-ssl-versions, admin-https-ssl-ciphersuites, admin-https-ssl-banned-ciphers)
CWE-326: Inadequate Encryption Strength; NIST SP 800-52 Rev.2

FORTIOS-SYS-013.risk: A TCP half-open timer above ~30s keeps embryonic (SYN-received) connections in the session table far longer than needed. During a SYN flood, this lets an attacker exhaust the session/connection table with half-open entries, degrading or denying service for legitimate traffic. Business impact: reduced resilience to volumetric SYN-flood DoS against the firewall and protected servers.
steps: Check current timers: get system global | grep tcp-half
Enter global context: config system global
Lower the half-open timer: set tcp-halfopen-timer 30
Keep the half-close timer sane: set tcp-halfclose-timer 120
Apply: end
Pair with DoS protection on WAN (config firewall DoS-policy with tcp_syn_flood anomaly) for layered defense.
Monitor session table under load: diagnose sys session stat.
Re-run the scan to confirm timers are within bounds.
FORTIOS-SYS-013.gui: No GUI field for these timers; CLI-only (config system global). DoS policies: Policy & Objects > DoS Policy. FortiManager: push via CLI Template / Device DB > System > Global.
FORTIOS-SYS-013.cli:
config system global
  set tcp-halfclose-timer 120
  set tcp-halfopen-timer 30
end
FORTIOS-SYS-013.verify: get system global | grep tcp-halfopen-timer  -> tcp-halfopen-timer : 30   (and tcp-halfclose-timer : 120)
FORTIOS-SYS-013.rollback: config system global / set tcp-halfopen-timer <previous> / end. Raising it again only weakens SYN-flood resilience; no functional benefit for normal traffic.
FORTIOS-SYS-013.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Affects only how long half-open sessions persist; established sessions are unaffected.
references: FortiOS CLI Reference — config system global (tcp-halfopen-timer, tcp-halfclose-timer)
FortiOS Administration Guide — DoS protection / session TTL and SYN flood mitigation
Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide — mitigate SYN flood attacks
CWE-400: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption; NIST SP 800-53 SC-5

FORTIOS-SYS-014.risk: With admin-scp disabled, administrators cannot pull configuration backups over SCP (SSH-encrypted copy) using the admin account; teams may fall back to less secure or ad-hoc transfer methods for backups. This is a low-severity availability/best-practice gap: enabling SCP provides an authenticated, encrypted channel for config backup/restore. Business impact: minor — weaker backup workflow rather than a direct exposure.
steps: Check state: get system global | grep admin-scp
Ensure SSH admin access is already restricted to trusted hosts and uses strong crypto.
Enable SCP: config system global / set admin-scp enable / end
Confirm the SSH service is permitted on the management interface allowaccess.
Test a backup over SCP from an authorized host: scp admin@<mgmt-ip>:sys_config <local-file>
Store the retrieved config as a secret (enable private-data-encryption per FORTIOS-SYS-018).
Restrict which hosts may reach SSH/SCP (trusthost / local-in policy).
Re-run the scan to confirm admin-scp is enabled.
FORTIOS-SYS-014.gui: No GUI toggle; admin-scp is CLI-only (config system global). FortiManager: push via CLI Template / Device DB > System > Global.
FORTIOS-SYS-014.cli:
config system global
  set admin-scp enable
end
FORTIOS-SYS-014.verify: get system global | grep admin-scp  -> admin-scp : enable
FORTIOS-SYS-014.rollback: config system global / set admin-scp disable / end. Disabling blocks SCP-based config retrieval; some organizations intentionally keep it off to reduce config-exfil paths, so weigh backup convenience against that.
FORTIOS-SYS-014.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot, no session drops. Enables an SSH-based file-transfer capability for the admin account. Ensure SSH is already restricted to trusted management hosts.
references: FortiOS CLI Reference — config system global (admin-scp)
FortiOS Administration Guide — Backing up the configuration over SCP
CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information (SCP provides the encrypted alternative)
Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide — secure configuration backup

FORTIOS-SYS-015.risk: An SSH grace time above 120s lets an unauthenticated SSH connection stay open longer while waiting for credentials. This enlarges the window for pre-auth resource consumption and connection-slot exhaustion, and gives attackers more time for interactive brute-force or exploitation of any pre-auth SSH weakness. Business impact: modestly increased exposure and DoS surface on the management SSH service.
steps: Check state: get system global | grep admin-ssh-grace-time
Enter global context: config system global
Lower the grace time: set admin-ssh-grace-time 60
Apply: end
Restrict SSH to trusted management hosts (trusthost / local-in policy).
Confirm legitimate admins can still authenticate within the window.
Ensure failed SSH logins are logged and alerted.
Re-run the scan to confirm grace time <=120.
FORTIOS-SYS-015.gui: No GUI field; admin-ssh-grace-time is CLI-only (config system global). FortiManager: push via CLI Template / Device DB > System > Global.
FORTIOS-SYS-015.cli:
config system global
  set admin-ssh-grace-time 60
end
FORTIOS-SYS-015.verify: get system global | grep admin-ssh-grace-time  -> admin-ssh-grace-time : 60
FORTIOS-SYS-015.rollback: config system global / set admin-ssh-grace-time 120 / end (or prior value). Increasing it again widens the pre-auth window.
FORTIOS-SYS-015.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Only shortens how long a new, unauthenticated SSH session may wait before authenticating; established sessions unaffected.
references: FortiOS CLI Reference — config system global (admin-ssh-grace-time)
Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide — administrative access / SSH hardening
CWE-400: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
NIST SP 800-53 AC-17

FORTIOS-SYS-016.risk: FIPS-CC mode is not enabled, so the device is not enforcing FIPS 140-2/140-3-validated cryptography and self-tests. In environments that mandate FIPS (US/allied government, defense, some financial/health workloads), this is a compliance failure and permits use of non-validated algorithms. Business impact: contractual/regulatory non-compliance and, in some designs, use of weaker crypto than the accreditation requires. Note: only enable where FIPS is actually required.
steps: Confirm FIPS is genuinely required for this device/deployment before proceeding — enabling it is disruptive and largely one-way.
Verify the FortiOS build/model supports FIPS-CC and record firmware version: get system status.
Full config + certificate backup; schedule a maintenance window (device will reboot).
Ensure all VPNs/services use FIPS-approved algorithms (non-approved ciphers will be disabled).

From the serial console (FIPS cannot be enabled over SSH or the GUI), enable it: config system fips-cc / set status enable / end. The device warns and reboots; on some models it wipes to a FIPS baseline.
After reboot, bring interfaces back up and re-enable admin access from the CLI, set any required FIPS admin/lockout parameters if prompted, and re-verify connectivity.
Validate VPN/admin/service functionality end-to-end under FIPS.
Confirm status: get system status | grep -i fips.
FORTIOS-SYS-016.gui: No GUI toggle; FIPS-CC is enabled from the serial console only (config system fips-cc / set status enable) — it cannot be enabled over SSH or the web GUI — and triggers a reboot. FortiManager: enabling FIPS per-device must be done via console/CLI, not template push, due to the reboot/reset behavior.
FORTIOS-SYS-016.cli:
# NOTE: scanner REMEDIATION_COMMANDS['FORTIOS-SYS-016'] returns 'config system global / set fips-cc enable', which is not a valid path. FIPS-CC lives under its own config tree. Correct fix below (run from the serial console).
config system fips-cc
  set status enable
end
# Enabling FIPS-CC reboots the unit and restricts cipher suites; on some models it resets to a FIPS baseline.
FORTIOS-SYS-016.verify: get system status | grep -i fips  -> FIPS-CC mode: enabled
FORTIOS-SYS-016.rollback: Disabling FIPS-CC (config system fips-cc / set status disable, from console) also forces a reboot and, on many models, a factory reset back to a non-FIPS baseline (config loss). Plan rollback as a full rebuild-from-backup exercise; do not treat it as a quick toggle.
FORTIOS-SYS-016.impact: Highly disruptive: reboot required (all sessions drop — admin, SSL-VPN, IPsec, HA failover), non-FIPS-approved ciphers/features are disabled, all interfaces come up disabled after the reboot, and on some platforms enabling performs a factory reset. Mandatory maintenance window and console access. Do NOT enable unless FIPS compliance is required.
references: Fortinet Community KB — Technical Tip: How to enable FIPS-CC mode
FortiOS CLI Reference — config system fips-cc (status, entropy-token, self-test-period, key-generation-self-test)
FortiOS FIPS 140-2/140-3 Technical Note / Compliance documentation (docs.fortinet.com)
NIST FIPS 140-2 / 140-3 Cryptographic Module Validation Program
CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

FORTIOS-SYS-017.risk: A TCP half-close timer above 300s keeps sessions that have seen a FIN in one direction lingering in the session table. Under high connection churn or a deliberate flood of half-closed connections, this wastes session-table memory and can contribute to table exhaustion, degrading throughput for legitimate flows. Business impact: inefficient resource use and reduced DoS resilience on busy firewalls.
steps: Check current value: get system global | grep tcp-halfclose-timer
Enter global context: config system global
Set a bounded value: set tcp-halfclose-timer 120
Apply: end
Confirm the half-open timer is also sane: set tcp-halfopen-timer 30 (see FORTIOS-SYS-013).
Monitor session counts before/after: diagnose sys session stat.
Validate that long-lived but legitimately half-closed applications still behave correctly.
Re-run the scan to confirm the timer <=120.
FORTIOS-SYS-017.gui: No GUI field; tcp-halfclose-timer is CLI-only (config system global). FortiManager: push via CLI Template / Device DB > System > Global.
FORTIOS-SYS-017.cli:
config system global
  set tcp-halfclose-timer 120
end
FORTIOS-SYS-017.verify: get system global | grep tcp-halfclose-timer  -> tcp-halfclose-timer : 120
FORTIOS-SYS-017.rollback: config system global / set tcp-halfclose-timer <previous> / end. Raising it again increases session-table retention with no functional upside for normal traffic.
FORTIOS-SYS-017.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Affects only how long half-closed sessions persist; established, fully-open sessions are unaffected.
references: FortiOS CLI Reference — config system global (tcp-halfclose-timer)
FortiOS Administration Guide — session TTL and resource management
CWE-400: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption; NIST SP 800-53 SC-5
Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide — session table tuning

FORTIOS-SYS-018.risk: With private-data-encryption disabled, secrets stored in the configuration (LDAP/RADIUS bind passwords, VPN pre-shared keys, admin/service credentials) are encrypted with a key that is identical across all FortiGate installations. Anyone who obtains a config backup (misplaced file, backup repo, TAC upload, insider) can decrypt every stored credential offline and pivot into LDAP/RADIUS/VPN and beyond. The scanner ties this to CVE-2026-25815, reported as exploited in the wild. Business impact: mass credential compromise from a single leaked backup.
steps: Check state: get system global | grep private-data-encryption

Decide/record a device-unique 32-hexadecimal-digit key (16 bytes, AES-128) and store it in your secrets manager — you will need it to restore backups on another device. On FortiOS 7.6.1+ the key is auto-generated, so capture it after enabling.
Enable encryption: config system global / set private-data-encryption enable / end

When the CLI prompts ("Please enter private data encryption key (32 hexadecimal numbers):"), enter and re-enter the 32-hex-digit key. On 7.6.1+ FortiOS generates the key automatically instead of prompting — there is no separate 'set private-encryption-key' command.
Take a fresh, encrypted backup and confirm it restores only on a device that holds the same key.
Treat ALL previously exported configs as compromised: rotate LDAP/RADIUS bind passwords, VPN PSKs, and any stored service credentials.
Restrict and encrypt backup storage; audit who has old config exports.
Verify: get system global | grep private-data-encryption -> enable, and re-run the scan.
FORTIOS-SYS-018.gui: No GUI toggle; private-data-encryption is CLI-only (config system global) and the key is entered at the CLI prompt (or auto-generated on 7.6.1+). FortiManager: set per-device via CLI/console — never store the encryption key in shared templates; keep it in a secrets manager.
FORTIOS-SYS-018.cli:
config system global
  set private-data-encryption enable
end
# The CLI then prompts: "Please enter private data encryption key (32 hexadecimal numbers):" — enter and re-enter a 32-hex-digit (AES-128) key.
# FortiOS 7.6.1+ generates the key automatically instead of prompting. There is NO separate 'set private-encryption-key' command.
FORTIOS-SYS-018.verify: get system global | grep private-data-encryption  -> private-data-encryption : enable   (the key itself is never displayed)
FORTIOS-SYS-018.rollback: config system global / set private-data-encryption disable / end reverts secrets to the shared-default-key scheme (weaker). WARNING: if you lose the encryption key, encrypted backups become unrestorable, so escrow the key before enabling.
FORTIOS-SYS-018.impact: No reboot and no session drops, but operationally significant and effectively one-way: once enabled, config backups only restore on a device holding the same key. Losing the key means losing restore capability. Rotate previously stored credentials, since older backups used the shared default key.
references: Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide — Encrypting the configuration file (private-data-encryption)
Fortinet Community KB — Technical Tip: How to enable private-data-encryption feature on a standalone FortiGate
FortiOS CLI Reference — config system global (private-data-encryption)
CWE-1394: Use of Default Cryptographic Key
CVE-2026-25815 (per scanner finding; confirm against the Fortinet PSIRT FG-IR advisory)
Scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP[FORTIOS-SYS-018]: CIS 3.1.6 / PCI-DSS 3.5.1,8.3.2 / NIST SC-28,SC-12 / SOC2 CC6.1 / HIPAA 164.312(a)(2)(iv)

FORTIOS-UPDATE-001.risk: A FortiGuard subscription (AntiVirus, IPS, Web Filter, Anti-Spam or the UTP/ATP bundle) is reporting status 'expired' or 'disabled', so the FortiGate stops receiving new signature packages the moment the contract lapses. An attacker can then land any malware or exploit whose signature was published after the expiry date and the AV/IPS engines will pass it as clean - the box silently degrades from an active security control to a plain router. Business impact: undetected malware, ransomware detonation, and IPS-bypass of newly disclosed CVEs, plus a direct PCI-DSS/HIPAA finding because anti-malware and IDS/IPS are no longer 'current'.
steps: Confirm which subscription lapsed and its entitlement: run 'diagnose autoupdate versions' and 'get system fortiguard' and note the 'Contract Expiry Date' for each package (AV, IPS, App Control, Web/DNS Filter).

Log in to FortiCare at https://support.fortinet.com with the account that owns the device serial number and confirm the unit is registered (Dashboard > Status shows the serial and registration state).
Purchase/renew the correct subscription SKU (e.g. UTP, ATP, or Enterprise Protection bundle) for that serial number and apply the contract in FortiCare so the entitlement is attached to the serial.
Pull the refreshed entitlement onto the box: 'diagnose fdsm contract-controller-update' to force a contract sync with the FortiGuard Distribution Network (FDN).
Trigger an immediate signature refresh once the contract is valid: 'execute update-now'.
Re-verify licensing: 'diagnose autoupdate versions' should now show a future 'Contract Expiry Date' and a recent 'Last Updated' timestamp for each package.
Ensure the box will keep itself current going forward: 'config system autoupdate schedule' / 'set status enable' / 'set frequency automatic' / 'end'.
In the GUI confirm the FortiGuard license widget (Dashboard > Status) shows every subscription green/Licensed rather than grey/Expired.
Document the renewal date and set a calendar reminder ~60 days before the next expiry so protection never lapses again.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-001.gui: FortiGate: Dashboard > Status (FortiGuard / Licenses widget) to see per-service status, and System > FortiGuard to view contract state and force an update. Registration/renewal is done in the FortiCare portal at support.fortinet.com. FortiManager (if used as a local FDS): FortiGuard pane > License, and Device Manager to confirm the managed unit's entitlement.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-001.cli: Upgrade FortiOS firmware via System > Firmware. Download from https://support.fortinet.com
FORTIOS-UPDATE-001.verify: diagnose autoupdate versions   -> each package (AV Engine, AV Definitions, IPS Engine, IPS Definitions) shows 'Contract Expiry Date' in the future and 'Last Updated' within the last 24h; and Dashboard > Status FortiGuard widget shows every subscription as 'Licensed' (green) rather than 'Expired'.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-001.rollback: No rollback is appropriate - renewing an expired subscription and pulling fresh signatures only restores protection and cannot break traffic. If a contract sync briefly shows stale data, re-run 'diagnose fdsm contract-controller-update' and 'execute update-now'. The only thing to watch is that you applied the renewal to the correct serial number in FortiCare.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-001.impact: Non-disruptive. Contract sync ('diagnose fdsm contract-controller-update') and 'execute update-now' pull data from FortiGuard over HTTPS; the AV/IPS engines hot-load new signatures without a reboot and without dropping SSL-VPN, IPsec, HA or admin sessions. No maintenance window required. Note: the actual license purchase happens in the FortiCare portal, not on the CLI, and the reused REMEDIATION_COMMANDS text here points at the firmware/registration workflow rather than a config command.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - FortiGuard/Updates recommendation (tool COMPLIANCE_MAP: 10.1.1)
PCI-DSS v4.0 - 5.3 (anti-malware kept current), 6.3.3 (security patches applied), 11.5 (IDS/IPS)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 - SI-2 Flaw Remediation, SI-3 Malicious Code Protection
SOC 2 - CC7.1
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'FortiGuard' chapter (license & subscription status)
Fortinet Document Library - FortiCare & FortiGuard Licenses
CWE-1104: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components

FORTIOS-UPDATE-002.risk: The FortiGuard Distribution Network (FDN) shows as not connected, meaning the FortiGate cannot reach Fortinet's update/rating servers at all - typically caused by blocked outbound 443/8888/8890, broken DNS resolution of *.fortiguard.net, or a mis-set FortiGuard server location/protocol. Even with a valid license, no new AV/IPS definitions, web-filter ratings, or botnet C&C lists are downloaded, and live category/URL rating lookups fail (fail-open or fail-closed depending on config). An attacker exploiting a freshly disclosed CVE or serving malware from a newly categorized malicious site sails straight through because the appliance is frozen at its last-known signature set. Business impact: silent protection decay, stale web/DNS filtering, and audit failure for 'up-to-date' controls.
steps: Confirm the connection state and which service is down: 'diagnose autoupdate status' (look for 'FDN availability: available') and 'diagnose debug rating' (rating/web-filter server list with live RTT).
Test name resolution and reachability from the box: 'execute ping service.fortiguard.net' and 'execute ping update.fortiguard.net'; if DNS fails, fix the system DNS under 'config system dns'.

Ensure the correct transport is set (anycast HTTPS is the modern default): 'config system fortiguard' / 'set fortiguard-anycast enable' / 'set fortiguard-anycast-source fortinet' / 'set protocol https' / 'set port 443' / 'set update-server-location automatic' / 'end'.

On upstream firewalls/proxies, permit outbound from the FortiGate to FortiGuard: TCP 443 for anycast (and legacy TCP 8890 for FDS updates, UDP/TCP 8888 for rating) - do not SSL-inspect or block these flows.
If the device egresses through an explicit web proxy, set it: 'config system fortiguard' / 'set proxy-server-ip <ip>' / 'set proxy-server-port <port>' / 'set proxy-username <user>' / 'end'.
Force a fresh connection and update: 'execute update-now'.
Re-check status: 'diagnose autoupdate status' should now read 'FDN availability: available' and 'diagnose debug rating' should list servers with non-zero RTT.
If still down, restart the update daemon context by toggling the schedule ('config system autoupdate schedule' / 'set status disable' then 'enable' / 'end') and re-run 'execute update-now'.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-002.gui: FortiGate: System > FortiGuard - the top of the page shows the FortiGuard connection status (server location, protocol, port) and lets you override the server location; Dashboard > Status FortiGuard widget shows red when disconnected. FortiManager (if acting as the local FDS): FortiGuard pane > Settings and confirm managed devices point to the FMG via central-management.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-002.cli:
config system fortiguard
    set fortiguard-anycast enable
    set fortiguard-anycast-source fortinet
    set protocol https
    set port 443
    set update-server-location automatic
end
execute update-now
FORTIOS-UPDATE-002.verify: diagnose autoupdate status   -> 'FDN availability: available at <timestamp>'; and diagnose debug rating   -> lists FortiGuard rating servers each with a measured RTT (not all 0/unreachable).
FORTIOS-UPDATE-002.rollback: Revert the FortiGuard transport to its prior values under 'config system fortiguard' (e.g. 'set fortiguard-anycast disable' to fall back to legacy unicast servers, or restore the previous protocol/port). Nothing in production traffic breaks from these changes - the only risk is choosing a wrong server location/protocol that the upstream firewall does not permit, which simply leaves FDN disconnected again.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-002.impact: Non-disruptive. Changing 'config system fortiguard' and running 'execute update-now' only re-establishes the management-plane connection to FortiGuard; it does not reboot the unit or drop SSL-VPN, IPsec, HA or admin sessions. The dependency is on upstream/DNS changes, which should be coordinated but are outside the FortiGate itself. No maintenance window required.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - FortiGuard connectivity recommendation (tool COMPLIANCE_MAP: 10.1.2)
PCI-DSS v4.0 - 6.3.3, 5.3 (controls kept current)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 - SI-2 Flaw Remediation

FORTIOS-UPDATE-003.risk: A FortiGuard subscription is valid today but expires within 30 days. If it lapses before renewal, the appliance quietly stops receiving AV/IPS signatures, web/DNS ratings and botnet lists - the same failure mode as an already-expired license (FORTIOS-UPDATE-001), just on a fuse. The danger is operational: renewals often slip through procurement, and the day after expiry the box is defenceless against any newly published threat while still appearing 'up'. Business impact: an avoidable protection gap and a scramble during an incident, plus a compliance lapse if the audit window straddles the expiry date.
steps: Identify the exact package and expiry date: 'diagnose autoupdate versions' shows the 'Contract Expiry Date' per component; 'get system fortiguard' confirms the registered services.
In FortiCare (https://support.fortinet.com), confirm the serial number is registered and start the renewal for the expiring SKU (UTP/ATP/Enterprise bundle) before the listed date.
Complete the purchase order early - allow lead time for procurement so the contract is applied to the serial number ahead of expiry.
After the renewal is booked in FortiCare, pull the new entitlement: 'diagnose fdsm contract-controller-update'.
Force a refresh so the box reflects the extended contract: 'execute update-now'.
Confirm the new expiry: 'diagnose autoupdate versions' should now show a 'Contract Expiry Date' roughly one year out.
Make sure automatic updating stays enabled so nothing else silently ages: 'config system autoupdate schedule' / 'set status enable' / 'set frequency automatic' / 'end'.
Set an internal reminder 60 days before the new expiry to repeat the cycle.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-003.gui: FortiGate: Dashboard > Status (FortiGuard/Licenses widget) shows the days-to-expiry per service; System > FortiGuard shows contract state. Renewal itself is performed in the FortiCare portal at support.fortinet.com. FortiManager: FortiGuard pane > License to monitor managed-device entitlements centrally.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-003.cli:
# Renewal is a FortiCare portal action (support.fortinet.com), not a config command.
# After the contract is applied to the serial number, sync and refresh on the FortiGate:
diagnose fdsm contract-controller-update
execute update-now
get system fortiguard
FORTIOS-UPDATE-003.verify: diagnose autoupdate versions   -> 'Contract Expiry Date' for AV/IPS/Web Filter now shows a date ~12 months in the future (well beyond the previous 30-day window); Dashboard > Status FortiGuard widget no longer shows an expiring/amber warning.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-003.rollback: Not applicable - extending a subscription only lengthens protection coverage and cannot disrupt traffic. If the sync does not reflect the new date immediately, re-run 'diagnose fdsm contract-controller-update' followed by 'execute update-now'.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-003.impact: Non-disruptive. Contract sync and 'execute update-now' are management-plane operations; no reboot and no impact to SSL-VPN, IPsec, HA or admin sessions. No maintenance window required.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - FortiGuard subscription currency (aligns with tool COMPLIANCE_MAP 10.1.1)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 - SI-2 Flaw Remediation
Fortinet Document Library - FortiCare & FortiGuard Licenses (renewal workflow)
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'FortiGuard' chapter
CWE-1104: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components

FORTIOS-UPDATE-004.risk: AntiVirus/IPS signatures are more than 7 days old even though these packages are meant to update daily (often multiple times per day for AV outbreak feeds). A signature set that lags a week misses the entire recent wave of malware variants and freshly weaponized CVEs - exactly the window attackers exploit fastest. An adversary distributing a current commodity loader or exploiting a just-patched vulnerability will pass the AV scan and evade IPS because the matching signature never landed on the box. Business impact: real malware infections and exploit success against an appliance the organization believes is protecting it, plus PCI/NIST findings for anti-malware and IPS not being current.
steps: Determine how stale each package is: 'diagnose autoupdate versions' shows the 'Last Updated' date/time for AV Engine, AV Definitions, IPS Engine and IPS Definitions.

Check why updates are not landing: 'diagnose autoupdate status' ('FDN availability' and 'Scheduled update' state) and 'diagnose debug rating' for connectivity - if FDN is down, resolve FORTIOS-UPDATE-002 first.
Ensure the update schedule is enabled and frequent: 'config system autoupdate schedule' / 'set status enable' / 'set frequency automatic' / 'end'.
Enable push updates so urgent AV outbreak signatures arrive immediately: 'config system autoupdate push-update' / 'set status enable' / 'end'.
Force an immediate catch-up update: 'execute update-now'.
Confirm the pull succeeded and versions advanced: 'diagnose autoupdate versions' should now show a 'Last Updated' timestamp within the last day and higher definition version numbers.
If the update fails, verify the license is valid (FORTIOS-UPDATE-001) and that outbound HTTPS to FortiGuard is permitted end-to-end.
Re-run 'diagnose autoupdate status' to confirm the next scheduled update is queued.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-004.gui: FortiGate: System > FortiGuard shows the AntiVirus and IPS definition versions with their last-update time and an 'Update' / 'Update Now' button; the Scheduled Updates control sets frequency there. FortiManager (as local FDS): FortiGuard pane > Package Management to push AV/IPS packages to managed devices.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-004.cli:
config system autoupdate schedule
    set status enable
    set frequency automatic
end
config system autoupdate push-update
    set status enable
end
execute update-now
FORTIOS-UPDATE-004.verify: diagnose autoupdate versions   -> 'AV Definitions' and 'IPS Definitions' each show 'Last Updated' within the last 24 hours (and a version number newer than the flagged one); diagnose autoupdate status shows 'FDN availability: available'.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-004.rollback: Rollback is neither needed nor advisable - newer signatures strictly improve detection. FortiOS retains the ability to roll back only the engine in rare false-positive situations, but definition currency should not be reverted. If a specific updated IPS signature causes false positives, tune that signature's action in the IPS sensor rather than reverting the whole package.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-004.impact: Non-disruptive. 'execute update-now' and enabling the schedule/push cause the AV and IPS engines to hot-load new definitions with no reboot and no drop of SSL-VPN, IPsec, HA or admin sessions; inline inspection continues during the reload. No maintenance window required.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Ensure Antivirus Definition Push Updates are Configured' (FortiGuard/Updates section)
PCI-DSS v4.0 - 5.3.1/5.3.2 (anti-malware current), 11.5.1 (IDS/IPS current)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 - SI-3 Malicious Code Protection, SI-2 Flaw Remediation
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'FortiGuard' chapter (AntiVirus & IPS updates, push updates)
Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide - keeping AV/IPS signatures current
CWE-1104: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components

FORTIOS-UPDATE-005.risk: A FortiGuard service (e.g. Web Filter, Application Control, Anti-Spam, or another non-AV/IPS package) has not updated in over 30 days. Over a month, category databases and reputation lists drift badly out of date: newly registered phishing and malware-hosting domains are still rated benign, new application signatures are missing, and outbreak intelligence is stale. An attacker hosting a fresh watering-hole or C2 domain benefits directly because the URL/DNS categories that would block it were never refreshed. Business impact: users reach malicious/uncategorized sites, weakened outbound C2 blocking, and a control-currency compliance gap.
steps: Identify the stale package and how long it has lagged: 'diagnose autoupdate versions' ('Last Updated' per component).
Check the delivery path: 'diagnose autoupdate status' and 'diagnose debug rating' - if FDN is unreachable, fix FORTIOS-UPDATE-002; if the license is expired, fix FORTIOS-UPDATE-001.
Confirm the schedule is on: 'config system autoupdate schedule' / 'set status enable' / 'set frequency automatic' / 'end'.
Force an update: 'execute update-now'.
Verify the package advanced: 'diagnose autoupdate versions' should now show a recent 'Last Updated' timestamp.
For live-rated services (Web/DNS Filter), confirm rating lookups work: 'diagnose debug rating' should show reachable rating servers with RTT.
If the package still will not update while others do, confirm that specific subscription is licensed in the FortiGuard widget (Dashboard > Status).
Re-run 'diagnose autoupdate status' to confirm the next scheduled run is queued.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-005.gui: FortiGate: System > FortiGuard lists each service's database version and last-update time with an update control; Dashboard > Status FortiGuard widget flags stale services. FortiManager (as local FDS): FortiGuard pane > Package Management / Web & DNS Filter to refresh and redistribute databases to managed units.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-005.cli:
execute update-now
config system autoupdate schedule
    set status enable
    set frequency automatic
end
diagnose autoupdate versions
FORTIOS-UPDATE-005.verify: diagnose autoupdate versions   -> the previously stale package shows 'Last Updated' within the last day; for Web/DNS Filter, 'diagnose debug rating' returns reachable rating servers (non-zero RTT).
FORTIOS-UPDATE-005.rollback: Not applicable - refreshing a database only makes ratings more accurate and current. If a newly refreshed web-filter category unexpectedly blocks a legitimate site, create a URL override/allow entry in the Web Filter profile rather than reverting the database.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-005.impact: Non-disruptive. Database refresh via 'execute update-now' is a management-plane pull; no reboot and no impact to SSL-VPN, IPsec, HA or admin sessions. No maintenance window required.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - FortiGuard service currency (FortiGuard/Updates section)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 - SI-2 Flaw Remediation
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'FortiGuard' chapter (Web Filter / rating updates)
Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide - keep FortiGuard databases current
CWE-1104: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components

FORTIOS-UPDATE-006.risk: The scheduled automatic FortiGuard update ('config system autoupdate schedule' status is not 'enable'), so the FortiGate will never pull new AV/IPS/web-filter definitions on its own - it relies on someone remembering to click Update Now. In practice that means the box's threat definitions freeze at whatever version was current when auto-update was turned off, and they rot silently. An attacker exploiting any post-freeze CVE or distributing any newer malware variant is not inspected against the missing signatures. Business impact: steadily widening detection blind spot, guaranteed drift toward the stale-signature findings above, and an audit failure for not maintaining current security controls.
steps: Confirm the current state: 'get system autoupdate schedule' (status/frequency) and 'diagnose autoupdate status' ('Scheduled update' line).

Enable the schedule with an automatic (frequent) cadence: 'config system autoupdate schedule' / 'set status enable' / 'set frequency automatic' / 'end'. (Alternatively 'set frequency every' with 'set time 01:00' to update hourly, or 'daily'/'weekly' if policy requires a fixed window.)
Enable push updates so critical outbreak signatures arrive between scheduled runs: 'config system autoupdate push-update' / 'set status enable' / 'end'.
Confirm FDN connectivity so the schedule actually succeeds: 'diagnose autoupdate status' should show 'FDN availability: available' (fix FORTIOS-UPDATE-002 first if not).
Kick off one immediate update to close the current gap: 'execute update-now'.
Verify the schedule is now active: 'get system autoupdate schedule' shows 'status: enable' and 'frequency: automatic'.
Verify definitions advanced: 'diagnose autoupdate versions' shows a recent 'Last Updated' timestamp.
If managed centrally, confirm no FortiManager provisioning template is overriding the schedule back to disabled.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-006.gui: FortiGate: System > FortiGuard > Scheduled Updates - toggle updates on and select the frequency (Automatic / Every / Daily / Weekly), and enable 'Allow Push Update' / AntiVirus & IPS Updates. FortiManager: Device Manager > Provisioning Templates (or System Template) to set the autoupdate schedule and push it to managed devices consistently.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-006.cli:
config system autoupdate schedule
    set status enable
    set frequency automatic
end
config system autoupdate push-update
    set status enable
end
FORTIOS-UPDATE-006.verify: get system autoupdate schedule   -> 'status: enable' and 'frequency: automatic' (or your chosen cadence); diagnose autoupdate status   -> 'Scheduled update: enable' and 'FDN availability: available'.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-006.rollback: To revert, set the schedule back off: 'config system autoupdate schedule' / 'set status disable' / 'end'. This is strongly discouraged because it re-opens the signature-drift blind spot; the only legitimate reason to disable it is when a FortiManager local FDS is authoritatively distributing packages instead - in which case the schedule points at the FMG rather than being disabled.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-006.impact: Non-disruptive. Enabling the schedule and push updates changes only management-plane behaviour; scheduled/pushed definition loads are hot-applied by the AV/IPS engines with no reboot and no drop of SSL-VPN, IPsec, HA or admin sessions. No maintenance window required.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark - 'Ensure Antivirus Definition Push Updates are Configured' / scheduled FortiGuard updates
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 - SI-2 Flaw Remediation, SI-3 Malicious Code Protection
Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'FortiGuard' chapter (scheduled and push updates)
Fortinet CLI Reference - 'config system autoupdate schedule' / 'config system autoupdate push-update'
CWE-1104: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components

FORTIOS-UPDATE-007.risk: The FortiGate is running FortiOS on an end-of-life branch (6.0 or 6.2) that no longer receives security patches from Fortinet. These branches are affected by multiple critical, actively-exploited pre-auth vulnerabilities that will never be fixed on this firmware - for example the SSL-VPN heap overflows/out-of-bounds writes tracked as CVE-2022-42475, CVE-2023-27997 (XORtigate) and CVE-2024-21762, plus the CVE-2018-13379 SSL-VPN path traversal. An unauthenticated attacker on the internet can chain these to gain remote code execution or steal VPN credentials directly on the perimeter device, then pivot into the internal network. Business impact: full perimeter compromise, credential theft, ransomware staging, and near-certain breach - this is a top-tier, exploited-in-the-wild exposure, not a theoretical one.
steps: Record the exact running version and model: 'get system status' (Version / build / serial) and confirm the branch is 6.0 or 6.2.
Use the Fortinet Upgrade Path tool (support.fortinet.com) to plan the validated hop sequence from your current build to a supported branch (7.0/7.2/7.4) - do NOT jump directly across major versions.

As an immediate compensating control until the upgrade window, restrict exposure: disable or IP-restrict SSL-VPN and admin access on WAN (trusthosts / local-in policy) to blunt the known exploited CVEs.
Back up the current configuration before touching firmware: 'execute backup config tftp <backup-file>.conf <tftp-server-ip>' (also keep a GUI backup).
Schedule a maintenance window - a firmware upgrade reboots the unit and interrupts all traffic (or triggers HA failovers on a cluster).

Upgrade via GUI (System > Firmware, upload the signed image and follow the upgrade path) or via CLI: 'execute restore image tftp <fortios-image.out> <tftp-server-ip>' - the unit reboots into the new image after each hop.
On an HA cluster, enable graceful upgrade to minimise downtime: 'config system ha' / 'set uninterruptible-upgrade enable' / 'end', then upgrade the cluster.
After reaching the supported branch, confirm the version and re-run FortiGuard updates ('execute update-now') and validate SSL-VPN/IPsec/policies function.
Subscribe to Fortinet PSIRT advisories and set a policy to stay within a vendor-supported branch going forward.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-007.gui: FortiGate: System > Firmware - upload the signed image, review the recommended upgrade path, back up config, then upgrade (device reboots). FortiManager: Device Manager > (select device/group) > Firmware Upgrade to stage the validated path and push images to managed units centrally.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-007.cli:
# A firmware upgrade replaces the OS image - it cannot be done with a config 'set' command.
# Use the validated Fortinet Upgrade Path tool (support.fortinet.com) and upgrade one hop at a time.
# GUI:  System > Firmware > upload signed image > Backup config first > Upgrade (unit reboots)
# CLI (TFTP):
execute backup config tftp <backup-file>.conf <tftp-server-ip>
execute restore image tftp <fortios-image.out> <tftp-server-ip>
# For HA, first: config system ha / set uninterruptible-upgrade enable / end
FORTIOS-UPDATE-007.verify: get system status   -> 'Version: FortiGate-<model> v7.2.x,build....' (or 7.0.x / 7.4.x) confirming the box is on a currently-supported, patched branch and no longer on 6.0/6.2; then 'get system fortiguard' / 'diagnose autoupdate versions' confirm signatures repopulate on the new build.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-007.rollback: FortiOS can be downgraded with 'execute restore image', but downgrading across major versions typically resets the device to factory defaults - the running config is NOT preserved. Therefore keep the pre-upgrade config backup from step 4; if the new build misbehaves, restore the prior firmware and then restore that matching config backup. Note that rolling back re-exposes the unit to the unpatched EOL CVEs, so downgrade only as a temporary measure.
FORTIOS-UPDATE-007.impact: Disruptive - requires a maintenance window. A firmware upgrade reboots the appliance, dropping all SSL-VPN and IPsec tunnels, admin sessions, and transit traffic during the reboot (single unit = full outage of a few minutes per hop). On an HA cluster, 'set uninterruptible-upgrade enable' upgrades members in turn to minimise downtime, but each member still reboots and a failover occurs. Plan for multiple reboots if the upgrade path requires intermediate hops.
references: Fortinet FortiGate Hardening Guide - 'Install firmware from a trusted source' / keep FortiOS up to date
Fortinet PSIRT FG-IR-22-398 (CVE-2022-42475, SSL-VPN heap overflow, exploited)
Fortinet PSIRT FG-IR-23-097 (CVE-2023-27997 'XORtigate', SSL-VPN heap overflow)
Fortinet PSIRT FG-IR-24-015 (CVE-2024-21762, SSL-VPN out-of-bounds write, exploited)
Fortinet PSIRT FG-IR-18-384 (CVE-2018-13379, SSL-VPN path traversal, exploited)
Fortinet Product Life Cycle / Supported Releases (6.0 & 6.2 End-of-Support)
PCI-DSS v4.0 - 6.3.3 (install applicable security patches); NIST SP 800-53 SI-2 Flaw Remediation
CWE-1104: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components; CWE-1329: Reliance on a Component That is Not Updateable

FORTIOS-USAGE-001.risk: A dormant allow policy that has matched no traffic since its counters were reset widens the attack surface with no business benefit. Unused rules accumulate as environments change; each one is a standing permission an attacker could leverage after a lateral move, and a rule nobody remembers is a rule nobody is maintaining.
steps: Confirm the policy is genuinely unused: compare its zero counters against device uptime (get system status) and account for seasonal / periodic traffic.
Check hit history over time via FortiAnalyzer or FortiManager Policy Optimizer rather than a single snapshot.
Disable the policy for an observation window: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set status disable / next / end.
If it remains unused after the window, remove it: config firewall policy / delete <id> / end.
Record the decision (owner, date) so the rule is not silently re-created.
Re-run the scanner to confirm the dormant rule is gone.
FORTIOS-USAGE-001.gui: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy: enable the 'Hit Count'/'Last Used' columns, disable then later delete rules with no usage. FortiManager > Policy Optimizer automates unused-rule detection and recertification.
FORTIOS-USAGE-001.cli:
config firewall policy
  edit <policyid>
    set status disable
  next
end
# after an observation window with still no traffic:
# config firewall policy
#   delete <policyid>
# end
FORTIOS-USAGE-001.verify: diagnose firewall iprope show 100004 <policyid>  (or the monitor API) shows zero bytes/packets; after disabling, confirm no service is broken over the observation window.
FORTIOS-USAGE-001.rollback: set status enable (or re-create from backup) if a required but infrequently-used flow breaks. Prefer disable-then-delete over immediate deletion.
FORTIOS-USAGE-001.impact: Disabling a truly-unused rule is non-disruptive; disabling a rule that only looked unused (short uptime, seasonal traffic) will break that flow - hence the observation window. No reboot; syncs on HA.
references: FireMon/industry practice - unused-rule recertification and cleanup
Fortinet - policy hit count / FortiManager Policy Optimizer
PCI-DSS 1.1.7, NIST SP 800-53 CM-7 & AC-4
CWE-1164: Irrelevant Code

FORTIOS-WF-001.risk: Safe Search is not enforced, so search engines (Google/Bing/YouTube) can return explicit and unfiltered results even where a web filter is otherwise applied. Beyond acceptable-use/HR exposure, unfiltered search results and image proxies can surface malicious/adult links that bypass category intent. Business impact: policy/compliance violations (especially in education/regulated environments) and an additional avenue to risky content.
steps: Identify the profile: 'show webfilter profile <name>' and check the 'config web' subsection.
Edit the profile: config webfilter profile / edit "<name>".
Enter the web subsection: config web.
Enforce safe search by URL and header rewrite: set safe-search url header.
(Optional) Restrict YouTube: set youtube-restrict strict (or moderate) as policy requires.
Close subsections and save: end / next / end.
Ensure the profile is applied on user policies with deep SSL inspection (safe search header rewrite requires HTTPS inspection for encrypted search).
Validate by searching an explicit term and confirming filtered results.
FORTIOS-WF-001.gui: Security Profiles > Web Filter > edit <profile> > Search Engines > enable 'Enforce Safe Search' (and YouTube restriction if needed). FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Web Filter.
FORTIOS-WF-001.cli:
config webfilter profile
    edit "<profile>"
        config web
            set safe-search url header
        end
    next
end
FORTIOS-WF-001.verify: show webfilter profile <profile> | grep safe-search  -> set safe-search url header. Live: perform a search on Google/Bing over an inspected policy and confirm SafeSearch is forced on.
FORTIOS-WF-001.rollback: Revert with 'config webfilter profile / edit <profile> / config web / unset safe-search / end / next / end'. Disabling safe search returns unfiltered search results; roll back only if it breaks a specific business workflow.
FORTIOS-WF-001.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Header-based enforcement over HTTPS search requires deep SSL inspection to take effect. Minimal user-visible impact beyond forced-safe search results.
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Web filter > Safe search / restrict YouTube access
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4 Security Profiles (Web Filter)
NIST SP 800-53 SC-7 Boundary Protection
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-WF-002.risk: The web filter profile exists but has no FortiGuard category filters configured, so it enforces nothing - malware-hosting, phishing, and high-risk categories are all effectively allowed. This is a silent failure: the policy appears to have web filtering, but there is no category-based blocking, giving attackers an open outbound web path for delivery and C2. Business impact: phishing/malware delivery and C2 egress despite an ostensibly configured control.
steps: Confirm the empty filter set: 'show webfilter profile <name>' shows 'config ftgd-wf' with no 'config filters' entries.
Verify the FortiGuard Web Filtering license is active (System > FortiGuard).
Edit the profile: config webfilter profile / edit "<name>" / config ftgd-wf / config filters.

Block security categories: edit 1 / set category 26 / set action block (Malicious Websites) / next; edit 2 / set category 61 / set action block (Phishing) / next; edit 3 / set category 88 / set action block (Newly Registered Domain) / next.
Add high-risk/liability categories per policy (e.g., Spam URLs, Dynamic DNS, proxy-avoidance) with block or warning.
Close and save: end / end / next / end.
Confirm the profile is bound to web-egress policies with deep SSL inspection.
Validate against a known-blocked test URL and confirm the block page and log entry.
Verify current category IDs in the GUI category list, since IDs can differ across FortiOS releases.
FORTIOS-WF-002.gui: Security Profiles > Web Filter > edit <profile> > FortiGuard Category Based Filter > set Security Risk (Malicious/Phishing/Newly Observed/Registered) and other high-risk categories to Block. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Web Filter.
FORTIOS-WF-002.cli:
config webfilter profile
    edit "<profile>"
        config ftgd-wf
            config filters
                edit 1
                    set category 26
                    set action block
                next
                edit 2
                    set category 61
                    set action block
                next
                edit 3
                    set category 88
                    set action block
                next
            end
        end
    next
end
FORTIOS-WF-002.verify: show webfilter profile <profile> | grep -A2 'config filters'  -> filter entries with action block. Live: browse a URL in a blocked category and confirm FortiGuard block page + Web Filter log.
FORTIOS-WF-002.rollback: Remove specific filters with 'config webfilter profile / edit <profile> / config ftgd-wf / config filters / delete <id> / end / end / next / end'. Removing category blocking re-opens malicious/phishing categories; prefer downgrading a specific over-blocking category to warning rather than clearing all filters.
FORTIOS-WF-002.impact: Non-disruptive: no reboot or session drops. Full HTTPS category enforcement needs deep SSL inspection. Overly broad category blocking can disrupt legitimate browsing - align with the organization's acceptable-use policy.
references: FortiOS 7.4 Administration Guide - Security Profiles > Web filter > FortiGuard category based filter
CIS Fortinet FortiGate 7.4.x Benchmark v1.0.1 - Section 4 Security Profiles (Web Filter)
Fortinet 'Hardening your FortiGate' guide
PCI-DSS v4.0 5.2
NIST SP 800-53 SC-7 Boundary Protection, SI-3 Malicious Code Protection
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-WIRELESS-001.risk: An SSID running Open, WEP, or basic WPA-Personal (TKIP) offers no real confidentiality: WEP RC4 keys fall to aircrack in minutes, Open is plaintext, and WPA/TKIP has Beck-Tews/Michael-MIC weaknesses and no forward secrecy while a WPA-Personal PSK is recovered via handshake capture plus offline dictionary/GPU cracking. An attacker within RF range joins the corporate WLAN, decrypts user traffic, harvests credentials, and pivots onto the internal VLAN. Business impact: breach of everything traversing WiFi (including PCI cardholder data), unauthorized network access, and regulatory penalties.
steps: Inventory every SSID and its mode: `show wireless-controller vap` and note any security value containing open/wep/wpa-personal.
For corporate SSIDs, stand up or confirm a RADIUS server for 802.1X: `config user radius` / `edit "corp-nps"` / `set server <radius-ip>` / `set secret <shared-secret>` / `next` / `end`.

Apply enterprise security to the VAP: `config wireless-controller vap` / `edit "<ssid-name>"` / `set security wpa2-only-enterprise` (or `wpa3-only-enterprise`) / `set auth radius` / `set radius-server "corp-nps"` / `next` / `end`.
Force AES/CCMP and never TKIP: within the same VAP edit run `set encrypt aes`.

For networks that cannot do 802.1X (guest/IoT), use WPA3-SAE with a long passphrase: `set security wpa3-sae` / `set sae-password "<32+ char secret>"`, or `set security wpa3-sae-transition` for mixed WPA2/WPA3 client fleets.
Migrate or remove every remaining open/wep64/wep128/wpa-personal SSID.
Push the config to the FortiAPs and confirm the radios reload the VAP.
Distribute 802.1X supplicant profiles / client certificates (or the new SAE passphrase) so clients can reassociate.
Re-run the scanner / `show wireless-controller vap` to confirm no weak modes remain.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-001.gui: FortiGate: WiFi & Switch Controller > SSIDs > edit the SSID > Security Mode = WPA2 Enterprise / WPA3 Enterprise (RADIUS) or WPA3 SAE Personal, Encryption = AES. (Menu appears only when the WiFi Controller feature is enabled under System > Feature Visibility.) FortiManager: Device Manager > AP Manager > SSIDs > edit SSID.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-001.cli:
config wireless-controller vap
  edit <ssid-name>
    set security wpa2-only-enterprise
  next
end
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-001.verify: show wireless-controller vap <ssid-name> | grep security  ->  expect `set security wpa2-only-enterprise` (or `wpa3-only-enterprise`). Confirm encryption with `show full-configuration wireless-controller vap <ssid-name> | grep encrypt`  ->  expect `set encrypt aes` (AES is the WPA2/WPA3 default and is hidden by a plain `show`, so use `show full-configuration`). Note: FortiOS `grep` supports -i/-n/-v/-f/-c/-A/-B/-C but not GNU-style `-e`, so filter one pattern at a time. Runtime confirmation: `diagnose wireless-controller wlac -c vap` lists the active VAPs/BSSIDs.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-001.rollback: Revert with `config wireless-controller vap / edit "<ssid-name>" / set security <previous-mode> / end`. Do NOT roll back to open/wep/wpa-personal (re-exposes the WLAN); if enterprise mode causes RADIUS problems, fall back to WPA3-SAE instead. Breaking risk: clients without the correct 802.1X supplicant/certificate or the new PSK cannot connect until reconfigured.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-001.impact: Wireless-only disruption: clients on the changed SSID are deauthenticated and must reassociate with the new method/credentials; enterprise mode also requires a working 802.1X supplicant and RADIUS reachability. No FortiGate reboot; does NOT affect SSL-VPN, IPsec, HA, or admin sessions. Schedule a WLAN maintenance window and pre-stage supplicant/cert configuration.
references: CIS FortiGate Benchmark 11.1.1 (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 4.2.1.2 (strong cryptography for wireless transmission of CHD) and Req 2.2
NIST SP 800-153 (Guidelines for Securing WLANs); NIST SP 800-97 (802.11i RSN)
IEEE 802.11i / Wi-Fi Alliance WPA3 specification
Fortinet FortiWiFi and FortiAP Configuration Guide - 'SSID security modes' / 'WPA3 Security'
CWE-326: Inadequate Encryption Strength

FORTIOS-WIRELESS-002.risk: Without intra-VAP privacy (client isolation), every station on the guest/visitor SSID shares one L2 broadcast domain. A malicious guest can ARP-spoof, port-scan, and directly attack other guests' devices (SMB, RDP, mobile shares), sniff peer traffic, and spread worms or ransomware laterally, using the guest network as a beachhead. Business impact: guest-to-guest compromise, reputational and legal liability, and a pivot point if the guest VLAN is under-segmented.
steps: Identify guest/visitor SSIDs: `show wireless-controller vap` and check `intra-vap-privacy`.
Enable client isolation on each guest VAP: `config wireless-controller vap` / `edit "<guest-ssid>"` / `set intra-vap-privacy enable` / `next` / `end`.

Because intra-vap-privacy blocks station-to-station traffic within the AP/VAP, also isolate at L3: ensure the guest VLAN uses a private/isolated VLAN or add a firewall policy that denies guest-subnet-to-guest-subnet traffic.
Constrain the guest VAP to Internet-only with a firewall policy: deny guest -> internal RFC1918, allow guest -> WAN with UTM (web filter/AV/IPS/app control).
Optionally add a captive portal and per-user bandwidth/rate limiting on the guest policy.
Push the config and confirm the APs reload the VAP.
Validate that legitimate guest Internet access still works.
