FORTIOS-WIRELESS-002.gui: FortiGate: WiFi & Switch Controller > SSIDs > edit the guest SSID > enable 'Block Intra-SSID Traffic'. FortiManager: Device Manager > AP Manager > SSIDs > edit SSID.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-002.cli:
config wireless-controller vap
  edit <guest-ssid>
    set intra-vap-privacy enable
  next
end
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-002.verify: show wireless-controller vap <guest-ssid> | grep intra-vap-privacy  ->  expect `set intra-vap-privacy enable`.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-002.rollback: `config wireless-controller vap / edit "<guest-ssid>" / set intra-vap-privacy disable / end` re-enables guest-to-guest traffic. Only needed if a legitimate peer service (AirPlay/Chromecast/local printing) must work on the guest SSID - better solved with a scoped mDNS/Bonjour policy than by disabling isolation.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-002.impact: Non-disruptive: existing guests keep Internet access; only station-to-station frames within the SSID are dropped. No reboot and no SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA/admin impact. Note it will break peer services such as screen mirroring or local printing on the guest SSID.
references: CIS FortiGate Benchmark 11.1.2 (scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 1.3 / 1.4 (restrict network access to/from the CDE; control connections between trusted and untrusted networks - segmentation)
NIST SP 800-153 (client isolation guidance)
Fortinet FortiWiFi and FortiAP Configuration Guide - 'Block intra-SSID traffic'
CWE-284: Improper Access Control

FORTIOS-WIRELESS-003.risk: Broadcasting an internal or management SSID advertises its existence and name, aiding target selection, evil-twin/Karma attacks, and social engineering against staff. Hiding the SSID is defense-in-depth (obscurity) only - a non-broadcast SSID is still discoverable when clients probe for it - but it reduces casual discovery and makes rogue-AP impersonation of a named internal network less trivial. Business impact: easier reconnaissance of the internal WLAN and a lower bar for evil-twin credential harvesting against a recognizable corporate SSID.
steps: Identify internal/management SSIDs still broadcasting: `show wireless-controller vap` and check `broadcast-ssid`.
Disable the broadcast: `config wireless-controller vap` / `edit "<internal-ssid>"` / `set broadcast-ssid disable` / `next` / `end`.
Pre-provision the (now hidden) SSID on managed endpoints via MDM/GPO so they join without a beacon.
Combine hiding with strong authentication (WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise) and PMF - obscurity alone is not a control.
Deploy a WIDS profile (see WIRELESS-004) to detect evil-twin APs broadcasting the same SSID name.
Push the config and verify.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-003.gui: FortiGate: WiFi & Switch Controller > SSIDs > edit the SSID; disabling the beacon is the CLI setting `set broadcast-ssid disable` (a 'Broadcast SSID' toggle appears on the SSID form in some builds). FortiManager: Device Manager > AP Manager > SSIDs.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-003.cli:
config wireless-controller vap
  edit <internal-ssid>
    set broadcast-ssid disable
  next
end
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-003.verify: show wireless-controller vap <internal-ssid> | grep broadcast-ssid  ->  expect `set broadcast-ssid disable`.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-003.rollback: `set broadcast-ssid enable` simply re-advertises the SSID; already-configured clients are unaffected. Breaking risk: onboarding new clients to a hidden SSID requires a manual/MDM profile, and some IoT devices cannot join non-broadcast SSIDs.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-003.impact: Low / near non-disruptive; the SSID may bounce briefly when the AP reloads the VAP. Hidden SSIDs complicate new-client onboarding and can break auto-join on certain IoT/mobile devices. No reboot and no SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA/admin impact.
references: Fortinet FortiWiFi and FortiAP Configuration Guide - 'Broadcast SSID'
NIST SP 800-153 (note: SSID hiding is not a substitute for encryption/authentication)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 2.2 (secure configuration standards)
CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

FORTIOS-WIRELESS-004.risk: With no WIDS profile - or a profile that has ap-scan disabled - the FortiGate cannot detect unauthorized/rogue APs, evil twins, honeypots, deauth/disassoc floods, ASLEAP, EAPOL floods, or wireless bridges. An attacker stands up a rogue AP advertising the corporate SSID and harvests 802.1X/PSK credentials via evil-twin, or a rogue employee AP bridges the trusted LAN onto an open WiFi. Business impact: undetected wireless intrusion and credential theft, a persistent wireless backdoor, and PCI-DSS non-compliance (rogue-AP detection is mandatory).
steps: Create a WIDS profile with rogue scanning and attack detection: `config wireless-controller wids-profile` / `edit "protect-wids"` / `set ap-scan enable` / `set asleap-attack enable` / `set deauth-broadcast enable` / `set eapol-start-flood enable` / `set eapol-logoff-flood enable` / `set wireless-bridge enable` / `set long-duration-attack enable` / `next` / `end`.

Apply the WIDS profile to each radio of the FortiAP profile: `config wireless-controller wtp-profile` / `edit "<ap-profile>"` / `config radio-1` / `set wids-profile "protect-wids"` / `end` / `config radio-2` / `set wids-profile "protect-wids"` / `end` / `next` / `end`.
Choose the scan mode: background scanning (serving radios go off-channel periodically) for coverage, or a dedicated monitor radio for continuous full-band WIDS.
Review legal/operational policy before enabling any automatic rogue suppression; start in detect-only.
Push to the APs and confirm each radio picks up the profile.
Monitor detections in WiFi & Switch Controller > Rogue APs; mark known-good APs as accepted and investigate the rest.
Forward WIDS events to FortiAnalyzer/syslog so rogue-AP and flood alerts are actioned.
Schedule the quarterly rogue-AP review required for PCI-DSS.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-004.gui: FortiGate: WiFi & Switch Controller > WIDS Profiles (create the profile and enable ap-scan + detections), then WiFi & Switch Controller > FortiAP Profiles > edit profile > set the per-radio WIDS Profile. Monitoring: WiFi & Switch Controller > Rogue APs (or the Dashboard WiFi widget). FortiManager: Device Manager > AP Manager > WIDS Profiles and FortiAP Profiles.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-004.cli:
config wireless-controller wids-profile
  edit "protect-wids"
    set ap-scan enable
    set asleap-attack enable
    set deauth-broadcast enable
    set eapol-start-flood enable
    set eapol-logoff-flood enable
    set wireless-bridge enable
    set long-duration-attack enable
  next
end
config wireless-controller wtp-profile
  edit "<ap-profile>"
    config radio-1
      set wids-profile "protect-wids"
    end
    config radio-2
      set wids-profile "protect-wids"
    end
  next
end
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-004.verify: show wireless-controller wids-profile protect-wids | grep ap-scan  ->  expect `set ap-scan enable`; and `show wireless-controller wtp-profile <ap-profile>` shows `set wids-profile "protect-wids"` under each radio. Operational check: WiFi & Switch Controller > Rogue APs lists detected off-network APs.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-004.rollback: Remove detection with `config wireless-controller wtp-profile / edit "<ap-profile>" / config radio-1 / unset wids-profile / end / next / end`, or `set ap-scan disable` in the WIDS profile. Only do this if background scanning measurably harms serving performance - prefer a dedicated monitor radio over disabling detection entirely.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-004.impact: Non-disruptive to the FortiGate. Background scanning adds brief off-channel dwell (minor client latency); a dedicated monitor radio removes that radio from serving clients. No reboot and no SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA/admin impact.
references: PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 11.2.1 (detect/identify authorized and unauthorized wireless access points)
NIST SP 800-153 (WLAN monitoring / rogue detection)
Fortinet FortiWiFi and FortiAP Configuration Guide - 'Wireless Intrusion Detection System (WIDS)' and 'Rogue AP detection'
CWE-778: Insufficient Logging

FORTIOS-WIRELESS-005.risk: max-clients=0 means the SSID accepts unlimited associations. An attacker can run an association/authentication flood - mass-associating spoofed clients (or a botnet of devices) - to exhaust the AP association table, DHCP pool, and RADIUS backend, denying service to legitimate users, while also driving airtime contention that degrades everyone. Business impact: wireless denial-of-service and outage of WiFi-dependent operations.
steps: Determine realistic per-radio capacity for good QoE (commonly 30-50 clients per radio).
Set a finite cap on the VAP: `config wireless-controller vap` / `edit "<ssid-name>"` / `set max-clients <N>` / `next` / `end` (0 = unlimited).
Size N with headroom for legitimate peak density so real users are not blocked.
Enable WIDS flood detection (EAPOL/assoc flood, see WIRELESS-004) to catch association floods early.
For guest/high-density SSIDs, also bound the DHCP scope and apply band steering / airtime fairness on the FortiAP profile.
Push the config and verify the limit is applied.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-005.gui: FortiGate: WiFi & Switch Controller > SSIDs > edit the SSID > set 'Maximum Clients' (restrict the number of clients). FortiManager: Device Manager > AP Manager > SSIDs.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-005.cli:
config wireless-controller vap
  edit <ssid-name>
    set max-clients <N>
  next
end
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-005.verify: show wireless-controller vap <ssid-name> | grep max-clients  ->  expect e.g. `set max-clients 50` (a non-zero value).
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-005.rollback: `set max-clients 0` removes the limit. Only revert for a legitimate high-density event, and even then raise the number and add APs rather than returning to unlimited.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-005.impact: Non-disruptive: existing sessions are retained and only new associations beyond N are refused (the intended behavior). No reboot and no SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA/admin impact. Set N with headroom to avoid blocking legitimate users during peaks.
references: NIST SP 800-153 (WLAN availability protection)
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 2.2 (secure configuration standards)
Fortinet FortiWiFi and FortiAP Configuration Guide - 'Maximum clients per SSID'
CWE-770: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling

FORTIOS-WIRELESS-006.risk: pmf=disable leaves 802.11 management frames (deauth/disassoc) unauthenticated. An attacker sends spoofed deauth/disassoc frames to knock clients off the network (the classic WiFi DoS), or forces reconnection to a rogue/evil-twin AP to capture handshakes and credentials; disabled PMF also facilitates KRACK-style and downgrade attacks. Business impact: targeted WiFi denial-of-service, evil-twin credential capture, and setup for session hijacking.
steps: Enable PMF on each SSID, starting in compatible mode: `config wireless-controller vap` / `edit "<ssid-name>"` / `set pmf optional` / `next` / `end`.
For high-security SSIDs (and mandatory with WPA3-SAE) require PMF: `set pmf enable` (enable = required).
Tune SA-query timers if needed: `set pmf-assoc-comeback-timeout 1` and `set pmf-sa-query-retry-timeout 2`.
Confirm the client base supports 802.11w (modern iOS/Android/Windows 10+ do); legacy clients may fail when PMF is required, so keep 'optional' for mixed fleets.
Verify WPA3-SAE SSIDs, which already force PMF required.
Push the config, confirm clients still associate, then promote optional -> enable after a monitoring period.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-006.gui: FortiGate: WiFi & Switch Controller > SSIDs > edit the SSID > Protected Management Frames (PMF) = Optional or Required. FortiManager: Device Manager > AP Manager > SSIDs.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-006.cli:
config wireless-controller vap
  edit <ssid-name>
    set pmf optional
  next
end
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-006.verify: show wireless-controller vap <ssid-name> | grep pmf  ->  expect `set pmf optional` (or `set pmf enable` for required).
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-006.rollback: `set pmf disable` reopens the deauth/disassoc DoS and evil-twin forcing - avoid it. If 'required' broke legacy clients, step down to `set pmf optional` rather than disabling PMF entirely.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-006.impact: 'optional' is non-disruptive: 802.11w-capable clients negotiate PMF and others fall back cleanly. 'enable' (required) may drop non-802.11w legacy clients, which must reassociate or be upgraded. No reboot and no SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA/admin impact.
references: IEEE 802.11w-2009 (Protected Management Frames)
Wi-Fi Alliance WPA3 specification (PMF mandatory for WPA3/SAE)
NIST SP 800-153
Fortinet FortiWiFi and FortiAP Configuration Guide - 'Protected Management Frames (PMF)'
CWE-345: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity

FORTIOS-WIRELESS-007.risk: dtls-policy clear-text disables DTLS on the CAPWAP data tunnel between the FortiAP and the FortiGate wireless controller. For tunnel-mode SSIDs, all wireless user traffic is carried across the wired network (AP -> controller) unencrypted. An attacker with access to that transport path (a span/mirror port, a compromised switch, or a tap on the AP uplink) can capture client traffic and credentials and inject frames. Business impact: cleartext exposure of all tunneled WLAN data, credential theft, and man-in-the-middle. (The CAPWAP control channel is DTLS-protected regardless; this finding concerns the data channel.)
steps: Identify FortiAP profiles carrying clear-text tunnels: `show wireless-controller wtp-profile` and check `dtls-policy`.
Enable DTLS on the data tunnel: `config wireless-controller wtp-profile` / `edit "<ap-profile>"` / `set dtls-policy dtls-enabled` / `next` / `end`.
On FortiAP models that support hardware DTLS offload, enable it to cut CPU load: `set dtls-in-kernel enable` within the same profile edit.
Note that DTLS protects tunnel-mode SSIDs; bridge/local-mode SSIDs bridge traffic locally at the AP and do not traverse the CAPWAP data tunnel - review each SSID's mode to understand exposure.
Push the config; the affected APs re-establish their CAPWAP tunnel (brief reconnect).
Verify the new policy and confirm SSIDs come back up.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-007.gui: FortiGate: WiFi & Switch Controller > FortiAP Profiles > edit the profile. The DTLS policy is a CLI setting (`set dtls-policy dtls-enabled`) and is not exposed on the standard FortiAP Profile form in most FortiOS builds. FortiManager: Device Manager > AP Manager > FortiAP Profiles.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-007.cli:
config wireless-controller wtp-profile
  edit "<ap-profile>"
    set dtls-policy dtls-enabled
    set dtls-in-kernel enable
  next
end
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-007.verify: show wireless-controller wtp-profile <ap-profile> | grep dtls-policy  ->  expect `set dtls-policy dtls-enabled`.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-007.rollback: `set dtls-policy clear-text` restores the unencrypted data tunnel (re-exposing traffic) and should be used only to troubleshoot throughput on low-power APs; changing the policy causes the APs to re-establish their tunnel.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-007.impact: Wireless disruption: APs using the profile re-establish their CAPWAP tunnel, briefly dropping tunnel-mode SSIDs while they reconnect; DTLS adds AP CPU load (use dtls-in-kernel on supported models). No FortiGate reboot and no SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA/admin impact. Schedule a short WLAN maintenance window.
references: RFC 5415/5416 (CAPWAP) and RFC 6347 (DTLS 1.2)
NIST SP 800-153
Fortinet FortiWiFi and FortiAP Configuration Guide - 'Encrypting CAPWAP data traffic with DTLS'
CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information

FORTIOS-WIRELESS-008.risk: 802.11r Fast Transition over the Distribution System (fast-roaming enabled with ft-over-ds enable) is affected by CVE-2017-13082 (KRACK), where the group/pairwise key is reinstalled during the FT reassociation, allowing an attacker to replay, decrypt, or forge frames as a client roams. FT-over-DS also carries the FT key exchange over the distribution system, widening the attack surface versus FT-over-Air. Business impact: on unpatched stacks, key reinstallation enables traffic decryption/injection during roaming; even when patched, FT-over-DS is unnecessary added exposure.
steps: Confirm FortiOS and FortiAP firmware are on a KRACK-patched build (Fortinet PSIRT advisory FG-IR-17-196); upgrade first if not.
Disable FT-over-DS while keeping 802.11r over the air: `config wireless-controller vap` / `edit "<ssid-name>"` / `set fast-roaming enable` / `set ft-over-ds disable` / `next` / `end`.
If fast roaming is not actually required on this SSID, disable it entirely: `set fast-roaming disable`.
Ensure PMF is enabled on the SSID (`set pmf optional` or `enable`), which mitigates several management-frame/KRACK issues.
Validate roaming behavior for latency-sensitive clients (voice/scanners) after the change - FT-over-Air is transparent for most clients.
Push the config and verify.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-008.gui: FortiGate: WiFi & Switch Controller > SSIDs > edit the SSID > Fast Roaming (802.11r) settings; the FT-over-DS toggle is the CLI setting `set ft-over-ds disable` in most builds. FortiManager: Device Manager > AP Manager > SSIDs.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-008.cli:
config wireless-controller vap
  edit <ssid-name>
    set fast-roaming enable
    set ft-over-ds disable
  next
end
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-008.verify: show wireless-controller vap <ssid-name> | grep ft-over-ds  ->  expect `set ft-over-ds disable` (with `set fast-roaming enable` still present if roaming is retained).
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-008.rollback: `set ft-over-ds enable` re-enables over-DS Fast Transition and re-introduces the exposure; only do so if a specific controller-less roam topology requires it AND the entire stack is confirmed KRACK-patched.
FORTIOS-WIRELESS-008.impact: Non-disruptive to the FortiGate: roaming clients simply reassociate using FT-over-Air, which is transparent for most. No reboot and no SSL-VPN/IPsec/HA/admin impact. Validate roam latency for voice clients after the change.
references: CVE-2017-13082 (KRACK - key reinstallation in the 802.11r FT handshake)
Fortinet PSIRT advisory FG-IR-17-196 (WPA2 key reinstallation / KRACK) - fortiguard.com/psirt/FG-IR-17-196
IEEE 802.11r-2008 (Fast BSS Transition)
NIST SP 800-153; Fortinet FortiWiFi and FortiAP Configuration Guide - 'Fast roaming (802.11r)'
CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

FORTIOS-ZTNA-001.risk: With no ZTNA access proxy configured, access to published applications relies on network-location trust (broad SSL-VPN/IPsec reach or open firewall policies) rather than per-request identity and device posture. An attacker who steals VPN credentials via phishing/credential-stuffing, or who exploits an SSL-VPN CVE such as CVE-2022-42475 or CVE-2023-27997, lands on the internal network with wide lateral-movement reach and no continuous check that the endpoint is managed, patched, or malware-free. Business impact: a single compromised credential or unmanaged device can pivot to crown-jewel systems, enabling data theft and ransomware, and the environment fails least-privilege / per-application access mandates.
steps: Confirm ZTNA is absent: 'show firewall access-proxy' returns an empty table.
Integrate FortiClient EMS so device-posture tags are available: config endpoint-control fctems / edit 1 / set server <EMS-IP> / next / end, then authorize the FortiGate on the EMS server.
Import the CA certificate that signs EMS-issued client device certificates so the FortiGate can validate managed endpoints: config vpn certificate ca / edit "EMS_CA" / set ca "<PEM>" / next / end.

Create the ZTNA server VIP: config firewall vip / edit "ztna-webapp" / set type access-proxy / set extip <public-ip> / set extintf <wan> / set server-type https / set ssl-certificate "<server-cert>" / next / end.
Create the access proxy bound to that VIP and require client certs: config firewall access-proxy / edit "ztna-webapp" / set vip "ztna-webapp" / set client-cert enable.

Add an API-gateway mapping to the real app servers: config api-gateway / edit 1 / set service https / config realservers / edit 1 / set address "<app-server-addr>" / set port 443 / next / end / next / end / next / end.

Create a ZTNA rule that enforces device-posture tags: config firewall proxy-policy / edit 0 / set proxy access-proxy / set access-proxy "ztna-webapp" / set srcintf "any" / set srcaddr "all" / set dstaddr "all" / set ztna-ems-tag "<managed-tag>" / set action accept / set schedule "always" / set logtraffic all / next / end.
Migrate users off broad SSL-VPN/firewall access onto the ZTNA rule and test from both a managed and an unmanaged endpoint to confirm posture enforcement.
Monitor Log & Report > ZTNA (or FortiAnalyzer) for allow/deny outcomes and tune tags/policies.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-001.gui: FortiGate: Policy & Objects > ZTNA (create ZTNA Server, ZTNA Tag, ZTNA Rule); EMS integration under Security Fabric > Fabric Connectors > FortiClient EMS (this menu is labelled External Connectors in FortiOS 7.2+). FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > ZTNA, plus per-device Security Fabric connectors.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-001.cli:
config firewall access-proxy
  edit <proxy-name>
    set vip <virtual-IP>
  next
end
FORTIOS-ZTNA-001.verify: show firewall access-proxy  ->  at least one proxy with a 'set vip <name>' binding; get firewall proxy-policy  ->  a policy with 'ztna-ems-tag' set; diagnose endpoint record-list  ->  managed endpoints with posture tags present.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-001.rollback: Remove the new objects: config firewall proxy-policy (delete the ZTNA rule), config firewall access-proxy / delete <name>, config firewall vip / delete <name>. Breaks: users of the ZTNA-published app lose access and must revert to the prior VPN/firewall path, so keep the legacy path until cutover is validated.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-001.impact: Adding ZTNA is non-disruptive (net-new objects, no reboot) and does not touch HA, admin, or existing SSL-VPN/IPsec tunnels. The cutover step (retiring the old broad-access policy) should be done in a maintenance window and can drop in-flight sessions for the migrated apps.
references: Scanner COMPLIANCE_MAP: CIS FortiGate Benchmark 14.1.1; PCI-DSS 1.2.1; NIST SP 800-53 AC-4, SC-7
Fortinet FortiOS 7.x Administration Guide - 'Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA)' > 'Basic ZTNA configuration' / 'ZTNA HTTPS access proxy'
NIST SP 800-207 Zero Trust Architecture
CWE-284: Improper Access Control

FORTIOS-ZTNA-002.risk: An access proxy with an empty api-gateway table has no mapping from the published virtual host to backend real servers and no per-service scoping. Depending on version it either fails closed (the app is unreachable, an availability gap that pressures admins to loosen other controls) or, combined with a permissive proxy policy, forwards traffic without the intended application-layer restriction, undermining the least-privilege promise of ZTNA. Attackers reaching the proxy face no service/host limits at the gateway, widening the reachable surface. Business impact: broken or overly broad application access plus a false sense that ZTNA is enforcing controls it is not.
steps: Identify the empty proxy: 'show firewall access-proxy' - the offending 'edit <name>' has no 'config api-gateway' block.
Confirm backend server address objects exist: show firewall address / show firewall addrgrp.
Edit the proxy: config firewall access-proxy / edit "<proxy-name>".
Add an API gateway entry and pick the service: config api-gateway / edit 1 / set service https (or http, tcp-forwarding, samlsp).
Bind real servers: config realservers / edit 1 / set address "<app-server-addr>" / set port 443 / set status active / next / end.
(Optional) Add resilience/persistence: set ldb-method round-robin and define additional realservers.
(Optional) Restrict by URL path/virtual host: set url-map "/app" / set url-map-type sub-string.
Close the nested blocks: end (realservers) / next / end (api-gateway) / next / end (access-proxy).
Ensure the ZTNA proxy-policy references this proxy and pins the correct EMS tag, then test reachability from a managed device.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-002.gui: FortiGate: Policy & Objects > ZTNA > ZTNA Servers > edit server > add entries under service/server mapping (API Gateway) and Real Servers. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > ZTNA > ZTNA Servers.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-002.cli:
config firewall access-proxy
  edit "<proxy-name>"
    set vip "<ztna-vip>"
    config api-gateway
      edit 1
        set service https
        config realservers
          edit 1
            set address "<app-server-addr>"
            set port 443
            set status active
          next
        end
      next
    end
  next
end
FORTIOS-ZTNA-002.verify: show firewall access-proxy  ->  the proxy now contains a populated 'config api-gateway' with a 'config realservers' block. Functional: from a managed client, 'curl -v https://<vhost>' returns the backend application (e.g. HTTP 200), confirming the gateway forwards to the real server.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-002.rollback: config firewall access-proxy / edit "<proxy-name>" / config api-gateway / delete 1 / end / next / end. Breaks: the published application becomes unreachable through the proxy again.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-002.impact: Non-disruptive additive change; no reboot; no impact to VPN/HA/admin sessions. Because a new gateway can immediately begin passing traffic, validate the real-server address/port before saving to avoid exposing an unintended backend.
references: Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'ZTNA' > 'ZTNA HTTPS access proxy' / 'Configuring the ZTNA server (access proxy) and API gateway'
NIST SP 800-207 Zero Trust Architecture (policy enforcement point)
CWE-284: Improper Access Control

FORTIOS-ZTNA-003.risk: With client-cert disabled, the access proxy does not request or validate the FortiClient-EMS-issued device certificate during the TLS handshake, so device identity is never verified. Unmanaged or attacker-controlled endpoints presenting only valid user credentials can reach published applications, defeating the 'trusted device' pillar of ZTNA; if empty-cert-action is left at accept, clients with no certificate are admitted outright. Combined with stolen credentials, an attacker connects from an unmanaged host and bypasses posture enforcement. Business impact: loss of device-based access control, higher exposure to credential replay and BYOD-borne malware reaching sensitive apps, and non-compliance with zero-trust device-trust requirements.
steps: Confirm the gap: 'show firewall access-proxy' - the proxy lacks 'set client-cert enable' (default is disable).
Ensure the CA that signed EMS device certs is imported: show vpn certificate ca (import via config vpn certificate ca / edit "EMS_CA" / set ca "<PEM>" if missing).
Edit the proxy: config firewall access-proxy / edit "<proxy-name>".
Require the client certificate: set client-cert enable.
Block clients presenting no/invalid cert: set empty-cert-action block (staging option: accept-unmanageable before hard-blocking).
Where supported, constrain acceptable issuers to the EMS CA so only managed-device certs are honored.
Save: next / end.
Ensure the ZTNA proxy-policy also enforces the managed-device EMS tag (set ztna-ems-tag "<managed>") so device cert AND posture are both required.
Test from a managed device (expect success) and an unmanaged device without a cert (expect block); review Log & Report > ZTNA.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-003.gui: FortiGate: Policy & Objects > ZTNA > ZTNA Servers > edit server > enable 'Client certificate' and set the empty-certificate action to Block. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > ZTNA > ZTNA Servers.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-003.cli:
config firewall access-proxy
  edit "<proxy-name>"
    set client-cert enable
    set empty-cert-action block
  next
end
FORTIOS-ZTNA-003.verify: show firewall access-proxy  ->  expect 'set client-cert enable' and 'set empty-cert-action block' on the proxy. Functional: an unmanaged client with no device cert is denied at the proxy.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-003.rollback: config firewall access-proxy / edit "<proxy-name>" / set client-cert disable / next / end. Breaks: once enabled, managed clients without a valid device certificate (EMS not yet deployed, expired cert) are blocked - confirm EMS enrollment coverage before enforcing to avoid a self-inflicted outage.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-003.impact: Non-disruptive to the appliance (no reboot; no HA/VPN/admin impact) but DISRUPTIVE to end users whose devices lack a valid EMS-issued certificate - they lose access immediately. Roll out in a maintenance window after confirming EMS certificate deployment; consider staging with empty-cert-action accept-unmanageable before hard-blocking.
references: Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'ZTNA' > 'ZTNA HTTPS access proxy with client certificate' (access-proxy client-cert / empty-cert-action reference)
NIST SP 800-207 Zero Trust Architecture (device identity); NIST SP 800-53 IA-2, IA-5
CWE-287: Improper Authentication (related CWE-295: Improper Certificate Validation)

FORTIOS-ZTNA-004.risk: SD-WAN is enabled but has no health-check (Performance SLA) probes, so the FortiGate selects and fails over between WAN members blind to real link quality - it keeps forwarding over a 'physically up' link that is black-holing, high-latency, or lossy. This causes brownouts for VoIP/ERP/security traffic and can silently route over an unintended or less-trusted path, defeating the expected failover design. An adversary who degrades or partially disrupts one ISP link can force traffic onto a path the design did not anticipate. Business impact: unreliable connectivity, failed failover during ISP incidents, and SLA breaches for revenue-critical applications.
steps: Confirm the gap: 'diagnose sys sdwan health-check' returns nothing while 'get system sdwan' shows status enable.
Enter SD-WAN config: config system sdwan.
Verify members exist (each WAN interface should be an SD-WAN member with a gateway): show (config members).
Create a health check: config health-check / edit "Internet".
Set probe targets and protocol: set server "8.8.8.8" "1.1.1.1" / set protocol ping (or http, dns, tcp-echo, twamp).
Tune detection: set interval 500 / set failtime 5 / set recoverytime 10 / set probe-timeout 500.
Bind to members by sequence number: set members 1 2.
Define SLA thresholds: config sla / edit 1 / set latency-threshold 250 / set jitter-threshold 50 / set packetloss-threshold 2 / next / end.
Save: next / end / end.
Reference this SLA in SD-WAN rules (see FORTIOS-ZTNA-005) so path selection uses the SLA, then verify link state.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-004.gui: FortiGate: Network > SD-WAN > Performance SLAs > Create New (server, protocol, participant interface members, SLA targets). FortiManager: Device Manager / Policy & Objects > SD-WAN Templates > Performance SLA.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-004.cli:
config system sdwan
  set status enable
  config health-check
    edit "Internet"
      set server "8.8.8.8" "1.1.1.1"
      set protocol ping
      set interval 500
      set failtime 5
      set recoverytime 10
      set members 1 2
      config sla
        edit 1
          set latency-threshold 250
          set jitter-threshold 50
          set packetloss-threshold 2
        next
      end
    next
  end
end
FORTIOS-ZTNA-004.verify: diagnose sys sdwan health-check  ->  each member shows state alive with measured latency/jitter/packet-loss and SLA pass/fail; show system sdwan  ->  the 'config health-check' block with its SLA thresholds is present; diagnose sys sdwan member  ->  member status.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-004.rollback: Remove any SD-WAN rule references first, then: config system sdwan / config health-check / delete "Internet" / end / end. Breaks: SD-WAN rules that referenced the SLA lose their measurement basis and path selection reverts to link-state-only.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-004.impact: Adding a health check is non-disruptive, needs no reboot, and does not drop VPN/HA/admin sessions; probes are lightweight. Note: once SD-WAN rules act on SLA results, traffic may shift paths as unhealthy links are marked out (expected) - validate during a change window if apps are latency-sensitive.
references: Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'SD-WAN' > 'Performance SLA' / SD-WAN health-check reference
Fortinet SD-WAN Deployment / Architecture for Enterprise Guide
NIST SP 800-53 CP-8 (Telecommunications Services / alternate paths)
CWE-754: Improper Check for Unusual or Exceptional Conditions

FORTIOS-ZTNA-005.risk: SD-WAN is enabled but has no service (SD-WAN rule), so traffic uses only implicit/default routing and load-balance behavior instead of quality- and business-priority-based path selection. Critical applications are not steered to the best-performing or most-trusted link and no policy binds traffic classes to SLAs. This weakens both performance assurance and the ability to force sensitive traffic down a specific vetted path. Business impact: unpredictable application performance, no guaranteed path for VoIP/ERP/security tooling, and failover that ignores application priority.
steps: Confirm the gap: 'show system sdwan' shows status enable but no 'config service' entries.
Ensure a health-check/SLA already exists (see FORTIOS-ZTNA-004): diagnose sys sdwan health-check.
Enter config: config system sdwan / config service.
Create a rule: edit 1 / set name "Critical-Apps".
Choose selection mode: set mode sla (or priority, load-balance, manual).
Match the traffic: set src "internal-net" / set dst "all" (or set internet-service enable / set internet-service-name "..." for ISDB/application matching).
Bind the SLA: config sla / edit "Internet" / set id 1 / next / end.
Set preferred egress order: set priority-members 1 2.
Save: next / end / end.
Validate selection with 'diagnose sys sdwan service' and confirm critical apps take the intended member (rules are evaluated top-down).
FORTIOS-ZTNA-005.gui: FortiGate: Network > SD-WAN > SD-WAN Rules > Create New (source/destination or Internet Service, Strategy = Lowest Cost (SLA)/Best Quality/Manual, required SLA target, interface preference). FortiManager: Policy & Objects > SD-WAN Templates > SD-WAN Rules.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-005.cli:
config system sdwan
  config service
    edit 1
      set name "Critical-Apps"
      set mode sla
      set src "internal-net"
      set dst "all"
      config sla
        edit "Internet"
          set id 1
        next
      end
      set priority-members 1 2
    next
  end
end
FORTIOS-ZTNA-005.verify: diagnose sys sdwan service  ->  the rule appears with its mode, SLA binding, and eligible/selected members in priority order; show system sdwan lists the 'config service' entry.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-005.rollback: config system sdwan / config service / delete 1 / end / end. Breaks: traffic reverts to default/implicit SD-WAN routing and any application steering or SLA-based path pinning is lost.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-005.impact: Non-disruptive additive change; no reboot; no VPN/HA/admin impact. Introducing a rule changes which member carries the matched traffic, so in-flight sessions for matched apps may re-path - validate rule ordering to avoid unintended steering.
references: Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'SD-WAN' > 'SD-WAN rules' / rule modes (SLA, priority, load-balance)
Fortinet SD-WAN Architecture for Enterprise Guide
NIST SP 800-53 CP-8, SC-7
CWE-693: Protection Mechanism Failure

FORTIOS-ZTNA-006.risk: Same root condition as FORTIOS-ZTNA-004, re-flagged by the logging/monitoring depth check (rated MEDIUM): SD-WAN is enabled with zero health-check probes, so WAN member failover is blind - the FortiGate cannot distinguish a degraded/black-holing link from a healthy one and may keep forwarding over a broken path. Beyond availability, blind failover can silently move traffic onto an unintended or less-trusted link. Business impact: unreliable failover during ISP degradation, brownouts for real-time apps, and no latency/jitter/loss telemetry for capacity planning or incident analysis. Remediating FORTIOS-ZTNA-004 clears this finding as well.
steps: Confirm: 'diagnose sys sdwan health-check' is empty while SD-WAN status is enable.
Enter config: config system sdwan / config health-check / edit "Internet".
Set probe targets/protocol: set server "8.8.8.8" "1.1.1.1" / set protocol ping.
Tune detection: set interval 500 / set failtime 5 / set recoverytime 10.
Bind members by sequence number: set members 1 2.
Define SLA thresholds: config sla / edit 1 / set latency-threshold 250 / set jitter-threshold 50 / set packetloss-threshold 2 / next / end.
Save: next / end / end.
Bind the SLA in SD-WAN rules (FORTIOS-ZTNA-005) and confirm probe results with diagnose sys sdwan health-check.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-006.gui: FortiGate: Network > SD-WAN > Performance SLAs > Create New. FortiManager: Device Manager / Policy & Objects > SD-WAN Templates > Performance SLA.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-006.cli:
config system sdwan
  set status enable
  config health-check
    edit "Internet"
      set server "8.8.8.8" "1.1.1.1"
      set protocol ping
      set interval 500
      set failtime 5
      set recoverytime 10
      set members 1 2
      config sla
        edit 1
          set latency-threshold 250
          set jitter-threshold 50
          set packetloss-threshold 2
        next
      end
    next
  end
end
FORTIOS-ZTNA-006.verify: diagnose sys sdwan health-check  ->  members alive with measured latency/jitter/packet-loss and SLA state; show system sdwan  ->  the configured 'config health-check' block is present.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-006.rollback: Remove any SD-WAN rule references first, then: config system sdwan / config health-check / delete "Internet" / end / end. Breaks: failover reverts to link-state-only and rules referencing the SLA lose their measurement basis.
FORTIOS-ZTNA-006.impact: Non-disruptive, no reboot, lightweight probes; no VPN/HA/admin impact. Functional duplicate of FORTIOS-ZTNA-004 (HIGH) - one remediation resolves both findings; once rules act on SLA data, traffic may re-path off unhealthy links (expected).
references: Fortinet FortiOS Administration Guide - 'SD-WAN' > 'Performance SLA' / SD-WAN health-check reference
NIST SP 800-53 CP-8 (alternate telecommunications paths)
CWE-693: Protection Mechanism Failure (as coded; closely related CWE-754)
Note: functional duplicate of FORTIOS-ZTNA-004 (HIGH severity) emitted by the depth/monitoring check

MITRE-SUMMARY-PASS.risk: This is an informational aggregate result, not a vulnerability. It is emitted only when zero MITRE-T* gaps are found — every tested ATT&CK technique mitigation is properly configured. There is no attacker scenario tied to this finding itself; the residual risk is posture drift (a control being weakened or a policy added without profiles after the scan) and coverage limited to the ~31 techniques the scanner tests.
steps: Treat this as a PASS — no remediation configuration is required.
Preserve the current baseline: back up the config to a secure location (execute backup config tftp <filename> <server-ip>, or via FortiManager), or ensure the running config is retained centrally.
Keep FortiGuard subscriptions active (AV, IPS, Web/DNS Filter, App Control) so the controls that earned the pass stay effective: get system fortiguard.
Keep FortiOS on a supported, patched train to maintain resilience against new CVEs: get system status.
Re-run this scanner on a schedule (and after every change window) to catch posture drift.
Ensure new firewall policies inherit the required UTM profiles (use FortiManager policy templates/profile groups).
Continue forwarding logs to FortiAnalyzer/SIEM so detection persists alongside prevention.
Periodically validate the controls with purple-team/BAS exercises mapped to ATT&CK.
MITRE-SUMMARY-PASS.gui: No change required. To sustain posture: System > Firmware (firmware updates), System > FortiGuard (subscription/license status), Dashboard > Status (FortiGuard license widget), and FortiManager > Policy & Objects for profile-group templates on new policies.
MITRE-SUMMARY-PASS.cli:
# No remediation required — informational PASS result.
# Maintain posture (do not disable existing controls):
get system fortiguard
get system status
execute backup config tftp <filename> <tftp-server-ip>
# (or back up via FortiManager / secure SCP)
MITRE-SUMMARY-PASS.verify: Re-run the scanner and confirm the category still returns MITRE-SUMMARY-PASS with no MITRE-T* findings. Spot-check with get system fortiguard (subscriptions valid) and get firewall policy (profiles still attached).
MITRE-SUMMARY-PASS.rollback: Not applicable — no change is made. Do not weaken or remove the security profiles that produced the pass, as that would reintroduce the corresponding ATT&CK technique gaps.
MITRE-SUMMARY-PASS.impact: None — informational only. No configuration change, no reboot, no service impact.
references: MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise Matrix (technique coverage baseline)
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 (overall security-profile posture)
Hardening your FortiGate (Fortinet Document Library) — ongoing maintenance
Fortinet FortiGuard subscription/licensing documentation

MITRE-SUMMARY-SCORE.risk: This is an informational scoring finding, not a discrete vulnerability. It reports a resilience percentage = (techniques passed / total tested) x 100 and is emitted whenever one or more MITRE-T* gaps exist. The business risk it represents is the sum of the individual MITRE-T* findings above it: the lower the score, the more ATT&CK techniques your FortiGate currently fails to mitigate, and the broader the real-world attack surface.
steps: Treat the score as a roll-up KPI — the fix is to remediate the underlying MITRE-T* findings, not the summary itself.
Sort the MITRE-T* findings by severity and address CRITICAL first (e.g., T1562 no external logging, T1595 WAN management, T1105 no AV).
Then remediate HIGH findings (IPS/AV/Web Filter/deep-inspection coverage, admin MFA, DoS, etc.) using their per-rule KB entries.
Finish with MEDIUM findings (DLP, App Control granularity, DNS category tuning).
After each batch, re-run the scanner and watch the score climb toward 100%.
Bake the required profiles into FortiManager templates so new policies do not regress the score.
Track the score over time as a board-level resilience metric.
Target and sustain 100% (zero gaps), which converts this finding into MITRE-SUMMARY-PASS.
MITRE-SUMMARY-SCORE.gui: No direct GUI target — remediate each underlying finding via its respective path (Security Profiles > IPS/AV/Web Filter/DNS Filter/Application Control/DLP/SSL-SSH Inspection, Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy / DoS Policy, System > Administrators, Log & Report > Log Settings, Network > Interfaces). FortiManager: Policy & Objects for fleet-wide profile enforcement.
MITRE-SUMMARY-SCORE.cli:
# No single CLI fix — this is an aggregate score.
# Remediate each MITRE-T* finding using its own CLI block, prioritized:
#   CRITICAL: MITRE-T1562-001, MITRE-T1595-001, MITRE-T1105-001
#   HIGH:     T1190, T1566, T1203, T1078, T1027, T1110, T1557, T1021,
#             T1041, T1573, T1486, T1572, T1571, T1189, T1210, T1567, T1498
#   MEDIUM:   T1133, T1059, T1071, T1048, T1090, T1046, T1219, T1568,
#             T1102, T1499, T1496
# Then re-run the scanner to recompute the score.
MITRE-SUMMARY-SCORE.verify: Re-run the scanner: the MITRE-SUMMARY-SCORE line shows score=<n>%, gaps=<g>/<total>. Expected trajectory is the score increasing as gaps drop to 0, at which point the scanner emits MITRE-SUMMARY-PASS instead.
MITRE-SUMMARY-SCORE.rollback: Not applicable — informational. Reverting any individual remediation lowers the score and reopens that technique's gap.
MITRE-SUMMARY-SCORE.impact: None directly — informational KPI. Service impact, if any, comes from the individual remediations it aggregates; consult each per-rule 'impact' field (notably SSL-VPN client-cert enforcement, WAN management removal, and deep SSL inspection have user-facing effects).
references: MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise Matrix (technique-to-control mapping)
CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 (aggregate security posture)
Fortinet Security Fabric / Security Rating (posture scoring analog)
Hardening your FortiGate (Fortinet Document Library)

MITRE-T1021-001.risk: One or more firewall policies allow any-source to any-destination on any-service. This flattens network segmentation: a single compromised host can move laterally over SMB, RDP, SSH, and WMI to any other zone with zero policy friction. Business impact: ransomware and hands-on-keyboard attackers spread from one foothold to the entire estate, turning a contained incident into a full-domain compromise.
steps: Enumerate the permissive rules: get firewall policy | grep -e policyid -e srcaddr -e dstaddr -e service and flag any with srcaddr all / dstaddr all / service ALL on an allow action.
Determine the legitimate flows the rule is actually carrying (review traffic logs / FortiView by policy).
Create address objects/groups for the real source and destination: config firewall address / edit ... / set subnet <net/mask> / next / end.
Replace the any/any/any rule with least-privilege values: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set srcaddr <src-obj> / set dstaddr <dst-obj> / set service <specific-services>.
Attach UTM (IPS at minimum) and enable logging: set utm-status enable / set ips-sensor <sensor> / set logtraffic all / next / end.
Add explicit inter-zone segmentation rules and rely on the implicit deny for the rest.
Stage the change, monitor for legitimately-broken flows, and add narrow allow rules as needed.
Re-scan to confirm zero any/any/any policies remain.
MITRE-T1021-001.gui: Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit the offending policy > set specific Source, Destination, and Service (replace 'all'/'ALL') > enable logging and IPS. Build objects under Policy & Objects > Addresses / Services. FortiManager: Policy Packages > Firewall Policy.
MITRE-T1021-001.cli:
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set srcaddr <specific-address>
    set dstaddr <specific-address>
    set service <specific-service>
    set utm-status enable
    set ips-sensor <sensor-name>
    set logtraffic all
  next
end
MITRE-T1021-001.verify: show firewall policy <id> | grep -e srcaddr -e dstaddr -e service  -> expect specific objects, not all/ALL. Fleet audit: get firewall policy and confirm no allow rule has all/all/ALL. FortiView > Policies shows only intended flows hitting the rule.
MITRE-T1021-001.rollback: Re-broaden the rule: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set srcaddr all / set dstaddr all / set service ALL / next / end. Reverting re-opens unrestricted lateral movement — instead of full rollback, add targeted allow rules above the tightened policy for any flow you inadvertently broke.
MITRE-T1021-001.impact: Potentially service-disrupting: tightening a permissive rule can break undocumented but legitimate flows. No reboot. Existing sessions may be cut when the rule narrows. Change during a window, keep the old rule disabled (not deleted) briefly, and monitor deny logs to catch collateral breakage.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.1.2 (Ensure policies do not use any/any)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy (least-privilege rules)
Hardening your FortiGate — Implement least-privilege firewall policies / segmentation
MITRE ATT&CK T1021 Remote Services
CWE-284 Improper Access Control

MITRE-T1027-001.risk: No AV profile has cloud sandbox (FortiGuard/FortiSandbox) analytics or outbreak prevention enabled, so detection is limited to static signatures. Packed, obfuscated, or zero-day payloads — exactly what modern loaders and ransomware use — sail past signature matching. Business impact: novel malware executes on endpoints before a signature ever exists, defeating the AV control you believe is protecting you.
steps: Review AV profiles for missing analytics/outbreak prevention: show antivirus profile.
Edit the profile: config antivirus profile / edit "av-block".
Enable outbreak prevention per protocol (real-time FortiGuard hash query): config http / set av-scan block / set outbreak-prevention block / end (repeat for smtp/pop3/imap/ftp).
Enable inline cloud sandbox submission per protocol: config http / set fortisandbox block / end (requires FortiSandbox or FortiGuard Cloud Sandbox subscription).
Point the FortiGate at the sandbox: config system fortisandbox / set status enable / set inline-scan enable / set server <fsa-ip-or-cloud> / end.
Enable the extended AV DB and AI/ML detection: config antivirus settings / set default-db extended / set machine-learning-detection enable / end.
Ensure FortiGuard AV/sandbox licenses are active: get system fortiguard.
Attach the profile to user policies and re-scan to confirm sandbox/outbreak is detected.
MITRE-T1027-001.gui: Security Profiles > AntiVirus > edit profile > enable 'Use FortiGuard Outbreak Prevention Database' and 'Send Files to FortiSandbox/Cloud for Inspection'. Configure sandbox under Security Fabric > Fabric Connectors > FortiSandbox (or Security Fabric > External Connectors). FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > AntiVirus.
MITRE-T1027-001.cli:
config antivirus profile
  edit "av-block"
    set feature-set flow
    config http
      set av-scan block
      set outbreak-prevention block
      set fortisandbox block
    end
  next
end
config system fortisandbox
  set status enable
  set inline-scan enable
  set server <fsa-ip-or-forticloud>
end
config antivirus settings
  set default-db extended
  set machine-learning-detection enable
end
MITRE-T1027-001.verify: show antivirus profile "av-block" | grep -e outbreak-prevention -e fortisandbox  -> expect block. get system fortisandbox | grep status -> status: enable. Confirm connectivity via the Security Fabric FortiSandbox connector (Security Fabric > Fabric Connectors shows Connected) and that AV logs show sandbox verdicts.
MITRE-T1027-001.rollback: config antivirus profile / edit "av-block" / config http / set outbreak-prevention disable / set fortisandbox disable / end / next / end. Reverting drops back to static-signature-only detection and re-exposes you to zero-day/obfuscated malware. Keep enabled unless sandbox latency is unacceptable.
MITRE-T1027-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Inline sandbox in block mode can add hold-and-inspect latency for first-seen files (use monitor for latency-sensitive flows). Requires an active FortiSandbox appliance or FortiGuard Cloud Sandbox subscription; without it, fortisandbox/outbreak settings have no effect.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.1 (AntiVirus)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > AntiVirus > Outbreak prevention & FortiSandbox inline scanning
MITRE ATT&CK T1027 Obfuscated Files or Information
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 5.1

MITRE-T1041-001.risk: DNS botnet/C2 domain filtering is not enabled in any DNS Filter profile, so malware freely resolves and connects to known command-and-control domains to receive tasking and exfiltrate data over the C2 channel. Business impact: an infected host maintains a live handler connection and streams stolen data out, extending dwell time and the blast radius of the intrusion.
steps: Confirm botnet blocking is off: show dnsfilter profile | grep block-botnet.
Enable botnet C2 domain blocking in each DNS filter: config dnsfilter profile / edit "dns-block" / set block-botnet enable / next / end.
Add FortiGuard malicious/phishing DNS category actions for defense-in-depth: config ftgd-dns / config filters / edit 1 / set category 26 / set action block / next / end / end.
Ensure the profile is attached to all outbound policies: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set dnsfilter-profile "dns-block" / set utm-status enable / next / end.
Complement with IPS Botnet C2 IP blocking on the policy: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set scan-botnet-connections block / next / end.
Verify the FortiGuard botnet/IP-reputation feeds are current: diagnose autoupdate versions | grep -i botnet.
Monitor DNS Filter and Botnet logs for blocked C2 resolutions and investigate the source hosts.
Re-scan to confirm botnet filtering is detected as enabled.
MITRE-T1041-001.gui: Security Profiles > DNS Filter > enable 'Redirect botnet C&C requests to Block Portal' (Block DNS requests to known botnet C&C). For IP-based C2, enable 'Block outgoing connections to Botnet sites' on the Firewall Policy. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > DNS Filter.
MITRE-T1041-001.cli:
config dnsfilter profile
  edit "dns-block"
    set block-botnet enable
    config ftgd-dns
      config filters
        edit 1
          set category 26
          set action block
        next
      end
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set dnsfilter-profile "dns-block"
    set scan-botnet-connections block
  next
end
MITRE-T1041-001.verify: show dnsfilter profile "dns-block" | grep block-botnet  -> expect: set block-botnet enable. Policy IP-C2: show firewall policy <id> | grep scan-botnet-connections -> block. Confirm feed: diagnose autoupdate versions shows a recent Botnet DB.
MITRE-T1041-001.rollback: config dnsfilter profile / edit "dns-block" / set block-botnet disable / next / end and set scan-botnet-connections disable on the policy. Reverting re-permits C2 resolution/connections — keep enabled; if a legitimate domain is misclassified, add a DNS domain-filter allow entry.
MITRE-T1041-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Botnet blocking is reputation-based with minimal latency. Rare false positives on a legitimately-hosted domain sharing infrastructure with a bad actor — resolve via allow-list. Requires an active FortiGuard subscription for the botnet feed.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.6 (DNS Filter)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > DNS Filter (Botnet C&C) and Firewall Policy Botnet C2 protection
MITRE ATT&CK T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 1.2.1

MITRE-T1046-001.risk: No IPS sensors are applied to inter-zone (east-west) policies, so a compromised internal host can run Nmap/masscan sweeps across zones to map internal services and pick targets without tripping any detection at the firewall. Business impact: attackers freely perform internal reconnaissance during the post-exploitation phase, identifying the next hop toward crown-jewel systems with no east-west alerting.
steps: Identify inter-zone allow policies without IPS: review policies where srcintf != dstintf and ips-sensor is empty (get firewall policy | grep -e srcintf -e dstintf -e ips-sensor).

Create/choose an IPS sensor tuned for reconnaissance and exploit signatures: config ips sensor / edit "east-west" / config entries / edit 1 / set severity medium high critical / set status enable / set action block / next / end / next / end.

Apply the sensor to each inter-zone policy: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set ips-sensor "east-west" / next / end. (Flow vs proxy inspection is a VDOM-level setting under config system settings, not a per-policy field; flow is the default and is sufficient for IPS.)
Ensure the internal segmentation policies themselves are least-privilege (see T1021) so scanning is constrained.
Enable logging on inter-zone policies and forward to FortiAnalyzer to alert on scan patterns.
Consider rate-based signatures for port-scan/host-sweep detection within the sensor.
Baseline in monitor to avoid blocking legitimate internal tooling, then move to block.
Re-scan to confirm inter-zone IPS is now present.
MITRE-T1046-001.gui: Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention (build 'east-west' sensor) then Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > enable IPS on each internal/inter-zone policy. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention.
MITRE-T1046-001.cli:
config ips sensor
  edit "east-west"
    config entries
      edit 1
        set severity medium high critical
        set status enable
        set action block
      next
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <inter-zone-policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set ips-sensor "east-west"
  next
end
MITRE-T1046-001.verify: show firewall policy <inter-zone-policy-id> | grep ips-sensor  -> expect: set ips-sensor "east-west". Fleet: confirm inter-zone policies (srcintf != dstintf) carry an IPS sensor. Trigger a test scan across zones and confirm IPS/reconnaissance logs.
MITRE-T1046-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset ips-sensor / next / end. Reverting removes east-west scan detection. If IPS false-positives disrupt legitimate internal tools, set the sensor entry action to pass+log rather than removing it.
MITRE-T1046-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Flow IPS on east-west links adds minimal latency but does consume inspection CPU on high internal throughput — size accordingly. Switching a VDOM to proxy inspection-mode can briefly reset sessions. Stage rate-based/block actions from monitor first.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 9.1.1 (IPS/anomaly) and 4.2.2 (IPS on policies)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention; internal segmentation firewall
MITRE ATT&CK T1046 Network Service Discovery
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 11.4

MITRE-T1048-001.risk: No Data Loss Prevention sensor is attached to any policy, so the firewall cannot recognize sensitive data leaving over alternative protocols (DNS, ICMP, FTP, HTTP POST, webmail). An insider or malware can siphon PII, source code, or card data out through non-standard channels with zero detection. Business impact: silent exfiltration, breach-notification and PCI/GDPR liability, and no tripwire on data egress.
steps: Confirm DLP is absent: get firewall policy | grep dlp-sensor (or dlp-profile on 7.2+).
Define what to detect — build dictionaries/data types for PII/PAN/keywords: config dlp dictionary (7.2+) or use predefined credit-card/SSN patterns.

Create a DLP sensor (FortiOS <=7.0): config dlp sensor / edit "dlp-pii" / config filter / edit 1 / set proto smtp http-post ftp / set filter-type credit-card / set action block / next / end / next / end.
On FortiOS 7.2+ wrap the sensor in a DLP profile: config dlp profile / edit "dlp-pii" / config rule / edit 1 / set sensor "dlp-pii" / set action block / next / end / next / end.

Attach to outbound policies: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set dlp-sensor "dlp-pii" (7.0) or set dlp-profile "dlp-pii" (7.2+) / set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection" / next / end.
Deep SSL inspection is required to see data inside HTTPS uploads.
Start in log/monitor to baseline false positives, then move to block.
Review DLP logs and re-scan to confirm a sensor is applied.
MITRE-T1048-001.gui: Security Profiles > Data Leak Prevention (build the sensor/dictionaries and rules), then Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > enable DLP on outbound policies. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > DLP. Note: menu label and 'dlp-profile' vs 'dlp-sensor' differ between 7.0 and 7.2+.
MITRE-T1048-001.cli:
config dlp sensor
  edit "dlp-pii"
    config filter
      edit 1
        set proto smtp http-post ftp
        set filter-type credit-card
        set action block
      next
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set dlp-sensor "dlp-pii"
    set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"
  next
end
# FortiOS 7.2+: use 'config dlp profile' + 'set dlp-profile' on the policy instead of dlp-sensor.
MITRE-T1048-001.verify: show firewall policy <id> | grep -e dlp-sensor -e dlp-profile  -> expect the sensor/profile set. Functional test: send a dummy card number over an inspected protocol and confirm a DLP block/log in Log & Report > DLP.
MITRE-T1048-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset dlp-sensor / next / end (or unset dlp-profile). Reverting removes egress data inspection. Prefer switching filter action from block to log if false positives disrupt business, rather than removing DLP entirely.
MITRE-T1048-001.impact: Non-disruptive to availability; no reboot. Proxy-based DLP inspection adds processing overhead and requires deep SSL inspection (endpoint CA needed) to see HTTPS. Aggressive block rules can interrupt legitimate transfers — baseline in monitor first. DLP config model changed in 7.2 (sensor->profile).
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.7 (Data Loss Prevention)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > DLP (profiles, sensors, data types)
MITRE ATT&CK T1048 Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
CWE-200 Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
PCI DSS v4.0 1.3.4 / 3.x

MITRE-T1059-001.risk: Application Control is applied to under 30% of active policies, so the firewall cannot recognize or block application-layer behaviors like web shells, script downloaders, PowerShell-over-HTTP, and living-off-the-land tooling that ride on allowed ports. An attacker who has landed a loader can fetch and run second-stage scripts and interpreters without tripping any control. Business impact: unimpeded execution stage of an intrusion and blind spots in outbound app telemetry.
steps: Identify policies without Application Control: get firewall policy | grep -e policyid -e application-list.
Create an app-control profile: config application list / edit "appctrl-block".
Block high-risk categories (P2P, Proxy): config entries / edit 1 / set category 2 6 / set action block / next / end (2 = P2P, 6 = Proxy; confirm IDs with set category ?).

Set default handling for unknown/other apps to pass+log first, then block after tuning: set other-application-action pass then set unknown-application-action pass (tighten to block after review) / next / end.
Attach to outbound/DMZ policies: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set application-list "appctrl-block" / set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection" / next / end.
Deep SSL inspection is required for AppCtrl to classify HTTPS apps — ensure it is applied.
Review Application Control logs for a week, then move actions from pass/monitor to block.
Push profile fleet-wide via FortiManager and re-scan to raise coverage above threshold.
MITRE-T1059-001.gui: Security Profiles > Application Control (create profile, set category actions) then Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > enable Application Control per policy. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Application Control.
MITRE-T1059-001.cli:
config application list
  edit "appctrl-block"
    config entries
      edit 1
        set category 2 6
        set action block
      next
    end
    set unknown-application-action pass
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set application-list "appctrl-block"
    set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"
  next
end
MITRE-T1059-001.verify: show firewall policy <id> | grep application-list  -> expect: set application-list "appctrl-block". Confirm classification is working via Log & Report > Application Control. Category IDs: config application list / edit x / config entries / edit 1 / set category ? lists valid IDs.
MITRE-T1059-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset application-list / next / end and delete profile config application list / delete "appctrl-block". Removing it restores the execution/telemetry blind spot; prefer relaxing specific category actions to pass+log over full removal.
MITRE-T1059-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Blocking broad categories can break sanctioned business apps (e.g., a legitimate remote tool), so stage in pass+log first. Requires deep SSL inspection for HTTPS classification, which needs the FortiGate CA on endpoints.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.4 (Application Control)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > Application Control
MITRE ATT&CK T1059 Command and Scripting Interpreter
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 1.2.1

MITRE-T1071-001.risk: DNS Filter is attached to under 30% of policies, so malware can resolve C2 and exfiltration domains, tunnel data over DNS, and use DNS-over-HTTPS to evade blocking. Every uninspected DNS path is a covert channel that survives even when web/AV filtering is present. Business impact: undetected command-and-control, beaconing, and slow data theft from infected hosts.
steps: Find policies missing DNS Filter: get firewall policy | grep -e policyid -e dnsfilter-profile.
Create/verify a DNS filter with botnet + FortiGuard categories: config dnsfilter profile / edit "dns-block" / set block-botnet enable.

Add FortiGuard DNS category blocking: config ftgd-dns / config filters / edit 1 / set category 26 / set action block / next / end / end (26 = Malicious Websites; also enable 61 Phishing and the Newly Registered / Newly Observed Domain categories — select by name in the GUI or confirm the numeric ID with set category ?) / next / end.
Enable detection of DoH/DoT where supported so encrypted DNS is not a bypass.
Attach to all outbound policies: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set dnsfilter-profile "dns-block" / next / end.
Point clients at the FortiGate/enterprise DNS and block external DNS (UDP/TCP 53) egress except via the resolver.
Review DNS Filter logs for blocked C2/tunnel hits, tune categories.
Push fleet-wide via FortiManager and re-scan for coverage.
MITRE-T1071-001.gui: Security Profiles > DNS Filter (enable Redirect botnet C2 / FortiGuard category actions) then Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > enable DNS Filter per policy. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > Security Profiles > DNS Filter.
MITRE-T1071-001.cli:
config dnsfilter profile
  edit "dns-block"
    set block-botnet enable
    config ftgd-dns
      config filters
        edit 1
          set category 26
          set action block
        next
      end
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set dnsfilter-profile "dns-block"
  next
end
MITRE-T1071-001.verify: show firewall policy <id> | grep dnsfilter-profile  -> expect set. show dnsfilter profile "dns-block" | grep block-botnet -> expect: set block-botnet enable. Live test: resolve a known-bad test domain and confirm a DNS Filter block log.
MITRE-T1071-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset dnsfilter-profile / next / end. Reverting restores the covert DNS channel. If a legitimate domain is over-blocked, add a DNS domain-filter allow entry rather than removing the profile.
MITRE-T1071-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. DNS Filter runs inline with minimal latency. Over-broad category blocking can break legitimate name resolution (tune with monitor/allow lists). Blocking external DNS egress requires clients use the sanctioned resolver first.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.6 (DNS Filter)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > DNS Filter (Botnet C2 / FortiGuard categories)
MITRE ATT&CK T1071 Application Layer Protocol (T1071.004 DNS)
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 1.2.1

MITRE-T1078-001.risk: One or more FortiGate administrator accounts have no second factor. A single reused/phished/brute-forced admin password gives an attacker complete control of the firewall — they can create backdoor admins, disable logging, alter policies, and man-in-the-middle all traffic. This is the highest-value credential in the environment. Business impact: total perimeter compromise and persistence that survives most incident response.
steps: List admins without two-factor: get system admin | grep -e name -e two-factor (or show system admin).
Provision a FortiToken (hardware or Mobile) for each admin: config system fortitoken / edit <serial> / set status active / next / end (or push FortiToken Mobile activation).
Assign the token and enforce 2FA per admin: config system admin / edit <admin> / set two-factor fortitoken / set fortitoken <serial> / set email-to <admin-email> / next / end.
For admins where hardware tokens are impractical, use email-based 2FA: set two-factor email / set email-to <addr>.
Constrain each admin with trusted hosts so stolen creds are unusable off-net: set trusthost1 <mgmt-subnet/mask>.
Repeat for every administrator; ensure at least two admins have working tokens before enforcing to avoid self-lockout.
Validate: log out and confirm the token prompt appears at next login.
Enable admin-login logging and forward to FortiAnalyzer to alert on new/failed admin logins.
MITRE-T1078-001.gui: System > Administrators > edit each admin > enable Two-factor Authentication > select the FortiToken (or Email). Register tokens under User & Authentication > FortiTokens. FortiManager: System Settings > Admin > Administrators.
MITRE-T1078-001.cli:
config system admin
  edit <admin-name>
    set two-factor fortitoken
    set fortitoken <token-serial>
    set email-to <admin-email>
  next
end
MITRE-T1078-001.verify: show system admin <admin-name> | grep two-factor  -> expect: set two-factor fortitoken. Fleet: get system admin | grep two-factor should show none disabled. Confirm the token challenge appears at login.
MITRE-T1078-001.rollback: config system admin / edit <admin> / set two-factor disable / next / end. Reverting drops the account back to password-only and re-exposes it to credential attacks. Keep it enabled; if a token is lost, re-provision rather than disabling 2FA.
MITRE-T1078-001.impact: Non-disruptive to traffic; no reboot. Administrative-access risk: if a token is misconfigured or not provisioned, that admin cannot log in. Always verify a second working admin+token exists before enforcing to avoid lockout. Existing admin sessions remain until logout.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 2.1.4 (Enable Admin MFA/Two-factor)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — System > Administrators > Two-factor authentication / FortiToken
Hardening your FortiGate — Enforce two-factor authentication for administrators
MITRE ATT&CK T1078 Valid Accounts
CWE-308 Use of Single-factor Authentication
PCI DSS v4.0 8.3.1

MITRE-T1090-001.risk: Application Control is not blocking proxy/tunnel applications (Tor, Ultrasurf, Psiphon, SOCKS/VPN tunnels). Adversaries and insiders use these to anonymize C2, bypass web filtering, and hide exfiltration inside encrypted tunnels. Business impact: your egress controls and web filtering are trivially circumvented, and malicious traffic is laundered through anonymizing infrastructure with no attribution.
steps: Confirm proxy/tunnel apps are not blocked: show application list and review category actions.
Edit the app-control profile: config application list / edit "appctrl-block".
Block the Proxy category: config entries / edit 1 / set category 6 / set action block / next / end (6 = Proxy; confirm with set category ?).
Add specific evasive-app signatures (Tor, Ultrasurf) by application ID if you want granular control: edit 2 / set application <app-id> / set action block / next / end.

Attach the profile with deep SSL inspection so tunnels inside TLS are classified: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set application-list "appctrl-block" / set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection" / set utm-status enable / next / end.
Complement with IPS Tor/anonymizer signatures and DNS/web filtering of anonymizer categories.
Monitor Application Control logs for blocked proxy/tunnel attempts and identify the originating hosts.
Roll out fleet-wide and re-scan.
MITRE-T1090-001.gui: Security Profiles > Application Control > set the 'Proxy' category action to Block (and specific apps like Tor/Ultrasurf) > attach to policies with SSL inspection. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Application Control.
MITRE-T1090-001.cli:
config application list
  edit "appctrl-block"
    config entries
      edit 1
        set category 6
        set action block
      next
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set application-list "appctrl-block"
    set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"
  next
end
MITRE-T1090-001.verify: show application list "appctrl-block"  -> expect an entry with category 6 (Proxy) action block. Live test: attempt a Tor/anonymizer connection and confirm a block in Log & Report > Application Control. Category IDs confirmable via set category ? in the entry context.
MITRE-T1090-001.rollback: config application list / edit "appctrl-block" / config entries / edit 1 / set action pass / next / end / next / end. Reverting re-permits anonymizing tunnels. Prefer action pass with logging (set log enable) if you need visibility without blocking a sanctioned proxy.
MITRE-T1090-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Blocking the Proxy category can affect legitimate business proxies/tools if any are in use — inventory first. Deep SSL inspection (endpoint CA) is needed to classify TLS-wrapped tunnels.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.4 (Application Control)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > Application Control (categories, evasive apps)
MITRE ATT&CK T1090 Proxy (T1090.003 Multi-hop/Tor)
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 1.2.1

MITRE-T1102-001.risk: With neither Application Control nor SSL deep inspection deployed, adversaries tunnel C2 through legitimate cloud services (Slack, Discord, Telegram, GitHub, Pastebin) over HTTPS. The traffic looks like normal SaaS use and its encrypted payload is invisible. Business impact: covert, hard-to-attribute command-and-control riding trusted domains that egress and reputation filtering will not block, extending attacker dwell time.
steps: Confirm the gap: get application list (empty) and no deep-inspection ssl-ssh-profile in use.
Build a deep-inspection SSL profile (see T1203; the built-in "deep-inspection" is READ-ONLY — edit "custom-deep-inspection" or clone your own) so cloud-service payloads become visible.

Create an Application Control profile to identify and control cloud/collaboration services: config application list / edit "appctrl-cloud" / config entries / edit 1 / set category 30 / set action pass / set log enable / next / end (30 = Cloud.IT; application-control entries have no 'monitor' action — 'monitor' = action pass with logging enabled; tighten unsanctioned services to block/limit after baselining).
Attach both to user policies: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set application-list "appctrl-cloud" / set ssl-ssh-profile "custom-deep-inspection" / next / end.
Add web/DNS filtering to control which specific cloud domains are permitted.
Distribute the FortiGate CA to endpoints and add ssl-exempt for pinned/privacy categories.
Review App Control + SSL logs to baseline sanctioned vs. anomalous cloud usage, then tighten.
Re-scan to confirm AppCtrl and deep inspection are both present.
MITRE-T1102-001.gui: Security Profiles > Application Control (control cloud/collaboration categories) and Security Profiles > SSL/SSH Inspection (Full/deep, via custom-deep-inspection) > attach both to user policies. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles.
MITRE-T1102-001.cli:
config firewall ssl-ssh-profile
  edit "custom-deep-inspection"
    set server-cert-mode re-sign
    config https
      set status deep-inspection
    end
  next
end
config application list
  edit "appctrl-cloud"
    config entries
      edit 1
        set category 30
        set action pass
        set log enable
      next
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set application-list "appctrl-cloud"
    set ssl-ssh-profile "custom-deep-inspection"
  next
end
MITRE-T1102-001.verify: show firewall policy <id> | grep -e application-list -e ssl-ssh-profile  -> expect both set. Confirm deep inspection status: show firewall ssl-ssh-profile "custom-deep-inspection" | grep status. Cloud-service usage should now appear classified in Log & Report > Application Control.
MITRE-T1102-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset application-list / set ssl-ssh-profile "certificate-inspection" / next / end. Reverting restores the cloud-C2 blind spot; prefer keeping AppCtrl in pass+log and relaxing SSL exemptions over full rollback.
MITRE-T1102-001.impact: Non-disruptive to uptime; no reboot. Deep SSL inspection requires the FortiGate CA on endpoints and can break cert-pinned apps unless exempted. Blocking cloud categories may disrupt sanctioned SaaS — start in pass+log and allow-list approved services.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.4 (Application Control) and 4.2.5 (SSL/SSH Inspection)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Application Control and SSL/SSH Inspection
MITRE ATT&CK T1102 Web Service (T1102.002 Bidirectional Communication)
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 1.2.1

MITRE-T1105-001.risk: No AntiVirus profiles are configured at all, so nothing scans file transfers. Post-exploitation tooling an attacker pulls onto a foothold — Mimikatz, Cobalt Strike beacons, PsExec, custom loaders — downloads over HTTP/FTP/SMB/mail without any inspection. Business impact: attackers freely stage their toolkit inside the network, accelerating privilege escalation and lateral movement. Rated CRITICAL because a core malware-blocking control is entirely absent.
steps: Confirm no AV profiles exist: get antivirus profile (empty).
Create an AV profile that blocks across all inspected protocols: config antivirus profile / edit "av-block" / set feature-set flow.

Enable scanning + blocking per protocol: config http / set av-scan block / end / config ftp / set av-scan block / end / config smtp / set av-scan block / end / config imap / set av-scan block / end / config pop3 / set av-scan block / end.
Add outbreak prevention and (if licensed) inline sandbox: config http / set outbreak-prevention block / set fortisandbox block / end.
Turn on the extended AV DB: config antivirus settings / set default-db extended / end.

Attach the profile to all traffic policies with deep SSL inspection: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set av-profile "av-block" / set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection" / next / end.
Verify FortiGuard AV licensing/signatures: diagnose autoupdate versions.
Test with the EICAR file over HTTP and confirm a block, then re-scan.
MITRE-T1105-001.gui: Security Profiles > AntiVirus > create a profile with Block action on all protocols > attach to every Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > AntiVirus.
MITRE-T1105-001.cli:
config antivirus profile
  edit "av-block"
    set feature-set flow
    config http
      set av-scan block
      set outbreak-prevention block
    end
    config ftp
      set av-scan block
    end
    config smtp
      set av-scan block
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set av-profile "av-block"
    set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"
  next
end
MITRE-T1105-001.verify: get antivirus profile  -> expect 'av-block' present. show firewall policy <id> | grep av-profile -> set. Functional test: download the EICAR test file over HTTP and confirm it is blocked with an AntiVirus log entry.
MITRE-T1105-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset av-profile / next / end (and delete the profile). Reverting removes malware/tool-transfer scanning entirely — do not roll back. If a specific legitimate transfer is falsely flagged, use an AV override/exempt rather than disabling AV.
MITRE-T1105-001.impact: Non-disruptive to availability; no reboot. Proxy-based AV can reduce throughput on large transfers (flow mode minimizes this). Full HTTPS scanning needs deep SSL inspection with the FortiGate CA on endpoints. Requires an active FortiGuard AV subscription.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.1 (AntiVirus)
