FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > AntiVirus (protocol scanning, blocking)
MITRE ATT&CK T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 5.1 / 5.2

MITRE-T1110-001.risk: Admin account lockout is not configured (threshold 0), so the management interface permits unlimited password-guessing attempts. Combined with any WAN/exposed management surface, an attacker can run an offline-speed online brute force or credential-stuffing run against admin logins until one succeeds. Business impact: firewall admin takeover with no automatic throttling, and no signal that a brute force is underway.
steps: Check current settings: get system global | grep -e admin-lockout-threshold -e admin-lockout-duration.
Set a low failed-attempt threshold: config system global / set admin-lockout-threshold 3.
Set a lockout duration (seconds): set admin-lockout-duration 300 / end.
Harden the login further: reduce set admin-ssh-grace-time 30 and set admintimeout 5 to shrink attack/idle windows.
Constrain admin sources with trusted hosts so brute force must originate from the mgmt network: config system admin / edit <admin> / set trusthost1 <mgmt-subnet/mask> / next / end.
Add a local-in policy or move management off any WAN interface (see T1595).
Enable and forward admin auth-failure logs to FortiAnalyzer and alert on repeated failures.
Validate by triggering three bad logins and confirming the lockout engages.
MITRE-T1110-001.gui: System > Settings > Administration Settings (or via CLI) for lockout threshold/duration. Trusted hosts under System > Administrators. FortiManager: System Settings > Admin > Admin Settings.
MITRE-T1110-001.cli:
config system global
  set admin-lockout-threshold 3
  set admin-lockout-duration 300
end
MITRE-T1110-001.verify: get system global | grep admin-lockout  -> expect: admin-lockout-threshold: 3 and admin-lockout-duration: 300. Behavioral test: 3 consecutive bad passwords should lock the source for 300s (confirm in Log & Report > System Events).
MITRE-T1110-001.rollback: config system global / set admin-lockout-threshold 0 / end. Reverting removes brute-force throttling. Note: overly aggressive settings can be abused to lock out legitimate admins (self-DoS) — pair lockout with trusted hosts rather than removing it.
MITRE-T1110-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot; no traffic impact. Administrative-access caveat: an attacker (or fat-fingered admin) hitting the threshold from your egress IP could temporarily lock legitimate admins out from that source — trusted-host scoping mitigates this.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 3.1.5 (Account lockout)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — System > Settings (admin-lockout-threshold / -duration)
Hardening your FortiGate — Limit administrative login attempts
MITRE ATT&CK T1110 Brute Force
CWE-307 Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts

MITRE-T1133-001.risk: SSL-VPN is exposed with password-only authentication (no client certificate). Given the history of credential leaks and pre-auth CVEs on FortiOS SSL-VPN, an attacker armed with credentials from an infostealer log, a phishing kit, or CVE-2018-13379 path-traversal harvesting can log in as a legitimate remote user and walk straight into the corporate network. Business impact: full remote-user impersonation, ransomware staging, and bypass of perimeter controls.
steps: Confirm SSL-VPN is enabled and lacks client-cert enforcement: get vpn ssl settings | grep -e status -e reqclientcert.

Issue/deploy client certificates from your PKI (or FortiAuthenticator) to every remote user's device and import the issuing CA on the FortiGate: config vpn certificate ca / edit "corp-ca" / set ca "<pem>" / next / end.
Enforce client-cert on the SSL-VPN: config vpn ssl settings / set reqclientcert enable / end.
Bind PKI users/peer groups for certificate-to-user mapping: config user peer / edit "vpn-users" / set ca "corp-ca" / next / end.
Also require MFA on the VPN user group as defense-in-depth (FortiToken): config user local / edit <user> / set two-factor fortitoken / next / end.
Restrict the SSL-VPN portal source with geo/ISDB or a local-in policy where feasible.
Notify users, then validate a cert-holding client can connect and a cert-less client is rejected.
Monitor VPN auth logs on FortiAnalyzer for rejected-no-cert events.
MITRE-T1133-001.gui: VPN > SSL-VPN Settings > enable 'Require Client Certificate'. Import CA under System > Certificates. Map users under User & Authentication > PKI (or User Groups). FortiManager: Device Manager > VPN Manager / SSL-VPN Settings.
MITRE-T1133-001.cli:
config vpn ssl settings
  set reqclientcert enable
end
config user peer
  edit "vpn-users"
    set ca "corp-ca"
  next
end
MITRE-T1133-001.verify: get vpn ssl settings | grep reqclientcert  -> expect: reqclientcert : enable. Functional test: a client without a valid cert must fail at TLS handshake; confirm in diagnose debug application sslvpn -1 or Log & Report > VPN Events.
MITRE-T1133-001.rollback: config vpn ssl settings / set reqclientcert disable / end. Reverting re-opens password-only VPN. Warning: if certs were not fully deployed, leaving it enabled locks out remote users — validate cert distribution before enforcing.
MITRE-T1133-001.impact: Potentially service-disrupting for remote workers: any user without a valid client certificate will be unable to connect once enabled. No reboot. Existing SSL-VPN tunnels stay up until renegotiation. Schedule during a maintenance window and confirm cert rollout first.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 5.1.3 (SSL-VPN authentication)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — SSL-VPN > Client certificate authentication
Hardening your FortiGate — Restrict and harden SSL-VPN
MITRE ATT&CK T1133 External Remote Services
CVE-2018-13379 (FortiOS SSL-VPN path traversal credential exposure)
CWE-308 Use of Single-factor Authentication
PCI DSS v4.0 8.3.1

MITRE-T1189-001.risk: Web Filter covers under half of policies, so users hitting compromised or watering-hole websites are not protected by FortiGuard URL categorization. A drive-by exploit or malicious redirect on a legitimate-but-hacked site delivers a payload straight to the browser. Business impact: web-borne initial access via trusted sites, bypassing reputation controls that would otherwise block the malicious URL.
steps: Find policies missing Web Filter: get firewall policy | grep -e policyid -e webfilter-profile.
Create/verify a Web Filter profile with FortiGuard rating: config webfilter profile / edit "wf-block".

Block malicious categories: config ftgd-wf / config filters / edit 1 / set category 26 / set action block / next / edit 2 / set category 61 / set action block / next / end / end (26 = Malicious Websites, 61 = Phishing).
Enable safe-search / URL rating options and block newly-observed/unrated where policy allows.

Attach to all user policies with AV + deep SSL inspection: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set webfilter-profile "wf-block" / set av-profile "av-block" / set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection" / next / end.
Confirm the FortiGuard Web Filter subscription is licensed: get system fortiguard.
Monitor Web Filter logs and tune category actions.
Push fleet-wide and re-scan for >50% coverage.
MITRE-T1189-001.gui: Security Profiles > Web Filter > enable FortiGuard category filtering (block Malicious/Phishing) > attach to Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy for all user egress. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Web Filter.
MITRE-T1189-001.cli:
config webfilter profile
  edit "wf-block"
    config ftgd-wf
      config filters
        edit 1
          set category 26
          set action block
        next
        edit 2
          set category 61
          set action block
        next
      end
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set webfilter-profile "wf-block"
    set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"
  next
end
MITRE-T1189-001.verify: show firewall policy <id> | grep webfilter-profile  -> expect set. get system fortiguard (and diagnose autoupdate versions) -> Web Filter contract valid / reachable. Browse a FortiGuard test URL in a blocked category and confirm the block page + Web Filter log.
MITRE-T1189-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset webfilter-profile / next / end. Reverting removes URL-reputation protection and re-exposes users to watering-hole/drive-by sites. Prefer moving specific categories from block to monitor if a business site is over-blocked.
MITRE-T1189-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Category over-blocking can interrupt legitimate browsing (tune with monitor/allow overrides). Full URL inspection of HTTPS requires deep SSL inspection with the FortiGate CA deployed to endpoints.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.3 (Web Filter)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > Web Filter (FortiGuard categories)
MITRE ATT&CK T1189 Drive-by Compromise
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 5.1

MITRE-T1190-001.risk: An internet-facing FortiGate policy with no IPS sensor lets an attacker throw known exploits (Log4Shell, Struts, ProxyShell, or FortiOS SSL-VPN CVEs) straight at your DMZ web/app/VPN servers with nothing inspecting the packet stream. A single unpatched Apache or Exchange box behind an un-inspected policy becomes an unauthenticated RCE foothold, leading to webshell drop, data theft, and pivot into the LAN. Business impact: breach of externally-reachable crown-jewel apps and regulatory exposure (PCI DSS 6.5/11.4).
steps: Identify inbound/DMZ policies missing IPS: get firewall policy | grep -e policyid -e ips-sensor -e dstintf (or Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy and sort by the Security Profiles column).
Create a hardened IPS sensor: config ips sensor / edit "protect_servers".
Add a broad block entry: config entries / edit 1 / set severity high critical / set status enable / set action block / next / end.

(Optional) Add a monitor entry for medium severity so you can tune before blocking (IPS has no 'monitor' action — monitor = pass + log): edit 2 / set severity medium / set status enable / set action pass / set log enable / next / end, then close with next / end.

Ensure UTM inspection is active on the target policy: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable. Note: flow vs proxy inspection mode is a VDOM-level setting (config system settings / set inspection-mode {flow|proxy}), not a per-policy field on a default FortiGate — flow mode is the default and is sufficient for IPS.

Attach the sensor and an SSL profile so encrypted exploits are also seen: set ips-sensor "protect_servers" / set ssl-ssh-profile "certificate-inspection" (use a full/deep profile for payload visibility) / next / end.
Repeat for every enabled inbound and DMZ policy; script it in FortiManager for fleet-wide coverage.
Ensure IPS engine/signatures are current: execute update-now, then diagnose autoupdate versions to confirm the IPS DB date.
Watch logtraffic and forward IPS events to FortiAnalyzer, then re-run the scanner to confirm coverage is above threshold.
MITRE-T1190-001.gui: Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention (create/edit the sensor) then Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit each inbound/DMZ policy > Security Profiles > toggle IPS on and select the sensor. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention, then attach in the Policy Package.
MITRE-T1190-001.cli:
config ips sensor
  edit "protect_servers"
    config entries
      edit 1
        set severity high critical
        set status enable
        set action block
      next
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set ips-sensor "protect_servers"
    set ssl-ssh-profile "certificate-inspection"
  next
end
MITRE-T1190-001.verify: show firewall policy <policy-id> | grep ips-sensor  -> expect: set ips-sensor "protect_servers". Fleet check: get firewall policy | grep ips-sensor lists each policy that carries a sensor — compare that line count to the number of enabled inbound policies. Confirm engine health with: diagnose test application ipsmonitor 1 (shows IPS engine info/running).
MITRE-T1190-001.rollback: Detach with config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset ips-sensor / set utm-status disable / next / end, and delete the sensor: config ips sensor / delete "protect_servers". Reverting removes exploit detection on that path; only do so if IPS is causing verified false-positive blocks (tune the entry action to pass/monitor instead of full rollback).
MITRE-T1190-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Flow-based IPS adds negligible latency. Switching a VDOM/policy from no-inspection to proxy inspection-mode can briefly reset in-flight sessions. False-positive signatures could block legitimate app traffic, so stage new sensors in monitor/pass before block.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.2 (Apply IPS Security Profile to Policies)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention
Hardening your FortiGate (Fortinet Document Library) — Enable IPS on internet-facing policies
MITRE ATT&CK T1190 Exploit Public-Facing Application
CVE-2022-42475 / CVE-2023-27997 (FortiOS SSL-VPN pre-auth RCE)
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

MITRE-T1203-001.risk: No SSL deep-inspection profile exists, so roughly 90%+ of traffic (all HTTPS) is opaque to IPS and AV. Exploit kits, drive-by downloads, and browser/Office exploit payloads delivered over TLS reach the endpoint completely uninspected, enabling client-side code execution. Business impact: encrypted malware delivery bypasses the entire UTM stack, undermining every other security profile you deploy.
steps: Confirm no deep-inspection profile is in use: get firewall ssl-ssh-profile | grep name and inspect each policy's ssl-ssh-profile for a full/deep profile.
Generate/import a CA the FortiGate will use to re-sign (a private CA the FortiGate holds the key for), or use the built-in Fortinet_CA_SSL.

Customize the editable deep profile — the built-in "deep-inspection" profile is READ-ONLY, so edit "custom-deep-inspection" (or clone it to your own name): config firewall ssl-ssh-profile / edit "custom-deep-inspection" / set server-cert-mode re-sign.
Enable deep inspection per protocol: config https / set status deep-inspection / set ports 443 / end (repeat for ftps/imaps/pop3s/smtps/ssh as needed).
Add exemptions for privacy/pinned categories: config ssl-exempt / edit 1 / set type fortiguard-category / set fortiguard-category 31 / next / end (31 = Finance and Banking; add Health) / next / end.
Distribute the FortiGate re-sign CA to all endpoints (GPO/MDM) as a trusted root to avoid certificate warnings.
Attach to user policies: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set ssl-ssh-profile "custom-deep-inspection" / next / end, alongside IPS/AV.
Pilot on a test group, watch for app breakage (cert-pinned apps), then expand.
Re-run the scanner to confirm a deep profile is detected.
MITRE-T1203-001.gui: Security Profiles > SSL/SSH Inspection > edit "custom-deep-inspection" (or clone the read-only "deep-inspection" profile to a new name) > set Inspection Method to 'Full SSL Inspection' > choose the CA cert > add category/address exemptions. Export the CA from System > Certificates and push to endpoints. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > Security Profiles > SSL/SSH Inspection.
MITRE-T1203-001.cli:
config firewall ssl-ssh-profile
  edit "custom-deep-inspection"
    set server-cert-mode re-sign
    config https
      set status deep-inspection
      set ports 443
    end
    config ssl-exempt
      edit 1
        set type fortiguard-category
        set fortiguard-category 31
      next
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set ssl-ssh-profile "custom-deep-inspection"
  next
end
MITRE-T1203-001.verify: show firewall ssl-ssh-profile "custom-deep-inspection" | grep -A2 config\ https  -> expect: set status deep-inspection. On a client, browse HTTPS and confirm the served leaf cert is re-signed by the FortiGate CA. Check no cert errors after CA distribution.
MITRE-T1203-001.rollback: Switch the policy back to certificate-inspection or none: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set ssl-ssh-profile "certificate-inspection" / next / end. Reverting re-blinds IPS/AV to encrypted threats. If specific apps break (cert pinning), add them to ssl-exempt rather than disabling the whole profile.
MITRE-T1203-001.impact: Non-disruptive to firewall availability; no reboot. HIGH user-impact risk: without the FortiGate CA installed on endpoints, every HTTPS site throws certificate warnings, and cert-pinned apps (banking, some mobile/desktop apps) break. Deploy CA and exemptions first; pilot before fleet rollout.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.5 (SSL/SSH Inspection)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > SSL/SSH Inspection (Full/deep inspection)
Hardening your FortiGate — Deploy the FortiGate CA certificate to endpoints
MITRE ATT&CK T1203 Exploitation for Client Execution
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

MITRE-T1210-001.risk: Without IPS on inter-zone policies, lateral exploitation between network segments goes undetected. A foothold host firing EternalBlue/MS17-010, PrintNightmare, or Log4Shell at internal servers crosses the firewall between zones with no exploit-signature inspection. Business impact: wormable exploits spread east-west unchecked, turning a single compromised workstation into a domain-wide breach — exactly the propagation path used by NotPetya/WannaCry-class incidents.
steps: Identify inter-zone allow policies lacking IPS (srcintf != dstintf, empty ips-sensor).

Build/choose an IPS sensor emphasizing server-exploit signatures: config ips sensor / edit "east-west" / config entries / edit 1 / set severity high critical / set status enable / set action block / next / end / next / end.

Apply to every inter-zone policy: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set ips-sensor "east-west" / next / end. (Flow vs proxy inspection is a VDOM-level setting under config system settings, not a per-policy field; flow is the default and is sufficient for IPS.)
Prioritize signatures for SMB (MS17-010), RDP (BlueKeep/CVE-2019-0708), spooler (PrintNightmare), and Log4j.
Patch the vulnerable internal services in parallel — IPS is a virtual patch, not a substitute.
Enable logging on inter-zone policies and alert on exploit-signature hits.
Baseline in monitor briefly, then enforce block.
Re-scan to confirm inter-zone IPS coverage.
MITRE-T1210-001.gui: Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention > enable server/exploit signature categories in the sensor > Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > apply IPS to each inter-zone policy. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention.
MITRE-T1210-001.cli:
config ips sensor
  edit "east-west"
    config entries
      edit 1
        set severity high critical
        set status enable
        set action block
      next
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <inter-zone-policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set ips-sensor "east-west"
  next
end
MITRE-T1210-001.verify: show firewall policy <inter-zone-policy-id> | grep ips-sensor  -> expect the 'east-west' sensor. Confirm the sensor blocks a known exploit signature (test in a lab / check IPS logs). diagnose test application ipsmonitor 1 shows IPS engine running.
MITRE-T1210-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset ips-sensor / next / end. Reverting removes east-west exploit detection and re-enables unchecked lateral exploitation. Keep enabled; tune specific signatures to pass+log if a legitimate internal app trips a rule.
MITRE-T1210-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Adds IPS inspection load on internal links (size the device for east-west throughput). Switching a VDOM to proxy inspection-mode can reset in-flight sessions. IPS complements but does not replace patching of the vulnerable services.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.2 (IPS on policies)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention (virtual patching)
MITRE ATT&CK T1210 Exploitation of Remote Services
CVE-2017-0144 (MS17-010/EternalBlue), CVE-2021-34527 (PrintNightmare), CVE-2019-0708 (BlueKeep)
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

MITRE-T1219-001.risk: Application Control is not blocking remote-access tool categories, so TeamViewer, AnyDesk, ScreenConnect, and RustDesk run freely. Adversaries deploy these legitimate tools as C2 to blend in with normal admin activity and evade malware detection — a hallmark of ransomware affiliates and tech-support scams. Business impact: attacker-controlled interactive access that looks like sanctioned remote support, enabling hands-on-keyboard control and data theft with minimal detection.
steps: Confirm RATs are not blocked: show application list and check the Remote.Access category action.
Edit the app-control profile: config application list / edit "appctrl-block".
Block the Remote.Access category: config entries / edit 1 / set category 7 / set action block / next / end (7 = Remote.Access; confirm with set category ?).
Whitelist only sanctioned tools by application signature: add an entry with set application <approved-app-id> / set action pass, ordered above the category block.

Attach with deep SSL inspection so TLS-based RATs are classified: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set application-list "appctrl-block" / set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection" / set utm-status enable / next / end.
Reinforce with web/DNS filtering of remote-access vendor domains.
Monitor Application Control logs for blocked RAT attempts and investigate originating hosts.
Roll out fleet-wide and re-scan.
MITRE-T1219-001.gui: Security Profiles > Application Control > set 'Remote.Access' category to Block, allow only approved tools by signature > attach to policies with SSL inspection. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Application Control.
MITRE-T1219-001.cli:
config application list
  edit "appctrl-block"
    config entries
      edit 1
        set category 7
        set action block
      next
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set application-list "appctrl-block"
    set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"
  next
end
MITRE-T1219-001.verify: show application list "appctrl-block"  -> expect an entry with category 7 (Remote.Access) action block, plus any allow overrides. Launch an unapproved RAT and confirm a block in Log & Report > Application Control.
MITRE-T1219-001.rollback: config application list / edit "appctrl-block" / config entries / edit 1 / set action pass / set log enable / next / end / next / end (application-control entries have no 'monitor' action — 'monitor' = action pass with logging enabled). Reverting re-permits unsanctioned remote-access tools. Keep the block and manage exceptions via per-app allow entries rather than disabling the category.
MITRE-T1219-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Will block sanctioned remote-support tools unless explicitly allow-listed — inventory approved tools first to avoid disrupting IT/helpdesk. Deep SSL inspection (endpoint CA) is needed to classify TLS-based RATs.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.4 (Application Control)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > Application Control (Remote.Access category)
MITRE ATT&CK T1219 Remote Access Software
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 1.2.1

MITRE-T1486-001.risk: Cloud sandbox and outbreak prevention are disabled, leaving only static signatures against ransomware. Modern ransomware families deploy fresh, polymorphic samples that have no signature at delivery time, so they detonate on the endpoint, encrypt file shares, and pivot before AV ever catches up. Business impact: mass data encryption, operational outage, extortion, and recovery costs — the classic ransomware worst case that signature-only AV cannot prevent.
steps: Review AV profiles for missing sandbox/outbreak prevention: show antivirus profile.
Enable outbreak prevention (real-time hash intelligence) per protocol: config antivirus profile / edit "av-block" / config http / set av-scan block / set outbreak-prevention block / end.
Enable inline sandbox submission of suspicious files: config http / set fortisandbox block / end (repeat smtp/ftp/imap/pop3).
Wire up the sandbox: config system fortisandbox / set status enable / set inline-scan enable / set server <fsa-or-forticloud> / end.
Turn on the extended DB and ML detection: config antivirus settings / set default-db extended / set machine-learning-detection enable / end.
Add IPS + botnet C2 blocking on egress to catch ransomware callbacks: set scan-botnet-connections block on policies.
Attach the AV profile to all user and server policies with deep SSL inspection.
Ensure independent, offline/immutable backups exist (compensating control) and re-scan to confirm sandbox is enabled.
MITRE-T1486-001.gui: Security Profiles > AntiVirus > enable FortiGuard Outbreak Prevention and Send Files to FortiSandbox/Cloud > attach to all policies. Configure FortiSandbox via Security Fabric > Fabric Connectors. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > AntiVirus.
MITRE-T1486-001.cli:
config antivirus profile
  edit "av-block"
    set feature-set flow
    config http
      set av-scan block
      set outbreak-prevention block
      set fortisandbox block
    end
  next
end
config system fortisandbox
  set status enable
  set inline-scan enable
  set server <fsa-ip-or-forticloud>
end
config antivirus settings
  set default-db extended
  set machine-learning-detection enable
end
MITRE-T1486-001.verify: show antivirus profile "av-block" | grep -e outbreak-prevention -e fortisandbox  -> expect block. get system fortisandbox | grep status -> enable. Confirm sandbox connectivity (Security Fabric > Fabric Connectors shows the FortiSandbox connector = Connected) and that AV logs show sandbox verdicts.
MITRE-T1486-001.rollback: config antivirus profile / edit "av-block" / config http / set outbreak-prevention disable / set fortisandbox disable / end / next / end. Reverting drops ransomware defense to signatures only — keep enabled. Backups remain the last line of defense regardless.
MITRE-T1486-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Inline sandbox block mode adds first-seen-file latency; requires a FortiSandbox appliance or FortiGuard Cloud Sandbox subscription to function. No data-plane outage. Pair with tested offline backups.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.1 (AntiVirus)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — AntiVirus > Outbreak prevention & FortiSandbox inline scanning
Fortinet Ransomware Protection Solution Guide
MITRE ATT&CK T1486 Data Encrypted for Impact
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 5.1 / backup controls

MITRE-T1496-001.risk: No Application Control profiles exist, so cryptomining traffic to mining pools (Stratum protocol) cannot be identified and blocked. A compromised internal host — or a malicious insider — can mine cryptocurrency undetected, and cryptomining is often the first observable symptom of a broader compromise. Business impact: stolen compute/electricity, degraded performance of production systems, and a missed early indicator of intrusion.
steps: Confirm no app-control exists: get application list (empty).

Create a profile and block cryptomining: config application list / edit "appctrl-block" / config entries / edit 1 / set application <cryptomining-signature-ids> / set action block / next / end (use the Cryptomining/Bitcoin/Stratum signatures) / next / end.
Alternatively filter by the risk/behavior technology so all mining apps are caught, then attach.
Attach to policies with deep SSL inspection: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set application-list "appctrl-block" / set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection" / next / end.
Enable botnet C2 blocking on egress to catch mining-pool callbacks: set scan-botnet-connections block on the policy.
Add IPS signatures for Stratum/mining and DNS/web filtering of mining-pool domains.
Monitor Application Control + Botnet logs for mining activity and investigate the source host as potentially compromised.
Re-scan to confirm an application-control profile now exists.
MITRE-T1496-001.gui: Security Profiles > Application Control > add the Cryptomining/Bitcoin/Stratum application signatures with Block action > attach to policies with SSL inspection and enable Botnet C&C blocking on the Firewall Policy. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Application Control.
MITRE-T1496-001.cli:
config application list
  edit "appctrl-block"
    config entries
      edit 1
        set application <cryptomining-signature-id>
        set action block
      next
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set application-list "appctrl-block"
    set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"
    set scan-botnet-connections block
  next
end
MITRE-T1496-001.verify: get application list  -> expect 'appctrl-block' present. show firewall policy <id> | grep -e application-list -e scan-botnet-connections -> expect set and block. Confirm mining/Stratum traffic is blocked in Log & Report > Application Control.
MITRE-T1496-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset application-list / set scan-botnet-connections disable / next / end. Reverting removes cryptomining detection/blocking; prefer setting the mining signatures to pass+log if you need visibility without blocking during investigation.
MITRE-T1496-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Cryptomining signatures are specific, so false positives are rare, but deep SSL inspection (endpoint CA) is needed to classify TLS-wrapped mining. scan-botnet-connections adds reputation lookups with negligible latency.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.4 (Application Control)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Application Control (cryptomining signatures) and Firewall Policy Botnet C2 protection
MITRE ATT&CK T1496 Resource Hijacking
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 1.2.1

MITRE-T1498-001.risk: No DoS protection policies are configured, so the FortiGate and the services behind it have no volumetric or protocol-anomaly defense. A SYN/UDP/ICMP flood or connection-exhaustion attack can saturate the firewall's session table and starve legitimate users, taking public services offline. Business impact: internet-facing service outage, revenue loss, and SLA breaches during a network denial-of-service event.
steps: Confirm no DoS policies exist: get firewall DoS-policy (empty) — for IPv6 also firewall DoS-policy6.
Create a DoS policy on the WAN interface: config firewall DoS-policy / edit 1 / set interface <wan> / set srcaddr all / set dstaddr all / set service all.

Enable anomaly protections with tuned thresholds: config anomaly / edit tcp_syn_flood / set status enable / set action block / set threshold 2000 / next / edit udp_flood / set status enable / set action block / set threshold 2000 / next / edit icmp_flood / set status enable / set action block / next / end.
Add connection-based anomalies (tcp_src_session, tcp_dst_session) sized to your capacity.
Close the policy: next / end.
Baseline normal peak rates first (from FortiView/traffic history) so thresholds do not clip legitimate bursts.
Start anomaly actions in monitor/pass, observe, then switch to block.
Re-scan to confirm at least one DoS policy is present, and monitor DoS anomaly logs.
MITRE-T1498-001.gui: Policy & Objects > DoS Policy (or IPv4/IPv6 DoS Policy) > Create New > select the WAN interface > enable and tune L3/L4 anomalies (tcp_syn_flood, udp_flood, icmp_flood, etc.). FortiManager: Policy Packages > DoS Policy.
MITRE-T1498-001.cli:
config firewall DoS-policy
  edit 1
    set interface <wan-interface>
    set srcaddr all
    set dstaddr all
    set service all
    config anomaly
      edit tcp_syn_flood
        set status enable
        set action block
        set threshold 2000
      next
      edit udp_flood
        set status enable
        set action block
        set threshold 2000
      next
      edit icmp_flood
        set status enable
        set action block
      next
    end
  next
end
MITRE-T1498-001.verify: get firewall DoS-policy  -> expect policy 1 on the WAN interface with anomalies enabled. During an attack, diagnose ips anomaly list shows the per-anomaly hit counters (id, ip, pps, freq); FortiView also shows the anomaly hits. show firewall DoS-policy confirms the thresholds.
MITRE-T1498-001.rollback: config firewall DoS-policy / delete 1 (or set status disable per anomaly). Reverting removes flood protection. If thresholds are clipping legitimate traffic, raise the threshold or set the anomaly action to monitor rather than deleting the whole policy.
MITRE-T1498-001.impact: Non-disruptive when thresholds are correctly sized; no reboot. Risk of self-inflicted DoS if thresholds are set below legitimate peak traffic (blocks real users) — baseline and stage in monitor first. DoS policies are evaluated before the policy table, so misconfiguration can drop good flows.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 9.1.1 (DoS protection)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Policy & Objects > DoS policies (anomaly protection)
Hardening your FortiGate — Configure DoS protection on internet-facing interfaces
MITRE ATT&CK T1498 Network Denial of Service
CWE-400 Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
PCI DSS v4.0 11.4

MITRE-T1499-001.risk: No IPS sensors are configured, so application-layer denial-of-service — HTTP Slowloris, RUDY, ReDoS, and connection-exhaustion against web servers behind the FortiGate — is not detected or rate-limited. Unlike volumetric floods, these low-and-slow attacks exhaust server worker threads with little bandwidth. Business impact: public web/app services are taken down by a single low-traffic attacker, causing outages that DoS-policy thresholds alone will not catch.
steps: Confirm no IPS sensors exist: get ips sensor (empty).

Create an IPS sensor with rate-based and DoS-relevant signatures: config ips sensor / edit "appdos" / config entries / edit 1 / set severity high critical / set status enable / set action block / next / end.
Enable rate-based signatures (e.g., HTTP.Slowloris, Slow.HTTP.DoS) with rate/track thresholds inside the sensor entry (set rate-count / set rate-duration / set rate-track dest-ip).
Attach the sensor to inbound web-server policies: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set ips-sensor "appdos" / next / end.
Pair with a DoS policy for L3/L4 flood coverage (see T1498).
Tune thresholds against baseline traffic so legitimate bursts are not throttled.
Enable logging and alert on rate-based signature hits.
Re-scan to confirm an IPS sensor now exists.
MITRE-T1499-001.gui: Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention > create a sensor and enable rate-based/DoS signatures (Slowloris, RUDY) with thresholds > attach to inbound web-server policies. Pair with Policy & Objects > DoS Policy. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention.
MITRE-T1499-001.cli:
config ips sensor
  edit "appdos"
    config entries
      edit 1
        set severity high critical
        set status enable
        set action block
        set rate-count 100
        set rate-duration 60
        set rate-track dest-ip
      next
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <inbound-web-policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set ips-sensor "appdos"
  next
end
MITRE-T1499-001.verify: get ips sensor  -> expect 'appdos' present. show firewall policy <id> | grep ips-sensor -> set. Under a controlled Slowloris test, confirm the rate-based signature triggers and blocks (IPS logs). diagnose test application ipsmonitor 1 confirms IPS engine running.
MITRE-T1499-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset ips-sensor / next / end (and delete the sensor). Reverting removes application-layer DoS detection. If rate thresholds clip legitimate spikes, raise the rate-count/duration or set action to monitor instead of removing.
MITRE-T1499-001.impact: Non-disruptive when thresholds are tuned; no reboot. Rate-based signatures set too low can throttle legitimate high-request clients (self-inflicted impact) — baseline and stage in monitor. Adds IPS CPU load on inbound web policies.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 9.1.1 (DoS/anomaly) and 4.2.2 (IPS)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention (rate-based signatures)
MITRE ATT&CK T1499 Endpoint Denial of Service (T1499.002/.003)
CWE-400 Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
PCI DSS v4.0 11.4

MITRE-T1557-001.risk: Cleartext HTTP administrative access is enabled on a WAN or DMZ interface. Any on-path attacker (rogue AP, ISP-level, compromised upstream, ARP/DNS spoofing on the DMZ) can intercept admin credentials and session data in plaintext, then replay or hijack the session to seize firewall control. Business impact: credential theft and adversary-in-the-middle takeover of the management plane from a hostile network segment.
steps: Identify offending interfaces: get system interface | grep -e name -e allowaccess (look for http on wan/dmz).

Remove HTTP from the interface's allowaccess, keeping only what is required: config system interface / edit <iface> / set allowaccess ping https / next / end (drop http; drop https/ssh too if this is WAN — see T1595).
Force HTTPS redirect globally so no cleartext admin path remains: config system global / set admin-https-redirect enable / end.
Enforce strong TLS on the admin GUI: set admin-https-ssl-versions tlsv1-2 tlsv1-3 under config system global.
Move the admin port off defaults if exposed: set admin-sport 8443 (document the change).
Restrict admin sources with trusted hosts (config system admin / set trusthost1 ...).
Confirm remaining admin reachability over HTTPS before you disconnect.
Re-scan and verify no interface lists http.
MITRE-T1557-001.gui: Network > Interfaces > edit the WAN/DMZ interface > Administrative Access > uncheck HTTP (leave HTTPS only if management on that interface is required). Enable HTTPS redirect under System > Settings. FortiManager: Device Manager > interface administrative access.
MITRE-T1557-001.cli:
config system interface
  edit <interface-name>
    set allowaccess ping https
  next
end
config system global
  set admin-https-redirect enable
end
MITRE-T1557-001.verify: show system interface <interface-name> | grep allowaccess  -> expect: set allowaccess ping https (no http). Global: get system global | grep admin-https-redirect -> enable. Attempt http://<ip> and confirm it is refused or redirected to HTTPS.
MITRE-T1557-001.rollback: config system interface / edit <iface> / set allowaccess ping https http / next / end. Reverting re-enables cleartext admin and the MitM exposure — do not roll back; if HTTP is needed for a legacy tool, restrict by trusted host and prefer HTTPS.
MITRE-T1557-001.impact: Non-disruptive to data traffic; no reboot. Any admin currently connected over HTTP to that interface will be dropped and must reconnect via HTTPS. Ensure you retain a working HTTPS/console path before applying to avoid lockout.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 2.1.1 (Disable HTTP administrative access / enforce HTTPS)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Network > Interfaces > Administrative access; System > Settings (admin-https-redirect)
Hardening your FortiGate — Disable unused administrative protocols
MITRE ATT&CK T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle
CWE-319 Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information

MITRE-T1562-001.risk: Neither FortiAnalyzer nor Syslog forwarding is enabled, so all logs live only on the appliance. An attacker who gains admin access (see T1078/T1595) simply clears local logs or reboots to wipe volatile memory logs, erasing the entire evidence trail of the intrusion. Business impact: no forensic reconstruction, failed breach notification/regulatory obligations, and attackers dwelling undetected. This is rated CRITICAL because it defeats detection and response for every other technique.
steps: Confirm no external logging: get log fortianalyzer setting | grep status and get log syslogd setting | grep status.
Configure FortiAnalyzer forwarding: config log fortianalyzer setting / set status enable / set server <FAZ-IP> / set upload-option realtime / set reliable enable / set enc-algorithm high / end.
Authorize the FortiGate on the FortiAnalyzer (Device Manager > add device / accept the registration).
As a secondary/independent sink, add Syslog to your SIEM: config log syslogd setting / set status enable / set server <syslog-IP> / set mode reliable / set port 514 / end.
Ensure the right event classes are logged: config log fortianalyzer filter / set severity information / set forward-traffic enable / end.
Enable per-policy logging so security events reach the collector: set logtraffic all on active policies.
Verify log flow reaches the collector and set retention/immutability on the FortiAnalyzer side.
Alert on 'log daemon' disable or configuration-change events as tamper indicators.
MITRE-T1562-001.gui: Log & Report > Log Settings > enable 'Send logs to FortiAnalyzer/FortiManager' (set IP, encryption, reliable) and/or 'Send logs to Syslog'. FortiManager/FortiAnalyzer: Device Manager > authorize the device; Log View to confirm ingestion.
MITRE-T1562-001.cli:
config log fortianalyzer setting
  set status enable
  set server <FAZ-IP>
  set upload-option realtime
  set reliable enable
  set enc-algorithm high
end
config log syslogd setting
  set status enable
  set server <syslog-IP>
  set mode reliable
  set port 514
end
MITRE-T1562-001.verify: get log fortianalyzer setting | grep -e status -e server  -> expect status: enable and the FAZ IP. Test connectivity: diagnose log test then execute log fortianalyzer test-connectivity -> expect 'Connection status: Connected' and log rate > 0.
MITRE-T1562-001.rollback: config log fortianalyzer setting / set status disable / end (and same for syslogd). Reverting removes the off-box audit trail and re-introduces the tamper risk — this control should remain on permanently; only change the destination, not the enable state.
MITRE-T1562-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Enabling realtime/reliable forwarding adds modest management-plane traffic to the FAZ/SIEM. Ensure firewall path to the collector (TCP/UDP 514, FAZ 514/OFTP) is open. No impact to data-plane traffic.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 6.1.1 / 6.1.2 (Centralized/remote logging)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Log & Report > Configuring FortiAnalyzer/Syslog logging
Hardening your FortiGate — Send logs to a secure, central location
MITRE ATT&CK T1562 Impair Defenses (T1562.006 Indicator Blocking) and T1070 Indicator Removal
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

MITRE-T1566-001.risk: With AntiVirus and Web Filter absent from most user-traffic policies, a phishing email attachment (macro-laden .docm, ISO/LNK loader) or a click on a credential-harvesting link passes through the firewall uninspected. The result is initial-access malware execution on an endpoint and stolen O365/VPN credentials, which typically precede ransomware. Business impact: account takeover, mailbox compromise, and downstream lateral movement across the estate.
steps: List user-egress policies lacking AV/Web Filter: get firewall policy | grep -e policyid -e av-profile -e webfilter-profile.

Confirm a scanning AV profile exists or create one: config antivirus profile / edit "av-block" / set feature-set flow / config http / set av-scan block / set outbreak-prevention block / end / next / end.

Confirm a Web Filter profile with FortiGuard categories: config webfilter profile / edit "wf-block" / config ftgd-wf / config filters / edit 1 / set category 26 / set action block / next / end / end / next / end (category 26 = Malicious Websites; also block 61 Phishing and the Spam URLs category — select by name or confirm the ID with set category ?).
Attach both profiles to each user policy: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set av-profile "av-block" / set webfilter-profile "wf-block".
Add SSL inspection so HTTPS phishing/download is visible: set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection" / next / end.
Verify FortiGuard AV and Web Filter subscriptions are licensed and current: diagnose autoupdate versions and get system fortiguard.
Enable logging of blocked events: set logtraffic all on the policy, forward to FortiAnalyzer.
Roll out to all internet-bound policies (script via FortiManager), then re-scan to confirm >50% coverage.
MITRE-T1566-001.gui: Security Profiles > AntiVirus and Security Profiles > Web Filter to build the profiles, then Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy > edit each outbound policy > Security Profiles > enable AntiVirus + Web Filter. FortiManager: Policy & Objects > Object Configurations > Security Profiles.
MITRE-T1566-001.cli:
config antivirus profile
  edit "av-block"
    set feature-set flow
    config http
      set av-scan block
      set outbreak-prevention block
    end
  next
end
config webfilter profile
  edit "wf-block"
    config ftgd-wf
      config filters
        edit 1
          set category 26
          set action block
        next
      end
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set av-profile "av-block"
    set webfilter-profile "wf-block"
    set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"
  next
end
MITRE-T1566-001.verify: show firewall policy <id> | grep -e av-profile -e webfilter-profile  -> expect both set. FortiGuard status: get system fortiguard (and diagnose autoupdate versions for AV/Web Filter contract + DB dates). Live test: fetch the EICAR file / browse a URL in a blocked category and confirm a block log in Log & Report > Web Filter / AntiVirus.
MITRE-T1566-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset av-profile / unset webfilter-profile / next / end. Reverting restores the phishing/malware exposure; prefer switching Web Filter categories from block to monitor over full removal if a business site is over-blocked.
MITRE-T1566-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Deep SSL inspection requires the FortiGate CA on endpoints or users see certificate warnings — deploy the CA first. Proxy-based AV can reduce throughput on high-volume policies; flow mode minimizes this. Over-aggressive Web Filter categories may block legitimate sites (tune via monitor).
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.1 (AntiVirus) and 4.2.3 (Web Filter)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > AntiVirus / Web Filter
MITRE ATT&CK T1566 Phishing (T1566.001/.002)
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 5.1 / 5.2

MITRE-T1567-001.risk: Neither DLP nor Application Control is deployed, so sensitive data can be uploaded to Google Drive, Dropbox, OneDrive, WeTransfer, or personal cloud storage without detection or policy enforcement. Business impact: insiders and malware exfiltrate PII/IP/regulated data to attacker-controlled or unsanctioned cloud accounts with no tripwire, driving breach-notification obligations and IP loss.
steps: Confirm both are absent: get dlp sensor / get dlp profile and get application list (empty).

Create a DLP sensor/profile for sensitive-data patterns (see T1048): config dlp sensor / edit "dlp-pii" / config filter / edit 1 / set proto http-post / set filter-type credit-card / set action block / next / end / next / end.

Create an Application Control profile controlling cloud storage: config application list / edit "appctrl-cloud" / config entries / edit 1 / set category 22 / set action block / next / end (22 = Storage.Backup; allow sanctioned services by signature) / next / end.

Attach both plus deep SSL inspection to egress policies: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set dlp-sensor "dlp-pii" / set application-list "appctrl-cloud" / set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection" / next / end.
On FortiOS 7.2+, use set dlp-profile instead of set dlp-sensor.
Allow-list corporate-sanctioned storage (e.g., approved OneDrive tenant) and block the rest.
Baseline in monitor, review DLP/AppCtrl logs, then enforce block.
Re-scan to confirm DLP and/or AppCtrl now cover cloud egress.
MITRE-T1567-001.gui: Security Profiles > Data Leak Prevention (sensitive-data sensor) and Security Profiles > Application Control (Storage.Backup / Cloud.IT categories) > attach to egress policies with SSL inspection. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles.
MITRE-T1567-001.cli:
config dlp sensor
  edit "dlp-pii"
    config filter
      edit 1
        set proto http-post
        set filter-type credit-card
        set action block
      next
    end
  next
end
config application list
  edit "appctrl-cloud"
    config entries
      edit 1
        set category 22
        set action block
      next
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set dlp-sensor "dlp-pii"
    set application-list "appctrl-cloud"
    set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"
  next
end
# FortiOS 7.2+: replace 'set dlp-sensor' with 'set dlp-profile'.
MITRE-T1567-001.verify: show firewall policy <id> | grep -e dlp-sensor -e dlp-profile -e application-list  -> expect DLP and AppCtrl set. Upload a dummy sensitive file to an unsanctioned cloud service and confirm a DLP/AppCtrl block log.
MITRE-T1567-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset dlp-sensor / unset application-list / next / end. Reverting restores unrestricted cloud exfiltration. Prefer relaxing DLP filter action to log or allow-listing a sanctioned service over full removal.
MITRE-T1567-001.impact: Non-disruptive to uptime; no reboot. Proxy DLP + deep SSL inspection add processing load and require the FortiGate CA on endpoints. Blocking cloud storage can disrupt legitimate workflows — allow-list sanctioned services and baseline in monitor first. DLP model changed in 7.2 (sensor->profile).
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.7 (DLP) and 4.2.4 (Application Control)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — DLP and Application Control (cloud storage categories)
MITRE ATT&CK T1567 Exfiltration Over Web Service (T1567.002 Cloud Storage)
CWE-200 Exposure of Sensitive Information
PCI DSS v4.0 1.3.4

MITRE-T1568-001.risk: A DNS Filter profile exists but has no FortiGuard DNS category filters, so it is not scoring domains by reputation. Malware using Domain Generation Algorithms (DGA) or fast-flux/newly-registered domains for dynamic resolution reaches its C2 because no category or reputation block catches the freshly-created domain. Business impact: resilient C2 that rotates domains faster than static blocklists, sustaining attacker control and exfiltration.
steps: Identify the empty profile: show dnsfilter profile and find the one with no ftgd-dns filters.

Add FortiGuard DNS category filters: config dnsfilter profile / edit "<name>" / config ftgd-dns / config filters / edit 1 / set category 26 / set action block / next / edit 2 / set category 61 / set action block / next / end / end (26 = Malicious Websites, 61 = Phishing). Also enable the 'Newly Registered Domain' and 'Newly Observed Domain' categories — the key DGA/fast-flux categories for this finding — selecting them by name in the GUI or confirming the numeric ID for your FortiOS build with set category ?.
Ensure botnet C2 blocking is also on: set block-botnet enable / next / end.
Confirm the profile is attached to outbound policies (see T1071).
Verify the FortiGuard DNS/domain reputation feed is current: diagnose autoupdate versions.
Optionally block/monitor Newly Observed Domains and Dynamic DNS categories, common DGA/fast-flux homes.
Alert on high-entropy/high-volume NXDOMAIN patterns per host (DGA indicator) via SIEM.
Re-scan to confirm the profile now has FTGD category filters.
MITRE-T1568-001.gui: Security Profiles > DNS Filter > edit the profile > under 'FortiGuard Category Based Filter' set Malicious/Phishing/Newly Registered Domains to Block > ensure Botnet C&C is on. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > DNS Filter.
MITRE-T1568-001.cli:
config dnsfilter profile
  edit "<profile-name>"
    set block-botnet enable
    config ftgd-dns
      config filters
        edit 1
          set category 26
          set action block
        next
        edit 2
          set category 61
          set action block
        next
      end
    end
  next
end
MITRE-T1568-001.verify: show dnsfilter profile "<profile-name>" | grep -A6 ftgd-dns  -> expect one or more filters with category set and action block. Resolve a known malicious/newly-registered test domain and confirm a DNS Filter block log.
MITRE-T1568-001.rollback: config dnsfilter profile / edit "<name>" / config ftgd-dns / config filters / delete 1 / delete 2 / end / end / next / end. Reverting removes reputation-based DGA/newly-registered blocking; prefer switching a specific category to monitor over deleting all filters.
MITRE-T1568-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. Blocking Newly Registered Domains can occasionally hit legitimate new sites (use monitor/allow overrides for known-good). Requires an active FortiGuard subscription for the DNS category/reputation data.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.6 (DNS Filter)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > DNS Filter (FortiGuard category filter)
MITRE ATT&CK T1568 Dynamic Resolution (T1568.002 DGA)
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 1.2.1

MITRE-T1571-001.risk: No Application Control profiles exist, so the FortiGate classifies traffic by port number alone. Adversaries run HTTP C2 on TCP/53, SSH tunnels on TCP/443, or any protocol on any non-standard port and it is misidentified and allowed. Business impact: port-based rules are trivially evaded, C2 and exfiltration hide on 'trusted' ports, and you have no application-layer visibility into what is actually crossing the firewall.
steps: Confirm no app-control profiles exist: get application list (empty).
Create a baseline profile: config application list / edit "appctrl-block".

Block high-risk/evasive categories: config entries / edit 1 / set category 2 6 / set action block / next / end (2 = P2P, 6 = Proxy; confirm IDs with set category ?). For baseline visibility of everything else, set unknown/other apps to pass and log (set unknown-application-action pass / set unknown-application-log enable) before tightening.
Enable protocol-port enforcement so mismatched app/port is caught: set enforce-default-app-port enable (blocks apps on non-standard ports) / next / end.

Attach to policies with deep SSL inspection so TLS apps are identified regardless of port: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set application-list "appctrl-block" / set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection" / next / end.
Review Application Control logs to see true app-vs-port usage, then tighten actions.
Roll out fleet-wide via FortiManager.
Re-scan to confirm an application-control profile now exists.
MITRE-T1571-001.gui: Security Profiles > Application Control > create a profile > set category actions and enable protocol enforcement > attach to policies with SSL inspection. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > Application Control.
MITRE-T1571-001.cli:
config application list
  edit "appctrl-block"
    config entries
      edit 1
        set category 2 6
        set action block
      next
    end
    set enforce-default-app-port enable
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set application-list "appctrl-block"
    set ssl-ssh-profile "deep-inspection"
  next
end
MITRE-T1571-001.verify: get application list  -> expect 'appctrl-block' present. show firewall policy <id> | grep application-list -> set. Confirm protocol enforcement flags app/port mismatches in Log & Report > Application Control.
MITRE-T1571-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset application-list / next / end and delete the profile. Reverting returns the firewall to port-only classification and the evasion blind spot; prefer relaxing enforce-default-app-port or specific categories to pass+log over full removal.
MITRE-T1571-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. enforce-default-app-port can block legitimate apps that intentionally use non-standard ports — baseline in pass+log first. Requires deep SSL inspection (endpoint CA) for HTTPS app identification.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.4 (Application Control)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > Application Control (protocol enforcement)
MITRE ATT&CK T1571 Non-Standard Port
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 1.2.1

MITRE-T1572-001.risk: No DNS Filter profiles exist at all, so DNS tunneling tools (iodine, dnscat2, DNSExfiltrator) can encode C2 and stolen data inside DNS queries/TXT records and long subdomains, exiting the network as ordinary-looking DNS. Business impact: a fully covert, firewall-traversing tunnel for command-and-control and exfiltration that bypasses web and application filtering entirely.
steps: Confirm no DNS filter profiles exist: get dnsfilter profile (empty).
Create a DNS filter with botnet + FortiGuard category blocking: config dnsfilter profile / edit "dns-block" / set block-botnet enable.

Add malicious-domain category blocking (and enable the Newly Registered / Newly Observed Domain categories by name): config ftgd-dns / config filters / edit 1 / set category 26 / set action block / next / end / end / next / end.
Attach to every outbound policy: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set utm-status enable / set dnsfilter-profile "dns-block" / next / end.
Add an IPS sensor with DNS tunneling / anomaly signatures on egress to catch the tunnel behavior itself.
Force clients through the enterprise resolver and block direct external DNS (UDP/TCP 53) plus DoH endpoints.
Alert on abnormal DNS volume/entropy per host (via FortiAnalyzer/SIEM).
Re-scan to confirm at least one DNS filter profile now exists and is applied.
MITRE-T1572-001.gui: Security Profiles > DNS Filter > create a profile (Botnet C&C + FortiGuard categories) > attach on Policy & Objects > Firewall Policy. Add DNS-tunneling IPS signatures under Security Profiles > Intrusion Prevention. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > DNS Filter.
MITRE-T1572-001.cli:
config dnsfilter profile
  edit "dns-block"
    set block-botnet enable
    config ftgd-dns
      config filters
        edit 1
          set category 26
          set action block
        next
      end
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set utm-status enable
    set dnsfilter-profile "dns-block"
  next
end
MITRE-T1572-001.verify: get dnsfilter profile  -> expect the 'dns-block' profile present. show firewall policy <id> | grep dnsfilter-profile -> set. Confirm block-botnet enable in the profile. Test a known tunneling/bad domain and check DNS Filter logs.
MITRE-T1572-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / unset dnsfilter-profile / next / end and config dnsfilter profile / delete "dns-block". Reverting reopens the DNS tunnel channel; prefer allow-listing a specific domain over removing the profile.
MITRE-T1572-001.impact: Non-disruptive; no reboot. DNS filtering is inline with negligible latency. Blocking external DNS egress requires clients use the enterprise resolver first, or name resolution breaks. Requires FortiGuard subscription for category/botnet data.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.6 (DNS Filter)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > DNS Filter; Intrusion Prevention (DNS tunneling signatures)
MITRE ATT&CK T1572 Protocol Tunneling
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 1.2.1

MITRE-T1573-001.risk: Without SSL deep inspection, adversaries wrap their C2 in ordinary TLS (HTTPS/443) and the FortiGate sees only an encrypted blob — IPS, AV, and Application Control are all blind to it. Beacons from Cobalt Strike, Sliver, or Mythic look like normal web traffic. Business impact: fully encrypted, undetectable command-and-control that survives every content-based control you have deployed.
steps: Confirm no deep-inspection profile exists/applied: get firewall ssl-ssh-profile and check each policy's ssl-ssh-profile.

Customize the editable deep profile (see T1203; the built-in "deep-inspection" is READ-ONLY — edit "custom-deep-inspection" or clone your own): config firewall ssl-ssh-profile / edit "custom-deep-inspection" / set server-cert-mode re-sign / config https / set status deep-inspection / end / next / end.
Distribute the FortiGate re-sign CA to endpoints via GPO/MDM as a trusted root.
Add ssl-exempt entries for privacy-sensitive/cert-pinned categories (finance/health).

Apply the deep profile to outbound user policies together with IPS/AV/App Control: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set ssl-ssh-profile "custom-deep-inspection" / set ips-sensor <sensor> / set application-list <appctrl> / next / end.
Enable the FortiGuard IPS botnet/C2 and SSL anomaly signatures within the IPS sensor.
Pilot, watch for pinned-app breakage, then expand to the fleet.
Re-scan to confirm deep inspection is detected.
MITRE-T1573-001.gui: Security Profiles > SSL/SSH Inspection > edit "custom-deep-inspection" (or clone the read-only "deep-inspection" profile) as a 'Full SSL Inspection (deep)' profile > attach to outbound policies alongside IPS/Application Control. Push the CA to endpoints from System > Certificates. FortiManager: Object Configurations > Security Profiles > SSL/SSH Inspection.
MITRE-T1573-001.cli:
config firewall ssl-ssh-profile
  edit "custom-deep-inspection"
    set server-cert-mode re-sign
    config https
      set status deep-inspection
      set ports 443
    end
  next
end
config firewall policy
  edit <policy-id>
    set ssl-ssh-profile "custom-deep-inspection"
    set ips-sensor <sensor-name>
    set application-list <appctrl-name>
  next
end
MITRE-T1573-001.verify: show firewall ssl-ssh-profile "custom-deep-inspection" | grep status  -> expect: set status deep-inspection. On a policy: show firewall policy <id> | grep ssl-ssh-profile. Validate on-wire re-signing by inspecting a served leaf certificate on a client (issued by FortiGate CA).
MITRE-T1573-001.rollback: config firewall policy / edit <id> / set ssl-ssh-profile "certificate-inspection" / next / end. Reverting re-blinds inspection to encrypted C2. If pinned apps break, add ssl-exempt entries instead of disabling deep inspection wholesale.
MITRE-T1573-001.impact: Non-disruptive to firewall uptime; no reboot. HIGH endpoint-impact: needs the FortiGate CA trusted on clients or HTTPS warnings appear; cert-pinned apps break unless exempted. Adds CPU load for TLS re-signing on high-throughput links; size the device accordingly.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 4.2.5 (SSL/SSH Inspection)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Security Profiles > SSL/SSH Inspection (Full/deep)
Hardening your FortiGate — Inspect encrypted traffic where policy allows
MITRE ATT&CK T1573 Encrypted Channel
CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure
PCI DSS v4.0 4.1

MITRE-T1595-001.risk: Management protocols (HTTPS/SSH/SNMP/HTTP) are reachable on a WAN interface, exposing the firewall's management plane directly to the internet. Attackers continuously scan for exposed FortiGate management and chain it with critical pre-auth vulnerabilities — this is the single most common FortiGate compromise vector. Business impact: internet-wide reconnaissance leads directly to exploitation of bugs like CVE-2024-21762 (SSL-VPN RCE) and CVE-2024-55591 (auth bypass), resulting in full device takeover. Rated CRITICAL.
steps: Identify management exposure on WAN: get system interface | grep -e name -e allowaccess and flag https/ssh/snmp/http on any wan-role interface.
Strip management from the WAN interface, leaving only what is operationally required (ideally just ping): config system interface / edit <wan> / set allowaccess ping / next / end.
Provide a safe admin path first: dedicated mgmt interface/VLAN, out-of-band, or SSL-VPN/IPsec-then-manage — verify it works before removing WAN access.

If any management must remain reachable, restrict it with a local-in policy from specific admin sources: config firewall local-in-policy / edit 1 / set intf <wan> / set srcaddr <admin-net> / set dstaddr all / set service HTTPS / set schedule always / set action accept / next / edit 2 / set intf <wan> / set srcaddr all / set dstaddr all / set service HTTPS / set schedule always / set action deny / next / end.
Constrain admins with trusted hosts: config system admin / edit <admin> / set trusthost1 <admin-net/mask> / next / end.
Ensure firmware is patched against known management/VPN CVEs (get system status for version; upgrade if vulnerable).
Enable admin-login logging/alerting to FortiAnalyzer.
Re-scan to confirm no management protocol remains on WAN.
MITRE-T1595-001.gui: Network > Interfaces > edit the WAN interface > Administrative Access > uncheck HTTPS/SSH/SNMP/HTTP (leave PING only). Add restrictions under Policy & Objects > Local In Policy. FortiManager: Device Manager > interface administrative access / Local-In policies.
MITRE-T1595-001.cli:
config system interface
  edit <wan-interface>
    set allowaccess ping
  next
end
config firewall local-in-policy
  edit 1
    set intf <wan-interface>
    set srcaddr <admin-net>
    set dstaddr all
    set service HTTPS
    set schedule always
    set action accept
  next
  edit 2
    set intf <wan-interface>
    set srcaddr all
    set dstaddr all
    set service HTTPS
    set schedule always
    set action deny
  next
end
MITRE-T1595-001.verify: show system interface <wan-interface> | grep allowaccess  -> expect only 'ping' (no https/ssh/snmp/http). Externally: an nmap of the WAN IP should show the mgmt ports filtered/closed. show firewall local-in-policy confirms the allow/deny pair.
MITRE-T1595-001.rollback: config system interface / edit <wan> / set allowaccess ping https ssh / next / end. Reverting re-exposes the management plane to the internet — do NOT roll back to internet-open management. If remote admin is required, use VPN-then-manage or tightly scoped local-in policies instead.
MITRE-T1595-001.impact: Potentially locks you out if WAN is your only admin path — establish an alternative (OOB/console/VPN) BEFORE applying. No reboot. Removing WAN mgmt drops any admin session currently connected over the WAN interface. Schedule with console access available as a fallback.
references: CIS Fortinet FortiGate Benchmark v1.2.0 — 2.1.7 (Restrict management access) and 2.2 (Trusted hosts)
FortiGate Administration Guide 7.4 — Network > Interfaces (Administrative access), Local-In policies
Hardening your FortiGate — Do not expose management to the internet
MITRE ATT&CK T1595 Active Scanning
CVE-2024-21762 (SSL-VPN OOB write RCE), CVE-2024-55591 (auth bypass), CVE-2022-40684
CWE-284 Improper Access Control
PCI DSS v4.0 1.2.1
